Type Ia supernovae (SNe Ia) are one of the most important standardizable extragalactic distance indicators with their high intrinsic luminosity ($\sim10^{42}\,$erg s–1). The physical mechanism(s) behind their explosion is a subject of intense debate. Among the most prominent proposed scenarios for this process, the double degenerate merger scenario, suggests that SNe Ia could be produced by a binary system of two white dwarfs that merge in a close-binaries orbit and experience a thermonuclear runaway at the center of the merger during the coalescence phase [1]. A number of studies over the past years have suggested that the double-degenerate scenario can successfully explain the main features of SNe Ia, including the observed light-curve plateau [2], the observed correlation between the peak luminosity and the decline rate after the maximum, as well as the observed high rate of asphericity in type Ia supernovae [3]. The double-degenerate scenario appears to be able to reproduce the observed correlation between the peak brightness of type Ia supernovae and the peak luminosity of their host galaxies. However, there are issues related to the progenitor stars that have remained open, such as those highlighted in the recent study by Kushnir et al. [4] where it is suggested that the presence of a dense circumstellar medium can affect the photometric properties of type Ia supernovae. - [1] Maoz et al. (2016). MNRAS 453, 615   - [2] Howell et al. Type III radio bursts are a type of solar radio emission that are thought to be caused by the acceleration and subsequent loss of electrons from the solar corona. These bursts are often observed in the interplanetary medium, and have been used to study the properties of the solar wind and the behavior of plasma waves in the solar system. In this paper, we explore the role of the speed and acceleration of electron beams in the triggering of interplanetary type III radio bursts. We find that the acceleration of the electron beams is a crucial factor in the emission of these bursts, and that the speed of the beams plays a lesser role. Our results suggest that the acceleration of the electron beams is a key factor in the generation of interplanetary type III radio bursts, and that the speed of the beams is of secondary importance. Type III radio bursts are a common type of solar radio emission that are observed in the interplanetary medium. These bursts are thought to be caused by the acceleration and subsequent loss of electrons from the solar corona, and are often associated with solar flares and other forms of solar activity. Type III radio bursts are characterized by their short duration and wide frequency range, and are often used to study the properties of the solar wind and the behavior of plasma waves in the solar system.

One of the key factors thought to be involved in the generation of type III radio bursts is the speed and acceleration of the electron beams that produce the emission. The acceleration of the electron beams is believed to be a crucial factor in the generation of the bursts, as it determines the amount of energy that is available for the emission process. The speed of the electron beams, on the other hand, is thought to play a lesser role in the generation of the bursts, although it may still be important in certain cases.

In this paper, we explore the role of the speed and acceleration of electron beams in the triggering of interplanetary type III radio bursts. We present results from a series of simulations that investigate the relationship between the acceleration and speed of the electron beams, and the emission of type III radio bursts. Our results suggest that the acceleration of the electron beams is a key factor in the generation of interplanetary type III radio bursts, and that the speed of the beams is of secondary importance. We find that the acceleration of the electron beams is a crucial factor in the emission of these bursts, and that the speed of the beams plays a lesser role. These findings have important implications for our understanding of the mechanisms behind type III radio bursts, and may help to improve our ability to predict and study these phenomena in the future.

We examine the properties of the dust emission from the HII region RCW 120 by utilizing the newly released 160 and 250 μm data of the Hi-GAL survey. The analysis was performed in two different ways: (i) by subtracting the dust emissivity and the temperature variation from the 160 and 250 μm data and (ii) through an iterative approach using the non-parametric methods of dust color correction to derive local \(\alpha_{\mathrm{MMS}}\) and the dust emissivity and temperature variation corrections in different directions in the field of view of the data. As a result, the physical properties of the dust in the same directions in the vicinity of RCW 120, namely; the high-mass star-forming region and the surrounding Galactic disc are discussed. Our results show that the dust emissivity as a function of the grain size and the temperature derived in the high-mass star-forming environment deviates significantly from a single \(\beta - T\) relation, thus deviating remarkably from previous single-point and integrated measurements towards Galactic and extragalactic regions. Although the dust temperature derived through the dust color relation is a little lower than that obtained via the emissivity correction, the dust temperature derived through the color correction approach is closer to the temperature derived from the 160 μm data through a dust emissivity correction in the high-mass star-forming regions. In comparison with the high-mass star-forming region, the dust temperature derived in the disc is slightly lower, while the dust emissivity of (\(8.4 \pm 0.6\)) × 10 −5 cm 2 mol −1 is nearly constant between temperatures of 20–23 K and it is less than (\(2 \pm 0.4\)) × 10 −5 cm 2 mol −1 at higher temperatures. The dust temperature and emissivity are the two most important parameters of the dust emission spectrum. The dust temperature, T\(_{\mathrm{d}}\), is a measure of the average energy of the dust grains. The dust emissivity is a measure of the amount of energy emitted by a dust grain of a certain size as a function of the grain size. The dust emissivity is also known as the dust opacity. The dust emissivity is a function of the grain size, the dust temperature, the wavelength of the observation and the dust composition.  The dust temperature, \(T_{\mathrm{d}}\ \), of the interstellar medium (ISM) in the Galaxy and external galaxies is a fundamental parameter that can be used to determine the physical properties of the dust grains. In the past decades, many methods have been developed to derive the dust temperature of the ISM. Among these, the dust color correction method is one of the most popular methods (e.g., Dunne et al. 1; Poglitsch et al. 2; Planck Collaboration et al. ; Poglitsch et al. 3; Bendo et al. 4; Dunne et al. 5; Madden et al. 6; Madden et al. 7). In this method, the dust color correction factor is derived from the ratio of the observed flux densities at two different wavelengths, \(\lambda_{1}\) and \(\lambda_{2}\). The dust color correction factor, \(\eta\), is defined as  \[ \eta = \frac{S_{\lambda _{1}}}{S_{\lambda _{2}}} \]  where. 1. We investigate the continuum limit of a model of interacting particles on a ring. Our model is based on a stochastic version of the asymmetric simple exclusion process on a lattice in one dimension and yields, in the continuum, a stochastic partial differential equation for the probability density distribution functional of the particle density. When the noise of the system is scaled appropriately, we recover the stochastic Burgers equation. We present numerical evidence for the agreement of the theoretical predictions concerning the distribution of shocks in the solutions of the Burgers equations with the properties observed in the solutions of the lattice systems. The asymmetric simple exclusion process (ASEP) is a paradigmatic model of interacting particles on a ring [1]. The model is defined as follows. Particles hop from one site to the next one at the same time with a given probability \(p\). If the next site is empty, a particle jumps with probability \(q=1-p\) and otherwise with probability \(1-q\). The particles jump in the direction of increasing site index. The particles do not interact with each other. The ASEP has been extensively studied for a long time and several aspects of the model have been investigated [2].  The main result of this paper is the derivation of the stochastic Burgers equation from the ASEP. The derivation is based on the scaling limit of the system when the number of particles tends to infinity. We show that the fluctuations of the particle density are small and therefore we can consider a continuum limit. The continuum limit is defined in such a way that the fluctuations of the particle density are scaled by a power of the number of particles.  The derivation of the stochastic Burgers equation from the AEP is based on the assumption that the particle density in the continuum limit is smooth. The assumption is justified by numerical simulations. We have investigated numerically the density fluctuations of the ASEP and of the Burgers equation. The results are shown in Fig. 1. We observe that the fluctuations of the ASEP are much larger than the fluctuations of the Burgers equation.  Figure 1: The fluctuations of the particle density (left) and of the Burgers equation (right) for \(q=0.5\). The stochastic Burgers equation has been derived from the ASEP. The derivation is based in the assumption that the particle density in the continuum is smooth. This assumption is justified by numerical simulations. The results of the numerical simulations are in agreement with the theoretical predictions.

When I wrote my Ph.D. thesis, I knew that, in order to directly observe an extrasolar planet, the observatory would need to have a resolving power well beyond          300  , and to cover hundreds of AU on the sky.          From that view, the only way to detect an extrasolar planet would be to have it close to Earth. For this reason, I believed that it was more realistic to perform the indirect detection, and thus to try to observe a          close-in planetary system. The idea that planets are more abundant in the outer regions of a star system was already suggested by the discovery of $\beta$ Pictoris b (Marois et al. 1995). This planet was found to orbit at a distance of 2.4 AU from the star $\beta$ Pictoris.    Why are planets more abundant in the outer regions?            There are two main hypotheses to explain this observation.     Hypothesis 1: The planets are more abundant in the outer regions of the star system because they are born in the outer regions.            Hypothesis 2: The planets are more abundant in the outer parts of the star system because they survive the formation process better than planets in the inner regions.             The discovery of $\beta$ Pictorus b (Marois et al., 1995) and $\epsilon$ Eridani b (Lagrange et al., 2003) provided a strong indication that planets are more abundant in the outer regions. However, a number of other extrasolar planets have been discovered in the inner parts of the star system (see for example the discovery of 51 Pegasi b, Marcy et al. 2004).    Why is the discovery of planets in the inner parts of the star system so rare?            One possibility is that the formation process is different in the inner and outer parts of the star system. This is the so-called migration scenario. In this scenario, the planets are formed in the inner regions of the star system, but are then transported to the outer regions by the interaction with the gas disk.    Why is the discovery of planetary systems in the inner parts of the star system so difficult?            The main problem is that the planets in the inner regions of the star system are very close to the host star, and thus they are not visible to our current instrumentation. This is because the telescope has to be focused on the star in order to detect the planet.    How does the migration scenario solve the problem of the detection of planets in the inner regions?             How does the migration scenario solve other problems?            In the migration scenario, the planets are transported to the outer regions of the star system. This means that the planets are much further away from the star than in the case of the standard planet formation scenario. As a result, the planets can be detected even if they are very close to the star. This is the main reason why the migration scenario solves the problem of the detection of planets in the outer parts of the star system.    How does the migration scenario explain the other problems?. The discovery of $\beta$ Pioris b and $\epsilon$ Eridani B has been a great achievement for exoplanet detection. However, the discovery of planets in the inner parts of star systems has been a great challenge for exoplanet detection. The migration scenario provides a solution to this problem. In this scenario, the planets are formed closer to the star than in the standard planet formation scenario. This means that the planets are much closer to the star than in the standard planet   formation scenario. As a result, the planets are visible to our current instrumentation even if they are very close to the star (see for example the discovery of $\beta$ Pioriis b and $\epsilon$ Eriani b). This is the main reason why the migration mechanism solves the problem of the detection of planets in outer parts of the star system. However, the migration mechanism does not explain the other problems.

In this paper, we propose a deep-reinforcement learning based multiple access control scheme for heterogeneous wireless networks. The goal of the proposed scheme is to improve the network performance in terms of network throughput, energy efficiency, and fairness among different types of users. Our proposed scheme uses a deep neural network to learn the optimal multiple access control policy based on the network state and the Q-learning algorithm to guide the learning process. We evaluate the performance of the proposed scheme through extensive simulations and compare it to traditional multiple access control schemes. The results show that the proposed scheme can significantly improve the network performance in terms of network throughput, energy efficiency, and fairness among different types of users. Wireless communication systems have become an essential part of our daily life. With the increasing demand for wireless services, the demand for wireless bandwidth and the number of wireless devices has also increased significantly. As a result, the design of multiple access control schemes for wireless networks has become a critical challenge.

Multiple access control schemes are responsible for managing the access of different users to the shared wireless resources, such as the radio frequency spectrum and time slots. Traditional multiple access control schemes, such as time division multiple access (TDMA) and frequency division multiple access (FDMA), have been widely used in wireless networks. However, these schemes are not always optimal for heterogeneous wireless networks, where different types of users with different requirements and capabilities coexist.

Deep reinforcement learning has emerged as a powerful tool for solving complex optimization problems. It has been successfully applied to various fields, including robotics, natural language processing, and network optimization. In this paper, we propose a deep-reinforcement learning based multiple access control scheme for heterogeneous wireless networks. The goal of the proposed scheme is to improve the network performance in terms of network throughput, energy efficiency, and fairness among different types of users. In this paper, we have proposed a deep-reinforcement learning based multiple access control scheme for heterogeneous wireless networks. The proposed scheme uses a deep neural network to learn the optimal multiple access control policy based on the network state and the Q-learning algorithm to guide the learning process. We have evaluated the performance of the proposed scheme through extensive simulations and compared it to traditional multiple access control schemes. The results show that the proposed scheme can significantly improve the network performance in terms of network throughput, energy efficiency, and fairness among different types of users. Therefore, the proposed scheme has the potential to be a promising solution for multiple access control in heterogeneous wireless networks.

In this work, we investigate the ability of arrays of dielectric nanospheres to guide light above the light line. The light line is a boundary in the dispersion diagram that separates propagating waves from evanescent waves. By engineering the refractive index and geometry of the nanospheres, we show that it is possible to achieve strong light confinement and guidance above the light line, leading to the possibility of creating novel photonic devices with enhanced light-matter interactions. Light guiding and confinement in photonic devices is crucial for many applications such as lasers, sensors, and displays. A common approach for achieving this is to use waveguides, which are structures that can guide light through the confinement of the electromagnetic field within a certain region. However, the performance of waveguides is limited by the light line, which is the boundary between propagating and evanescent waves in the dispersion diagram. Propagating waves can travel through a medium while evanescent waves decay exponentially and cannot be used for light guiding.

Recently, there has been increasing interest in using arrays of dielectric nanospheres as a platform for light guiding and confinement. Dielectric nanospheres have a high refractive index contrast with their surrounding medium, which allows for strong light confinement and guidance. By engineering the refractive index and geometry of the nanospheres, it is possible to achieve strong light confinement and guidance above the light line, leading to the possibility of creating novel photonic devices with enhanced light-matter interactions. In summary, we have demonstrated the ability of arrays of dielectric nanospheres to guide light above the light line. This opens up new possibilities for the design of photonic devices with enhanced light-matter interactions and could have important applications in fields such as sensing, imaging, and communication. Further work is needed to optimize the design and performance of these devices and to explore their potential in various applications.

In this paper, we review the possibility of non-minimal supersymmetric models as a Dark Matter candidate and discuss the constraints imposed by collider searches and direct searches of supersymmetric particles. We also address the problem of explaining the dark matter density, with a possible interplay of the thermal and non-thermal components. We also summarize the current constraints on the model parameters. The search for Dark Matter (DM) has been one of the most important and challenging tasks in particle physics. It is widely accepted that the DM is composed of non-baryonic particles that interact very weakly with ordinary matter.    In this paper, we discuss the possibility of non-minimal supersymmetric models as DM candidates. These models are based on the assumption that the Standard Model (SM) of particle physics is extended with additional scalar and fermionic fields that can couple to the SM particles. In the minimal supersymmetric extension of the SM (MSSM), these fields are the superpartners of the SM gauge bosons and Higgs bosons. In non-minimal supersymmetric models, the MSSM fields are supplemented by additional fields that may couple to the SM particles through the non-minimal couplings.    The non-minimal supersymmetric models are interesting from the theoretical point of view since they allow the possibility of the existence of additional neutral gauge bosons (\(Z^{\prime}\)), and additional fermions (\(N\)) that are not part of the MSSM. Moreover, these models are also interesting from the experimental point of view since they can lead to new signatures that can be tested at colliders. In this paper, we review the possibility of the non-minimal supersymmetric models as a DM candidate and discuss the constraints imposed by collider searches, direct searches of supersymmetric particles and the problem of the DM density. We also summarize the current constraints on the non-minimal model parameters.

Definitions and properties: Both the KN and dKN operators are defined in terms of a Baker-Akhiezer function, which is a special type of function that satisfies certain differential equations and boundary conditions. The KN operators act on the Baker-Akhiezer function and are defined in terms of certain integrals of this function over contours on the Riemann surface.

The dKN operators, on the other hand, act on the Baker-Akhiezer function defined on a discrete lattice, and are defined in terms of finite differences of this function. For example, the dKN operators have a discrete spectrum, while the KN operators have a continuous spectrum. Additionally, the dKN operators have a discrete set of eigenfunctions, while the KN operators have a continuous set of eigenfunctions.

Applications: Both the KN and dKN operators have a wide range of applications in mathematical physics and geometry. The KN operators are used to study a variety of integrable systems, including the Korteweg-de Vries equation and the sine-Gordon equation, and have applications in the study of soliton solutions and algebraic geometry.

They have also been used in the study of discrete differential equations and the discrete geometry of Riemann surfaces. In summary, the difference Krichever-Novikov (dKN) operators are a generalization of the Krichever-Novikov (KN) operators that are defined on a discrete lattice, rather than on a continuous Riemann surface. While the dKN operators are closely related to the KN operators and share many of their properties, they also have some important differences, including a discrete spectrum and a discrete set of eigenfunctions. We use the most current QCD sum rules [,,,,,, ] to calculate the masses. By matching the sum rule predictions with the available experimental data, we predict the masses of the ground state baryons. It is found that the masses of the charmed and bottom baryon satisfy well with those predicted by lattice QCD calculations, the differences are within 20MeV, which is consistent with the experimental observations. The predictions of the excited states are presented, which contribute to about 13% for the charmed baryon spectrum and about 20% for the ground states of bottom baryon. Benzonitrile has been detected near the southern edge of the Orion molecular cloud using the ISO Short-Wavelength Spectrometer. From the detection of the 428 and 993 MHz 13C lines detected with a position resolution of 5 arc sec, a column density for benzonitrile is derived of 1.9 -+ 1.2/+0.4 x 10/sup 13/cm/sup -2/, an order of magnitude smaller than that reported for benzonitrile in dark, diffuse cores. This result is consistent with the fact that the chemistry of benzonitrile may be different in dark clouds and dense molecular clouds. Interstellar chemistry is complicated by the presence of numerous molecules, including polyatomic molecules and small molecules such as nitriles, that are formed in the gas phase from a variety of precursor species. In particular, nitriles have been shown to be ubiquitous in dense molecular clouds (Lefloch et al. 1994). Benzonitrile has been observed in the interstellar medium (ISM) with the Infrared Space Observatory Short-Wavelength Spectrometer (ISO-SWS). It has been detected in the diffuse clouds in the Orion molecular cloud complex (Lefloch et al., 1994) and in dark clouds in the Taurus molecular cloud (Ferruit et al., 1996). The interstellar benzonitrile column density is in the range 5 x 10/sup 12/-10/sup 13/cm-/sup 2/. - Benzonitrile is a possible interstellar molecule, but the column density is a factor of 10-100 smaller than that reported for benzonitrile in dark clouds.   - It is possible that the chemistry of benzonitrile is different in dense molecular clouds than in dark clouds.

Crowdsourcing applications, such as ride-sharing and delivery services, have become increasingly popular in recent years. However, the use of these applications often requires the sharing of one's location data, which can pose a threat to an individual's privacy. This paper discusses the lack of location privacy in crowdsourcing applications and the potential risks and consequences for users. Crowdsourcing applications, which rely on a large number of users to contribute their skills, knowledge, or resources towards a common goal, have become a popular way to solve problems and perform tasks. These applications can be found in various industries, including transportation, delivery, and even healthcare.

While crowdsourcing applications can provide convenient and efficient solutions, they often require the sharing of personal information, including location data. This can pose a threat to an individual's privacy, as location data can reveal sensitive information about an individual's movements, habits, and relationships.

The lack of location privacy in crowdsourcing applications is a concern that has received little attention in the media and in research. However, it is an important issue that needs to be addressed, as it has the potential to impact the privacy and security of millions of users. In conclusion, the lack of location privacy in crowdsourcing applications is a serious issue that needs to be addressed. While these applications offer convenience and efficiency, they also pose a risk to an individual's privacy. It is important for users to be aware of the potential risks and to carefully consider the trade-off between convenience and privacy when using these applications. It is also important for crowdsourcing application developers to prioritize the protection of their users' privacy and to provide clear and transparent policies for the handling of location data.

The out-of-time-ordered correlator (OTOC) has played a central role in quantifying emergent many-body quantum chaos because of its capability of capturing entanglement across many-body local Hilbert spaces at the scrambling time, and it has been shown to scale differently for distinct quantum chaotic... The out-of-time-ordered correlator is defined as      $\mathcal{C}(t,t')=\langle \hat{\rho}(t)\hat{O}(t')\hat{\rho}(t)^\dagger\hat{O}(t')^\dagger\rangle$,    where $\hat{\rho}(t)$ is the density matrix at time $t$, and $\hat{O}(t)$ is an operator at time $t$.    The OTOC is often computed in the Heisenberg picture as       $\mathcal{C(t,t')}=\langle \hat{\rho(t)}\hat{O}(t')e^{-iH(t-t')}\hat{O}(t)^\dagger e^{iH(t-t')}\rangle$.    For a system with a large number of degrees of freedom $N$, the density matrix $\hat{\rho}$ can be written as $\hat{\rho}=\frac{1}{Z}e^{-\beta H}$, where $Z$ is the partition function and $\beta$ is the inverse temperature. The OTOC can be viewed as the time-dependent probability of finding an operator $\hat{O}$ at time $t$ in a state $\hat{\rho}(0)$, and then measuring it at time $t'$.  The OTOC is the probability of measuring an operator at time $t'$, when the operator was originally at time $t$, and measuring the same operator at time $t'$.

During laser powder-bed fusion additive manufacturing (L-PBF AM), the build plate is subjected to high plastic strains by the high-energy laser beam, which leads to the formation of a thick metal melt pool and to the formation of characteristic melt flow and solidification patterns with significant porosity. Pores and spatter as well as denudation zones, i.e. regions of the build plate where the melt has been expelled by the melt pool, are particularly detrimental for the mechanical properties of L-PBF components. In this review, the underlying physical mechanisms of the formation of pores, denudation zones and spatter will be discussed and the most important material variables determining the strength of printed components will be identified. It is demonstrated that a combination of the applied laser energy density, the layer height, the build orientation, the powder feedrate, the particle size and its distribution, as well as the ambient gas atmosphere and the heat-treatment have a strong influence on the defect formation mechanism. Laser powder-bed fusion additive manufacturing (L‐PBF AM) is an emerging technology for the manufacturing of complex metal components. In L‐PBF AM, a build plate is exposed to a laser beam, which is focused on the powder bed, and the material is deposited layer by layer. The main advantage of L‐PBF AM over conventional manufacturing processes is that it can be used to build components with complex geometries and complex topologies. Furthermore, L‐PBF AM is able to build components with a high degree of freedom in terms of material selection and geometry, and can be used to produce components with very high mechanical performance and a high degree of sustainability. However, the process also has some disadvantages. Firstly, the material is subjected to high plastic strains during the deposition process. As a result, the build plate is subjected to a large number of heat cycles, which leads to the formation of a thick melt pool and to the formation of characteristic melt flows and solidification patterns. Secondly, the melt is subjected to high temperatures, which leads to the formation of pores, spatter and denudation areas [1, 2, 3, 4, 1]. These defects can be detrimental for the mechanical performance of the component. 1. The physical mechanisms of the formation of pores, spatter and denuded zones have been studied in detail.   2. It has been shown that the applied laser energy density, the layer height and the build orientation are the main material variables that strongly influence the formation of pores, spatter and denuder zones.   3. It has been demonstrated that a combination of the applied laser energy, the layer height, the build orientation, and the powder feedrate, as well as the particle size and its distribution, as well the ambient gas atmosphere and the heat-treatment strongly influence the defect formation mechanism.   4. The results of this review provide a comprehensive understanding of the underlying physical mechanisms of the formation of defects during L-PBF AM. It can be used to develop more reliable process models and to develop new material strategies for the mitigation of defects.

The author obtains a four-point distortion theorem for complex polynomials with no zero in the interval [-1, 1] and whose derivatives have at most (deg(p) - 1) zeros in the interval, where deg(p) denotes the degree of p. This theorem applies to the class of complex polynomials having at most one zero in the interval [-1, 1] and whose derivatives have at most (deg(p) - 1) zeros in this interval. Let f(z) = ∑n=0∞anzn be a complex polynomial of degree n (n = 0, 1, 2,...), where an ≠ 0 (n = 0,..., n). We define the four-point modulus of f(z) by   M4(f) = max{|an| : |z| = 1}   where |z| = 1.   We also define the four-point modulus of f(1/z) by   M4(f(1/z)) = max{|an| / |z|n : |z| = 0}   where |z| > 0.   It is known (see [2]) that   M4(f) ≤ M4(f(1/z) + f(1/z)) ≤ 2M4(f).   The first inequality is called the four-point distortion theorem for complex polynomials.    In this paper, we obtain a four-point distortion theorem for complex polynomials of the form   p(z) = zdeg(p) + ∑n=deg(p)+1anzn   where an ≠ 0 (n - deg(p),..., n). This theorem applies to the class of complex polynomial having at most one zero in the interval (-1, 1) and whose derivatives have at most (deg (p) - 1) zeros there. Let   p(z) = zdeg (p) + ∑n=deg (p)+1anzn   be a complex polynomial of degree n (deg(p) ≥ 1), where an ≠ 0 (deg(p) + 1,..., n).   Then   M4(p) ≤ M4(p(1/z)) ≤ 2(1 + M4(p)).

In this paper, we present a formal synthesis method for control strategies of positive monotone systems. Positive monotone systems are a class of dynamical systems that exhibit monotonicity in their state and input variables, and have important applications in various fields such as biology, economics, and engineering. We show that the control synthesis problem for positive monotone systems can be cast as a linear matrix inequality, which can be efficiently solved using convex optimization techniques. We also demonstrate the effectiveness of our method through numerical simulations. Positive monotone systems are a class of dynamical systems that have received significant attention in recent years due to their widespread applications in various fields such as biology, economics, and engineering. These systems are characterized by the property that the state and input variables are monotonically related, which means that an increase in the state or input variables always results in an increase in the output variables. This property is useful in many practical situations, as it allows for the prediction of system behavior based on the current state and input values.

Despite their importance, the synthesis of control strategies for positive monotone systems has been a challenging problem due to the nonlinear and nonconvex nature of the system dynamics. In this paper, we present a formal synthesis method that allows for the efficient design of control strategies for positive monotone systems. Our method is based on the observation that the control synthesis problem can be cast as a linear matrix inequality, which can be efficiently solved using convex optimization techniques. In this paper, we have presented a formal synthesis method for control strategies of positive monotone systems. Our method is based on the observation that the control synthesis problem can be cast as a linear matrix inequality, which can be efficiently solved using convex optimization techniques. We have also demonstrated the effectiveness of our method through numerical simulations. Our work contributes to the development of a systematic framework for the design of control strategies for positive monotone systems, and has the potential to facilitate the application of these systems in various fields.

In this paper we define (graded) bundles with structure on Lie groupoids and prove some basic properties. We show that this algebraic set up is the necessary background for defining and studying the equivariant homotopy theory of graded bundles on Lie groupoids (not necessarily smooth) and use this to show that when the Lie groupoids is not necessarily smooth, the homotopy groups associated to this graded bundle satisfy different properties in comparison to the smooth setting. This paper is the first in a series of three. In [1] we introduce the notion of graded bundles on Lie groups and prove some basic properties. In [2] we show that this algebraic set up is the necessary mathematical background for defining and studying the equivariant homotopy theory and show that the homotopy groups associated to this graded bundle satisfy the same properties in comparison to the smooth setting. In this paper we extend the above results to the setting of Lie groupoids. In [3] we show that the graded bundles on Lie groupoids is the necessary background for defining and studying the graded homotopy theory of Lie groupoids. The triangular Lagrangian points, also known as the L4 and L5 points, are locations in space where the gravitational forces of two large bodies, such as the Earth and the Moon, balance the centrifugal forces felt by a smaller object, such as a satellite. The Poynting-Robertson effect, also known as radiation pressure drag, describes the process by which small particles in orbit around a star lose energy and angular momentum due to the absorption and re-emission of electromagnetic radiation. In this study, we investigate the effect of Poynting-Robertson drag on the stability of satellites in the triangular Lagrangian points. The triangular Lagrangian points, denoted L4 and L5, are positions in space where the gravitational forces of two large bodies, such as the Earth and the Moon, balance the centrifugal forces experienced by a smaller object, such as a satellite. These points are located approximately 60 degrees ahead of and behind the larger body in its orbit, and are stable locations for satellite orbits. However, the stability of these orbits can be affected by various factors, including the presence of other objects in the system and non-gravitational forces, such as the Poynting-Robertson effect.

The Poynting-Robertson effect, also known as radiation pressure drag, describes the process by which small particles in orbit around a star lose energy and angular momentum due to the absorption and re-emission of electromagnetic radiation. This effect can be significant for small particles, such as dust grains and asteroids, but is generally negligible for larger objects, such as satellites. However, the Poynting-Robertson effect can still have a measurable impact on the orbits of satellites over long periods of time.

In this study, we investigate the effect of Poynting-Robertson drag on the stability of satellites in the triangular Lagrangian points. We use numerical simulations to model the orbits of satellites under the influence of both gravitational and non-gravitational forces, and compare the results to the orbits of satellites in the absence of Poynting-Robertson drag. Our results show that the Poynting-Robertson effect has a measurable, but small, impact on the stability of satellite orbits in the triangular Lagrangian points. Over long periods of time, the effect of Poynting-Robertson drag can cause the orbits of satellites to slowly drift away from the Lagrangian points. However, the magnitude of this drift is generally small and can be compensated for through regular orbit maintenance and adjustments.  Overall, the triangular Lagrangian points remain stable locations for satellite orbits, even in the presence of Poynting-Robertson drag.

In this paper, we derive a formula, in terms of the local time, that permits to compute the Laplace transform of q-potential measures corresponding to the spectrally negative Lévy process $X$, given the law of the first excursion time below level $b$ of $X$, $L^1(X;b)$. This formula was derived in [Bienvenu and Nourdin, J. Funct. Anal., 141, 581-595] using a purely probabilistic approach. The approach proposed here is based on a functional limit theorem of Kunita-Watanabe type. Let $X$ be a spectrally negative Lévy process and $T_1(X)$ its first excursion time below level $0$. For every $b>0$, we consider the law $L^1(X;b;\cdot)$ of the first excursion time below level $0$ of $X$ conditioned to have length at least $b$. The q-potential measure of $X$ with respect to $L^1(X;b,\cdot)$ is defined by   $$V_q(L^1(X;b;\,\cdot))(\,\cdot\,)=\int_0^\infty \frac{1}{(1+q)^t} \, \mathbb{P}\left(X_t\in \,\cdot\, \big| X_0=0, T_1(X)\geq b\right) \,dt.$$    In [Bienvenu and Nourdine, J. Funct. Anal.,   141, 581- 595], the authors show that   $$V_q(L^{1}(X;b))(\,\cdot\, )=\mathbb{E}\left[ \mathbb{E}\left[  e^{-qT_1(X)} \,| \,L^1(X;b;\mathbb{R}_{>0})\right] \, |\, L^1(X;b;\mathbb R_{<0})\right].$$   This formula was derived by using the fact that the process $X$ is a Markov process and the Markov property of the local time of $X$ at $0$.    We propose a different approach to derive this formula. We use a functional limit theorem of Kunita-Watanabe type, which is a functional version of the classical Kunita-Watanabe theorem (see e.g. [Bertoin, Lévy Processes, Bertoin, 2006]). The proof of this functional limit theorem is given in Section 4.2.    Section 1: A brief introduction to the q-potential measures of spectrally negative Lévy processes. In this paper, we derive a formula, which is a functional version of the classical formula of Kunita-Watanabe, in order to compute the q-potential measure of spectrally negative Lévy processes with respect to the law of the first excursion time below level b of the process.

Suppose C is a set of smooth circles in the plane. A local rigidity is a property relating the local configuration of circles in C, and the geometric or topological type of C. A well-known property in this context is the rigidity of circle packings with respect to local deformations. Another property is the local injectivity. It is well described for circle packings in  $${\mathbb{R}}^{n}$$  . We describe the local rigidity for inversive distance circle packings in the plane with finitely many singularities. Moreover, we show that the local injectivity is a weaker condition than the local rigidity for inversive distance circle packings in the plane. $$   In this paper, we study the local rigidity of inversive distance circle packings in the plane with singularities. Let C be a set of smooth circles in the plane.   A local configuration of C is a finite subset of C. Let $S$ be a finite subset of C. We say that a local configuration $S'$ is a local deformation of $S$ if $S'\subset S+B(r)$, where $B(r)$ is the open ball centered at the origin of radius $r$.   A local deformation $S'$ is a local inversion if there exists a point $p\in\mathbb{R}^2$ and a Möbius transformation $M$ such that $S'=M(S)$.   We say that a local configuration $S$ is locally injective if there exists a small number $r>0$ such that if $S'$ is a local deformation such that $S'\subset S+B_r(0)$ then $S'=S$. $$   We show that the local rigidity for inversive distance circle packings is a stronger condition than the local injectivity. $$   A circle packing is called a distance circle packing if all circles have the same radius.  Let $\rho$ be a distance circle packing in the plane.  A point $p\in\mathbb{C}$ is called a singularity of $\rho$ if there exists a circle $C$ of $\rho$ such that $p$ is the center of $C$.  We denote by $\rho_0$ the restriction of $\rho$ to the set of smooth circles. We say that a circle packing $\rho$ is a distance circle packing with singularities if all circles of $\rho$ have the same radius and $\rho_0$ is a distance circle packing.    Theorem 1:   $$   Let $\rho$ be a locally injective distance circle packing with finitely many singularities. Then there exists a small number $r> 0$ such that if $\rho'$ is a distance circle packing with singularities such that $\rho'\subset \rho+B_r(0)$ then there exists a Möbius transformation $M$ such that the set of circles of $\rho'$ is $M(\rho)$.   $$   Theorem 2:   $$   Let $C$ be a set of smooth circles in the plane such that there exists a Möbius transformation $M$ which maps the set of circles of $C$ onto the set of circles of $M(C)$.  Suppose that $C$ is a locally injective distance circle packing with finitely many singularites. Then $M(C)$ is a locally injective distance circle packing with the same number of singularites.   $$   Theorem 3:   $$   Let $S$ be a locally injective distance circle packing with a finite number of singularities. Then there exists a small number $$r>0$$ such that if $S'$ is an inversive distance circle packing with singularities such that $S'\subset S +B_r(0)$ then the set of circles of $S'$ is the set of circles of an inversive distance circle packing with singularities $S''$ such that $S''\subset S+B_r(0$).   $$   We note that a similar result is obtained by [M. L. Sullivan](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007%2FBF00402709) in [The local rigidity of distance circle packings in the plane](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S002232930600041X). However, in his paper, the condition $\rho'\subset \mathbb{R}^2+B_r(0)$ is not assumed.

The energy stable multigrid method is a numerical technique that has been developed to simulate the behavior of local and non-local hydrodynamic models for freezing. It is particularly useful in situations where traditional numerical methods may not be efficient or accurate enough, such as when dealing with complex geometry or large temperature gradients. The method is based on the idea of using a hierarchy of grid levels to represent the solution, with each level being coarser than the previous one. The solution is then iteratively refined until convergence is achieved. In this paper, we will describe the energy stable multigrid method in detail and discuss its various applications in the simulation of freezing processes. Freezing is a common phenomenon that occurs in many different fields, including engineering, physics, and biology. It is characterized by the transformation of a liquid into a solid state, often accompanied by the release of latent heat. The dynamics of freezing processes can be complex and depend on various factors, including temperature, pressure, and the nature of the material.

Numerical simulation of freezing processes is often necessary to understand and predict the behavior of materials under different conditions. However, traditional numerical methods can be inefficient or inaccurate when dealing with large temperature gradients or complex geometry. The energy stable multigrid method is a promising alternative that has been developed to address these challenges. In conclusion, the energy stable multigrid method is a powerful tool for the simulation of local and non-local hydrodynamic models for freezing. It is particularly useful in situations where traditional numerical methods may not be sufficient, such as when dealing with complex geometry or large temperature gradients. The method is based on the idea of using a hierarchy of grid levels to represent the solution, with each level being coarser than the previous one. The solution is then iteratively refined until convergence is achieved. We have described the energy stable multigrid method in detail and discussed its various applications in the simulation of freezing processes.

We have developed high-sensitivity and high-voltage voltage actuation force sensors and actuators based on carbon-nanotube (CNT) networks. By using CNTs grown by the chemical vapor deposition method, this paper demonstrates carbon nanotube-based sensing and actuation devices. High-quality sensing and actuation functions, such as force generation, sensing operation, and deflection measurement, have been successfully demonstrated. The invention of the carbon-nanotube (CNT) network [1], which is a 1D nano-structure, has led to the development of a variety of nano-devices [2], such as field-effect transistors (FETs) [3], and sensors [4]. These devices have demonstrated promising features, such as high-speed operation [5], high-sensitivity [6], and high-temperature operation [7]. In addition, CNT networks have been applied to various fields, such as the development of sensors and actuators [8,9], and the development of new materials [10]. The use of CNTs in sensor and actuator devices has a number of advantages, such as high sensitivity [11], high resolution [12], and high temperature operation [13]. In addition, CNT-based sensors and actuators can be applied to the development of a variety of new materials, such as composites [10] and adhesives [14]. In this paper, we propose a novel approach to infer visual attention using a deep learning model, called Deep Learning-Inspired Representation Learning (DIS). DIS is a novel representation learning model that learns to represent attention by combining the information from multiple sources, including the object and the target, and the information from the user. In this way, the model learns to learn to learn to predict the target and the object. In this way, DIS can be used to predict the attention of a large number of users, and can be used to predict the attention of a small number of users. Our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance on a number of visual attention tasks, including attention prediction, visual attention prediction, and visual attention prediction. The proposed approach is applied to three attention prediction tasks, viz. attention prediction, visual attention prediction, and visual attention prediction. In the first attention prediction task, we trained a model that learns to infer visual attention from the input images, and we used a deep neural network to learn the attention distribution. In the second attention prediction task, we trained a model that learns to infer visual attention from the input images, and we used a deep neural network to learn the attention distribution. Finally, we applied DIS to a visual attention task, where we trained a model that learns to infer visual attention from the input images, and we used a deep neural network to learn the attention distribution. Experiments on two attention prediction tasks demonstrate that DIS outperforms the state-of-the-art on two attention prediction tasks, viz. attention prediction and visual attention prediction. A. J. In this paper, we study the moduli space of toric vector bundles on a smooth projective toric variety. We describe the construction of the moduli space and its relationship to the geometry of the toric variety. We also discuss some of its basic properties, including its dimension and the presence of natural compactifications. Toric varieties are a class of algebraic varieties that are defined by a special kind of fan in the vector space. They have a rich structure and have been studied extensively in algebraic geometry. One interesting aspect of toric varieties is the presence of torus-invariant vector bundles, which are vector bundles that are equivariant with respect to the action of the torus. In this paper, we are interested in the moduli space of such vector bundles, which parametrizes isomorphism classes of toric vector bundles with fixed topological invariants.

The construction of the moduli space of toric vector bundles is similar to that of the moduli space of vector bundles on a projective variety. One starts by considering the space of all toric vector bundles on the toric variety and then quotients out by the action of the automorphism group. However, there are some additional complications that arise in the toric case due to the presence of the torus action. For example, the automorphism group of a toric vector bundle may not be connected, which can lead to singularities in the moduli space.

Despite these difficulties, the moduli space of toric vector bundles has a number of interesting properties. For example, it has a natural compactification by allowing semistable toric vector bundles, which are vector bundles that are not necessarily stable but can be obtained by successive extensions of stable vector bundles. This compactification has a nice geometric interpretation in terms of the geometry of the toric variety. In this paper, we have studied the moduli space of toric vector bundles on a smooth projective toric variety. We have described the construction of the moduli space and discussed some of its basic properties, including its dimension and the presence of natural compactifications. This is an active area of research and there are many open questions that remain to be explored.

Multiwinner voting rules are used to determine the winners in elections where there are multiple seats to be filled. While there has been significant research on the complexity of such voting rules, much of this work has focused on the case where the number of winners is fixed. In this paper, we consider the complexity of multiwinner voting rules with a variable number of winners. We show that several well-known voting rules, including Proportional Representation (PR) and Single Transferable Vote (STV), are NP-hard to compute when the number of winners is allowed to vary. We also discuss the potential implications of these results and suggest directions for future research. Elections are a crucial component of modern democratic societies, as they allow citizens to choose their representatives and shape the direction of their community. One important aspect of elections is the voting rule that is used to determine the winners. In a single-winner election, the winner is simply the candidate who receives the most votes. However, in many elections, there are multiple seats to be filled, and a voting rule is needed to determine which candidates should be chosen as winners.

Multiwinner voting rules are designed to fill multiple seats in an election. There are several different types of multiwinner voting rules, including party list systems, single transferable vote (STV), and proportional representation (PR). These voting rules have been the subject of extensive research, and there is a rich literature on their properties and computational complexity.

One aspect of multiwinner voting rules that has received relatively little attention is the case where the number of winners is variable. In some elections, the number of winners may be fixed in advance (e.g., in a district with three seats, there will always be three winners), while in others, the number of winners may be determined by the results of the election (e.g., in a party list system, the number of winners may be determined by the percentage of votes received by each party).

In this paper, we consider the complexity of multiwinner voting rules with a variable number of winners. Specifically, we show that several well-known voting rules, including PR and STV, are NP-hard to compute when the number of winners is allowed to vary. We also discuss the potential implications of these results and suggest directions for future research. In this paper, we have considered the complexity of multiwinner voting rules with a variable number of winners. We have shown that several well-known voting rules, including PR and STV, are NP-hard to compute when the number of winners is allowed to vary. These results suggest that it may be difficult to design efficient algorithms for computing these voting rules in the case where the number of winners is variable.

Future research may focus on finding efficient algorithms for computing these voting rules, or on developing alternative voting rules that are easier to compute in the case of variable numbers of winners. Additionally, it may be interesting to consider the implications of these results for the design of elections and for the evaluation of different voting rules.

A systematic application of graph theoretical models is introduced for searching for new resonances, which are not easily detected in conventional physics searches, close to and possibly beyond known regions of the parameter space. The methods are successfully used to identify 20900 new resonances using the most recent data for pp→pγ+{X,Z} events collected by the ATLAS detector in collisions at the LHC ( √ s =13 TeV). Resonances are among the most fascinating and challenging phenomena in the high energy physics. They are predicted in a variety of models and can be searched for in different channels, e.g., in the production of pairs of jets or leptons. In many cases, they can be identified by a specific topology in the final state, which can be described in terms of a topology of the Feynman diagrams.   However, this method is not always successful. For example, the recent searches for a new scalar resonance in the diphoton final state by ATLAS [1] and CMS [2] using 139 fb -1 and 35.9 fb -1 of data, respectively, found no evidence for a new resonance.   On the other hand, the latest searches for a heavy vector boson Z' decaying to a pair of quarks (top quarks) in the dilepton final state by CMS [3] and ATLAS [4] using 36.1 fb -1 and 35.4 fb -1 of data, respectively, did not find any evidence for a new resonance.   In these searches, the data were analyzed by the event selection based on the invariant mass of the diphoton (dilepton) system, which was used to identify the presence of a new resonance. However, the method cannot be applied to the events with a photon (lepton) in the final state, where the invariant mass of the photon (lepton) system does not allow to reconstruct the mass of the resonance. Mammographic density is a strong predictor of breast cancer risk, and its assessment is important in clinical practice and research. However, manual visual assessment of mammographic density is subjective and time-consuming, and fully automatic calculation methods have been developed as an alternative. In this study, we compared the agreement between visual assessment and fully automatic calculation of mammographic density on a multivendor dataset. We found that the agreement between the two methods was moderate, with a kappa value of 0.53. These results suggest that while fully automatic calculation may be a useful tool, it should not be used in isolation and should be interpreted in conjunction with visual assessment. Mammographic density is the amount of fibroglandular tissue visible on a mammogram and is an important predictor of breast cancer risk. High mammographic density is associated with an increased risk of breast cancer, while low mammographic density is associated with a lower risk. The assessment of mammographic density is important in clinical practice and research, as it can help to identify women who may benefit from additional breast cancer screening or prevention strategies.

Manual visual assessment of mammographic density is the gold standard method, but it is subjective and time-consuming. To overcome these limitations, fully automatic calculation methods have been developed to quantify mammographic density. These methods use image analysis algorithms to calculate the percentage of mammographic density in an image.

However, the agreement between visual assessment and fully automatic calculation of mammographic density has not been well studied, particularly on a multivendor dataset. In this study, we aimed to compare the agreement between visual assessment and fully automatic calculation of mammographic density on a multivendor dataset. In this study, we found that the agreement between visual assessment and fully automatic calculation of mammographic density was moderate, with a kappa value of 0.53. These results suggest that while fully automatic calculation may be a useful tool, it should not be used in isolation and should be interpreted in conjunction with visual assessment. Further research is needed to determine the optimal use of fully automatic calculation in clinical practice and research.

Predicting the ratings of reviews is a valuable task for businesses, as it can help them improve their products and services. However, most existing approaches for review rating prediction rely on the availability of large amounts of labeled data from the same domain, which is not always possible. In this paper, we propose a new approach for review rating prediction that leverages aspect phrase embeddings to handle cross-domain data. We evaluate our approach on several real-world datasets and demonstrate its effectiveness in predicting review ratings across different domains. Predicting the ratings of reviews is an important task for businesses, as it can help them understand customer sentiment and improve their products and services. There are several approaches to review rating prediction, including traditional machine learning approaches and more recent deep learning approaches. However, most existing approaches rely on the availability of large amounts of labeled data from the same domain, which is not always possible. This limits the generalizability of these approaches and makes it difficult to apply them to reviews from different domains.

To address this issue, we propose a new approach for review rating prediction that leverages aspect phrase embeddings to handle cross-domain data. Aspect phrases are short phrases that express a particular aspect or feature of a product or service, such as "battery life" or "customer service." By representing these aspect phrases in a continuous vector space, we can capture the semantics of the phrases and use them to predict the ratings of reviews. In this paper, we have proposed a new approach for review rating prediction that leverages aspect phrase embeddings to handle cross-domain data. We have evaluated our approach on several real-world datasets and demonstrated its effectiveness in predicting review ratings across different domains. This approach can be useful for businesses that want to predict the ratings of reviews from different domains and can help them improve their products and services based on customer feedback.

In this paper, we study the presence and properties of triangles in K_r-minor free graphs. A K_r-minor is a subgraph of a graph that is isomorphic to the complete graph K_r. A graph is K_r-minor free if it does not contain any K_r-minor. We investigate the impact of the absence of K_r-minors on the existence and structure of triangles in the graph. We also explore the relationship between the size of the graph and the number of triangles it contains, as well as the connectivity of the graph and the presence of triangles. Triangles are one of the most basic and well-studied structures in graph theory. They are defined as sets of three vertices that are all connected by edges. In many real-world networks, triangles are known to play a key role in determining the overall structure and function of the network. For example, in social networks, triangles often represent relationships between people, such as friendship or shared interests. In biological networks, triangles can represent regulatory or signaling pathways.

Despite the importance of triangles, there are certain types of graphs where they may be scarce or absent altogether. One such class of graphs is the K_r-minor free graphs. These are graphs that do not contain any K_r-minors, where a K_r-minor is a subgraph of the graph that is isomorphic to the complete graph K_r. In other words, K_r-minor free graphs do not contain any complete subgraphs of size r.

In this paper, we study the presence and properties of triangles in K_r-minor free graphs. We aim to understand how the absence of K_r-minors impacts the existence and structure of triangles in the graph, and how this in turn affects the overall structure and function of the graph. In this paper, we have studied the presence and properties of triangles in K_r-minor free graphs. We have shown that the absence of K_r-minors can significantly impact the number and structure of triangles in the graph, and that this has important consequences for the overall structure and function of the graph. Our results suggest that the presence or absence of K_r-minors should be taken into account when analyzing real-world networks, and that further research is needed to understand the full impact of K_r-minors on the structure and function of graphs.

The Red Belly Blockchain is a relatively new distributed ledger technology that has garnered attention due to its high transaction throughput and low energy consumption. In this document, we will evaluate the Red Belly Blockchain by examining its key features, performance, and potential use cases. The Red Belly Blockchain is a distributed ledger technology that was developed by researchers at the University of Sydney and the Australian National University. It is designed to be highly scalable and energy efficient, with the ability to process hundreds of thousands of transactions per second. The Red Belly Blockchain utilizes a unique consensus algorithm called Red Belly Consensus, which allows for fast and secure transaction validation without the need for energy-intensive proof-of-work mining.

Key features:
One of the key features of the Red Belly Blockchain is its high transaction throughput. As mentioned above, the Red Belly Blockchain is capable of processing hundreds of thousands of transactions per second, making it suitable for applications that require fast and reliable transaction processing. In addition, the Red Belly Blockchain utilizes a novel consensus algorithm that allows for fast and secure transaction validation without the need for proof-of-work mining. This makes the Red Belly Blockchain more energy efficient compared to other distributed ledger technologies that rely on proof-of-work.

Performance:
In terms of performance, the Red Belly Blockchain has demonstrated impressive results in benchmarking tests. In a recent study, the Red Belly Blockchain was able to process over 300,000 transactions per second on a network of 100 nodes, with a latency of less than 1 second. These results are significantly higher than those achieved by other distributed ledger technologies, such as the Bitcoin and Ethereum networks.

Potential use cases:
The high transaction throughput and low energy consumption of the Red Belly Blockchain make it well-suited for a variety of use cases. Some potential applications include supply chain management, financial transactions, and real-time data processing. The Red Belly Blockchain could also be used in industries such as healthcare, government, and logistics, where fast and reliable transaction processing is essential. In conclusion, the Red Belly Blockchain is a promising distributed ledger technology that offers high transaction throughput and low energy consumption. Its unique consensus algorithm and impressive performance in benchmarking tests make it a strong contender in the world of distributed ledger technologies. While it is still in the early stages of development, the Red Belly Blockchain has the potential to revolutionize a variety of industries and applications.

In this study, we investigate the impact of event selection on jetlike correlation measurements in $d$+Au collisions at $\sqrt{s_{\rm{NN}}}=200$ GeV. We find that event selection can significantly affect the observed jetlike correlations, particularly at large relative azimuthal angle ($\Delta \phi$) and small relative pseudorapidity ($\Delta \eta$). These effects are most pronounced for events with high transverse momentum (pT) hadron triggers, and can be mitigated by applying more restrictive event selection criteria. Our results suggest that careful consideration of event selection is necessary in order to accurately extract jetlike correlations in $d$+Au collisions. Jetlike correlations are an important tool for studying the properties of the hot, dense medium produced in heavy ion collisions. They provide information about the energy and momentum transfer between jets and the medium, as well as the properties of the medium itself. In this study, we focus on jetlike correlations in $d$+Au collisions at $\sqrt{s_{\rm{NN}}}=200$ GeV, which provide a baseline for comparison to $A$+$A$ collisions and allow for the study of cold nuclear matter effects.

One challenge in studying jetlike correlations is the presence of background correlations, which can arise from various sources such as resonance decays, fragmentation, and instrumental effects. Event selection is a common technique used to reduce the background and enhance the signal. However, the impact of event selection on jetlike correlations has not been well studied. In this work, we aim to fill this gap by systematically investigating the effect of event selection on jetlike correlations in $d$+Au collisions. In summary, we have studied the effect of event selection on jetlike correlation measurements in $d$+Au collisions at $\sqrt{s_{\rm{NN}}}=200$ GeV. We find that event selection can significantly affect the observed correlations, particularly at large $\Delta \phi$ and small $\Delta \eta$. These effects are most pronounced for events with high pT hadron triggers, and can be mitigated by applying more restrictive event selection criteria. Our results highlight the importance of careful event selection in order to accurately extract jetlike correlations in $d$+Au collisions.

In this paper, we prove Pleijel's theorem for Schr\"odinger operators with radial potentials. This theorem states that the eigenvalues of such operators are interlaced with the zeros of the Bessel function of the first kind. We begin by introducing the necessary background on radial potentials and Bessel functions, and then proceed to the proof of the theorem. As a corollary, we also show that the eigenvalues of these operators are bounded from below. Schr\"odinger operators play a central role in quantum mechanics, and the study of their spectral properties is an important area of research. In particular, Pleijel's theorem provides a connection between the eigenvalues of these operators and the zeros of Bessel functions, which have a wide range of applications in physics and engineering. In this paper, we focus on Schr\"odinger operators with radial potentials, which are of particular interest due to their symmetry and simplicity.

Pleijel's theorem states that the eigenvalues of a Schr\"odinger operator with a radial potential are interlaced with the zeros of the Bessel function of the first kind. To prove this theorem, we will first need to review some basic properties of radial potentials and Bessel functions.

Radial potentials are functions that depend only on the distance from the origin, and they have the property that they are invariant under rotations around the origin. This means that the Schr\"odinger equation for such potentials can be written in spherical coordinates, and the solutions will also be invariant under rotations. Bessel functions, on the other hand, are a special type of function that arise in the solution of differential equations with cylindrical or spherical symmetry. They are defined as solutions of the Bessel differential equation, and they have the property that they are finite at the origin and behave as oscillating functions at large distances.

With this background in mind, we are now ready to prove Pleijel's theorem for Schr\"odinger operators with radial potentials. In this paper, we have proved Pleijel's theorem for Schr\"odinger operators with radial potentials. This theorem states that the eigenvalues of such operators are interlaced with the zeros of the Bessel function of the first kind. As a corollary, we have also shown that the eigenvalues of these operators are bounded from below. Our proof relied on the symmetry and simplicity of radial potentials and the properties of Bessel functions. This result is of importance in the study of the spectral properties of Schr\"odinger operators and has numerous applications in physics and engineering.

In this paper, we present a domain decomposition preconditioner for the solution of high-frequency Helmholtz problems with absorption. The preconditioner is based on the transmission conditions at the interfaces between subdomains and a local solver on each subdomain. We demonstrate the effectiveness of the preconditioner through numerical experiments on a range of test problems. The Helmholtz equation arises in a variety of applications, including the simulation of wave propagation in various media. In many cases, the frequency of the waves is high, leading to highly oscillatory solutions and a corresponding increase in the difficulty of the numerical solution. Absorption, which models the attenuation of waves as they propagate through a medium, can also be present in the Helmholtz equation, further increasing the complexity of the problem.

To address the challenges posed by high-frequency Helmholtz problems with absorption, various preconditioning techniques have been proposed. One promising approach is domain decomposition, which involves dividing the computational domain into subdomains and solving the problem locally on each subdomain, with appropriate transmission conditions at the interfaces between subdomains.

Numerical experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of the preconditioner on a range of test problems. This approach shows promise for the efficient solution of high-frequency Helmholtz problems with absorption.
