In a companion paper we focused on two measures of importance for directed networks, namely Katz’ information centrality and weighted node diversity, and we proposed two algorithms, a Monte Carlo based one and a random walk based one, to identify, with these measures, which nodes or links are critical for the structural properties of the whole network and for particular subgraphs to which their information or diversity were relevant, in the sense that when the centrality of a subgraph was artificially enhanced or reduced, the latter were able to preserve other structural properties, although those of the whole network were destroyed. Here, we test the algorithms on a collection of directed social networks and we consider two further variants of the Katz centrality measure. Our results show that the Katz measures and the random walk based algorithm work well for all the studied networks and also for different types of subgraph, while the Monte Carlo based algorithm is better in less well connected networks. We also show how the three algorithms can be combined to improve the identification of important nodes. In a companion paper (Tailored graph ensembles as proxies or null models for random graphs II) we proposed two algorithms to identify, with two centrality measures, which nodes or links are critical for a network. The first is a Monte Carlo based algorithm that uses a null model where the links of the original network are replaced by a random set of links that preserve the degree sequence of the original network. The second is a random walk based algorithm that uses the original network as a proxy for a random walker that explores the network. We tested both algorithms on two types of centrality measures, namely Katz’ information centrality [1] and weighted node diversity [2], and we focused on directed networks, where the directed versions of the two centrality measures were more successful than their undirected counterparts.  In the present paper, we test the algorithms on a collection of 16 directed social networks (see Table 1). The results show that the Katz measures and the random-walk based algorithm work well for all the studied networks. The Monte Carlo based algorithm is better in less well-connected networks. We also show how the three algorithms, when combined, can be used to improve the identification of important nodes. 1. Katz centrality measures and the random walk based algorithm work well on directed networks, while the Monte Carlo based algorithm is better on less well-connected networks.   2. Katz centrality measures and the random walk based algorithms are useful to identify important nodes in directed networks.   3. Katz centrality measures and the random walk based approaches are complementary to each other and can be combined to improve the identification of important links or nodes.

Self-assembled quantum dots (QDs) embedded in a planar cavity of a photonic crystal membrane are studied by means of a rate measurement of the spontaneous emission of the individual QDs. The cavity mode distribution is investigated by using a finite element method based on a local field approximation. The measured polarization dependent intensities of the radiative and nonradiative decay channels is well reproduced by the simulations. The spontaneous emission in the nonradiative channel is larger for linear polarization where the field is maximum in the center of the QD, while in the case of circular polarization it is larger for orthogonal linear polarization where the field is maximum at the perimeter of the QD. The obtained results show that in a quantum dot-coupled photonic crystal cavity the polarization dependent spontaneous emission rate can be controlled by the polarization of the excitation. Quantum dot (QD) lasers and single photon sources (SPSs) have attracted a great deal of interest in the past decade due to their potential for future applications in quantum information processing [1]. In recent years, it has been demonstrated that the emission of a single QD can be tuned to a wavelength close to the telecommunication wavelength [2]. However, in order to realize these devices, a strong coupling regime between the QD and the photonic crystal (PhC) cavity is needed [3]. In this regime, the cavity mode is strongly coupled to the QD and the QD emission is strongly enhanced [4]. The strong coupling regime can be achieved by either increasing the coupling constant [5] or by decreasing the size of the QD [6].  The emission rate of a QD in a photonic crystal cavity has been studied theoretically [7]. It has been shown that the spontaneous emission rate depends on the polarization of the excitation, which can be used to control the QD emission rate in a photonic crystal cavity [8]. In a photonic crystal membrane, a QD is embedded in a slab of a PhC membrane [9]. The spontaneous emission rate of the QD can be controlled by the position of the QD in the PhC membrane [10]. In this paper, we study the polarization dependence of the spontaneous emission of a single QD in a PhC membrane cavity. We use a rate measurement technique to investigate the polarization dependent spontaneous emission rates of the QDs. We find that the spontaneous emission rate of the QD is larger for linear polarization where the field is concentrated in the center of the QD, while in case of circular polarization it is larger for orthogonal linearly polarized excitation. The results are well reproduced by the simulations. The spontaneous emission rate of a single QD in a PhC membrane is investigated by means of a rate measurement technique. The results show that the polarization dependent spontaneous emission rate of the QDs can be controlled by the polarization of the excitation, which can be useful for quantum information processing.    Methods:    A PhC membrane is grown by molecular beam epitaxy on a GaAs substrate [11]. The membrane consists of a 100 nm thick GaAs slab, which is embedded in a PhC membrane. The PhC membrane has a square lattice of air holes with a lattice constant of 360 nm and a hole radius of 220 nm. The PhC membrane is surrounded by a 200 nm thick Al0.3Ga0.7As cladding layer and a 200 nm thick GaAs substrate. A single InAs QD is embedded in the slab of the PhC membrane and the position of the QD is determined by scanning transmission electron microscopy [12]. The spontaneous emission rate of the QDs is measured by using a rate measurement technique [13]. A continuous wave (cw) laser is used to excite the QD. The emission rate of the QD is measured by using a single photon counting system (Perkin Elmer C13367). The single photon counting system is coupled to a monochromator (Acton SP-2500) and an avalanche photodiode (Perkin Elmer SPCM-AQRH-14). The polarization of the excitation light is controlled by a half-wave plate and a quarter-wave plate.    Simulation:    The polarization dependence of the spontaneous emission rate of a QD is investigated by using a finite element method (FEM) based on a local field approximation [14]. The FEM is based on the eigenmode expansion method [15]. The simulation is performed by using the commercial software package COMSOL Multiphysics [16]. The PhC membrane is assumed to be infinite in the direction perpendicular to the membrane. The FEM is based on the eigenmode expansions for the electric and magnetic fields. The eigenmodes of the PhC membrane are calculated by using the plane wave expansion method [17, 18]. The eigenmode expansion method is used to calculate the field distribution of the photonic crystal membrane. The electric and magnetic field distribution of the eigenmodes are used to calculate the local field enhancement and the local density of states. We have fabricated an InGaAs/InP single-photon avalanche photodiodes (SPAPs) in a novel structure that allows us to realize a high-speed detection scheme without additional electrical loading. We show that the device is characterized by a pulse response speed comparable to those of InGaAs/GaAs SPAPs, while exhibiting much lower dark count rates and a smaller dark bias current increase due to the depletion of the avalanche region. We also provide evidence showing that the reduced dark current and the higher multiplication efficiency can be explained, through the calculation using a classical gain–bandwidth product (GBW) model, by the optimized surface passivation design. The results also indicate that with a proper bias level and doping concentrations, InGaAs/InP SPAPs could provide a higher avalanche gain with a lower multiplication efficiency and that this in turn helps to compensate the decrease in the avalanche gain of InGaAs/InP SPAPs.  FigureWe report the performance of InGaAs/InP single-photon avalanche photodiodes, fabricated in a novel structure that allows us to realize a high-speed detection scheme without additional electrical loading. Single-photon avalanche photodiodes (SPAPs, also called SPADs) are a promising candidate for the realization of single-photon detectors with fast time response. They offer the advantage of being able to detect a single photon with high efficiency and with a high timing resolution.   In addition, SPAPs are compatible with standard electronics and can be integrated with photonic integrated circuits (PICs).   To date, the highest performance SPADs have been realized in InGaAs/GaAs [1] and InGaAs/AlGaAs [2] heterostructures. However, the use of GaAs in SPAD fabrication is limited by the lack of compatible growth and fabrication techniques. In addition, the use of AlGaAs is hampered by the fact that the bandgap of AlGaAs is not compatible with the wavelength of the single photons that are of interest.   In this work, we have fabricated InGaAs/InP SPAPs in a novel structure that allows us to realize fast and high-speed SPADs.   We show that the device exhibits a similar timing response speed to that of InGaAs/GaAs SPADs, while exhibiting a much lower dark count rate (DCR) and a smaller dark bias current increase due to depletion of the avalanche region. We also provide strong evidence showing that the reduced DCR and the higher multiplication efficiency can be explained by the optimized surface passivation design.   We have also measured the dark current versus bias characteristics of the InGaAs/InP SPADs and have found that the device has a higher gain with a lower multiplication efficiency and that this is in turn responsible for the higher dark current and lower gain of InGaAs/InP SPADs.   The results obtained in this work indicate that the InGaAs/InP SPADs can provide a higher avalanche gain with a lower multiplication   efficiency and that this in turn helps to compensate for the decrease in the avalanche gain of InGaAs/ InP SPADs. InGaAs/InP SPADs have been fabricated in a novel structure that allows us to realise fast and high-speed SPADs.   The results obtained in this study indicate that the InGaAs/InP SPADs have a higher gain with a lower multiplication efficiency and this is in turn responsible for the higher dark count rate and lower gain of InGaAs/InP SPAD.   The InGaAs/InP SPADs could provide a higher avalanche gain with a lower  multiplication efficiency and that this in turn helps to  compensate for the decrease in the avalanche gain of InP SPADs.

The Mixtape application allows users to create personalized playlists by selecting songs from a visual map of music genres and subgenres. In this paper, we describe the methodology behind the Music Map feature and present the results of a user evaluation study conducted to assess its effectiveness. The Music Map is based on a combination of manual curation and machine learning techniques, and utilizes a hierarchical taxonomy of genres and subgenres to organize the songs in the database. The evaluation study showed that the Music Map is easy to use and helps users discover new music that they enjoy. In recent years, there has been a proliferation of music streaming services that offer vast libraries of songs from a wide range of genres and subgenres. While this abundance of choice can be a benefit to users, it can also be overwhelming and make it difficult for users to find music that they enjoy. The Mixtape application aims to address this problem by providing a user-friendly interface that allows users to create personalized playlists by selecting songs from a visual map of music genres and subgenres.

The Music Map is a key feature of the Mixtape application, and is based on a combination of manual curation and machine learning techniques. Our team of music experts has carefully selected and organized the songs in the database into a hierarchical taxonomy of genres and subgenres, based on a range of factors including musical characteristics, cultural context, and historical significance. The Music Map utilizes this taxonomy to visually represent the relationships between different genres and subgenres, and allows users to easily browse and select songs that align with their musical preferences.

In order to assess the effectiveness of the Music Map feature, we conducted a user evaluation study with a sample of Mixtape users. The study included a survey and interviews with participants, as well as usability testing of the Music Map interface. The results of the user evaluation study showed that the Music Map is easy to use and helps users discover new music that they enjoy. The visual representation of the relationships between genres and subgenres was particularly useful for users, as it allowed them to explore and find music in a more intuitive and efficient manner. Overall, the Music Map was well-received by the participants and we believe it is a valuable asset for the Mixtape application. Based on the findings of this study, we plan to continue refining and improving the Music Map in order to enhance the user experience and promote the discovery of new music for our users.

In recent years, BESIII has been very successful in providing very important information on exotic states. Besides the newly discovered $J^P=10^--$, $3^--$, $4^--$ and $X(4050)$, BESIII has accumulated large samples of events for several other hidden charm states which are interesting in their own right. As a result, some exotic and charmonium(-like) states have been newly discovered by BESIII and some candidate states have also been tentatively established. In this article, we review the latest results on exotic and charmonium(-like) states by BESIII. Exotic states are a class of particles with very unusual properties, which do not fit into the conventional quark model. They are usually identified by their large decay widths and narrow mass spectra. BESIII is a general purpose detector with a large solid angle coverage at the Beijing Electron Positron Collider (BEPCII). It was designed to study the properties of particles with different charges, masses and spins. In recent years, BESIII has been successful in identifying many exotic states and has accumulated large samples of events for several hidden charm states. The recently discovered $J^P=10_{10}^-$, $3_{10}^-$,$4_{10}^-$ and $X(4053)$ are very interesting in their own right. BESIII has accumulated large samples of events and the mass and width of these states have been measured very precisely. The newly observed $X(3872)$ is a good candidate for a $c\bar{c}$ state and it is the first charmonium state to be observed with a mass around 3.872 GeV. Some other hidden charm states, such as $X(3915)$ and $X(4160)$, are also very interesting in their own right.

We consider a four-dimensional stationary black hole with a horizon of positive Schwarzschild curvature immersed in an electrovac space-time with a 3+1 form of electromagnetic energy-momentum tensor as the source. We study geodesic motion of electromagnetic waves and find that only particles with radial energy higher than a certain threshold can escape the black hole. We also study the motion of radiation in the neighbourhood of the horizon and establish conditions for the ringdown of a perturbation wave. It is found that a perturbation wave with a frequency not larger than the minimal frequency of the emitted photons has a frequency gap. This phenomenon is similar to the one observed previously in the background of Schwarzschild black hole surrounded by massless particles - a wave emitted by a particle with a specific energy cannot pass over. We consider a stationary black hole with a horizon of positive Schwarzschild   curvature immersed in an electrovac space-time and study the motion of   electromagnetic waves in the neighbourhood of the horizon. We find that   only particles with radial energy higher than a certain critical value   can escape the black hole.    The paper is organized as follows. In Section 2 we consider the geodesic   motion of photons and particles in the electrovac space-time. In Section 3   we investigate the motion of electromagnetic waves in the neighbourhood   of the horizon and find the frequency gap in the ringdown of a perturbation   wave. Section 4 is devoted to the conclusions. The obtained results can be used for the analysis of ringdown of gravitational waves in the background of a stationary black hole immersed in an electrovac space-time. In this document, we study the hyperalgebra of the loop algebra ${\widehat{\frak{gl}}_n}$. We begin by introducing the necessary background on loop algebras and hyperalgebras, and then proceed to define and analyze the hyperalgebra of ${\widehat{\frak{gl}}_n}$. We conclude by discussing some of the main properties and applications of this hyperalgebra. Loop algebras are a class of infinite-dimensional Lie algebras that are closely related to the Kac-Moody algebras and affine Lie algebras. They are defined as the set of Laurent polynomials in a formal variable $z$ with coefficients in a finite-dimensional Lie algebra ${\frak{g}}$. Specifically, the loop algebra ${\widehat{\frak{g}}}$ is defined as ${\widehat{\frak{g}}=\frak{g}[z,z^{-1}]}$.

Hyperalgebras, on the other hand, are a generalization of Lie algebras that involve higher-order brackets. These brackets are defined using the concept of a multilinear map, which assigns a multilinear function to each element of the hyperalgebra. In general, hyperalgebras have a rich structure that includes both algebraic and geometric aspects.

In this document, we focus on the hyperalgebra of the loop algebra ${\widehat{\frak{gl}}_n}$. This hyperalgebra is particularly interesting because it is closely related to the representation theory of ${\widehat{\frak{gl}}_n}$, as well as to the theory of vertex operator algebras. In conclusion, the hyperalgebra of the loop algebra ${\widehat{\frak{gl}}_n}$ is a rich and interesting mathematical object with many applications in representation theory and vertex operator algebras. Further study of this hyperalgebra is likely to yield important insights into the structure and representation theory of ${\widehat{\frak{gl}}_n}$.

Here, we study acoustic properties of piezoelectric composites with microstructured layers with a negative Poisson's ratio. We perform numerical and theoretical analysis of these structures. We find (1) that a microstructured piezoelectric layer with a negative Poisson's ratio can provide high impedance value for an incident acoustic wave in a wide range of frequencies and wavenumbers, and (2) that the wave impedance of these structures is higher than that of a homogeneous piezoelectric layer with the same relative permittivity at low frequencies and the same thickness of the layers. We believe that these properties of this material have practical applications in various acoustic sensors such as acoustic emission sensors and sonar arrays. It should be noted that since the materials of the piezoelectric composites, especially the piezoelectric polymer composites, exhibit nonlinear response to incident acoustic waves, we model an effective medium model to analyze the acoustic behavior of this material. Furthermore, we suggest that the effective moduli of the effective medium of the microstructured piezoelectric layers differ significantly from those of their constituents and should be used in further analysis of acoustic properties of these structures. © 2012 Wiley Periodicals, Inc. Acoustic metamaterials are artificial materials with unusual acoustic properties such as negative refraction, invisibility cloaks, and superlensing. The acoustic metamaterials are usually fabricated by using periodic structures in the form of two-dimensional (2D) or three-dimensional (3D) lattices. In this regard, the 2D periodic arrays of elastic inclusions with the negative Poisson's ratio (NPR) have been studied extensively, and these structures can exhibit negative refraction, negative mass density, and negative stiffness [1–4]. Furthermore, acoustic metamaterials with the negative Poisson's ratio have been fabricated using 2D periodic arrays of elastic inclusions [5]. In addition, the acoustic metamaterials with the negative Poisson's ratio can be fabricated using 3D periodic arrays of elastic inclusions [6]. In addition, the acoustic metamaterials with negative Poisson's ratio can also be fabricated using 3D periodic arrays of piezoelectric inclusions [7].  In recent years, it has been shown that the acoustic metamaterials can be used in various applications such as acoustic sensors, acoustic metamaterials, and acoustic cloaks [8]. In this regard, the acoustic metamaterials with the negative Poisson' ratio have been proposed for applications in acoustic sensors [9]. In particular, the acoustic metamaterials with the negative Poisson' s ratio have been suggested as the acoustic sensors for the detection of cracks in structures [10]. In addition, the acoustic metamaterials with NPR have been proposed for acoustic cloaking applications [11, 12].  In this work, we study acoustic properties of piezoelectric composites with the microstructured layers with the negative Poisson's ratio. We analyze the acoustic properties of these structures using the effective medium theory. We find that the microstructured piezoelectric layers with the negative Poisson's ratio can provide high impedance values for an incident acoustic wave in a wide range (0–20 kHz) of frequencies and wavenumbers, and that the impedance of these structures is higher than that of the homogeneous piezoelectric layers with the same relative permittivity at low frequencies and equal thickness of the layers. We believe that the acoustic properties of this material have practical applications in various types of acoustic sensors such as acoustic emission sensors and sonar array. The acoustic properties of piezoelectric composites with microstructured layers can be used in various acoustic sensors such as acoustic emission sensors, sonar array, and acoustic cloaks. In this paper, we present a new approach to deriving the Seiberg-Witten equations from the perspective of Fedosov deformation quantization of endomorphism bundles. Our approach is based on the idea of using Fedosov's formalism to quantize the symplectic structures on moduli spaces of flat connections, and then using these quantized symplectic structures to obtain the Seiberg-Witten equations as quantum mechanical equations of motion. We show that this approach leads to a natural and intuitive derivation of the Seiberg-Witten equations, and provides a new perspective on their geometric meaning. The Seiberg-Witten equations are a set of nonlinear partial differential equations that play a central role in the study of four-dimensional gauge theories with N=2 supersymmetry. These equations have been extensively studied in the context of mathematical physics and have been used to derive various important results, such as the existence of smooth four-dimensional manifolds with special holonomy and the classification of four-dimensional topological field theories. Despite their importance, the derivation of the Seiberg-Witten equations is not well-understood and various approaches have been proposed in the literature.

In this paper, we present a new approach to deriving the Seiberg-Witten equations based on the idea of using Fedosov's formalism to quantize the symplectic structures on moduli spaces of flat connections. Fedosov's formalism is a method for deforming the algebra of functions on a symplectic manifold in such a way that the resulting deformed algebra is equipped with a new, noncommutative product that is compatible with the symplectic structure. This approach has been successfully applied to a wide range of problems in mathematical physics, including the quantization of gauge theories.

We show that by applying Fedosov's formalism to the moduli spaces of flat connections on four-dimensional manifolds, it is possible to obtain the Seiberg-Witten equations as quantum mechanical equations of motion. Our approach provides a natural and intuitive derivation of the Seiberg-Witten equations, and gives a new perspective on their geometric meaning. Our approach is based on the idea of using Fedosov's formalism to quantize the symplectic structures on moduli spaces of flat connections, and then using these quantized symplectic structures to obtain the Seiberg-Witten equations as quantum mechanical equations of motion. We have shown that this approach leads to a natural and intuitive derivation of the Seiberg-Witten equations, and provides a new perspective on their geometric meaning. This work represents a significant contribution to the understanding of the Seiberg-Witten equations and their role in the study of four-dimensional gauge theories with N=2 supersymmetry.

In this paper, we study the problem of learning over multitask graphs, where each task is represented as a node in a graph and the edges represent shared information or dependencies between tasks. We focus on the stability of learning algorithms in this setting, specifically analyzing the impact of the graph structure on the convergence and generalization performance of the learning algorithm. We propose a novel stability analysis framework and demonstrate its effectiveness through experiments on synthetic and real-world multitask graph datasets. Multitask learning has gained increasing attention in recent years as a way to improve the performance of machine learning models on related tasks. In this setting, a single model is trained to perform multiple tasks simultaneously, leveraging shared information between tasks to improve generalization. However, the structure of the relationships between tasks can significantly impact the performance of multitask learning algorithms. In particular, the multitask graph, which represents the dependencies between tasks, plays a crucial role in determining the stability of the learning algorithm.

In this paper, we aim to provide a better understanding of the stability of learning algorithms over multitask graphs. We focus on the impact of the graph structure on the convergence and generalization performance of the learning algorithm, and propose a novel stability analysis framework to quantify this effect. By analyzing the stability of the learning algorithm, we can better understand how the multitask graph affects the performance of the model and identify graph structures that are more conducive to successful multitask learning. In this paper, we have presented a novel stability analysis framework for learning over multitask graphs. Through experiments on synthetic and real-world multitask graph datasets, we have demonstrated the effectiveness of our framework in quantifying the stability of learning algorithms in this setting. Our results provide valuable insights into the impact of the multitask graph on the performance of learning algorithms, and can be used to guide the design of multitask learning systems. In future work, we plan to extend our analysis to a wider range of learning algorithms and multitask graph structures, and to explore the use of our stability analysis framework for active learning and transfer learning scenarios.

In this paper, we consider nonlocal elliptic problems of the form
$$ (-\Delta)^s u + V(x) u = f(x,u) $$
where $(-\Delta)^s$ is the fractional Laplacian, $V(x)$ is a potential function, and $f(x,u)$ is a nonlinear reaction term. We study the stability of the Morse index, defined as the number of negative eigenvalues of the linearized operator at a non-trivial solution, under perturbations of the potential function $V(x)$. We prove that for certain classes of nonlinearities $f(x,u)$, the Morse index is stable under small perturbations of $V(x)$. This result has important implications for the study of nonlocal elliptic equations and their applications in various fields. Nonlocal elliptic equations, also known as fractional Laplacian equations, have attracted a lot of attention in recent years due to their wide range of applications in various fields, including physics, biology, and finance. One important aspect of the study of nonlocal elliptic equations is the investigation of the stability of their solutions under perturbations of the coefficients. In particular, the Morse index, defined as the number of negative eigenvalues of the linearized operator at a non-trivial solution, is an important topological invariant that characterizes the local behavior of the solution.

$$ (-\Delta)^s u + V(x) u = f(x,u) $$
and studied the stability of the Morse index under perturbations of the potential function $V(x)$. We have proven that for certain classes of nonlinearities $f(x,u)$, the Morse index is stable under small perturbations of $V(x)$. This result has important implications for the study of nonlocal elliptic equations and their applications in various fields. We propose a simple method of evaluation of the corotation radius in a spiral galaxy on the basis of the observed kinematical and structural parameters of the stellar component of a galaxy. The method is described briefly. The corotation radius (CR) is an important parameter for the interpretation of the results of the galactic dynamical studies. It is the radius where the angular velocity of the rotation of the gas in the spiral arms coincides with the angular velocity of the rotation of the stars in the disk of a galaxy. This is an important condition for the existence of the spiral structure in a galaxy. The CR is also an important parameter for the determination of the mass of the galactic disk. - the method of the evaluation of the CR on the basis of the observed parameters of the stellar disk is simple and can be applied to the determination of the CR in any galaxy.   - the method is based on the assumption that the observed stellar disk of a galaxy is a thin disk, i.e. that the vertical scale height of the stellar disk is small as compared with the radial scale length.   - the method can be used for the determination of the CR in the galaxies with the different morphological types.

Narrowband oscillations are rhythmic patterns of neural activity that occur at specific frequencies within a narrow range. These oscillations have been observed in various brain regions and have been associated with a range of cognitive and behavioral functions. Asynchronous neural activity, on the other hand, refers to the non-rhythmic, irregular firing of individual neurons. In this review, we discuss the relationship between asynchronous neural activity and narrowband oscillations and the potential mechanisms underlying this relationship. Oscillations in the brain are thought to play a key role in coordinating neural activity and communication between different brain regions. Narrowband oscillations, in particular, have been the focus of much research due to their strong correlation with various cognitive and behavioral processes. These oscillations are characterized by their narrow frequency range and are typically observed in the gamma (30-80 Hz), beta (13-30 Hz), and alpha (8-12 Hz) frequency bands.

Asynchronous neural activity, on the other hand, refers to the irregular firing of individual neurons that is not synchronized with the activity of other neurons. While asynchronous activity has traditionally been viewed as noise or interference in the brain, more recent research has suggested that it may play a functional role in brain function. The relationship between asynchronous neural activity and narrowband oscillations is a complex and multifaceted one. While asynchronous activity can disrupt or interfere with narrowband oscillations, it can also modulate their strength and frequency. Further research is needed to fully understand the role of asynchronous activity in the generation and regulation of narrowband oscillations and its potential impact on brain function.

The chemical composition of evolved stars in the open cluster IC 4651 was studied using high-resolution spectroscopy. The cluster was found to have a metallicity similar to the Sun, with a mean value of [Fe/H] = -0.05 ± 0.03. The presence of several evolved stars in the cluster allowed for the analysis of their chemical abundance patterns, which showed a general trend of increasing s-process element enhancements with increasing evolutionary stage. This suggests that the s-process is a significant contributor to the chemical evolution of these stars. Additionally, the study found that the chemical compositions of the evolved stars in IC 4651 are consistent with those of field stars of similar evolutionary stage, indicating that the cluster has not undergone any unusual chemical evolution processes. Open clusters are groups of stars that are born at the same time and place and therefore have similar ages and chemical compositions. The study of open clusters allows for the understanding of the chemical evolution of stars in these systems, as well as the history of the Galactic disk. IC 4651 is an open cluster located in the constellation Scorpius, and has an estimated age of 1.2 billion years. In this study, we aim to investigate the chemical composition of evolved stars in IC 4651 and its implications for the chemical evolution of the cluster. The chemical composition of evolved stars in the open cluster IC 4651 was found to be similar to that of the Sun and field stars of similar evolutionary stage. The presence of s-process element enhancements in these stars suggests that the s-process is a significant contributor to the chemical evolution of these stars. These findings indicate that IC 4651 has not undergone any unusual chemical evolution processes and is similar to other open clusters in the Galactic disk. Further studies of open clusters like IC 4651 will provide valuable insights into the chemical evolution of the Galactic disk and the processes that shape the chemical abundances of stars.

In this paper, we present a free field realization of the current superalgebra $gl(m|n)_k$ in terms of bosonic and fermionic fields. We demonstrate that this realization satisfies the defining relations of the superalgebra and discuss the properties and applications of this construction. Current superalgebras, also known as Kac-Moody superalgebras, are a generalization of Lie superalgebras that have been studied in the context of mathematics and theoretical physics. In particular, the current superalgebra $gl(m|n)_k$ is an extension of the general linear superalgebra $gl(m|n)$ by an abelian current algebra, and it is of interest in the study of two-dimensional conformal field theories and integrable models.

One way to study current superalgebras is through the use of free field realizations, which express the generators of the superalgebra in terms of fields that satisfy certain commutation or anticommutation relations. In this paper, we construct a free field realization of $gl(m|n)_k$ in terms of bosonic and fermionic fields, and we show that this realization satisfies the defining relations of the superalgebra.

We then discuss the properties and applications of this construction, including its connection to integrable models and its potential use in the study of conformal field theories. In this paper, we have presented a free field realization of the current superalgebra $gl(m|n)_k$ in terms of bosonic and fermionic fields. We have demonstrated that this realization satisfies the defining relations of the superalgebra and discussed its properties and applications. This construction provides a useful tool for studying the properties and applications of current superalgebras in mathematics and theoretical physics.

Self-knowledge distillation (SLL) is a powerful method for personalized diagnosis and treatment of a wide range of medical conditions. However, the traditional SLL approach relies on the assumption that the individual has a self-aware knowledge of the diagnosis. In this paper, we propose a new SLL approach that uses self-knowledge to self-correct for misclassification errors. We first introduce a new self-aware model, called the self-knowledge distillation model, that learns the label of the patient from the knowledge of the individual. Then, we develop a novel SLL method that uses the knowledge of the individual to automatically classify the self-aware model. Finally, we present a novel SLL method that uses the knowledge of the individual to learn the self-aware model to self-correct for misclassification errors. Our approach achieves high performance in the most common medical conditions, including heart disease, lung cancer, and multiple sclerosis. In this paper, we study the relationship between Galois gerbs and the Lefschetz number formula for Shimura varieties of Hodge type. Using techniques from algebraic geometry and representation theory, we show that the Lefschetz number formula can be expressed in terms of Galois gerbs, which are certain geometric objects associated with the action of the absolute Galois group on the cohomology of the Shimura variety. As a consequence, we obtain a new proof of the Lefschetz number formula for these varieties. Shimura varieties are important objects in arithmetic geometry, which are defined as quotients of the product of a reductive algebraic group and a symmetric space by a lattice in the group. They have a rich structure and are connected to many areas of mathematics, including the Langlands program, the Riemann hypothesis, and the study of abelian varieties. The Lefschetz number formula is a fundamental result in the study of these varieties, which relates the number of fixed points of an automorphism to the trace of the induced action on the cohomology of the variety.

Galois gerbs are geometric objects associated with the action of the absolute Galois group on the cohomology of a variety. They provide a way to encode the action of the Galois group on the cohomology in a geometric way, and have proven to be a useful tool in the study of Shimura varieties. In this paper, we explore the relationship between Galois gerbs and the Lefschetz number formula for Shimura varieties of Hodge type. We have shown that the Lefschetz number formula for Shimura varieties of Hodge type can be expressed in terms of Galois gerbs. This allows us to provide a new proof of the formula, which is based on the geometry of Galois gerbs rather than the representation theory of the absolute Galois group. Our results provide a new perspective on the Lefschetz number formula for these varieties and open up the possibility for further study of the relationship between Galois gerbs and the arithmetic of Shimura varieties.

Based on the general splitting function formalism, the vacuum and matter part of the angular dependence of angular distributions (θ, φ ) of the 3-jet (or 4-jet) state is studied at O( α 2 s ) in QCD. The vacuum part of the higher order contributions to (θ, φ ) and (ψ, φ ) distributions is derived, to O( α 3 s ) by using the two phase approximation for the soft gluon emission. The matter part of the angular distributions is obtained in the same way, but in the two phase approximation for soft gluon emission in QCD at finite density ( p 0 ). In vacuum, the contribution of order O α 2 s to (θ, φ ) distribution is positive. That of order O α 3 s to (ψ, φ ) distribution has a contribution in the vicinity of 4–70° depending on the color-octetness of the participating partons. At finite density, only the color-singlet initial-state partons are involved. At large values of ( θ and φ ), the contribution from the real gluons to (θ, φ ) distribution is negative. As ( θ and φ ) → 90°, φ gets the following values: φ =2⋅90° for gluon and C ∗ gq =2⋅63 (with corresponding C ∗ qq =−0⋅79 and non-zero singlet initial-state and jet C ∗ gg =0⋅24). For jets whose momenta are in a plane perpendicular to the jet–beam direction, the angular distribution has a local minimum of the type 345° ∼ 545°. In dense QCD matter, the contributions of order O α 2 s to (θ, φ ) distribution are all negative for the initial gluons and to the order O α 3 s, for the colored partons, the contributions are positive at θ → 90°. The effect of different initial parton flavors and color-octetness is studied as well. We point out that the soft gluon emission in vacuum leads to a finite asymmetry in the distributions with respect to φ (if the jet axis is chosen properly), while in the dense QCD matter the effect of soft gluon emission vanishes due to the absence of real gluon emission. - The angular distributions (θ, φ ) and 2D angular distributions (ψ, φ ) of the 3- or 4-jet final states are widely used in the analysis of jet events at hadron colliders. In QCD, the angular distributions are related to the transverse momentum distribution of the emitted gluon or parton. The transverse momentum distribution is determined by the emission of soft gluons, which is the leading contribution at large angles (θ). The soft gluon emission is treated by the two-phase approximation (TPA). The TPA is a useful approximation in the context of the study of QCD in a dense QCD medium, where the emission of soft gluons can be described in terms of the two-phase approximation. The study of dark energy model as dark energy component with constant equation of state parameter is not well defined. In this paper, an attempt is made to consider the new variable modified Chaplygin gas model with a variable parameter. We consider the model which can explain the observed acceleration of the universe at late times. Using observational data including luminosity distance data, baryonic acoustic oscillations data, and Type Ia supernova data, we constrain the model's parameters. We use the Markov chain Monte Carlo method to analyze the data. We found that the new variable model may fit the observational data better than the modified Chaplygin model. Also, we conclude that our model in general fits the data better than the Lambda model. -  The standard model of cosmology is the \(\Lambda\)CDM model [1]. The \(\Lambda\)CDM model is based on the assumption that the universe is filled with a single component, namely, dark energy. The cosmological constant (\(\Lambda\)) is the simplest candidate for dark energy, but it suffers from two major problems, namely, the cosmological constant problem and the coincidence problem. In order to overcome these problems, a new class of dark energy models is proposed [2, 3].    The Chaplygin gas model [4, 5] is an alternative to the cosmological constant. The Chaplygin gas model is characterized by a constant equation of state parameter (\(\omega_{\rm{CH}}=-1/3\)). The Chaplygin gas model can provide a good fit to the cosmological data and has been used in many studies [6, 7, 8,9]. However, the Chaplygin gas model has some problems. The model can only be applied to the late universe. It is well known that the Chaplygin gas model cannot explain the late-time acceleration of the universe.    In order to explain the late-time acceleration of the universe, many authors proposed the modified Chaplygin gas model [10, –11]. The modified Chaplygin gas model is characterized by a variable equation of state parameter \(\omega_{\rm{mCH}}\). The modified Chaplygin gas model can explain the late-time acceleration of the universe and can be applied to the early universe. The modified Chaplygin gas model has been applied in many studies [12, –].    In this paper, we consider the new variable modified Chaplygin gas (NVMCG) model with a variable equation of state parameter. We consider the model which can explain both the early-time acceleration of the universe and the late-time acceleration of the universe. We consider the NVMCG model with a variable equation of state parameter in the context of the \(\Lambda\)CDM and \(\Lambda\)CDM+\(\rm{r}\)CDM models. The \(\Lambda\)\(\rm{r}\)\(\rm{CDM}\) model is a combination of the \(\Lambda\) CDM model and the \(\rm{r}\) CDM model [13, ]. The \(\Lambda\rm{r}\)\({\rm{CDM}}\) model is also used in [14, 15].    In this paper, observational data including luminosity distance data, baryonic acoustic oscillation data, and Type Ia supernova data are used. We use the Markov chain Monte Carlo method (MCMC) to analyze the data. -  The NVMCG model can fit the observational data better than the modified Chapleygin model. Also, the NVMCG model in general fits the data better than the \(\Lambda\)CDM, \(\Lambda\)CDM+rCDM, and \(\Lambda\)CDM models. The generalized domain wall equations in the 3+1 dimensional reduced generalized Born-Infeld- f(T) gravity theory with a Maxwell electro-vac background in which the tension of the domain wall is a free function of the (3+1)-dimensional coordinate are derived. We have checked that when the tension of the domain wall does not depend on the x0-coordinate the solutions are exactly the same as the corresponding solutions in the context of Einstein gravity. However, when the tension of the domain wall depends on the x 0 -coordinate there is an extra contribution to the domain wall energy density (pressure). We have numerically obtained the domain wall solutions in the form of brane and studied the influence of the extra term on the profile of the domain wall. The results are summarized and discussed in detail. The generalized domain wall equations in the 3+ 1 dimensional reduced Born-Infeld- $f(T)$ gravity theory with a Maxwell electro-vac background have been studied in the context of Einstein gravity 1. In the present work we shall investigate the generalized domain wall equations in the 3+3 dimensional reduced Born-Infeld- $F(T)$ gravity theory with a Maxwell electromagnetic background.    The paper is organized as follows: In Sec. 2 we present the field equations for the reduced Born-Infeld- $F (T)$ gravity theory. In Sec. 3 we obtain the generalized domain wall equations in the 3 + 3 dimensional reduced Born-Infeld- $$F(T)$$ gravity theory with a Maxwell electro-vac background. In Sec. 4 we present the numerical solutions of the domain wall equations and in Sec. 5 we discuss the results and draw our conclusions. The authors have investigated the generalized domain wall equations in the 3-dimensional reduced Born-Infeld-$F(T)$ gravity theory with Maxwell electro-vac background. It has been shown that the tension of the domain wall is a free parameter and the solutions are exactly the same as the corresponding ones in the context of Einstein gravity. However, there is an extra contribution to the domain wall tension which depends on the x 0 -coordinate. The numerical solutions of the domain wall equations are presented.

Argumentation frameworks (AAFs) is the most widely studied and researched formalism for qualitative reasoning in Artificial Intelligence. In this paper, we investigate the integration of answer-set programming (ASP) into the formalism of AAFs. We propose a novel method to encode AAF argumentation problems and to obtain their solutions based on ASP-style answer sets. Our new approach, called ASP-ArgA, extends the general ASP encoding methodology, called ASPEN, which supports answer to set programming problems. Our new approach contains the following peculiarities: we have designed a new framework for the integration of ASP into the formalism of AAFs in order to solve the problem of identifying all possible stable extensions for a given AAF; ASP-ArgA is the first work on the integration of ASP into the formalism of AAFs that presents an approach to the encoding of argumentation problems based on the definition of a class of ASP-answer sets for AAF argumentation problems. We implemented ASP-ArgA using ASP4J with a new translation function which is defined for the encoding of stable (resp. preferred, complete) extensions in AAFs based on the well-known definitions of these types of extensions for AAFs and an ASP-solver. Argumentation frameworks (AAFs) [1] are the most widely studied and researched formalism for qualitative argumentation in Artificial Intelligence. AAF argumentation problems are defined by an AAF and a set of arguments, which are used to express the information about the domain of interest. Each argument in the AAF represents a statement which is considered to be either true or false in the domain of interest. The set of arguments is used to express the information about the domain of the interest. An AAF argumentation problem can be represented as an undirected graph where the nodes represent the arguments and the edges represent the attacks between them. An argument is a source of attack if it is attacked by another argument. The set of arguments that are not sources of attack is called the support of the argument. The goal of an AAF argumentation problem is to find a stable extension of the AAF. In other words, the goal of an AAF argumentation problem is the set of all arguments that can be added to the AAF in order to obtain a stable extension. The contribution of the paper is twofold. First, we have designed a new framework for the integration  of answer-set programming (ASP) [2] into the formalism of AAFs in order to identify all possible stable extensions for a given AAF. Our new approach, called ASP-Arg A, extends the general ASP encoding methodology, called  ASPEN, which supports answer to set programming (ASP) problems. Our new approach contains the following peculiarities. We have designed a new framework for the integration of answer-set programming (ASPs) into the formalism of AAFs in order to obtain a stable extension for a given AAF. Our new approach contains the following peculiarities. First, we have designed a new framework that can be used for the integration of ASP into the formalism of AAF, which can be used for the identification of all possible stable extensions for a given AAF, i.e., ASP-ArgA. Second, we have proposed a translation function for the encoding of stable (resp. complete, preferred) extensions in AAFs based on the well known definitions of these types of extensions for AAFs. Finally, we have implemented ASP-ArgA using ASP4J [3] with a new translation function which is defined for stable (resp. complete, preferred) extension in AAFs based on the well-known definition of these types of extensions for AAFs and a solver.

There is a strong motivation to use large cell metal foams for accelerating beam liners which are a major focus of research in many laboratories in Europe and in the USA. Several metal foams are available with cells of up to 3 m in diameter and up to 30 m in length. The manufacturing processes for closed cell metal foams may be adapted to make open cell metal foams. For comparison with closed cell metal foams, the Young’s modulus of open cell closed cell foams may be determined using dynamic compression testing. However, open cell metal foams show unusual deformation behaviour which results in difficulties in measuring the Young’s modulus for open cell foams, since small deviations in strain due to the curvature of the beam lead to large variations in the Young’s modulus. Closed cell metal foams have been shown to have an increased Young’s modulus with increasing porosity. The Young’s modulus is therefore expected to decrease as the cell wall thickness of the foam increases. For large cell open cell foams, the Young’s modulus can be determined using nano indentation tests. This paper describes the results of Young’s modulus measurements of several steel and aluminium open cell foams with different porosity and cell wall thickness. The findings are discussed in terms of the effect of the porosity and cell wall thickness on the Young’s modulus. Metal foams are a class of lightweight materials with high specific stiffness and strength. They are used for many applications, including automotive and aerospace industries, and medical implants. The use of metal foams for high energy density applications, such as accelerator components, has been studied in recent years. Beam liners are a major focus of research in many laboratories worldwide. The Young’s modulus of open cell metal foams is found to be dependent on the porosity and cell wall thickness. A simple relationship between the Young’s modulus and the porosity is proposed. The Young’s modulus of open cell foams is found to be between 35% and 50% of the Young’s modulus of closed cell metal foams. The Young’s modulus of open cell metal foam is found to be independent of the cell wall thickness.

We report the synthesis, single crystal X-ray diffraction analysis, magnetic properties, and magnetization analyses of synthetic pharmacosiderite from the Rovno mines, Ukraine. The compound crystallizes in triclinic space group P-1 with the following crystallographic parameters: a = 5.8309(6) Å, b = 6.6222(6) Å, c = 7.6931(8) Å, α = 76.210(9)°, β = 73.272(10)°, γ = 62.043(10)°, V = 264.78(5) Å3, Z = 1. Crystal structure studies show that pharmacosiderite, with a molecular formula of [Cu2Fe2(SO4)3(H2O)9Cl2], is a new mineral of the uranyl–sulphate group with the formula [Cu3Fe3(SO4)6Cl2]•H2O, which crystallizes in the primitive cubic lattice and represents a new coordination isomer with tetrahedral Cu2Fe2 clusters [N5MnN5]6+, forming the first example of their structure type. The magnetic susceptibility is analyzed in the framework of a 2D Heisenberg model on a simple cubic sublattice. According to Monte Carlo simulations, the spin–spin interaction is antiferromagnetic in nature and the ground state with S = 10/2 is realized. Pharmacosiderite, Cu2Fe2(SO4)3(OH)6Cl2·4H2O, is a new mineral from the Rovno mines, Ukraine. It was found in association with the uranyl–sulphate mineral roznarite, Cu5(UO2)4(SO4)3Cl2·3H2O, and the sulpho–halite mineral zyklon, Cu5(SO4)4Cl2·5H2O.      The crystal structure of pharmacosiderite is a new example of a mineral with tetrahedral clusters [N5MnN5]7+, forming a new coordination isomer of the [N4MnN4]7+ cluster.      The magnetic properties of pharmacosiderite are analyzed in the framework of the 2D Heisenberg model on a simple cubic lattice. The magnetic interaction is antiferromagnetic and the ground state is a spin-10/2 Heisenberg antiferromagnet.      The results are discussed in the framework of the structure and magnetic properties of uranyl–sulphate minerals. - The compound Cu2Fe2(SO4)3Cl2·4H2O is a new mineral of the uranyl–sulfate group with the formula [Cu3(SO4)6Cl2]·H2O, which crystallizes in the simple cubic lattice.   - The magnetic susceptibility of pharmacosiderite is analyzed in the framework of a 2d Heisenberg model on a simple cubic lattice.   - The spin–spin interaction is antiferromagnetic in nature and it is described by the 2D Heisenberg model with the ground state with S = 1/2 and S = 10/2.   - The results are discussed in the framework of the structural and magnetic properties of uranyl–sulphate compounds.    Text S1. Experimental details.    Synthesis:    Pharmacosiderite was synthesized in the laboratory of the Institute of Mineralogy of the Ukrainian Academy of Sciences using a hydrothermal technique [1]. The synthetic pharmacosiderite was characterized by single-crystal X-ray diffraction and magnetic susceptibility measurements.    X-ray diffraction:    The X-ray diffraction study of pharmacosiderite was performed on a Bruker D8 Quest diffractometer using Mo Kα radiation. The data were collected in the ω-scan mode at room temperature using an ω-scan step of 0.2° and a scan step of 0.02°. The data were reduced using the Bruker SAINT software package. The structure was solved by direct methods and refined by full-matrix least-squares methods using the SHELXL-2014 program package [2]. The hydrogen atoms were placed in calculated positions and refined using the riding model. The crystallographic data and structure refinement parameters are summarized in Table S1.    Magnetic measurements:    The magnetic susceptibility measurements of pharmacosiderite were carried out using a Quantum Design Magnetic Properties Measurement System (MPMS). The magnetic susceptibility was measured in the temperature range 2–300 K. The magnetic field was applied parallel to the crystallographic a-axis.

In this paper, we prove a generalization of Besov's type embedding theorem for bilateral grand Lebesgue spaces. This result extends the classical Besov embedding theorem to a broader class of function spaces, allowing for the treatment of functions with singularities on both the left and right sides of the real line. As a consequence of this result, we obtain a characterization of the continuity of functions in bilateral grand Lebesgue spaces in terms of their smoothness and decay at infinity. Besov spaces are a class of function spaces that are widely used in the field of harmonic analysis and partial differential equations. They are defined in terms of the smoothness of a function and its decay at infinity. The classical Besov embedding theorem states that if a function belongs to a certain Besov space, then it must also belong to a certain Hölder space, which is a space of continuously differentiable functions. This result has many important applications, including the well-known Sobolev embedding theorem, which is a key tool in the study of elliptic partial differential equations.

However, the classical Besov embedding theorem only applies to functions defined on the real line, and does not cover the case of functions with singularities on both the left and right sides of the real line. In order to address this limitation, the concept of bilateral grand Lebesgue spaces was introduced. These spaces are defined in terms of the smoothness and decay of a function not only at infinity, but also at negative infinity.

In this paper, we prove a generalization of Besov's type embedding theorem for bilateral grand Lebesgue spaces. This result extends the classical Besov embedding theorem to a broader class of function spaces, allowing for the treatment of functions with singularities on both the left and right sides of the real line. As a consequence of this result, we obtain a characterization of the continuity of functions in bilateral grand Lebesgue spaces in terms of their smoothness and decay at infinity. In conclusion, we have proved a generalization of Besov's type embedding theorem for bilateral grand Lebesgue spaces, extending the classical Besov embedding theorem to a broader class of function spaces. This result has important implications for the study of functions with singularities on both the left and right sides of the real line, and allows for the characterization of the continuity of such functions in terms of their smoothness and decay at infinity.

The spatial distribution of chromospheric emission in three spectral lines, Fe i 630.2 nm, Ca ii lines at 3968 Å  and 3967 Å  have been studied using SOHO/MDI data for a quietSun region where a large number of flares and coronal mass ejections has been observed in the chromospheric and coronal domains. The observed chromospheric features are compared with those of the coronal emission that arise from low temperatures. The observed distribution of Fe i 630.2 nm emission is compared with that of H$\alpha$. The observations show the presence of small and large blobs of emission observed at a range of heights in the chromosphere which are due to intermittent reconnection events. In the absence of small emission, the large blobs dominate the Fe i emission in the observed region. The observed distributions of Ca ii 3968 Å and 3967 Å emission are also compared with that of H$\alpha$. The Ca ii emission occurs above that of H$\alpha$. Therefore we conclude, on the basis of these observations, that quiet Sun plasma is replenished by mass flows that are continuous in nature. The solar chromosphere is a very dynamic region in the solar atmosphere and is one of the most interesting regions in solar physics. It is one of the most important sites of magnetic reconnection in the solar atmosphere. It has been suggested that the chromosphere is a magnetically dominated region (Klimchuk 1999). The chromospheric plasma is heated by the magnetic field and the magnetic energy is converted into thermal energy and kinetic energy of the plasma (Parker 1978; Parker & Forbes 1991). The plasma in the chromosphere is accelerated by the reconnection processes and subsequently forms spicules (De Pontieu et al. 2000; De Pontieu et al. 2011). The spicules are observed as bright and dark structures in the chromospheric emission lines. The spicules are believed to be a signature of the magnetic reconnection in the chromosphere (De Pontieu et al., 2000; De Pontiu et al., 2011; De Pontieu et al., 2104; De Pontieu et al.,2015). The Ca ii emission occurs   above that of H$\alpha$. Thus we conclude,   on the basis of these observations, that quiet   Sun plasma is replenished by mass flows   that are continuous in nature. In this paper, we present a flexible data reduction software package, based on the C++ language, to process the data obtained with fiber-fed multiobject echelle spectrographs on the ground and space platforms. This software runs in a single process over the raw data obtained with IRAF. The data in standard files can be converted into calibrated wavelength data in single step after reducing the spectra by the software. Besides the capability of working on the individual frame, this package also supports to reduce all the data together by multi-thread system in parallel. It also takes full advantage of the multi-core CPU architecture by enabling the processing of sub-sets of frames concurrently. This package will accelerate the data reduction of large-scale galaxy surveys. The interface of the software is designed to be extensible and to simplify the management and organization of a large spectroscopic data set and the data reduction of fiber-fed echelle spectrographs. Fiber-fed multiobject echelle spectrographs have been developed for many years to obtain high-resolution spectra of a large number of objects simultaneously. In the last decade, the 4-meter class telescopes such as the 8-meter class telescopes are getting larger and more powerful, and the capability of fiber-fed echelle spectrographs has been further improved. These facilities have opened up a new window to the study of the large scale structure of the Universe. The software presented in this paper is flexible and modular. It can be used on the individual frames and on the data obtained by the fiber-fed multiobject echelle spectrograph on the ground and space platforms. It also takes full advantage of the multithread system of the CPU architecture and of the multi-core CPU architecture to process the data in parallel. The software is extensible and easy to use.

Natural Evolution Strategies (NES) are a type of optimization algorithm that mimic the principles of natural evolution to find the best solution to a problem. These algorithms use techniques such as recombination, mutation, and selection to search for the optimal solution in a given space. NES algorithms have been applied to a variety of problems, including those in machine learning and other fields, and have shown promising results in terms of both efficiency and accuracy. Optimization algorithms are essential tools for finding the best solution to a given problem. In machine learning, optimization algorithms are used to find the best model parameters that minimize a given loss function. There are many different types of optimization algorithms, each with its own set of strengths and weaknesses. Natural Evolution Strategies (NES) are a class of optimization algorithms that are inspired by natural evolution and use techniques such as recombination, mutation, and selection to search for the optimal solution.

NES algorithms are based on the idea that a population of potential solutions can be evolved over time through the application of evolutionary principles. These principles include recombination, in which two or more solutions are combined to create a new solution, mutation, in which a solution is modified slightly to create a new solution, and selection, in which the most fit solutions are chosen for reproduction. These processes are repeated iteratively until the optimal solution is found.

One advantage of NES algorithms is that they can search for the optimal solution in a continuous space, rather than being limited to a discrete set of values as some other optimization algorithms are. This makes them particularly well-suited for problems where the solution space is large or continuous. NES algorithms have been applied to a variety of problems, including those in machine learning, and have shown promising results in terms of both efficiency and accuracy. In summary, Natural Evolution Strategies (NES) are a class of optimization algorithms that use techniques inspired by natural evolution to search for the best solution to a given problem. These algorithms have been applied to a variety of problems and have shown promising results in terms of efficiency and accuracy. While NES algorithms have their own set of strengths and weaknesses, they offer a valuable tool for optimizing complex systems and finding the best solution to a wide range of problems.

The Very High Energy (VHE) blazar MAGIC J2001+439 is a promising target for multi-wavelength studies due to its high-energy emission and variable behavior. In this work, we present the first broadband characterization and redshift determination of MAGIC J2001+439 using data from various instruments across the electromagnetic spectrum. Our analysis reveals a redshift of z=0.94 for the blazar, which is consistent with previous estimates. Additionally, we find that the spectral energy distribution of MAGIC J2001+439 can be well described by a leptonic model, with the emission being dominated by synchrotron radiation in the radio to optical bands and inverse Compton scattering in the X-ray to gamma-ray bands. These results provide important insights into the physical processes at play in MAGIC J2001+439 and contribute to our understanding of VHE blazars in general. Blazars are a subclass of active galactic nuclei (AGN) that exhibit strong and variable emission across the electromagnetic spectrum. They are characterized by a jet of plasma that is oriented at a small angle to the line of sight and emits radiation through various physical processes, including synchrotron radiation and inverse Compton scattering. Among the different types of blazars, VHE blazars are of particular interest due to their high-energy emission, which extends into the very high energy (VHE, E>100 GeV) gamma-ray range.

The VHE blazar MAGIC J2001+439 was first detected by the MAGIC telescope in 2005 and has been the subject of several follow-up studies in the years since. However, despite its importance as a high-energy emitter, little is known about the broadband properties and redshift of MAGIC J2001+439. In this work, we aim to address this gap in knowledge by performing a broadband characterization and redshift determination of MAGIC J2001+439 using data from various instruments across the electromagnetic spectrum. In this study, we have presented the first broadband characterization and redshift determination of the VHE blazar MAGIC J2001+439. Our analysis reveals a redshift of z=0.94 for the blazar, which is consistent with previous estimates. We also find that the spectral energy distribution of MAGIC J2001+439 can be well described by a leptonic model, with the emission being dominated by synchrotron radiation in the radio to optical bands and inverse Compton scattering in the X-ray to gamma-ray bands. These results provide important insights into the physical processes at play in MAGIC J2001+439 and contribute to our understanding of VHE blazars in general. Future multi-wavelength observations of MAGIC J2001+439 will be crucial in further refining our understanding of this fascinating object.

In this document, we discuss the process of performing next-to-leading order (NLO) calculations in quantum field theory using dimensional regularization and the projection method to four dimensions. We begin by introducing the concept of dimensional regularization and its use in regularizing ultraviolet divergences. We then describe the projection method and its application to NLO calculations. Finally, we conclude by discussing the advantages and limitations of using these techniques for NLO computations in four dimensions. In quantum field theory, perturbative calculations often encounter ultraviolet (UV) divergences, which are singularities that appear when the momentum of virtual particles becomes infinite. These divergences can be regulated by introducing a small mass scale or by changing the number of dimensions in which the theory is defined. The latter approach, known as dimensional regularization, allows for the computation of physical quantities in the limit as the number of dimensions approaches four.

Dimensional regularization is a powerful tool for regularizing UV divergences because it preserves symmetries and maintains gauge invariance. It works by replacing the spacetime dimension D with a complex number d = D - 2eps, where eps is a small parameter. This allows for the calculation of Feynman diagrams in a dimensionally-continued space, where the divergences can be identified and subtracted using counterterms.

However, physical quantities in quantum field theory are usually computed in four dimensions, and it is often necessary to project the results of dimensional regularization back to four dimensions in order to obtain physically meaningful results. This process is known as the projection method.

One of the main applications of dimensional regularization and the projection method is in the calculation of next-to-leading order (NLO) corrections to physical quantities. NLO calculations are important because they capture the leading radiative corrections to physical observables and provide a more accurate prediction of their values. In summary, dimensional regularization and the projection method are essential tools for performing NLO calculations in quantum field theory. They allow for the regularization of UV divergences and the projection of physical quantities back to four dimensions, providing a more accurate prediction of physical observables. While these techniques have many advantages, they also have some limitations, such as the need for the introduction of counterterms and the complexity of NLO calculations. Despite these limitations, dimensional regularization and the projection method remain a valuable tool for performing precise NLO computations in four dimensions.

Most applications of belief-net (BN) models rely on approximate, but simple, methods to evaluate the posterior marginal distributions and their mean and variance. To date, no such approximations have been derived specifically for belief-net structural models. In this paper, we describe an alternative method, network doubling, for finding approximate solutions to the belief Net system of moment equations; we present simple extensions to the double algorithm for approximate inference in large belief nets. Our results indicate that the performance of the proposed method is excellent when compared to the methods employed by the BN-GEN package and JAGS, and better than the best known approximations for other types of graphical models. The proposed method is based on the observation that the belief net system of moment equations can be viewed as a system of linear equations in the moments of the posterior distributions. We then derive a simple, yet effective, procedure for solving the resulting system of linear equations. We also provide simple extensions to the proposed method for approximate inference in large belief nets. Orbital angular momentum (OAM) of light is a fundamental property that plays a crucial role in various fields of optics and photonics, including high-capacity optical communication, optical manipulation, and quantum information processing. Accurate and reliable detection of OAM states is therefore of great importance. In this work, we present a novel method for the high fidelity detection of OAM states based on time mapping. Our approach involves the use of a spatial light modulator (SLM) to map the OAM states onto the time domain, which can then be easily detected using a standard photodetector. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our method through experimental measurements, showing high fidelity detection of OAM states over a wide range of OAM values. The orbital angular momentum (OAM) of light is a fundamental property that describes the helical phase structure of a light beam. It is characterized by an integer value, known as the topological charge, which determines the number of twists in the phase structure. OAM states of light have received significant attention in recent years due to their potential applications in a variety of fields, including high-capacity optical communication, optical manipulation, and quantum information processing.

Accurate and reliable detection of OAM states is essential for these applications. However, detecting OAM states can be challenging due to the complex phase structure of the light beam. Traditional methods for detecting OAM states, such as the use of a spiral phase plate or a holographic grating, are limited in their accuracy and reliability.

In this work, we propose a novel method for the high fidelity detection of OAM states based on time mapping. Our approach involves the use of a spatial light modulator (SLM) to map the OAM states onto the time domain, which can then be easily detected using a standard photodetector. By utilizing this technique, we are able to achieve high fidelity detection of OAM states over a wide range of OAM values. In conclusion, we have presented a novel method for the high fidelity detection of OAM states of light based on time mapping. Our approach involves the use of a spatial light modulator (SLM) to map the OAM states onto the time domain, which can then be easily detected using a standard photodetector. Through experimental measurements, we have demonstrated the effectiveness of our method in achieving high fidelity detection of OAM states over a wide range of OAM values. This method represents a significant advance in the field of OAM detection and has the potential to enable a wide range of applications in optics and photonics.

In this paper, we explore the concept of stationary processes with pure point diffraction, a type of mathematical model used to describe the behavior of certain physical systems. We also discuss some of the applications and implications of this model in various fields, including solid state physics and number theory. A stationary process is a type of mathematical model used to describe the behavior of a physical system over time. It is called "stationary" because the statistical properties of the process do not change with time. One important class of stationary processes is known as pure point diffraction processes, which are characterized by the presence of a specific type of diffraction pattern.

Diffraction is the bending of waves around an obstacle or through an aperture, and it is a fundamental physical phenomenon that occurs in many different contexts, including light waves, sound waves, and particle waves. In summary, stationary processes with pure point diffraction are a powerful mathematical tool for understanding the behavior of certain physical systems. They offer a rich set of mathematical properties and have a wide range of applications in various fields. Further research in this area has the potential to yield important insights and new understanding of the underlying physical phenomena.

The Brusselator model is a mathematical model that describes the self-sustaining oscillations of chemical reactions. In this study, we examine the formation and stability of stationary localized structures in the Brusselator model with delayed feedback. Our results show that the presence of delayed feedback can significantly affect the stability of these structures, leading to their destabilization or stabilization depending on the delay time and strength. This has important implications for the use of the Brusselator model in predicting and controlling chemical reactions in real-world systems. The Brusselator model, originally developed by Pierre Gilles de Gennes and John L. Blackburn in the 1970s, is a widely-used mathematical model that describes the self-sustaining oscillations of chemical reactions. It is characterized by a set of nonlinear differential equations that describe the time evolution of the concentrations of two chemical species, A and B, as they interact and react with each other. The model has been successfully applied to a wide range of chemical systems, including oscillating reactions in biological systems and oscillating polymerization reactions.

One of the key features of the Brusselator model is the formation of stationary localized structures, also known as stationary patterns or target patterns. These structures are spatially-varying concentrations of the chemical species that are stationary in time and exhibit a well-defined spatial structure. They have been observed in a variety of chemical systems and have been shown to play an important role in the dynamics of chemical reactions.

In this study, we investigate the effect of delayed feedback on the formation and stability of stationary localized structures in the Brusselator model. Delayed feedback refers to a feedback loop in which the output of a system at a given time is used as input at a later time. It has been shown to have a significant impact on the dynamics of various systems, including chemical reactions, and has been used as a control strategy in a number of applications. In this study, we have examined the effect of delayed feedback on the formation and stability of stationary localized structures in the Brusselator model. Our results show that the presence of delayed feedback can significantly affect the stability of these structures, leading to their destabilization or stabilization depending on the delay time and strength. This has important implications for the use of the Brusselator model in predicting and controlling chemical reactions in real-world systems, as well as for the design of control strategies that exploit the properties of delayed feedback.
