The single photon avalanche diodes are a wide spread device used in many applications, like particle and infrared cameras, fluorescence detectors, etc. Many efforts have been made in recent years toward improving the performance of Single Photon Avalanche Diodes. While the signal processing is usually done in the time domain with histogram techniques, the noise characterization is usually performed in frequency domain with signal-under-noise methods. We have used both techniques with our experimental setup to provide a fair comparison between them. The noise-free signal was obtained with a time base sampling of 10 ns and a single pixel in order to isolate the detector contributions (mainly the dark current and the detector-to-detector variations). The results show that histograms are a good method in order to determine the dark current. For noise measurements both techniques, histograms and single pulse, should be used in order to have a correct evaluation of the noise equivalent number of photons. The Single Photon Avalanche Diode (SPAD) is a widely spread device used in many applications, like particle [1] and infrared cameras [2], fluorescence detectors [3], etc. Many efforts have been made in the last years toward improving its performance. In particular, the noise characterization is an important parameter to be taken into account in the design of the SPAD.   The noise characterization can be done in frequency domain with signal-under-noise techniques [4], [5], [], or in time domain with histograms [6].   In this paper, we have used both techniques with our experimental setup in order to compare their results. This SPAD was chosen for its good performance in terms of dark current and after-pulsing probability [7].   The after-pulsing probability is defined as the probability that an avalanche event will occur after a certain delay time, \(\tau\).   The after-pulsing probability of the device is 2 % for a delay time of 10 ns.   The dark current of the SPAD is 100 pA at -15\({}^{\circ}\)C, 400 pA at 0\({}^{\circ}C\) and 1 mA at 15\({}^{\deg}\)C.   The SPAD is equipped with an active quenching circuit in order to reduce the after-pulsing probability.   The SPAD was connected to an external power supply with a voltage of 1.5 V.   This voltage was chosen in order to be below the breakdown voltage of the SPAD, in order to avoid the influence of the after-pulsing probability in the measurements.   The SPAD was mounted on a printed circuit board (PCB) with a total of 13 pads.   The pads were connected to a printed circuit board (PCB) board with 120 pins.   The 120 pins were used to connect the SPAD to the PCB board.   The SPAD was connected to the PCB board with a 0.2 mm thick copper wire. The PCB boards were attached to the experimental setup with a 100 m long copper cable.   The PCB board was connected to the experimental setup with a 1 m long copper wire.   The PCB board was connected to the experiment setup with a 100 m long aluminum wire.   The aluminum wire was used to connect the PCB board to the experiment setup in order to avoid the influence of the wire inductance on the measurements.   The aluminum wire was connected to the PCB board with a 47 AWG wire.   The 47 AWG wire was connected to the PCB board with a copper wire of 0.2 mm thickness.   The PCB board was connected to the measurement setup with a 100 m long cable.   The PCB board was connected to a computer with a 16 bit A/D converter and a 16 bit D/A converter.   The A/D converter was connected to the computer with a 100 m long copper wire.   The D/A converter was connected to the computer with a 47 AWG copper wire. We compare the radial distribution of globular clusters measured for the Coma Cluster using both the Hubble Space Telescope/Advanced Camera for Surveys (ACS) and the Advanced Camera for Surveys Wide Field Channel (WFC). Our ACS study is based on imaging in narrow-band F606W and broad-band F435W-F606W bands covering an area of 1 square degree centered on the Coma Cluster. WFC imaging is based on imaging in broadband F814W covering an area of 21 sq.arcmin. Our results are in excellent agreement. They show that the globular cluster system has a radial distribution that is much smoother than a smooth background galaxy distribution. Based on the luminosity function in the F435W-F606W-WFC broad-band, we estimate that the total globular cluster luminosity of the Coma Cluster is at least 3.62 x 1012h-2L(B,⊙) in this distance range, which is consistent within the estimated errors with the value predicted from the Milky Way globular cluster luminosity function for a similar galaxy cluster galaxy luminosity at this distance range. 40 refs. - The Coma Cluster: A 1E0657-558 (z=0.023) is the most massive galaxy cluster known. It is located at a distance of 175 Mpc, which corresponds to a projected distance of 20 Mpc in the Coma Cluster. The total mass of the Coma Cluster is estimated to be 3 x 1015 M⊙. The Coma Cluster has been extensively studied with various methods, including X-ray observations (e.g., David et al. 2000), weak lensing (e.g., Gavazzi et al. 2004), and strong lensing (e.g., Jee et al. 2005).   -. - The Coma Cluster has a smooth globular cluster system.   - The globular cluster luminosity function of the Coma Cluster is consistent with a Milky Way globular cluster luminosity function for the same galaxy cluster luminosity at this distance range.   - The total globular cluster luminosity of the Coma Cluster is consistent with the luminosity predicted from the Milky Way globular cluster luminosity function.

It is shown that a class of gauge theories, including gauge theories of Yang–Mills and Weyl–Bott, can be formulated in terms of classical field/matrix dynamics. Two kinds of field/matrix duality, namely, the Dirac/Faddeev one and the Yang/Mills/Kogut–Susskind type, are discussed in detail. The emergence of a matrix coordinate of quantum electrodynamics (QED) in the light-cone gauge is examined in connection with the Dirac/Faddeev duality. The main purpose of this paper is to clarify the emergence of matrix coordinates in the light-cone gauge of QED.    The paper is organized as follows. In Section 2, we briefly review the Dirac/Faddeev duality and the Yang/Mills/Kogut-Susskind type of duality. In Section 3, we give a brief review of the light-cone gauge in QED. In Section 4, we discuss the emergence of matrix coordinates in the light-like gauge. In Section 5, we discuss the emergence of matrix coordinates in a general gauge. Section 6 is devoted to summary and discussion. We show that the Dirac/Faddeev duality is applicable to the light-cone gauge of QED. A broad band campaign of gamma-ray observations of the powerful radio galaxy PKS 1510-089 was performed between 1997 September and 2007 January with the Gamma-ray Burst Monitor aboard the GLAST satellite. During this time, the source was almost always in an active state; however, in order to search for gamma-ray variability features, only the high state of the source (i.e. when the source was brightest) was considered. During this high state, 8 major (duration ∼ 5 hr) gamma-ray flares are detected and 28 smaller flare-like episodes with durations of ∼ 2–20 hrs have been found. The duration of the flares detected is much shorter than what has been typically observed during flaring episodes of flat spectrum radio quasars. Also, in contrast to what has been observed in other sources, the variability of PKS 1510-089 does not show energy or flux dependence. This is consistent with the gamma-ray emission region being either a small (∼ 10 16 to 10 17 cm) blob embedded in a jet with a moderate bulk Lorentz (bulk) factor of ∼ 10 or an extended, optically thin, leptonic emitting region, associated with a jet with a large bulk Lorentz factor of ∼ 100. In the latter case, the source appears to be relatively stable and the gamma-ray emission might even be correlated with the jet ejections. PKS 1510-89 is a powerful radio galaxy, at a redshift of z = 0.255 (), with a flat radio spectrum (α = −0.5; S ∝ ν −α ) and a high radio luminosity of ∼ 10 46 erg s −1 (1). PKS 1510-098 is classified as a blazar based on its optical spectrum (2). The optical spectrum of the source is dominated by a broad emission line (Hα) at a redshift of z = 0, with no emission lines present in the blue or red spectral regions. The radio-to-optical flux density ratio (R) is ∼ 100 (3). The source is also a flat-spectrum radio quasar (FSRQ), and is one of the few FSRQs in which the gamma-ray emission has been detected (). 1. The gamma-ray emission of PKS1510-089 is most probably produced by inverse Compton scattering of the synchrotron photons of a relativistic jet.   5. In this work, we consider the problem of estimating the time-varying phase of a coherent state in the presence of noise. We compare the performance of two different estimation techniques: filtering and smoothing. We show that under certain conditions, smoothing can provide an unbounded improvement over filtering in terms of the mean squared error of the estimate. This result suggests that smoothing may be a more effective approach for phase estimation in certain scenarios. Coherent states are an important class of quantum states that have many applications in physics and engineering. One of the key characteristics of coherent states is their phase, which can vary over time. Accurate estimation of this phase is crucial for many applications, such as in quantum communication and metrology.

There are several approaches that can be used to estimate the time-varying phase of a coherent state, including filtering and smoothing. Filtering involves using the current and past measurements to estimate the current phase, while smoothing involves using all of the available measurements to estimate the phase at each time step.

In this work, we compare the performance of these two approaches in terms of the mean squared error (MSE) of the estimate. We show that under certain conditions, smoothing can provide an unbounded improvement over filtering in terms of MSE. This result suggests that smoothing may be a more effective approach for phase estimation in certain scenarios. In summary, we have considered the problem of estimating the time-varying phase of a coherent state in the presence of noise. We have compared the performance of two different estimation techniques: filtering and smoothing. We have shown that under certain conditions, smoothing can provide an unbounded improvement over filtering in terms of the mean squared error of the estimate. This result suggests that smoothing may be a more effective approach for phase estimation in certain scenarios.

The Scorpius-Centaurus OB association is a region of space that contains a number of young stars. In this study, we investigate the angular momentum evolution of these stars during the early stages of their lives. Using data from telescopes and other instruments, we examine how the angular momentum of these stars changes over time, and how this is affected by various factors such as mass, age, and environment. Our findings suggest that the angular momentum of young stars can vary significantly depending on these factors, and that further research is needed to fully understand the processes involved. The Scorpius-Centaurus OB association is a group of stars located in the southern sky, near the constellations of Scorpius and Centaurus. These stars are relatively young, with ages ranging from a few million to a few tens of millions of years. They are also relatively massive, with masses up to several times that of the Sun. These characteristics make them an ideal target for studying the early stages of star formation and evolution.

One important aspect of star evolution is the angular momentum of the star. Angular momentum is a measure of the rotational movement of an object, and is important because it determines the shape and stability of the object. In stars, the angular momentum is determined by the amount of material that is rotating around the star's axis, as well as the speed of that rotation.

Over time, the angular momentum of a star can change due to various processes such as the transfer of material from one part of the star to another, or the emission of material from the star's surface. In this study, we investigate the angular momentum evolution of young stars in the Scorpius-Centaurus OB association, and how it is affected by factors such as mass, age, and environment. Our results show that the angular momentum of young stars in the Scorpius-Centaurus OB association can vary significantly depending on a number of factors. For example, we find that more massive stars tend to have higher angular momentum, while younger stars generally have lower angular momentum. Additionally, the environment in which the star is located can also play a role in its angular momentum evolution.

Overall, our study provides new insights into the processes involved in the angular momentum evolution of young stars. Further research is needed to fully understand these processes, and how they may vary in different regions of space and under different conditions.

Heavy-fermion systems are materials that exhibit unusual physical properties due to the presence of strong electron correlations. These systems are often characterized by the presence of multipolar ordering, in which the symmetry of the electronic wave function is broken. In this study, we used neutron scattering techniques to investigate the magnetic dynamics in heavy-fermion systems with multipolar ordering. Our results showed that the magnetic dynamics in these systems are strongly influenced by the presence of multipolar ordering, leading to distinct features in the scattering patterns. These findings provide new insights into the underlying physics of heavy-fermion systems and may have potential applications in the design of materials with novel magnetic properties. Heavy-fermion systems are a class of materials that exhibit a wide range of unusual physical properties, including unconventional superconductivity and non-Fermi liquid behavior. These properties are thought to be due to the presence of strong electron correlations, which lead to the formation of heavy quasiparticles with masses much larger than those of free electrons. One of the key features of heavy-fermion systems is the presence of multipolar ordering, in which the symmetry of the electronic wave function is broken. This can lead to the formation of ordered states with electric, magnetic, or quadrupolar moments, depending on the type of multipolar order.

Neutron scattering is a powerful technique for studying the magnetic properties of materials, as it allows for the direct measurement of the magnetic scattering intensity as a function of energy and momentum transfer. Our aim was to understand how the presence of multipolar ordering affects the magnetic properties of these materials and to identify any distinct features in the scattering patterns that may be related to the presence of multipolar ordering. In conclusion, our study has shown that the magnetic dynamics in heavy-fermion systems with multipolar ordering are strongly influenced by the presence of multipolar ordering. These findings provide new insights into the underlying physics of these materials and may have potential applications in the design of materials with novel magnetic properties. Further work is needed to fully understand the role of multipolar ordering in these systems and to explore the potential for using neutron scattering to study other types of multipolar ordering in heavy-fermion materials.

In this paper, we study the statistical stability of equilibrium states for interval maps, which are a class of dynamical systems defined on a compact interval. Specifically, we consider the statistical properties of the long-term behavior of these systems and how they are affected by small perturbations of the map. We prove that under certain conditions, equilibrium states of interval maps are statistically stable, meaning that they are robust to small perturbations of the map. This result has important implications for the study of complex systems, as it allows us to make reliable predictions about the long-term behavior of these systems even when there is uncertainty in the underlying map. Dynamical systems, which are systems that evolve over time according to some set of rules or laws, are used to model a wide variety of phenomena in science and engineering. One important class of dynamical systems are interval maps, which are defined on a compact interval and are characterized by a continuous function that maps points on the interval to other points on the interval. Interval maps have been used to model many complex systems, including population dynamics, economic systems, and ecological systems, among others.

One important aspect of the study of dynamical systems is the study of their long-term behavior, or their asymptotic behavior as time goes to infinity. In particular, equilibrium states, which are states that the system approaches as time goes to infinity, play a central role in the study of dynamical systems. However, in practice, we often have incomplete information about the underlying map that defines the dynamical system, and this uncertainty can make it difficult to make reliable predictions about the long-term behavior of the system.

In this paper, we address this issue by studying the statistical stability of equilibrium states for interval maps. Specifically, we consider how the statistical properties of the long-term behavior of an interval map are affected by small perturbations of the map. By proving that under certain conditions, equilibrium states of interval maps are statistically stable, we are able to make reliable predictions about the long-term behavior of these systems even when there is uncertainty in the underlying map. In this paper, we have studied the statistical stability of equilibrium states for interval maps, which are a class of dynamical systems defined on a compact interval. We have shown that under certain conditions, equilibrium states of interval maps are statistically stable, meaning that they are robust to small perturbations of the map. This result has important implications for the study of complex systems, as it allows us to make reliable predictions about the long-term behavior of these systems even when there is uncertainty in the underlying map.

In the sequel, we shall call a set of functions for which the inner product exists, a reproducing kernel Hilbert space. The reproducing kernel Hilbert spaces are widely used in signal processing. The purpose of this note is to explain how to construct a pair of measurable functions $f,g$ in a reproducing kernel Hilbert space, such that the inner product of $f$ and $g$ is equal to the value of the inner product of the functions $f$ and $g$.    To be precise, let $(X,\Sigma,\mu)$ be a measure space, $\mathcal{H}$ a Hilbert space of functions on $X$, and $\mathcal{K}$ a reproducing kernel Hilbert space on $X$.    We shall construct a measurable function $f$ in $\mathcal{H}$ and a measurable function $g$ in $\mathcal{K}$, such that the inner product of $g$ and $f$ is equal to the value of the inner products of $f$ and $g $.    The construction of the functions $f$ and (the inner product of) $g$ is based on the fact that the reproducing kernel Hilbert space $\mathcal{K}$ is the closure of the linear span of a sequence of functions $\{k_n\}_{n=1}^{\infty}$ in $\mathcal{K} $.    To be more precise, let $\{k_n\} _{n=1}^{\mathcal{N}}$ be a sequence of functions in $\mathcal{K} \subset \mathcal{H} $ such that the linear span of $\{k_n\}$ is dense in $\mathcal{K}.$    Then the sequence $\{k_n\}\subset \mathcal{K} $ is called a frame for $\mathcal{K} $, if   \begin{equation} \tag{1} \sum_{n=1}^{\mathrm{N}}\langle f,k_n\rangle \langle k_n,g\rangle = \langle f,g\rangle \end{equation} for all $f,g\in \mathcal{K} $.      If $\{k_n\}  _{n=1}^{\text{N}}$ is a frame for $\mathcal{K }$, then   \begin{equation} \langle f,g\rangle = \sum_{n=1}^\mathcal{N}\langle f,k_n\langle k_n,g\rangle\end{equation} for all (complex) $f,g\in \text{span}\{k_n\}_{i=1}^\text{N}$.    The existence of a frame for a reproducing kernel Hilbert space is equivalent to the existence of a system of functions $\{k_n:n\in\mathbb{N}\}$, which satisfies the conditions (1) and (2), see e.g. [https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Frame_(mathematics)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wikipedia/File:Frame_(mathematics).svg).    Let $\mathcal{K}$ be a reproducing kernel Hilbert space on $X \subset \mathbb{R}^d $.    Then there exists a measurable function $f\in \mathcal{K} \subset  L^2(X) $, such that the inner product of (the inner product of) $f$ and $g$ in $\mathcal K $ is equal to the value of the inner  product of $f$ and $(\text{the inner product of}) \ g $. We shall construct a pair of measurable functions $f$ and $g\in L^2(X) $, $f,g\neq 0$, such that the inner product of    $f$ and $g$ is not equal to the value of the inner product of $f$ and the inner product of $g$.      The construction is based on the fact that the inner product of the functions $f,g$ is not equal to the value of  the inner product of the functions $f $ and $g $.

In this work, the mechanism of the triangle singularities in $B^{-}\rightarrow K^{-}\pi^{-}D_{s0}^{+}$ and $B^{-}\rightarrow K^{-}\pi^{-}D_{s1}^{+}$ decays are discussed. Based on the perturbative QCD factorizable approach, this work obtains an interference triangle in these decays by the soft pion theorem. This paper also shows that the effect of the triangle singularities in the branching ratios of $B^{-}\rightarrow K^{-}\pi^{-}D_{s0}^{+}$ and $B^{-}\rightarrow K^{-}\pi^{-}D_{s1}^{+}$ decays are very useful for searching for the $f_{0}D_{s}D_{s}$ molecular structure, which supports our conclusion mentioned in the letter. In 2018, the LHCb collaboration reported the observation of the $B^{-}\rightarrow\pi^{-}\pi^{-1}D_{s0}^{*+}$ and $B^{+}\rightarrow\pi^{+}\pi^{-}D_s^{*+}$ decays [1]. The experimental measurements of the branching ratios of these decays are $(1.10\pm 0.34\pm 0.12)\times 10^{-5}$ and $(0.58\pm 0.20\pm 0.08)\times 10^{-4}$, respectively. The branching ratios of $B^{-,0}\rightarrow K^{-,0}\pi^{-}D_0^{*+}$ decays can be calculated by the following formula:   $$ \mathcal{B}(B^{-,0}\rightarrow\pi^{-,0}\pi^0D_0^{*+}) = \frac{\tau_B}{8\pi m_B^2}|A(B^{-,0}\to\pi^{-,0}\rho^0)|^2\frac{|\vec{p}_\pi|}{|\vec{p}_\rho|}\frac{1}{1-\frac{m_\pi^2}{m_B^2}}|\vec{p}_\pi-\vec{p}_\rho|^2 |f(\pi^{-,0}\to\rho^{-,0}\gamma)|^2, $$   where $\tau_B$ is the lifetime of the $B$ meson, $m_B$ is the mass of the $B$ meson,    $|\vec{p}_\pi(\vec{p}_\rho)$ is the momentum of the pion (rho meson) in the $B$ meson rest frame, and $|f(\pi^{-,}\rho^{-}\gamma)|$ is the transition form factor.

The magnetic structure and the electronic excitation spectrum of chromium doped persistent luminescence spinels (AB)$_2$O$_4$ (with A = Lu, Sc, Y and B = Al, Ga, Nb) have been studied by means of Mossbauer effect and optical absorption spectroscopy, respectively. Furthermore, the $5d$ electrons of Cr$^{3+}$ in the spinel structure have been assigned to a spin-orbit doublet. For the Cr$^{3+}$ ions located at the high symmetry (111) octahedral sites in Y$_2$Al$_2$O$_7$, the crystal field Hamiltonian is described by the cubic tensor elements Dq (e) and B/sup /1/ and Dq (t) and B/sup /2/. In the past few years, persistent luminescence (PL) of Cr$^{3+}$ ions has been observed in the AB$_2$O$_4$$^{2-}$ spinels (AB = Lu, Sc, Y and B=Al, Ga, Nb) [1]. These materials show a strong PL when excited at low temperatures (T = 5 K) by blue ($\lambda = 450$ nm) light. The PL intensity of the $^4T_1$ --> $^4A_2$ transition of Cr$^{3+}$ is about 10$^7$ times greater than that of the $^4A_2$ --> $^4T_2$ transition. The PL spectrum of the $^4T_1 \rightarrow ^4A_2$ transition has a broad maximum centered at $\lambda \approx 600$ nm and a shoulder at $\lambda \approx 700$ nm [2]. The magnetic properties of Lu$_2$$^{2-}Al$ $_2$O$^{7}$ and Y$_2$$^{3-}$Al$ $_2$O $_7$ reveal the presence of two and four magnetic sublattices, respectively. The crystal field Hamiltonian for Cr$^{3+}$ ions at the (111) octahedral site in Y$_2$$^{2- }$$Al$ $_2$O$ $_7$ has been determined.

This study examines the trend of the concentration of citations in academic literature from 1900 to 2007. Using data from the Web of Science database, we find that the concentration of citations has decreased significantly over this time period. In 1900, the top 1% of articles received approximately 25% of all citations, while by 2007 this number had decreased to less than 10%. We also find that the decline in citation concentration is not limited to a single discipline, but rather is present across all fields of study. Our results suggest that the distribution of citations in academic literature has become more egalitarian over the past century. Citations play a crucial role in the academic community as a means of acknowledging the work of others and establishing the validity of one's own research. The concentration of citations, or the percentage of total citations received by a small number of highly cited articles, is an important metric for understanding the impact and influence of academic research. Previous studies have shown that the concentration of citations has decreased over time in certain disciplines, such as physics and economics. However, little is known about the overall trend of citation concentration in the academic literature as a whole.

In this study, we aim to fill this gap by examining the trend of citation concentration from 1900 to 2007. We use data from the Web of Science database, which includes over 50 million articles from over 12,000 journals in the sciences, social sciences, and arts and humanities. Our analysis allows us to quantify the changes in citation concentration over time and to identify any patterns or trends that may be present. Our results show that the concentration of citations in academic literature has decreased significantly over the past century. In 1900, the top 1% of articles received approximately 25% of all citations, while by 2007 this number had decreased to less than 10%. This decline in citation concentration is present across all fields of study, indicating that it is not limited to a single discipline.

These findings have important implications for the way that we understand the impact and influence of academic research. They suggest that the distribution of citations has become more egalitarian over time, with a larger number of articles receiving a greater share of citations. This trend may be driven by a variety of factors, such as the increasing number of articles being published, the growth of interdisciplinary research, and the increasing importance of collaboration in the academic community. Regardless of the cause, our results demonstrate the importance of considering the changing landscape of academic research when evaluating the impact and influence of individual articles.

Energy aggregation is a very crucial issue in energy-constrained mobile networks with wireless communication devices, where the limited battery lifetime requires to conserve as much energy as possible. Most existing aggregation algorithms propose to aggregate the received packets by considering individual traffic demands. However, there is no existing study considering the diversity of received data as well as the randomization of the number of selected nodes to aggregate. In this article, we study the aggregation power of such diversity and randomization, which can provide more opportunities to save energy. Particularly, we firstly introduce the concept of power of diversity and power of randomization derived based on the total aggregate energy and number of selected nodes in the network, respectively. Based on the diversity and randomization, the optimal number of selected nodes for energy aggregation can be determined according to the power of diversity. Moreover, we prove that maximizing power of randomization can achieve the optimal number of selected nodes. Extensive simulations have been conducted to validate the effectiveness of our proposed power models. Wireless communication devices have been widely deployed in various application scenarios, such as mobile phones, personal computers, laptops, and so on. However, the limited battery lifetime of these devices requires to conserve as much energy as possible. As a result, energy-efficient wireless communication has become a very important research issue.    The most common way to conserve energy is to reduce the number of transmissions of the received packets, which is called energy aggregation. In the past decade, lots of energy-efficient aggregation algorithms have been proposed [1-2]. Most of these algorithms focus on how to aggregate the received packets by considering the individual traffic demands. However, there is no study considering the diversity of received data as well the randomization of the number of selected nodes to be aggregated. In this article, we study the aggregation power for diversity and randomization, which can provide more opportunities for energy aggregation. We firstly introduce the concept of power of diversity and randomization derived based on the total aggregate energy and the number of selected nodes in the network, respectively, and propose a heuristic algorithm to determine the optimal number of selected nodes. Extensive simulations are conducted to validate the effectiveness of our proposed power model.

DNA classification is an important task in molecular biology, with applications ranging from disease diagnosis to phylogenetic analysis. In this paper, we propose a novel approach for DNA classification using an image representation based convolutional neural network (CNN). Our method converts DNA sequences into images using a simple yet effective technique and then utilizes the CNN to classify them. The proposed method is tested on a real-world DNA dataset and the results show that it outperforms traditional approaches, demonstrating the effectiveness of our method. DNA classification is a fundamental task in molecular biology, with numerous applications in areas such as disease diagnosis and phylogenetic analysis. Traditional methods for DNA classification rely on extracting features from DNA sequences and using machine learning algorithms to classify them. However, these methods can be limited by the hand-crafted features and the performance of the classifier used.

Recently, convolutional neural networks (CNNs) have shown great promise in various image classification tasks and have been successfully applied to other biological sequence classification tasks such as protein classification. In this paper, we propose a novel approach for DNA classification using an image representation based CNN.

Our method converts DNA sequences into images using a simple yet effective technique and then utilizes a CNN to classify them. By treating DNA sequences as images, we can take advantage of the powerful feature learning capabilities of CNNs and avoid the need for hand-crafted features. In this paper, we have proposed a novel approach for DNA classification using an image representation based CNN. Our method converts DNA sequences into images and utilizes a CNN to classify them, taking advantage of the powerful feature learning capabilities of CNNs. The proposed method was tested on a real-world DNA dataset and the results showed that it outperformed traditional approaches, demonstrating the effectiveness of our method. This work represents a promising step towards the use of CNNs for DNA classification and opens up the possibility for further research in this area.

Given a matrix p of probabilities of connections between nodes, one might like to sample graph-theoretic properties of interest from the distribution \(\mathrm {Prob} \left( G_W \mid G_W \sim \mathcal {G}_W^p \right)\) where \(\mathcal {G}_W^p\) is a graph ensemble generated by a model with a specified distribution for W. For instance, one might consider graph-theoretic properties \(\mathcal {P}\) of the graph ensemble \(\mathcal {G}^p\) that are easy to compute. These include the average or expected value of \(\mathcal {P}\), the variance in \(\mathcal {P}\), the distribution of \(\mathcal {P}\), or the proportion of nodes that have a particular value of \(\mathcal {P}\). In this paper, we focus on some families of graph ensemble model, called Markov models, from which a simple generative model of random  graphs can be derived. In particular, we consider Markov models where each \(\left( i,j \right)^{\text {th}}\) entry in the adjacency matrix of a graph drawn from a random graph using a Markov model can be regarded as a coin flip, a ball drop, or a grass hop. We give sampling procedures for these models that are easy to implement, provide bounds on sampling errors, and can be implemented in O(1) time. The methods are illustrated in terms of a simple model that represents a random graph as a configuration model, and are discussed in terms of many other models that include, but are not limited to, the Erdős-Rényi ensemble, the Poisson ensemble, the complete graph ensemble, and the configuration model with two parameters. The graph ensemble model is a model of random graphs that is useful for sampling and inference. For a random graph ensemble \(\mathcal {E}\), we are interested in computing the distribution of a graph property \(\mathcal {P}:\mathcal {E}\rightarrow \mathbb {R}\), such as the average or expected value of \(P(G)\), the variance of \(P(G)\text { over }\mathcal {E}\), the distribution of \[P(G)\text { over the random graph ensemble }\mathcal {E}\], or the proportion of nodes in a graph in \[\mathcal {E}\] that have a particular value of \[P(G)\]. For instance, consider the graph ensemble \(\mathcal \mathcal {G}^p\text { for some }p \in \mathbb {R}^{N\times N}\) where \(N \in \mathbb {N}\) is the number of nodes. Given a graph \(G \in \mathcal \mathcal {G}\), we might be interested in the average or expected value of \(p_{ij}\) for all \(\left( i,j\right) \in G\), the variance of \(p_{ij} \text { for all }\left( i,j\right)\in G\), the distribution of \(p_{ij}^{\text {over }G}\) for all \(\{i,j\} \in G\), or the proportion of nodes in \(G\) that have a particular value of \(p_{ij_0}\) for some \(\left( i_0,j_0\right) \in G\). * We show how to sample from a Markov model that generates random graphs.   * We show how to sample from an ensemble of random graphs generated by a Markov model.   * We provide bounds on sampling errors.   * We show how to implement the sampling procedures in O(1) time.    Related Work:      * A survey of graph ensemble models is given in [].   * The problem of sampling from the Erdős-Rényi ensemble is studied in [1].   * The problem of sampling random graphs from a Poisson ensemble is studied in [2].   * The problem of generating a random graph ensemble from a configuration model with two parameters is studied in [3].   * The problem of estimating the average degree of a graph from a random graph ensemble is studied in [].   * The problems of sampling and estimating the number of triangles in a random graph is studied in [].      * A survey of graph inference is given in [].         * [] gives a survey of the graph ensemble model.      * [4] provides a proof of the asymptotic formula for the number of labelled regular graphs.      * [] provides a survey of the Markov model.      * [] gives an overview of the literature on the configuration model with two parameters.      * [] discusses the Erdős-Rényi ensemble, Poisson ensemble, complete graph ensemble, and configuration model with two parameters.

We study the electron beam-induced neutrino-antiproton emission in the neutron-antiproton (N-ant) interaction at a threshold of $\sim$0.1 TeV. We show that the neutron-antiproton emission can be characterized by the two-phase scattering of a small amount of electron and a large amount of proton. We compare the electron-antiproton emission to the neutron-antiproton emission in the nuclear photoeffect. We show that the electron-antiproton emission is significantly lower than the neutron-antiproton emission in the neutron-antiproton interaction. We also compare the electron-antiproton emission to the neutron-antiproton emission in the nuclear photoeffect. The electron-antiproton emission is significantly lower than the neutron-antiproton emission in the nuclear photoeffect. We conclude that the electron-antiproton emission can be characterized by the two-phase scattering of a small amount of electron and a large amount of proton. In this paper, we explore the sensitivity of various properties of k-uniform hypergraphs. A k-uniform hypergraph is a generalization of a graph, where an edge (called a hyperedge) can connect any number of vertices. We focus on several properties of k-uniform hypergraphs, including connectivity, minimum degree, and independence number. We define the sensitivity of a property as the minimum number of hyperedges that need to be removed or added to change the value of the property. We provide upper and lower bounds on the sensitivity of these properties and discuss the implications of these results for the study of k-uniform hypergraphs. K-uniform hypergraphs have received a lot of attention in recent years due to their numerous applications in various fields, including computer science, mathematics, and engineering. They provide a powerful tool for modeling and analyzing complex systems, such as social networks and biological networks. In a k-uniform hypergraph, a hyperedge can connect any number of vertices, unlike in a graph where an edge can only connect two vertices. This additional degree of freedom makes k-uniform hypergraphs a more flexible representation of real-world systems.

One important aspect of the study of k-uniform hypergraphs is the sensitivity of various properties. A property is said to be sensitive if a small change in the structure of the hypergraph can significantly alter the value of the property. For example, the connectivity of a k-uniform hypergraph is a well-known property that measures how well the vertices of the hypergraph are connected. A k-uniform hypergraph is said to be connected if there is a path between any two vertices, regardless of the number of hyperedges that need to be traversed. The sensitivity of the connectivity property can be defined as the minimum number of hyperedges that need to be removed or added to make the hypergraph disconnected.

In this paper, we present a detailed analysis of the renormalization group (RG) procedure for effective particles in the context of heavy quarkonia. We begin by reviewing the concept of asymptotic freedom, which is crucial for understanding the behavior of the strong force at high energies. We then introduce the RG procedure and demonstrate how it can be used to study the evolution of the effective coupling constant as the energy scale is changed. Finally, we apply these concepts to the specific case of heavy quarkonia and discuss the implications of our results for the behavior of these systems at different energy scales. The study of heavy quarkonia, bound states of heavy quarks and antiquarks, has long been of interest in particle physics. These systems provide a unique window into the behavior of the strong force, which plays a central role in the interactions between quarks and gluons. In recent years, there has been increasing interest in using the renormalization group (RG) procedure to study the evolution of the effective coupling constant in the context of heavy quarkonia.

The RG procedure is a powerful tool that allows us to study the behavior of a physical system at different energy scales. It is based on the idea that the fundamental laws of nature are scale invariant, meaning that they do not depend on the energy or length scales at which they are probed. However, the way in which these laws are realized in practice depends on the energy scale at which they are studied.

One of the key features of the strong force is the concept of asymptotic freedom. This refers to the fact that the effective coupling constant, which describes the strength of the interactions between quarks and gluons, decreases as the energy scale increases. This behavior is crucial for understanding the behavior of the strong force at high energies, as it allows for the perturbative expansion of scattering amplitudes.

In this paper, we will first review the concept of asymptotic freedom and its relevance to the study of heavy quarkonia. We will then introduce the RG procedure and demonstrate how it can be used to study the evolution of the effective coupling constant as the energy scale is changed. Finally, we will apply these concepts to the specific case of heavy quarkonia and discuss the implications of our results for the behavior of these systems at different energy scales. In this paper, we have presented a detailed analysis of the renormalization group (RG) procedure for effective particles in the context of heavy quarkonia. We have reviewed the concept of asymptotic freedom and demonstrated how the RG procedure can be used to study the evolution of the effective coupling constant as the energy scale is changed. Finally, we have applied these concepts to the specific case of heavy quarkonia and discussed the implications of our results for the behavior of these systems at different energy scales. Our results provide a deeper understanding of the behavior of heavy quarkonia and the strong force at different energy scales.

This paper aims to explore the presence of supernovae in indigenous astronomical traditions and whether or not they have played a significant role in these traditions. Supernovae are some of the most spectacular and powerful events in the universe, and their impact on human cultures and civilizations has been significant. This paper will examine the various ways in which supernovae have been recorded and interpreted by indigenous cultures, and how they have influenced the development of these cultures' astronomical traditions. Supernovae are the explosive deaths of massive stars, resulting in the emission of a tremendous amount of energy and radiation. These events are among the most powerful and dramatic in the universe, and have been observed and recorded by cultures around the world for centuries. In many cases, supernovae have played a significant role in the development of human cultures and civilizations, and have influenced the development of their astronomical traditions.

Indigenous cultures, in particular, have a rich history of observing and interpreting celestial events, and supernovae are no exception. Many indigenous cultures have recorded the appearance of supernovae and have incorporated these events into their astronomical traditions and cosmologies. This paper will examine the various ways in which supernovae have been recorded and interpreted by indigenous cultures, and how they have influenced the development of these cultures' astronomical traditions. In conclusion, it is clear that supernovae have played a significant role in the development of indigenous astronomical traditions. Many indigenous cultures have recorded the appearance of supernovae and have incorporated these events into their cosmologies and traditions. While the specifics of these records and interpretations may vary from culture to culture, it is clear that supernovae have had a lasting impact on the development of indigenous astronomical traditions.

Renormalization group (RG) flows are a powerful tool for understanding the behavior of quantum field theories (QFTs) at different energy scales. In this work, we explore the constraints on perturbative RG flows in six dimensions, focusing on the role of global symmetry and the structure of the gauge group. We find that global symmetry plays a crucial role in constraining the form of the RG flow, while the structure of the gauge group can also have significant implications. In the conclusion, we discuss the potential implications of these results for the study of QFTs in six dimensions and beyond. The study of RG flows has a long history in the field of QFT, with numerous applications in both high energy physics and condensed matter systems. In general, RG flows describe how the coupling constants and other parameters of a QFT change as the energy scale is varied. These flows can be classified as perturbative or non-perturbative, depending on whether the coupling constants are small or large, respectively. In this work, we focus on perturbative RG flows in six dimensions, which have received relatively little attention in the literature compared to lower dimensional cases.

One of the key features of RG flows is the role of global symmetry. In many cases, global symmetries can constrain the form of the RG flow, leading to important predictions about the behavior of the QFT at different energy scales. For example, in four dimensions, global symmetries can lead to the emergence of fixed points, which are points in the coupling constant space where the RG flow stops. In six dimensions, however, the situation is more complicated due to the additional degrees of freedom and the possibility of additional gauge group structures. In this work, we have explored the constraints on perturbative RG flows in six dimensions, focusing on the role of global symmetry and the structure of the gauge group. We have found that global symmetry plays a crucial role in constraining the form of the RG flow, while the structure of the gauge group can also have significant implications. These results have potential applications in the study of QFTs in six dimensions and beyond, and may provide new insights into the behavior of these theories at different energy scales.

In this paper, we propose a novel multiple access scheme for millimeter wave (mmWave) drone communication systems that utilizes non-orthogonal multiple access (NOMA) and multi-antenna transmission. By allowing multiple drones to transmit simultaneously on the same frequency band, NOMA can significantly improve the spectral efficiency of the communication system. Additionally, the use of multi-antenna transmission can further enhance the performance by increasing the spatial diversity of the system. Simulation results show that the proposed scheme can achieve a significant improvement in both spectral efficiency and energy efficiency compared to traditional orthogonal multiple access schemes. In recent years, there has been a growing interest in using drones for various applications, such as package delivery, search and rescue, and surveillance. One of the key challenges in enabling reliable drone communication is the limited availability of wireless spectrum, especially in the millimeter wave (mmWave) frequency bands. To overcome this challenge, multiple access schemes that allow multiple drones to share the same frequency band are needed.

Orthogonal multiple access (OMA) schemes, such as frequency division multiple access (FDMA) and time division multiple access (TDMA), are widely used in traditional wireless communication systems. However, these schemes may not be efficient for mmWave drone communication due to the high frequency-selectivity of the mmWave channels. Non-orthogonal multiple access (NOMA) schemes, on the other hand, can significantly improve the spectral efficiency by allowing multiple users to transmit simultaneously on the same frequency band.

In this paper, we propose a NOMA-based multiple access scheme for mmWave drone communication systems that utilizes multi-antenna transmission. By using multiple antennas at the transmitter and receiver, the proposed scheme can increase the spatial diversity of the system, which can further improve the performance. In this paper, we have presented a novel multiple access scheme for mmWave drone communication systems that utilizes NOMA and multi-antenna transmission. Simulation results show that the proposed scheme can achieve a significant improvement in both spectral efficiency and energy efficiency compared to traditional OMA schemes. This makes it a promising candidate for enabling reliable and efficient communication in mmWave drone systems.

This study presents the results of a detailed analysis of the radio and infrared observations of the high-mass young stellar object G345.4938+01.4677. Our analysis reveals the presence of a slow ionized wind emanating from the object, as well as a rotating disklike system with a radius of approximately 1000 au. The wind is found to have a velocity of around 20 km/s and a mass-loss rate of 10^-5 solar masses per year, while the disk exhibits signs of ongoing accretion. These findings provide important insights into the early evolution and mass-loss processes of high-mass young stellar objects. High-mass young stellar objects (HMYSOs) are a class of objects that represent the early stages of the formation of massive stars. They are characterized by the presence of strong radiation, powerful winds, and large amounts of dust and gas, and are thought to play a significant role in the chemical evolution and structure of the interstellar medium. Despite their importance, the physical processes underlying the evolution of HMYSOs are still not fully understood.

One key aspect of HMYSO evolution is the role of mass loss. Mass loss can occur through various mechanisms, including the ejection of material in the form of winds and jets, as well as the accretion of material onto the central object. Understanding the nature and evolution of these mass-loss processes is crucial for understanding the overall evolution of HMYSOs.

In this study, we present the results of a detailed analysis of the radio and infrared observations of the HMYSO G345.4938+01.4677. Our analysis reveals the presence of a slow ionized wind emanating from the object, as well as a rotating disklike system with a radius of approximately 1000 au. We also determine the velocity and mass-loss rate of the wind, and examine the signs of ongoing accretion in the disk. The results of our analysis provide important insights into the early evolution and mass-loss processes of HMYSOs. The presence of a slow ionized wind and a rotating disklike system in G345.4938+01.4677 suggests that both ejection and accretion are important mechanisms for mass loss in this object. Further studies of other HMYSOs will be needed to determine the prevalence and importance of these processes in the overall evolution of these objects.

The concept of surface/state correspondence, also known as the holographic principle, is a fundamental principle in theoretical physics that suggests that the information contained in a region of space can be encoded in the degrees of freedom of its boundary. This principle has been successful in explaining the behavior of black holes and has also been applied to other areas of physics, including the study of quantum gravity and the fundamental nature of spacetime. In this paper, we will explore the concept of surface/state correspondence and discuss its potential as a generalized holography, capable of explaining a wide range of physical phenomena. In the case of the holographic principle, the information contained within a region of space is thought to be encoded in the degrees of freedom of its boundary, such as the shape and size of the boundary or the properties of the matter and energy contained within it.

Further research and exploration of the holographic principle may provide new insights into the fundamental nature of the universe and our place within it.

In this paper, we present stable one-dimensional integral representations of one-loop N-point functions in the general massive case, with a focus on three-point functions. We demonstrate that these representations can be used to accurately calculate the one-loop corrections to various physical quantities in a systematic and efficient manner. One-loop corrections are an important aspect of quantum field theory, as they can provide insight into the behavior of physical systems at the quantum level. In particular, N-point functions, which describe the probability amplitudes for the production of N particles, play a crucial role in understanding the interactions between particles. However, their calculation can be complex, especially in the case of massive particles.

In this work, we aim to provide a stable and reliable method for calculating one-loop N-point functions in the general massive case. To do this, we derive one-dimensional integral representations of these functions, which can be easily evaluated using numerical techniques. We focus specifically on three-point functions, as they play a particularly important role in many physical processes. In summary, we have presented a stable and efficient method for calculating one-loop N-point functions in the general massive case, with a focus on three-point functions. These integral representations provide a reliable means of calculating the one-loop corrections to various physical quantities, and can be easily implemented using numerical techniques. This work is an important step towards a better understanding of the quantum behavior of massive particles and the interactions between them.

The dual flux-tube picture for the scattering amplitudes in the antisymmetric bi-fundamental ABJM model is presented. It is based on the observation that the dual operator describing the excitation of n off-shell fundamental strings has to create an additional vacuum on top of the one describing the ground state of the dual ABJM field theory (1+1-dimensional U(N)×U(N) gauge model) in the limit n → ∞. Starting from the known flux-tube picture for the symmetric matrix model (N=M in ABJM model), we first find the dual flux tube picture for the antisymmetric model, then we generalize it to the case where one of the two matrices has some different rank. The dual flux tube picture for the scattering amplitudes in the symmetric ABJM model [1-6] was first introduced in [7] and then developed in [8-12]. The basic idea is the following. The dual operator describing the excitation of n off-mass shell fundamental strings has to create an additional vacuum on the top of the one describing the ground state of dual ABJM field theory (1+3-dimensional U(N)×U(M)×U(N) gauge theory) in the limit n → ∞. In other words, in the limit of large number of strings, the dual flux tube is effectively a fundamental string. The dual flux tube picture for the symmetric ABJM model was generalized to the case where one of the two matrices is of different rank in [13].    The goal of this paper is to present the dual flux-tube picture for the antisymmetric ABJM model. It is based on the observation that the antisymmetric model is dual to the symmetric model in the limit of large number of strings. In other words, in the limit of very large number of strings, the dual flux tube of the antisymmetric model is dual to the symmetric flux tube of the symmetric model. The dual flux tube picture for the antisymmetric ABJM model was introduced in [14]. The basic idea is the following. In the limit of very large number of strings, there is a dual description of the ABJM theory in terms of the symmetric ABJM model. Therefore, the dual flux tube picture for the antisymmetric ABJM can be obtained by applying the dual flux tube picture for the symmetric ABJM to the symmetric ABJM model.

By using a bilayer composed of monolayer molybdenum disulfide and monolayer tungsten disulfide as the source/drain channel and a monolayer of hexagonal WS2 as the emission layer, we realized a simple but effective valley polarization device driven by spin-polarized spin injection. Spin-polarized electrons injected into the monolayer WS2 could excite spin-polarized excitons in the channel layer to efficiently enhance its light emission. 2D materials have attracted considerable attention due to their intriguing electronic and optical properties, and have shown great potential for applications in electronics, photonics, and spintronics [1][2][3]. Among them, transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDCs) are a family of layered materials with a single-layer thickness of less than 1 nm. They have the unique properties of semiconducting and metallic properties, and are thus suitable for a variety of applications in the field of electronic devices [4,5].    The monolayer of TMDCs is an indirect band gap material, and thus it is not a good candidate for a light-emitting diode (LED). However, the band gap of TMDCs can be tuned by applying an electric field, which makes them very suitable for use in LED applications [6]. In addition, TMDCs can be used to construct van der Waals (vdW) heterostructures, which can be used as an efficient light-emitting channel in LEDs [7].    However, to date, there have been few reports on the use of TMDCs as light emitters [8,9]. In this work, we demonstrate the efficient light emission of a monolayer WS2 by spin-polarized excitons. We then demonstrate a simple, effective, and tunable valley polarization device by using a bilayer composed of monolayer molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) and monolayer tungsten disulfide (WS2) as the source/drain channel and a single-layer of hexagonal WS2 as the emission layer. Spin-polarized electrons injected into the monolayer TMDC could excite spin-polarized excitons in the channel to efficiently enhance its light emission. Materials and Methods:    1. Synthesis of Monolayer WS2.    2. Fabrication of the MoS2/WS2 Bilayer.    3. Fabrication of the MoS2/WS2/WS2 Monolayer.    4. Fabrication of the MoS2/WS2 vdW Heterojunction LED.    5. Fabrication of the MoS2/WS2 Monolayer LED.    6. Fabrication of the MoS2/WS2 LED.

This paper presents a novel and effective way of Data Acquisition System (DAS) designed for a Photo-Neutron Source (PNS) which is working on the research on the nuclear reaction mechanism in heavy-ion fusion. The system consists of data acquisition and control modules. The latter is made up by a personal computer (PC), waveform digitizer modules, DAQ (Data AcQuisition) board and an industrial PC (IP card) to transfer the data through Ethernet. The DAQ card has a good scalability, high real time and high voltage resolution. Besides, we built a waveform digitizer module, which is based on a parallel dataflow data acquisition system FPGA and a LabVIEW platform. It is able of working in the wide frequency band of photoelectric detectors (PE detectors), which makes up for shortcomings of the conventional digitizer cards and the PC data acquisition card, as low frequency band and low voltage resolution respectively. It is an important means for the PNS DAS’ function improvement from two aspects. On one hand, it is able of acquiring waveform data at the maximum speed and the lowest voltage resolution, so the detector has a comprehensive data acquisition performance. On the other hand, it is able of realizing a high frequency detection for PE detectors, as well as the multi-channel data acquisition by means of multi-channel parallel dataflow. The PNS is a facility of nuclear reaction mechanism research in heavy-ion fusion which is based on the photo-neutron reaction (14 MeV/u 12C+197Au). It has been constructed in the Institute of Modern Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, and has been commissioned for operation since 2013. The experiment uses the 200 MeV/u 12C beam, which is produced by the Heavy-Ion Medical Accelerator in Lanzhou (HIMAL), to bombard a gold target with a thickness of 10 mg/cm2. The target is placed in a 10-cm-diameter stainless steel can, which is surrounded by a 10-cm-thick water shield. The target and shield are placed in a vacuum chamber with a pressure of 10-5 Torr. The 200 MeV/u 13C beam, which is produced by the HIMAL, is used to bombard a lead target with a thickness of 50 mg/cm2. The target is also placed in a vacuum chamber with a pressure of about 10-5 Torr.    The Photo-Neutron Source     The experiment uses the PE detectors to detect the photo-neutron reactions and the 200 MeV/u 7Li beam to bombard the lead target. The PE detectors are made up of the 20-mm-thick and 3-mm-diameter Si(Li) detectors. The 7Li beam is accelerated by the 150-MeV cyclotron, and the beam current is about 2.5 mA. The PE detectors are placed at 300 mm from the target.    The Data Acquisition System     The data acquisition system consists of a waveform digitizer module, a data acquisition card, a personal computer and a industrial computer. The waveform digitizer module is the main module of the data acquisition system. It is able of acquiring waveform data at the maximum sampling rate, as well as the lowest voltage resolution. The data acquisition card is an industrial PC (IP card) which is used to transfer the data through Ethernet. The personal computer is used to display and control the data acquisition system. The industrial computer is used to transfer data to the personal computer.    The Waveform Digitizer Module     The waveform digitizer module is made up of a parallel dataflow data acquisition system FPGA and an FPGA-based waveform digitizer card. It is able of working in the wide range of photoelectric detectors’ frequency band, which makes up for the shortcomings of the conventional digitizer cards and the PCIe-based card. The parallel dataflow data acquisition system FPGA is used to realize the high sampling rate data acquisition. The waveform digitizer card is used to digitize the waveform data, and the waveform data is sent to the FPGA through the PCIe interface.    The FPGA-based waveform digitizer card     The waveform digitizer card is based on a parallel dataflow data acquisition module. The parallel dataflow data acquisition module is used to realize the high sampling rate data collection. The FPGA-based waveform digitizer card is used to digitise the waveform data, and the waveform data is transferred to the FPGA through the PCIe interface. It can digitize the waveform data of the PE detectors with a sampling rate of 200 MS/s. The sampling rate of the waveform digitizer card is higher than the sampling rate of the conventional digitizer card.    The FPGA-based waveform data acquisition system     The FPGA-based waveform data acquisition system is made up of a waveform digitizer module, a data collection card, a personal computer and an industrial computer. The waveform digitizer module is used to digitize the waveform data of the PE detector, and the waveform data is transferred to the data collection card through the PCIe interface. The data collection card is used to transfer the waveform data to the personal computer through Ethernet. The personal computer is used to control the data acquisition system. The industrial computer transfers the data to the personal computer. The data acquisition system of the PNS is designed and implemented. The waveform digitizer module, which is based on the parallel dataflow data acquisition system, is able of working in the wide frequency band. It is an important means for the P NS DAS’ function improvement. The waveform digitizer card, which is based on the parallel dataflow, is able of digitizing the waveform data of the PE detectors with a high sampling rate. It is an important means for the P N S DAS’ function improvement.

We study coverings of rigid varieties over a base field which preserve all ultrametric topologies. We prove that if the covering admits a local description by means of rational functions, the covering itself can be given in this way -- thus giving several examples of coverings satisfying a local description, which, moreover, is actually a description of the covering locally. \begin{itemize}     \item The paper [L] (see also [B]) contains a general study of coverings of Berkovich curves. The main result there (Theorem 2.2) says that the existence of a local description of the covering $p:Y\to X$ (i.e. a rational function $f:Y\to\mathbb{P}^1$ such that $p=f\circ q$, where $q:Y\to X$ is the structure morphism) is equivalent to the existence of a local description of the covering by means of rational functions $f_i:Y\to\mathbb{P}_i$ ($i\in I$).     \item We will use this result in Section 2.2 to prove that, if $X$ is a Berkovich curve and $p:Y\to X$ is a covering which admits a local description by means of rational functions $f_1,\ldots,f_n:Y\to\mathbb{P}$, then $p$ can be given by means of rational functions $f_{ij}:Y\to\mathbb{P}\times\mathbb{P}$ ($i\in I$, $j\in J_i$).     \item In Section 2.3 we will apply the above result to obtain several examples of coverings satisfying a local description, all of which are actually descriptions of the covering locally.     \item In Section 3 we will apply the above result to prove that, if $X$ and $Y$ are Berkovich curves, then a covering $p:Y\to X\times\mathbb{P}^1$ which admits a local description by means of rational maps $f_i:Y\to X\times\mathbb P^1$ ($i\in I$; here we use the same notation as in [L]) is actually a description of the covering locally. This generalizes the result of [L].   \end{itemize}. \begin{itemize}     $\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\qquad$     \item The result of [L] (Theorem 2.2) can be seen as a generalization of the classical theorem of Zariski ([Z], Theorem 2.1), which states that, if $X$ is a smooth affine curve over a field $k$, then any covering $p:Y\to X^{\mathrm{an}}$ is locally trivial.     $\quad\quad\quad\;\;$     \item The above result can be seen as a generalization of the result of [B] (Theorem 1.1), which states that, if a proper and flat morphism $p:Y\to X$ between Berkovich curves admits a local description by means of rational functions which are not constant on $Y$, then $p$ is étale.     $\quad\quad\quad$     \item The above result can also be seen as a generalization of the result of Section 2.2 of [L], which states that, if $X=\operatorname{Spa}(A,A^+)$ is a Berkovich curve, $p:Y\to X$ a proper and flat morphism, and $f:Y\to\mathbb P^1$ a rational function on $Y$, then $f$ can be seen as a rational function $f':Y\to\mathbb P^1\times\mathbb P^1$.     $\quad\quad\quad $     \item The above result can easily be seen as a generalization of the result of Theorem 2.1 of [B], which states that, if $k$ is a field, $X$ a smooth affine curve over $k$, and $p:Y\to X^\mathrm{an}$ a covering which admits a local description by means rational functions, then $p$ is étale.      $\quad\quad\quad $      \item The above result can also easily be seen as a generalization of the result stated in Theorem 2.2 of [B], which states that if $X$ is a smooth affine $k$-curve, $k$ a complete discrete valuation ring of mixed characteristic, $p:Y\to X^*$ a covering which admits a local description by rational functions, then $p$ is locally trivial.     $\quad \quad\quad $   \end{itemize}    I would like to thank A. Berkovich for suggesting the topic of this paper.

We study the beamline at SOLEIL, the largest and most powerful of its kind, in the hard X-ray range. The beamline is the most energetic of its kind, with an average mass of 1.3 GeV and a luminosity of 10 TeV, with a maximum mass of about 100 GeV. The X-ray luminosity is derived from the photometric data of the photometric detector, and from the photometric data of the optical detector. We find that the beamline is able to reach the maximum mass of about 100 GeV, and to reach the maximum luminosity of about 100 GeV, when the luminosity is at least a few orders of magnitude smaller than the photometric data. The beamline can be interpreted as a "black hole", in which the luminosity is not sufficiently large to explain the X-ray flux, and as a "black hole" in which the X-ray flux is too small to explain the X-ray emission. We conclude that the beamline is a "black hole", and that it is a result of the photometric data, rather than the X-ray flux, that the X-ray flux is not sufficient to explain the X-ray emission. We consider a fluid-solid interaction problem under a control and a state constraint. We study the variational formulation of the associated state-constrained optimal control problem. A regularized version of the state constraint is enforced by means of a penalization term with a singular parameter ε which vanishes when ε → 0. The main problem is the presence of an unbounded term in the adjoint system, which requires for technical reasons to introduce a cutoff operator to circumvent this issue. This paper is devoted to the analysis of well-posedness properties and the derivation of the optimality conditions for the problem involving the cutoff operator. We establish sufficient conditions for the well-posedness of the optimality system. Finally, some numerical simulations illustrate the behavior of the corresponding regularized problem as well as the convergence towards the original optimal control as the regularization parameter vanishes. \begin{abstract}     \label{abstract}       \begin{center}         \begin{tabular}{c}           \includegraphics[width=0.7\textwidth]{figures/pde_system_1}           \end{tabular}         \end{center}       \end{abstract}   \end{abstract}    This paper is devoted to the analysis of the optimal control problem of a PDE model of fluid-solid interaction with a state constraint. The main problem is the presence of an adjoint variable which is unbounded in the state constraint. This problem is the so-called \emph{penalization problem}, which is a typical obstacle to the application of the standard tools of the calculus of variations. In order to overcome this obstacle, we introduce a cutoff operator and consider the corresponding regularized problem. We establish sufficient conditions for the well-posednes of the optimality system. Finally, some numerical illustrations illustrate the behavior of the regularized problem as well as the convergence towards the optimal control as the regularization parameter vanishes. \end{document}. \begin{itemize}     \item The regularized problem can be solved numerically with standard optimization algorithms, provided the optimal control is regular enough.     \item The regularized problem converges towards the optimal control as the regularization parameter vanishes, provided the optimal control is regular enough. \end{itemize}  \end{document}.

Polar codes are a class of error-correcting codes that have been shown to achieve the symmetric capacity of a given channel. However, traditional decoding algorithms for polar codes, such as successive cancellation decoding, can have a high complexity. In this paper, we propose a new decoding algorithm called dynamic-SCFlip decoding, which is based on the idea of flipping the bits in the decision region of the decoding tree in order to correct errors. We demonstrate that dynamic-SCFlip decoding can achieve a similar error-correction performance to successive cancellation decoding, but with a lower complexity. Polar codes, first proposed by Arıkan in 2009, have gained significant attention in the field of error-correction due to their capacity-achieving properties and low encoding/decoding complexity. The key idea behind polar codes is the concept of channel polarization, where the bits in the original message are transformed into a new sequence of bits through a series of XOR operations, such that the reliability of each bit in the new sequence is different. The bits with higher reliability can be decoded directly, while the bits with lower reliability need to be estimated through the use of an error-correcting code.

One of the most popular decoding algorithms for polar codes is successive cancellation decoding (SCD), which traverses the polar code's decoding tree in a left-to-right manner and makes decisions on the bits based on the previously decoded bits. However, SCD can have a high complexity, especially for long codes or codes with high error-correction performance.

In this paper, we propose a new decoding algorithm called dynamic-SCFlip decoding, which is based on the idea of flipping the bits in the decision region of the decoding tree in order to correct errors. The decision region is defined as the set of bits that have not yet been decoded and are used to make decisions on the remaining bits. By flipping the bits in the decision region, we can effectively "flip" the decision made by the decoder, thereby correcting any errors that may have been made. We have presented a new decoding algorithm for polar codes called dynamic-SCFlip decoding, which is based on the idea of flipping the bits in the decision region of the decoding tree in order to correct errors. We have demonstrated that dynamic-SCFlip decoding can achieve a similar error-correction performance to SCD, but with a lower complexity. This makes dynamic-SCFlip decoding an attractive alternative for decoding polar codes, especially in situations where low complexity is a concern.

Exoplanets, or planets outside of our solar system, have garnered significant attention in recent years due to their potential to harbor life. Understanding the atmospheres of these planets is crucial for identifying biomarkers and determining their habitability. The ARCiS (Atmospheric and Radiative Characterization of exoplanetS) framework is a tool for studying exoplanet atmospheres, specifically focusing on the transport of clouds and their effect on the planet's radiative balance. This paper will introduce the ARCiS framework and discuss the development and implementation of the Cloud Transport Model within it. Exoplanets are fascinating objects of study due to their potential to harbor life and their diversity in size, composition, and orbit. In order to understand the atmospheres of these planets, it is necessary to develop tools that can simulate and analyze the various processes occurring within them. The ARCiS framework is one such tool, designed to study exoplanet atmospheres through the use of numerical simulations. 

One aspect of exoplanet atmospheres that the ARCiS framework is particularly well-suited for is the transport of clouds. Clouds play a critical role in the radiative balance of a planet, as they can either absorb or reflect incoming solar radiation depending on their composition and thickness. The Cloud Transport Model within the ARCiS framework is designed to simulate the movement and evolution of clouds within exoplanet atmospheres, taking into account factors such as atmospheric circulation and the presence of other atmospheric constituents. The ARCiS framework is a valuable tool for studying exoplanet atmospheres, and the Cloud Transport Model is a key component of its capabilities. By simulating the movement and evolution of clouds within exoplanet atmospheres, the ARCiS framework is able to provide insight into the role that clouds play in the radiative balance of these planets. This is crucial for understanding the habitability of exoplanets and for identifying biomarkers that may be present in their atmospheres.

The intracluster medium (ICM) in galaxy clusters is a hot, diffuse plasma that is permeated by magnetic fields. Understanding the properties of this magnetized ICM is important for our understanding of the dynamics and evolution of galaxy clusters. In this work, we use observations of gas sloshing in the galaxy cluster Abell 2204 to constrain the properties of the ICM. By analyzing the spatial distribution and kinematics of the gas, we find that the ICM in Abell 2204 is highly magnetized, with a magnetic field strength of several microgauss. Our results also suggest that the ICM in Abell 2204 is in approximate hydrostatic equilibrium, with little evidence for significant non-thermal pressure support. Galaxy clusters are the largest gravitationally bound structures in the universe, consisting of hundreds or thousands of galaxies, hot gas, and dark matter. The hot gas, known as the intracluster medium (ICM), is a diffuse plasma that fills the space between the galaxies and accounts for a significant fraction of the cluster's mass. The ICM is highly dynamic, with gas flowing and mixing on a wide range of scales. One important process that drives this dynamics is gas sloshing, which occurs when the gravitational potential of the cluster is perturbed, such as by the passage of a subcluster or a merger event.

Gas sloshing can be observed as characteristic patterns in the spatial distribution and kinematics of the gas. By studying these patterns, we can gain insight into the properties of the ICM, such as its temperature, density, and magnetic field strength. In this work, we focus on the galaxy cluster Abell 2204, which is known to have undergone a recent merger event and exhibits clear signs of gas sloshing. By analyzing observations of Abell 2204, we aim to constrain the properties of its magnetized ICM. In conclusion, we have used observations of gas sloshing in the galaxy cluster Abell 2204 to constrain the properties of its magnetized intracluster medium. Our analysis shows that the ICM in Abell 2204 is highly magnetized, with a magnetic field strength of several microgauss. We also find that the ICM is in approximate hydrostatic equilibrium, with little evidence for significant non-thermal pressure support. These results provide important constraints on our understanding of the dynamics and evolution of galaxy clusters, and can be used to improve models of the ICM and its role in the formation and evolution of galaxies.

The Dirac equation is a fundamental equation in particle physics that describes the behavior of massive spin-1/2 particles, such as electrons and neutrinos. In this paper, we extend the Dirac equation to include the effects of a de Sitter expansion, a type of cosmological expansion that is characterized by a positive cosmological constant. We apply this extension to the case of massive neutrinos, using modern Kaluza-Klein theory to incorporate the additional dimensions required by the de Sitter expansion. Our results show that the inclusion of the de Sitter expansion leads to significant modifications to the behavior of massive neutrinos, and may have important implications for our understanding of the early universe and the evolution of the cosmos. Our results show that the inclusion of the de Sitter expansion leads to significant modifications to the behavior of massive neutrinos, and may have important implications for our understanding of the early universe and the evolution of the cosmos.
