We present the results of a study on the dusty environment surrounding the Seyfert 1 nucleus of NGC 3783 using MIDI/VLTI interferometry. Our observations reveal the presence of a compact dust structure at a distance of 0.1 pc from the central supermassive black hole. The dust structure has a temperature of approximately 1000 K and is composed of small (sub-micron) sized grains. The structure is found to be partially obscured by a foreground screen of dust, which is located at a distance of approximately 0.5 pc from the central black hole. Our observations provide new insights into the structure and properties of the dusty environment in the immediate vicinity of a Seyfert 1 nucleus. Seyfert galaxies are a class of active galactic nuclei (AGN) characterized by the presence of a central supermassive black hole and the emission of strong optical and ultraviolet radiation. The nucleus of a Seyfert galaxy is typically surrounded by a dusty torus, which is thought to play a key role in the AGN's radiation output. However, the structure and properties of the dusty environment in the immediate vicinity of a Seyfert 1 nucleus remain poorly understood.

In this study, we used MIDI/VLTI interferometry to probe the dusty environment of the Seyfert 1 nucleus in NGC 3783, a nearby (z=0.00973) galaxy with a well-studied AGN. MIDI (Mid-Infrared Interferometric instrument) is a facility at the Very Large Telescope (VLT) in Chile that allows for the measurement of the visibility of astronomical sources at mid-infrared wavelengths, while VLTI (Very Large Telescope Interferometer) is a system that combines the light from multiple telescopes to achieve higher angular resolution. By using these instruments, we were able to obtain high-resolution images of the dusty environment surrounding the nucleus of NGC 3783. Our observations of NGC 3783 using MIDI/VLTI interferometry have revealed the presence of a compact dust structure located at a distance of 0.1 pc from the central supermassive black hole. The dust structure has a temperature of approximately 1000 K and is composed of small (sub-micron) sized grains. The structure is found to be partially obscured by a foreground screen of dust, which is located at a distance of approximately 0.5 pc from the central black hole. These results provide new insights into the structure and properties of the dusty environment in the immediate vicinity of a Seyfert 1 nucleus and may have implications for our understanding of the AGN radiation output and fueling process.

In this paper, we present a probabilistic analysis of a discrete-time evolution process in recombination. Recombination is a fundamental process in genetics that involves the exchange of genetic material between chromosomes during the formation of gametes. Understanding the probabilistic behavior of recombination is important for predicting the outcomes of genetic crosses and for studying the evolution of genetic diversity. We use a Markov chain model to analyze the evolution of recombination in a population over time and derive analytical expressions for the probability of different recombination outcomes. Our results provide insights into the role of recombination in shaping the genetic structure of populations and can inform the development of statistical models for analyzing genetic data. Recombination is a key process in genetics that plays a critical role in the formation of new combinations of genetic material. It occurs during the formation of gametes (sperm and eggs) and involves the exchange of DNA between chromosomes. Recombination is important for maintaining genetic diversity within populations and for generating the genetic variation that drives evolution.

There are several different types of recombination, including crossing-over, gene conversion, and transposition. Crossing-over is the most common form of recombination and occurs during meiosis, the process by which gametes are formed. During crossing-over, segments of DNA on homologous chromosomes are exchanged, resulting in the formation of new combinations of genetic material.

The probability of a particular recombination outcome is influenced by several factors, including the genetic distance between the recombining loci, the recombination rate, and the genetic makeup of the population. Understanding the probabilistic behavior of recombination is important for predicting the outcomes of genetic crosses and for studying the evolution of genetic diversity within populations.

In this paper, we present a probabilistic analysis of a discrete-time evolution process in recombination using a Markov chain model. We derive analytical expressions for the probability of different recombination outcomes and use these results to provide insights into the role of recombination in shaping the genetic structure of populations. In summary, we have presented a probabilistic analysis of a discrete-time evolution process in recombination using a Markov chain model. Our results provide insights into the role of recombination in shaping the genetic structure of populations and can inform the development of statistical models for analyzing genetic data. Our analysis highlights the importance of considering the probabilistic behavior of recombination in understanding the evolution of genetic diversity within populations.

The Harish-Chandra isomorphism is a fundamental result in representation theory, connecting the center of a universal enveloping algebra with the invariant polynomials on a Lie algebra. In this paper, we generalize the Harish-Chandra isomorphism to the setting of reductive group schemes over an arbitrary base scheme. As a consequence, we obtain a new proof of the classical Harish-Chandra isomorphism in the case of complex semisimple Lie algebras. Representation theory is the study of the ways in which a group or algebra can act on a vector space. One of the central problems in representation theory is to understand the relationship between the representation theory of a group and the representation theory of its Lie algebra. In the case of a complex semisimple Lie algebra, the Harish-Chandra isomorphism provides a solution to this problem by establishing an isomorphism between the center of the universal enveloping algebra of the Lie algebra and the invariant polynomials on the Lie algebra.

In this paper, we generalize the Harish-Chandra isomorphism to the setting of reductive group schemes over an arbitrary base scheme. This generalization allows us to extend the classical result to a much wider class of groups and algebras, including those defined over fields of positive characteristic. The generalized Harish-Chandra isomorphism is a powerful tool for understanding the representation theory of reductive group schemes. By establishing a connection between the center of the universal enveloping algebra and the invariant polynomials on the Lie algebra, it provides a new way of understanding the relationship between the representation theory of a group and the representation theory of its Lie algebra. As a consequence of our generalization, we have also obtained a new proof of the classical Harish-Chandra isomorphism in the case of complex semisimple Lie algebras.

This article is an extended version of the work published as an extended abstract at the International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR) (Li et al., 2020). In this version, we propose a new private sequential learning algorithm for general convex and strongly-convex loss functions under local differential private constraints, with sample complexity matching the lower bounds established in Liu et al (2019). We also prove a new general privacy guarantee, i.e. differential privacy, for the proposed algorithm as compared to the local differential privacy. Further, we propose an improved algorithm that achieves the same sample efficiency as the state of the art, while improving the privacy parameter while requiring weaker convergence guarantees. We establish the privacy property of the proposed algorithm and prove a more refined privacy guarantee for the improved algorithm. Finally, a detailed analysis on the proposed algorithm and its improved version, and the results from our experiments are presented. The problem of private sequential learning has received significant attention in recent years (Gupta et al., 2017; Kairouz et al., 2017). This problem is motivated by a wide range of applications including healthcare (Dwork et al., 2012), recommendation systems (Liu et al., 2019), and online advertising (Wang et al., 2017b). In such applications, the data is collected sequentially, and each time a user accesses a database, her data is exposed to the database. In order to protect the privacy of the users, the database must learn the users’ data from the released data, and this learning process is private.    A common assumption in the literature is that the data is generated from a fixed distribution \(\mu\). However, in many applications, the data is generated according to a stochastic process \(X_{1},\dots,X_{n}\sim\mu\). In this case, the problem becomes challenging, as the data at different time points are dependent. A common solution is to assume that the data at different time points are independent, i.e., \(X_{1},X_{2},\dots\) are i.i.d. (Li et al., 2100; Wang et al., 2017a). In this paper, we consider the case where the data is generated from a stationary Markov chain. We propose a private sequential learning algorithm for general convex and strongly convex loss functions under local differential private constraints, with the sample complexity matching the lower bounds established in Liu and Wang (2019). We prove a new general privacy guarantee, i.i.d. differential privacy, for the proposed algorithm as well as the improved algorithm. Further, we propose an improved algorithm that has a better privacy parameter and requires weaker convergence guarantees. Finally, we present a detailed analysis of the proposed algorithm and the improved algorithm, and present the results from our experiments.    Related Work:    Private sequential learning has been studied in several works in the literature (Li et al., 2200; Wang et al., 17a; Wang et al., 2008; Wang et al., 2020; Xu et al., 2019; Yu et al., 2018; Yu et al., 2020b). In Li et al. (2019), the authors considered the problem of learning a Markov chain under local differential privacy constraints. In this work, the authors proposed a private sequential learning algorithm that has a sample complexity that matches the lower bounds established in Liu and Wang 2019. In Xu et al. (2019a), the authors considered a more general case where the data at each time point is generated from a general distribution. In this work, the authors proposed a differentially private sequential learning algorithm that has a sample complexity matching the lower bounds established in Liu 2019. In Yu et al. (2020b), the authors considered a more general case of the problem considered in this paper, where the data at each time point is generated according to a stationary Markov chain. In this work, the authors proposed a privacy preserving sequential learning algorithm that has a sample complexity matching that of the lower bounds established in Liu et al. (2019b). In this work, we propose a private sequential learning algorithm for general convex loss functions under local differential privacy constraints, with sample complexity matching the lower bounds. We also propose an improved algorithm that has a better privacy guarantee. We establish the privacy property of the proposed algorithms and prove a more refined privacy guarantee for the proposed algorithms. Symbol tables are a crucial component of many software systems, as they provide a mapping between symbolic names and their corresponding values. However, the design and implementation of symbol tables can be a complex and error-prone task, especially when the system needs to support multiple languages or has frequent updates. In this paper, we propose a novel approach to symbol table management that utilizes meta modeling and code generation techniques to automatically generate customized symbol table infrastructures. Our approach allows for easy modification and adaptation of the symbol table design, without the need for manual coding or debugging. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach through a case study on a multi-language software system, showing significant improvements in development time and stability. A symbol table is a data structure that stores a mapping between symbolic names and their corresponding values. Symbol tables are used in various contexts, such as compilers, interpreters, and run-time environments, to manage the binding of variables and functions to their values. The design and implementation of symbol tables can be a challenging task, as they need to support fast lookup and insertion of symbols, as well as handle conflicts and errors.

Traditionally, symbol tables are implemented manually, which can be time-consuming and error-prone. Moreover, the design of symbol tables is often specific to a particular language or system, making it difficult to reuse or adapt the implementation for other contexts. This can lead to maintenance issues, especially when the system needs to support multiple languages or has frequent updates.

To address these challenges, we propose a novel approach to symbol table management that utilizes meta modeling and code generation techniques. Specifically, we use a meta model to represent the abstract syntax and semantics of a symbol table, and generate customized symbol table infrastructures based on this meta model. The generated code can then be integrated into the target system, providing a flexible and adaptable symbol table implementation. In this paper, we have presented a novel approach to symbol table management that utilizes meta modeling and code generation techniques to generate customized symbol table infrastructures. Our approach allows for easy modification and adaptation of the symbol table design, without the need for manual coding or debugging. We have demonstrated the effectiveness of our approach through a case study on a multi-language software system, showing significant improvements in development time and stability. Our approach has the potential to greatly simplify the task of symbol table management in a variety of software systems.

The Landweber iteration method is a popular method for solving ill-posed inverse problems, but it can suffer from slow convergence and instability. In this paper, we propose an iteratively regularized Landweber iteration method that aims to improve the convergence rate and stability of the original Landweber method. We analyze the convergence of the proposed method using Hölder stability and provide numerical results to support our analysis. Inverse problems arise in a wide range of fields, including image reconstruction, electrical impedance tomography, and weather forecasting, to name a few. These problems involve finding the unknown input given the output and a known forward operator. However, in many cases, the forward operator is either poorly known or the problem is ill-posed, meaning that small errors in the data can lead to large errors in the solution.

One method for solving ill-posed inverse problems is the Landweber iteration method, which is based on the gradient descent algorithm. The Landweber method iteratively updates the estimate of the solution using the gradient of the objective function and a step size parameter. However, the convergence rate of the Landweber method can be slow and it can suffer from instability, especially for highly ill-posed problems.

To improve the convergence and stability of the Landweber method, we propose an iteratively regularized Landweber iteration method. In this method, we add a regularization term to the objective function at each iteration and adjust the regularization parameter based on the current estimate of the solution. By properly choosing the regularization parameter, we can improve the convergence rate and stability of the method. In this paper, we have proposed an iteratively regularized Landweber iteration method for solving ill-posed inverse problems. We have analyzed the convergence of the method using Hölder stability and provided numerical results to support our analysis. Our method demonstrates improved convergence rate and stability compared to the original Landweber method, especially for highly ill-posed problems. Further research is needed to study the performance of the proposed method in more general settings and to compare it with other regularization methods.

The magnetosphere of the ultracool dwarf DENIS 1048-3956 is a topic of increasing interest due to the potential impacts on the habitability of exoplanets that may orbit within its habitable zone. In this study, we utilize data from X-ray and ultraviolet observations to investigate the properties and dynamics of the magnetosphere of DENIS 1048-3956. Our analysis reveals a complex and variable magnetic field with a strength ranging from 0.1-0.5 kG and significant magnetic reconnection events. We also find evidence for the presence of a hot corona and high-energy particles, which may have implications for the atmospheric evolution and potential habitability of exoplanets orbiting within the habitable zone of DENIS 1048-3956. Ultracool dwarfs are low-mass, cool stars that occupy the mass range between brown dwarfs and late M-dwarfs. They have effective temperatures below 2500 K and radii ranging from 0.1-0.8 solar radii. These objects are of particular interest for the study of exoplanets, as they are thought to be capable of hosting habitable planets within their habitable zones. The magnetic activity of ultracool dwarfs is also of interest due to its potential impacts on the atmospheres and habitability of exoplanets orbiting within their habitable zones.

DENIS 1048-3956 is an ultracool dwarf located approximately 20 parsecs away in the constellation of Vela. It has an effective temperature of 2300 K and a mass of 0.06 solar masses. Previous studies have suggested that DENIS 1048-3956 may have a strong and variable magnetic field, with a strength ranging from 0.1-0.5 kG. In this study, we utilize data from X-ray and ultraviolet observations to investigate the properties and dynamics of the magnetosphere of DENIS 1048-3956. Our analysis of the X-ray and ultraviolet data reveals a complex and variable magnetic field with a strength ranging from 0.1-0.5 kG and significant magnetic reconnection events. We also find evidence for the presence of a hot corona and high-energy particles. These results have implications for the atmospheric evolution and potential habitability of exoplanets orbiting within the wearable zone of DENIS 1048-3956. Further studies will be needed to fully understand the impacts of the magnetosphere on exoplanetary atmospheres and habitability.

The La(Mn,Zn)AsO alloy is a promising material for spintronic applications due to its ability to exhibit both ferromagnetic and antiferromagnetic behavior. In this work, we explore the effect of carrier doping on the spin state of the La(Mn,Zn)AsO alloy. By introducing excess carriers through chemical doping or external electrical gating, we demonstrate that the spin state can be effectively controlled and tuned. Our results suggest that the La(Mn,Zn)AsO alloy is a highly tunable material for spintronic devices. Spintronics is a rapidly growing field that utilizes the spin of electrons in addition to their charge for the development of novel electronic devices. One promising material for spintronic applications is the La(Mn,Zn)AsO alloy, which exhibits both ferromagnetic and antiferromagnetic behavior depending on temperature and composition. The ability to control and tune the spin state of this material is essential for the development of spintronic devices.

One way to control the spin state of a material is through carrier doping, where excess carriers are introduced through chemical doping or external electrical gating. Carrier doping can affect the spin state of a material by altering the density of states and the exchange interaction between spins.

In this work, we investigate the effect of carrier doping on the spin state of the La(Mn,Zn)AsO alloy. We demonstrate that the spin state can be effectively controlled and tuned by introducing excess carriers through chemical doping or external electrical gating. Our results show that the La(Mn,Zn)AsO alloy is a highly tunable material for spintronic devices due to its ability to exhibit both ferromagnetic and antiferromagnetic behavior. By introducing excess carriers through chemical doping or external electrical gating, we demonstrate that the spin state can be effectively controlled and tuned. These findings suggest that the La(Mn,Zn)AsO alloy has great potential for use in spintronic devices and could lead to the development of new and improved spintronic technologies.

In this paper, we study quantum graph vertices that minimize the number of passbands, or energy ranges where the quantum states are allowed to pass through. We show that these vertices can be constructed using a combination of potential wells and barriers, and demonstrate how the number of passbands can be controlled through the geometry of the vertex. Our results may have applications in the design of quantum devices, such as quantum computers and sensors. Quantum graph vertices are important building blocks in the construction of quantum devices, such as quantum computers and sensors. These vertices allow for the manipulation of quantum states, and the number of passbands, or energy ranges where the quantum states are allowed to pass through, can have a significant impact on the performance of the device. In this paper, we focus on the design of quantum graph vertices that minimize the number of passbands. In conclusion, we have shown that quantum graph vertices with a minimal number of passbands can be constructed using a combination of potential wells and barriers. By carefully controlling the geometry of the vertex, it is possible to tune the number of passbands and optimize the performance of quantum devices. These results may have important implications for the design and operation of quantum devices, such as quantum computers and sensors.

In this study, we present the results of a search for heavy resonances and resonant diboson production using the ATLAS detector at the Large Hadron Collider (LHC). The analysis is based on proton-proton collision data at a center-of-mass energy of 13 TeV, corresponding to an integrated luminosity of 36.1 fb−1. We perform a model-independent search for resonances decaying into pairs of gauge bosons (WW, WZ, and ZZ), as well as searches for specific resonance models predicted by various theories beyond the Standard Model. No significant excess above the Standard Model expectation is observed in any of the search channels. Upper limits are set on the production cross sections of the studied resonances, and exclusion limits are derived for the masses of the studied resonance models. One of the main goals of particle physics is to search for new phenomena beyond the Standard Model (SM) of particle physics. The SM has been extremely successful in explaining a wide range of experimental observations, but it is known to be incomplete and leaves several questions unanswered. One of the most promising ways to search for new physics beyond the SM is through the study of high-energy proton-proton collisions at the Large Hadron Collider (LHC). The LHC is a powerful tool for probing the fundamental structure of matter at extremely high energies, and the ATLAS detector is one of the main experiments at the LHC designed for this purpose.

One class of new physics models that can be probed at the LHC is that of heavy resonances. These are particles with masses much larger than those of the SM particles, which can be produced in high-energy collisions and then decay into pairs of other particles. The search for heavy resonances is an important part of the physics program at the LHC, as they can reveal the existence of new symmetries or interactions beyond those predicted by the SM.

In this study, we present the results of a search for heavy resonances and resonant diboson production using the ATLAS detector at the LHC. The analysis is based on proton-proton collision data at a center-of-mass energy of 13 TeV, corresponding to an integrated luminosity of 36.1 fb−1. We perform a model-independent search for resonances decaying into pairs of gauge bosons (WW, WZ, and ZZ), as well as searches for specific resonance models predicted by various theories beyond the SM. In conclusion, we have presented the results of a search for heavy resonances and resonant diboson production using the ATLAS detector at the LHC. No significant excess above the SM expectation is observed in any of the search channels. Upper limits are set on the production cross sections of the studied resonances, and exclusion limits are derived for the masses of the studied resonance models. These results provide important constraints on the parameter space of various new physics models and will be useful for future studies on the search for heavy resonances at the LHC.

In this document, we will discuss the handover process between macrocells and femtocells in Universal Mobile Telecommunications System (UMTS) based networks. The handover process is an important aspect of any wireless communication system as it allows for seamless connectivity and uninterrupted service to the user. We will discuss the various handover techniques and algorithms used in UMTS networks and their relative advantages and disadvantages. We will also discuss the challenges and considerations involved in implementing handover between macrocells and femtocells in UMTS networks. UMTS is a third-generation mobile communication system that provides high-speed data and voice services to users. It is widely used in many countries around the world and is an important component of the global telecommunications infrastructure. In UMTS networks, a macrocell is a large wireless coverage area that is typically served by a high-power base station. A femtocell, on the other hand, is a small wireless coverage area that is served by a low-power base station and is intended for use in indoor or small outdoor areas.

The handover process in UMTS networks involves transferring a mobile device's connection from one base station to another while maintaining continuity of service. This is necessary when a mobile device moves out of the coverage area of one base station and into the coverage area of another. In the case of handover between macrocells and femtocells, the process becomes slightly more complex due to the differences in the coverage areas and the base station capabilities.

There are several handover techniques and algorithms that can be used in UMTS networks to facilitate the handover process between macrocells and femtocells. These include the Active Set Update (ASU) algorithm, the Measurement Reporting (MR) algorithm, and the Connected Mode Inter-RAT (CM-IRAT) algorithm. Each of these algorithms has its own advantages and disadvantages, and the choice of which algorithm to use depends on the specific requirements of the network and the user. In conclusion, the handover process between macrocells and femtocells in UMTS networks is an important aspect of maintaining uninterrupted service to the user. There are several handover techniques and algorithms available for use in UMTS networks, each with its own advantages and disadvantages. Careful consideration must be given to the specific requirements of the network and the user when selecting the appropriate handover algorithm. Implementing handover between macrocells and femtocells in UMTS networks can be a complex process, but with careful planning and consideration, it is possible to achieve seamless connectivity and uninterrupted service for the user.

Multi-particle collision dynamics (MCD) is a mesoscopic hydrodynamic model, where macroscopic properties of fluids such as density, shear viscosity, and thermal diffusivity can be calculated using micro-scale particle movement data. This simulation technique has already been applied to describe thermal convection of hard-sphere fluids and viscous fluids. In this study, we proposed a generalized multi-particle collision dynamics (gMCD) model to describe viscoelastic fluids whose elasticity depends on the applied load. This model was constructed based on the fundamental assumption that viscoelastic response occurs primarily at the molecular scale rather than macroscopic scale. As a result, the gMCD model could incorporate physical phenomena such as strain-induced structural change and molecular orientation. Simulations were performed for two types of shear flow and it was demonstrated that the present model could predict the shear-thinning characteristics of polymeric solutions and the time-dependent extensional behavior of the entangled polymeric fluid. The simulation of complex fluids is challenging, because it requires not only the calculation of macroscopic properties, but also the description of the microscopic mechanisms of the fluids. In this regard, a mesoscopic hydrodynamic model called multi-particle collision dynamics (MCD) has been proposed, which is based on the molecular dynamics (MD) method. MCD was originally developed by Gompper et al. [1] and it has been successfully applied to describe thermal convection of hard-sphere and viscous fluids [2, 3]. However, the previous MCD model cannot be applied to viscoelastic fluids because the elasticity of viscoelastic fluids is known to occur at the molecular scale rather than the macroscopic scale [4]. In this study, we proposed a generalized multiparticle collision dynamics (gMCD) for the description of viscoelastic fluids. This model was constructed based on the fundamental assumptions that viscoelasticity occurs primarily at the molecular scale rather than the macroscopic scale. As a result, the g-MCD model could incorporate physical phenomena such as structural change and molecular orientation. Simulations were performed to demonstrate that the present model could predict the shear- thinning characteristics of polymeric solutions and the time-dependent extension of the entangled polymeric fluid.

In this paper, we present a method for updating the routing position in ad hoc networks using adaptive position. Ad hoc networks are decentralized networks that do not rely on a fixed infrastructure, and therefore require efficient routing mechanisms to enable communication between nodes. The routing position of a node is a crucial factor in determining the efficiency of routing in ad hoc networks. In this work, we propose a method for updating the routing position of a node based on the node's mobility and the change in the network topology. Our method uses a combination of position prediction and error correction to adaptively update the routing position of a node, resulting in improved routing efficiency and reduced overhead. Ad hoc networks are decentralized networks that consist of nodes that can communicate with each other without the need for a fixed infrastructure. These networks are often used in situations where it is not possible or practical to deploy a fixed infrastructure, such as in military or disaster relief operations. However, the lack of a fixed infrastructure presents challenges for routing in ad hoc networks. Routing in ad hoc networks is typically performed using one of two approaches: proactive or reactive. Proactive routing protocols maintain routing information for all nodes in the network at all times, while reactive routing protocols only create routes when they are needed.

One important factor in determining the efficiency of routing in ad hoc networks is the routing position of a node. The routing position of a node is the location of the node relative to the rest of the network, and it is used by routing protocols to determine the best path for data to travel. However, the routing position of a node can change over time due to the node's mobility and changes in the network topology. Therefore, it is important to have a method for updating the routing position of a node in order to maintain efficient routing in ad hoc networks. In this paper, we have presented a method for updating the routing position in ad hoc networks using adaptive position. Our method combines position prediction and error correction to adaptively update the routing position of a node based on the node's mobility and the change in the network topology. By using this method, we can improve the efficiency of routing in ad hoc networks and reduce overhead. We believe that this method has the potential to significantly improve the performance of ad hoc networks in a variety of applications.

In this paper, a collection of solar observations of reconnection flux is compiled from the Solar Dynamics Observatory (SDO), including high-resolution observations from the Atmospheric Imaging Assembly (AIA) onboard SDO, the Heliospheric and Magnetic Imager (HMI) onboard SDO, and the Global Oscillation Network Group (GONG). We examine this collection by identifying the location of magnetic flux rope (MFR) axis within 5,000-20,000 km from disk center in the AIA (193 Å) and (304 Å) passbands using both image and time-distance heliocentric correlation functions. This analysis yields a catalog of 129 events that show evidence of magnetic flux reconnection. This set shows a striking correlation with active region (AR) complex, with 88 of the flares having associated ARs and 32 of the flares associated with AR clusters. The remaining events, having associated AR locations but limited in their association with AR clusters, are not included. We obtain the magnetic flux content of these events based on both AIA and HMI line-of-sight magnetograms. This study provides, to our knowledge, the largest sample of flare/RM studies available from the SDO era. - Solar flares are a common phenomenon that occur on the Sun and are associated with magnetic flux emergence, cancellation, and reconnection. Magnetic reconnection can release magnetic energy stored in the magnetic field, which can be released in the form of X-ray and gamma-ray emission, which can be observed by the Solar Dynamics Observatory (SDO) and other space-borne instruments (e.g., RHESSI, Fermi). Magnetic reconnection is a fundamental process in solar physics, with the release of energy and particle acceleration playing a key role in many astrophysical phenomena, including coronal mass ejections (CMEs) and solar flares. Understanding the physical processes that occur during magnetic reconnection is therefore an important goal in solar physics.   - The reconnection flux is one of the fundamental parameters that characterizes the magnetic reconnection process. A variety of methods have been developed to measure the reconnection flux. One approach is to use photospheric magnetograms to estimate the amount of magnetic flux that is reconnected. This method has been used to estimate the reconnection flux in many studies (e.g., Lin et al., 1; Liu et al., 2; Cheng et al., 3; Liu et al., 4; Qiu et al., 5; Wang et al., 6). Another approach is to estimate the amount of magnetic flux that is involved in magnetic reconnection. This approach has been used to estimate the reconnection flux in studies that use magnetohydrodynamic (MHD) simulations (e.g., Karpen et al., 7; Masson et al., 8; Karpen et al., 9; Karpen, 10; Masson et al, 11). - In this paper, we present a catalog of 129 flare ribbons that show evidence of magnetic reconnection. The catalog includes 88 flares associated with active regions (ARs), 32 flares associated with active region clusters (ARs), and 19 flares that have no associated ARs. We find that the majority of the events have reconnection flux values that are comparable to or larger than the unsigned magnetic flux of the associated AR.

We prove an approximate equipartition of energy in some weighted energy space for the nonlinear Schrödinger / Gross-Pitaevskii (NLS/GP) equations with spherically symmetric initial data. The main result provides uniform control on the total mass and radial average of the solution together with a lower bound on the relative entropy of the solution. The nonlinear Schrödinger / Gross-Pitaevskii equations (NLS/GP) are used in the description of Bose-Einstein condensates (BEC), a class of quantum gases characterized by their macroscopic wave function. They are given by      \begin{equation*}   \begin{cases}     \partial_t \psi = i\Delta\psi - \frac{1}{2}V(|x|)\psi + \frac{\beta}{2}|\psi|^{2}\psi, \quad \text{in}\; \mathbb{R}^3, \\     \psi(0,x) = \psi_0(x).   \end{cases}   \end{equation*}      Here $\psi = \psi(t,x) \in \mathbb{C}$ is the macroscopic wave function, $\Delta$ is the Laplacian and $V$ is a potential function. The parameter $\beta \in \mathbb{R}$ measures the strength of the interaction between the particles. The main result provides uniform control on the total energy and the kinetic energy together with a lower bound on the relative entropy. We also provide an estimate on the total mass and the radial average of the solution.    The result is proved using a combination of the ``entropy method'' and the method of characteristics.    The result is the first result of this type in the literature for the GP equations.

Heterogeneous networks (HetNets) have emerged as a promising solution to address the increasing demand for high-capacity and high-quality wireless communication services. HetNets consist of a combination of macro cells, which provide coverage over a wide area, and small cells, which provide high capacity and low latency in hotspot areas. In recent years, millimeter wave (mmWave) small cells have attracted significant attention as a potential solution to further improve the capacity and coverage of HetNets. However, the integration of mmWave small cells into HetNets has posed several challenges, including interference management, resource allocation, and network architecture. In this paper, we propose a decoupled HetNet architecture with mmWave small cells to address these challenges. The proposed architecture decouples the control and data planes of the mmWave small cells, which allows for more efficient resource allocation and interference management. We also discuss the challenges and potential solutions for implementing the proposed architecture in practice. The exponential growth of wireless communication services has led to a significant increase in the demand for high-capacity and high-quality wireless networks. Heterogeneous networks (HetNets) have emerged as a promising solution to meet this demand by combining macro cells, which provide coverage over a wide area, and small cells, which provide high capacity and low latency in hotspot areas. Small cells can be deployed in various frequency bands, including sub-6 GHz and millimeter wave (mmWave) bands.

MmWave small cells have attracted significant attention in recent years due to their potential to significantly improve the capacity and coverage of HetNets. However, the integration of mmWave small cells into HetNets has posed several challenges, including interference management, resource allocation, and network architecture. To address these challenges, various approaches have been proposed, such as coordinated multi-point (CoMP) transmission and interference coordination.

In this paper, we propose a decoupled HetNet architecture with mmWave small cells to address the challenges of integrating mmWave small cells into HetNets. The proposed architecture decouples the control and data planes of the mmWave small cells, which allows for more efficient resource allocation and interference management. We also discuss the challenges and potential solutions for implementing the proposed architecture in practice. In this paper, we have proposed a decoupled HetNet architecture with mmWave small cells to address the challenges of integrating mmWave small cells into HetNets. The proposed architecture decouples the control and data planes of the mmWave small cells, which allows for more efficient resource allocation and interference management. We have discussed the challenges and potential solutions for implementing the proposed architecture in practice. The proposed architecture has the potential to significantly improve the capacity and coverage of HetNets, and it can be a promising solution to meet the increasing demand for high-capacity and high-quality wireless communication services.

The article gives an overview of the regulation of financial institution activities and outlines the main points regarding the role of the financial institution in the system of payments management, considering the experience of modern telecommunication operators, as well as considering the trends of the mobile operators in the payments systems. The main elements of operational efficiency of the payments system, especially a possible increase of service quality management and of the payments information quality, are discussed. Special attention was paid to the analysis of the potential role of the financial institution for efficient management of the system of payments in the context of the increasing competition in telecommunication markets. The article provides the basis for further expansion of the analysis of the potential benefit of financial institutions for payments in the context of competition. The article concludes with the presentation of the opportunities for the financial institutions in the future 4G and internet services market. 1. Introduction   2. We first prove that a Sinkhorn-Knopp algorithm (S.K.A.) converges to a matrix scaling solution more easily than an alternating optimization algorithm (A.O.A.). Next, we give the explicit form of the convergence to a limiting point of S.K.A.. This analysis is also shown to be applicable to the analysis of A.O.A. Finally, we give the error analysis of both S.K.A. and A.O.A. by using a different approach from the standard approach in this area of research. This analysis may also be useful in analyzing other related iterative algorithms for matrix scaling. Given a symmetric matrix $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$, the matrix scaling problem [1] is to find a matrix $\mathbf{S} \in \mathbb{R^{n \times n}}$ such that $\mathbf{X} \approx \mathbf{S} \mathbf{S}^{T}$. In the context of graph partitioning, this problem is called the graph bisection problem [2]. Topic clouds are a popular method for visualizing the main topics within a document or group of documents. By analyzing the frequency and relative importance of specific words or phrases, a topic cloud can provide a quick overview of the main themes within a document. In this article, we will discuss the various techniques and tools available for creating topic clouds, and how they can be used to improve the organization and understanding of large volumes of text data. In the age of big data, it is becoming increasingly important to have effective tools for organizing and understanding large volumes of text data. One popular method for visualizing the main themes within a document or group of documents is through the use of topic clouds. A topic cloud is a graphical representation of the most important words or phrases within a document, with the size and position of each word or phrase indicating its relative importance or frequency.

Topic clouds can be created using a variety of techniques, including natural language processing algorithms, manual tagging, and clustering methods. Some popular tools for creating topic clouds include WordCloud, Tagxedo, and Many Eyes. These tools allow users to input a document or set of documents, and generate a visual representation of the most important topics within the text. Topic clouds can be a valuable tool for organizing and understanding large volumes of text data. By providing a visual representation of the most important words or phrases within a document, topic clouds can help users quickly identify the main themes and topics discussed. Whether using natural language processing algorithms, manual tagging, or clustering methods, there are a variety of techniques and tools available for creating topic clouds and gaining insights from text data.

We propose a novel transmit beamforming algorithm for a multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) system with a limited resolution analog-to-digital converter (ADC). In this document, we explore several ways to calculate the number of ways to make change using different denominations of coins. We present several simple proof techniques and demonstrate how they can be applied to find the total number of ways to make change for a given amount. Making change is a common task that we encounter in everyday life, whether we are paying for items at a store or trying to divide a bill among friends. Given a set of denominations of coins, the problem of making change asks how many ways there are to represent a certain amount using those coins. For example, if we have coins with denominations of 1, 5, and 10, how many ways can we make change for the amount of 15? 

One simple way to solve this problem is by using a brute force approach, where we consider all possible combinations of coins and count the number of ways that add up to the desired amount. However, this method can become computationally expensive as the number of denominations and the target amount increase. In this document, we present several more efficient proof techniques that allow us to calculate the number of ways to make change more quickly. In this document, we have presented several proof techniques for calculating the number of ways to make change using a given set of denominations. These methods can be applied to find the total number of ways to make change for any given amount, and are more efficient than a brute force approach. By understanding these techniques, we can better solve problems involving making change in everyday life.

Graphene is a two-dimensional material composed of carbon atoms arranged in a hexagonal lattice. Its unique electronic properties have led to numerous applications in fields such as electronics and energy storage. One of the most striking features of graphene is its chiral quasiparticle excitations, known as chiral quasiparticles. In this paper, we present a theory of the local density of states (LDOS) maps of chiral quasiparticles in graphene. Graphene is a single layer of carbon atoms arranged in a honeycomb lattice. It has attracted significant attention due to its unique electronic properties, including high electron mobility, tunable bandgap, and low mass. These properties make it an ideal material for applications such as electronics, energy storage, and sensors.

One of the most striking features of graphene is the existence of chiral quasiparticle excitations. Chiral quasiparticles are characterized by a linear dispersion relation and a chiral pseudospin degree of freedom. They can be thought of as massless particles with a definite chirality, or handedness. The chiral quasiparticle excitations in graphene are associated with the Dirac points in the Brillouin zone, which are points in momentum space where the energy dispersion relation is linear.

The local density of states (LDOS) is a measure of the probability of finding an electron in a given energy state at a particular location in space. The LDOS can be measured experimentally using techniques such as scanning tunneling microscopy (STM). LDOS maps provide a powerful tool for understanding the electronic structure of materials and can be used to study the properties of chiral quasiparticles in graphene. In this paper, we have presented a theory of the LDOS maps of chiral quasiparticles in graphene. These maps provide valuable insights into the electronic structure and properties of this important material. The study of chiral quasiparticles in graphene has the potential to lead to new applications in fields such as electronics and energy storage. Further research in this area is likely to yield important advances in our understanding of the unique properties of graphene.

In this paper, the authors study conditional limit theorems of empirical processes for stationary, strictly ergodic random variables when the dependence condition is introduced, which is a conditional version of the neighborhood dependence. Under the assumption of conditional weak dependence, the authors obtain some new central limit theorems of empirical processes, which improve and extend some results of Li and Fan (2010). As applications, the asymptotic behavior of the associated partial sum process as well as the empirical process of linear processes are discussed. The study of the asymptotic behavior of empirical processes has attracted much attention in the past decades (see, for example, [1, 2]). The main goal of this paper is to study the asymptotic behavior of the associated partial sum process and the empirical process of linear processes under the conditional weak dependence.  Let \((\Omega,\mathcal{F},P)\) be a probability space, and \(\{X_{i}\}_{i\in\mathbb{Z}}\) be a stationary sequence of random variables on \((\Omega,\mathcal F,P)\) with mean zero and finite variance. For each \(t\in\mathbb{R}\), we denote \(S_{n}(t)=\sum_{i=1}^{n}X_{i}\mathbf{1}_{\{X_{i}\leq t\}}\) and \(S_{n}=S_{n}(0)\). Denote by \(S_{n}^{*}\) and \(S_n^{**}\) the corresponding upper and lower partial sum processes. We are interested in the asymptotic behavior of \(S_{n}^*\) and \(S_{[nt]}^{**}\). (1) The authors establish the conditional limit theorems of \(S_{n}\) and \(S_[nt]\) under the conditional weak dependence, which improve and extend some results of Li (2010).   (2) The authors study the associated partial sum process of linear processes under conditional weak dependence, and obtain the conditional central limit theorem.

We perform hydrodynamic calculations with the newest generation of mixing length theory and the most recently developed version of the mixing length formalism for the modelling of stellar convection, pulsation and semiconvection. In order to validate and benchmark these numerical implementations, we compare simulated convective overshooting with the linear stellar pulsation results of a classical Mira, and explore the evolution of a 2.0 M⊙ star with semiconvection effects. - We describe the newest version of the mixing length formalism for the modelling   of stellar convection, pulsation and semiconvection,   based on a numerical implementation of the mixing length theory.   - We compare simulated convective overshooting with the linear   stellar pulsation results of a classical Mira, and investigate the   evolution of a 2.0 M⊘ star with semiconvection effects.   - We also discuss the effects of convective overshooting on   the pulsation modes of a 1.0 M⊙ star. - We find that the newest version of the mixing length formalism provides a better description of the convective overshoot in comparison to the original one.  - The effects of convective overshooting on the pulsation modes of a 1.0M⊙ star are also studied.

In this work, we consider the problem of establishing a secret key among a group of users over state-dependent wireless broadcast channels. We propose a protocol that allows the users to exchange information and generate a common secret key, even in the presence of an active eavesdropper. We analyze the security and efficiency of our protocol and demonstrate its performance through numerical simulations. Secret key agreement is a fundamental problem in modern communication systems, with applications ranging from secure communication to authentication and access control. In a wireless setting, the presence of an active eavesdropper can pose a significant threat to the security of the key agreement process. This is particularly true in the case of broadcast channels, where the transmitted messages are received by all users in the network, including the eavesdropper.

To address this issue, we propose a protocol for group secret key agreement over state-dependent wireless broadcast channels. The key idea behind our protocol is to exploit the state-dependent nature of the channel, which allows the users to coordinate their key agreement process based on the current channel state. This enables the users to generate a common secret key, even in the presence of an active eavesdropper.

We analyze the security and efficiency of our protocol and demonstrate its performance through numerical simulations. Our results show that the proposed protocol is able to establish a secret key with high probability, even in the presence of an active eavesdropper. In this work, we have presented a protocol for group secret key agreement over state-dependent wireless broadcast channels. We have analyzed the security and efficiency of our protocol and demonstrated its performance through numerical simulations. Our results show that the proposed protocol is a promising solution for secure communication over wireless broadcast channels.

The effect of non-Gaussian curvature perturbations (NGPs) on the formation of primordial black holes (PBHs) and the associated constraints are considered. The results of the calculation of the NGP variance are compared with the results of a recent CMB observation. This observation is an independent calculation and, therefore, it allows us to test whether the theoretical calculation is consistent or inconsistent with the CMB observations. Assuming that the CMB observations are correct, we investigate the consequences of the observationally allowed variance value. Our results show that a more accurate NGP spectrum has a larger effect on the PBH abundance. Also, our results show that the effect of non-Gaussianity on the formation of PBHs (especially its late time effect) is more significant than previously expected. The constraints obtained from the CMB NGP statistics are in agreement with our previous results, however the bounds on the power at the scale of PBHs (k∼ 10−14cm−1) are more stringently constrained for the CMB observation than in the case of previous works. We also find that the constraints on the power at the scales higher than the scale of PBHs are much stronger than in the previous works. We also consider the contribution of non-Gaussian perturbations associated with the PBHs to the power spectrum from other perturbations, which can affect the CMB power spectrum. In the case of non-Gaussianity associated with the PBHs, we show that the effect on the power spectrum is a small correction to the effect on the angular power spectrum. Primordial black holes (PBHs) are formed from quantum fluctuations during the radiation era. The formation of PBHs is a generic prediction of inflationary cosmology (1, 2, 3). The PBHs may have a significant contribution to the dark matter density of the universe (4, 5). If PBHs have formed in the early universe, then they would leave a distinctive signature on the cosmic microwave background (CMB) (6, 7, 8, 9, 10). This signature can be studied by looking at the non-Gaussianity of the CMB. The non-Gaussianity of the CMB is a powerful tool for studying the physics of inflation and the origin of the primordial density perturbations (11, 12, 13, 14, 15, 16, 17, 18).  The non-Gaussianity of the CMB is a statistical quantity that describes the deviation of the CMB from the Gaussian distribution. This non-Gaussianity is quantified by the non-Gaussianity parameter, \(f_{\rm NL}\). The non-Gaussianity parameter \(f_{\rm NL(\phi)}\) is related to the amplitude of the non-Gaussian curvature perturbation (19, 20, 21). The non-Gaussianity parameter \[f_{\rm NL(\phi^{2})}\]  is related to the amplitude of the non-Gaussianity of the curvature perturbation \[\phi^{2}\]. The effect of non-Gaussianity on the formation of primordial black holes and the associated constraints are considered. The results are compared with the results of a recent CMB observational study. This study is an independent calculation and, therefore, it can be used to test whether the theoretical calculation is consistent or not with the CMB observations. We consider the contribution of the non-Gaussian curvature perturbations associated with the primordial black holes to the CMB power spectrum. We also consider the contribution of the non-Gaussian curvature perturbation associated with the primordial black holes to the angular power spectrum. We find that the effect of non-Gaussianity on the CMB power spectrum is a small correction to the effect of non-Gaussianity on the angular power spectrum.

In this paper, we study the behavior of heterotic strings on G_2 orbifolds, which are compact spaces that have a G_2 symmetry group. We investigate the different ways in which these strings can be compactified and the resulting effects on the low-energy effective theory. We also explore the dualities that exist between heterotic strings on G_2 orbifolds and other string theories, such as type IIB and M-theory, and discuss the implications for understanding the fundamental nature of the universe. Heterotic strings are a class of string theories that are based on the assumption that the fundamental building blocks of the universe are one-dimensional objects called strings. These strings vibrate and interact with each other, giving rise to the various particles and forces that we observe in the world around us. One of the key features of heterotic strings is that they possess both left-moving and right-moving modes, which are related to the spin of the string.

G_2 orbifolds, on the other hand, are spaces that have a particular type of symmetry known as G_2 symmetry. These spaces can be thought of as being obtained by taking a higher-dimensional space and "folding" it in a specific way, resulting in a space with a smaller number of dimensions that still exhibits the same symmetry properties. Compactifying heterotic strings on G_2 orbifolds involves wrapping the strings around these compact spaces, leading to a low-energy effective theory that exhibits certain properties determined by the G_2 symmetry. In this paper, we have examined the behavior of heterotic strings on G_2 orbifolds and the resulting effects on the low-energy effective theory. We have also explored the dualities that exist between heterotic strings on G_2 orbifolds and other string theories, and discussed the implications for our understanding of the fundamental nature of the universe. This work provides a valuable step forward in our quest to uncover the fundamental laws of the universe and to understand how they can be unified within the framework of string theory.

Joint polarization and phase tracking in coherent receivers, using the same reference signal, is affected by a reduction of the phase error due to the modulation format. Therefore a proper design of the feedback signal using orthogonal codes is mandatory to compensate the different effects in the different modulation formats. The paper presents a joint symbol timing and symbol phase estimation, employing orthogonal training sequences and a reduced signal bandwidth, for the -QAM and -OFDM modulations. The evaluation is performed on a 400Mbaud single polarization transmission, using a DSP-based DSP receiver, based on a turbo-MAP algorithm, that performs a frame synchronization, a symbol timing and a symbol phase estimation. The analysis is performed using two identical coherent receivers, one working with -QAM and the other with -OFDM. The results show that the joint estimation of the symbol timing and the symbol phase is still possible with the -QAM and -OFDM modulation, but there is a degradation of the tracking-loop performance due to the different effects caused by the different modulation formats on the two receiver receivers. The paper describes a joint estimation method for the symbol timing, based on the analysis of the orthogonal training symbols of the orthogonal QAM and-OFDM modulation, while the estimation of the symbol phase is performed using the same estimation for both modulations. In order to track the phase error introduced by the transmission channel, the phase tracking loop of a coherent receiver is usually based on the use of a reference signal, that is transmitted in the same frequency band of the data signal and is modulated in the same way [1]. In this way, the phase tracking loop is not affected by the different effects on the data signal caused by the different modulation formats. The reference signal is usually a signal that is modulated with a different modulation format from the data signal, such as a signal modulated with a different quadrature amplitude modulation (QAM) or an orthogonal frequency division multiplexing (OFDM) signal.    The main limitation of this technique is that the use of the same reference signal for the two different modulation formats may affect the phase tracking loop performance, since the phase error is not compensated by the same amount for the two modulation formats.    The paper presents a joint symbol timing and symbol phasing estimation, employing orthogonal training sequences and a reduced bandwidth signal, for the -QAM and - OFDM modulations. The evaluation is performed on a -QAM (400Mbaud) and -OFDM (800Mbaud) transmission, using a DSP-based DSP receiver. The results show that the joint estimation of symbol timing and symbol phase is still possible with the -QAM and -OFDM modulation, but there is an additional degradation of the tracking-loop performance due to different effects caused by the different modulation formats on two receiver receivers. The paper presents a joint estimation method for the symbol timing, using the analysis of the orthogonal training symbols of the -QAM and -OFDM modulation. The estimation of the symbol phase is performed using the estimation of the symbol timing. The results show that the joint estimation of both symbol timing and symbol phase is still possible with -QAM and -OFDM modulation, while there is an additional degradation of the tracking- loop performance due to different effects caused by the different modulations on two receiver receivers.

Superoxide reductase (SOR) is a metalloenzyme that plays a critical role in the detoxification of superoxide radicals, which are highly reactive oxygen species that can cause damage to cellular components. SOR is found in a variety of microorganisms and is characterized by the presence of an iron(III) peroxo species in the active site. In this study, we sought to identify the iron(III) peroxo species in the SOR from Desulfoarculus baarsii using a combination of spectroscopic techniques and computational modeling. Our results suggest that the iron(III) peroxo species in the active site of SOR from Desulfoarculus baarsii is composed of a pentacoordinated iron(III) ion bound to a peroxide ligand and coordinated by three histidine residues. Superoxide reductase (SOR) is a metalloenzyme that plays a critical role in the detoxification of superoxide radicals, which are highly reactive oxygen species that can cause damage to cellular components. SOR belongs to the class of enzymes known as radical S-adenosylmethionine (SAM) enzymes, which use a radical SAM intermediate to transfer a hydrogen atom from a substrate to a target molecule. SOR is found in a variety of microorganisms and is characterized by the presence of an iron(III) peroxo species in the active site. This iron(III) peroxo species is thought to play a key role in the catalytic mechanism of SOR.

In order to understand the role of the iron(III) peroxo species in the catalytic mechanism of SOR, it is important to identify the precise nature of this species. Previous studies have used a variety of spectroscopic techniques, including electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR), infrared (IR) spectroscopy, and X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS), to characterize the iron(III) peroxo species in SOR from various microorganisms. However, these studies have often yielded conflicting results, making it difficult to identify the precise nature of the iron(III) peroxo species.

In this study, we sought to identify the iron(III) peroxo species in the SOR from Desulfoarculus baarsii using a combination of spectroscopic techniques and computational modeling. Desulfoarculus baarsii is a gram-negative, facultatively anaerobic, sulfate-reducing bacterium that is found in a variety of environments, including sediment, soil, and wastewater. Our results suggest that the iron(III) peroxo species in the active site of SOR from Desulfoarculus baarsii is composed of a pentacoordinated iron(III) ion bound to a peroxide ligand and coordinated by three histidine residues. These findings provide insight into the catalytic mechanism of SOR and may facilitate the development of drugs or other therapeutic agents that target SOR. Further studies are needed to confirm and extend these results, as well as to investigate the role of the iron(III) peroxo species in other enzymes and biological processes.

Linear inverse problems arise in a wide range of applications, including image restoration, signal processing, and machine learning. Regularization is a key technique for stabilizing the solution of these problems and avoiding overfitting. In this paper, we propose a new regularization method based on the Hessian Schatten norm, which is a generalization of the matrix trace and the matrix nuclear norm. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our method on several benchmark inverse problems and compare it with other popular regularization methods. Linear inverse problems involve estimating an unknown variable x from a linear measurement y = Ax + e, where A is a known matrix and e is an additive noise term. These problems are common in many fields, including image processing, signal processing, and machine learning. However, they often suffer from ill-posedness, meaning that there may be multiple solutions or the solution may be highly sensitive to small changes in the data. This can lead to unstable solutions and overfitting.

To address these issues, regularization techniques are often used to stabilize the solution and promote generalization. One popular approach is to impose a penalty on the complexity of the solution, such as the l1 or l2 norm. Another approach is to use a priori knowledge about the structure of the solution, such as smoothness or sparsity.

In this paper, we propose a new regularization method based on the Hessian Schatten norm, which is a generalization of the matrix trace and the matrix nuclear norm. The Hessian Schatten norm is defined as the sum of the singular values of the Hessian matrix of the solution, which captures the curvature of the solution at each point. By penalizing the Hessian Schatten norm, our method encourages solutions that are smooth and well-behaved. In this paper, we introduced a new regularization method based on the Hessian Schatten norm for linear inverse problems. We demonstrated the effectiveness of our method on several benchmark inverse problems and compared it with other popular regularization methods. Our results show that the Hessian Schatten norm regularization can provide stable and accurate solutions with good generalization performance.

In this paper, we present a new approach for dense RGB-D semantic mapping using a Pixel-Voxel neural network. Our method is designed to handle high-resolution RGB-D data, and produces dense, per-pixel semantic labels in real-time. We evaluate our method on several challenging RGB-D datasets, and show that it outperforms state-of-the-art approaches in terms of both accuracy and speed. Semantic mapping, the process of generating a map of an environment that includes the meanings of objects and regions, is an important task in robotics and computer vision. Many existing methods for semantic mapping rely on LiDAR or other depth sensors, but these approaches can be expensive and prone to noise. RGB-D cameras, which combine RGB and depth sensing in a single device, offer a more cost-effective and accurate alternative. However, previous approaches to dense RGB-D semantic mapping have been limited in their ability to handle high-resolution data, and often require computationally intensive post-processing steps.

In this paper, we introduce a new method for dense RGB-D semantic mapping that leverages the capabilities of a Pixel-Voxel neural network. Our network is designed to handle high-resolution RGB-D data, and produces dense, per-pixel semantic labels in real-time. We evaluate our method on several challenging RGB-D datasets, and show that it outperforms state-of-the-art approaches in terms of both accuracy and speed. In this paper, we have presented a new approach for dense RGB-D semantic mapping using a Pixel-Voxel neural network. Our method is able to handle high-resolution RGB-D data, and produces dense, per-pixel semantic labels in real-time. We have evaluated our method on several challenging RGB-D datasets, and have shown that it outperforms state-of-the-art approaches in terms of both accuracy and speed. Our approach has the potential to enable a wide range of applications in robotics and computer vision, and we believe it represents a significant step forward in the field of dense RGB-D semantic mapping.

Returns     -------     solution : array         Approximate solution. Returns     -------     """     self.x_0 = self.initial_point     self.y_0 = self.initial_point + self.initial_step    def _initialize_step(self):     """     Computes the next point in the sequence.      Returns     -------       next_point : array         New point in the sequence.     """     self.x_next = self.x_0     self.y_next = self.y_0    def _initialize_step_next(self):     """     Updates the next point in the sequence. The author presents for the first time a space-time segmentation method capable of studying the vertical structure and evolution of solar supergranules using the local helioseismology records of GOLF. As an example, the time variations of the size, area, diameter of the supergranular cells and the lifetimes of their bright and dark patches were obtained in the active NOAA region 10844. Solar supergranulation is a coherent flow of plasma in the solar convection zone. It is one of the most important features of solar magnetic fields and plays a key role in the formation of solar active regions. However, the detailed characteristics of supergranulation are still not well understood. It is well known that the Sun is a complex and dynamic object. The most important processes are the emergence of new magnetic flux from the surface of the Sun, the formation of the supergranular cells, the evolution of their boundaries and the emergence of new supergranular cells. However, the spatial and temporal resolution of helioseismic data is insufficient for a detailed analysis of these processes.      In this paper, a new space-time segmentation method is proposed for studying the evolution of solar supergranulation based on the local helioseismology data provided by the Global Oscillation Network Group (GOLF). - The proposed method is capable of studying the vertical structure and evolution of supergranulation from the GOLF data.   - The method was applied to the active NOAA region 10843.   - The time variations of the size, area, diameter and lifetimes of the supergranular cells and the bright and dark patches were obtained.

Frame operators are a class of linear transformations that are used to analyze and represent signals in a redundant and overcomplete way. They are often used in signal processing and image processing applications, where they can provide robustness to noise and other perturbations. In this paper, we review a range of de-noising procedures that can be applied to frame operators in order to improve their performance in the presence of noise. These procedures include regularization techniques, denoising algorithms, and various other approaches that can be used to reduce the impact of noise on the output of a frame operator. Frame operators are a type of linear transformation that can be used to represent signals in a redundant and overcomplete way. They are often used in signal and image processing applications, where they can provide robustness to noise and other perturbations. However, the presence of noise can still significantly impact the performance of frame operators, leading to degraded results and reduced accuracy. As such, it is important to consider de-noising procedures that can be applied to frame operators in order to improve their performance in the presence of noise.

One common approach to de-noising frame operators is to apply regularization techniques. These techniques involve adding a penalty term to the optimization problem that is being solved in order to encourage solutions that are smooth or have other desirable properties. For example, the use of ℓ1 or ℓ2 regularization can help to reduce the impact of noise by encouraging solutions that are sparse or smooth, respectively.

Another approach to de-noising frame operators is to use denoising algorithms. These algorithms are designed to remove noise from signals by exploiting the structure of the signal itself. For example, wavelet denoising algorithms can be used to remove noise from signals that have a wavelet representation, while singular value decomposition (SVD) based denoising algorithms can be used to remove noise from signals that can be represented as a sum of rank-1 matrices.

In addition to regularization techniques and denoising algorithms, there are a number of other approaches that can be used to de-noise frame operators. These include methods such as filtering, thresholding, and other techniques that can be used to reduce the impact of noise on the output of a frame operator. In this paper, we have reviewed a range of de-noising procedures that can be applied to frame operators in order to improve their performance in the presence of noise. These procedures include regularization techniques, denoising algorithms, and various other approaches that can be used to reduce the impact of noise on the output of a frame operator. By applying these de-noising procedures, it is possible to significantly improve the accuracy and reliability of frame operators in the presence of noise, leading to improved results in a range of signal and image processing applications.

As the continuous deployment pipeline development cycles decreases a lot, security support becomes more important. For instance, the deployment pipeline development cycle of a company which is operated in the Internet of Things should be as long as 5 years. Hence, it is critical to secure the system continuously in the entire development pipeline life cycle. This paper presents an architecture of a continuous deployment pipeline security support framework on which there are several security components. This framework will assist developers to set the secure pipeline deployment environment and maintain a secure pipeline deployment environment continually through the entire pipeline life cycle. The continuous deployment pipeline development cycle of a company which is run in the Internet of Things (IoT) should be as long as 5 years. The continuous deployment pipeline is the process of a software development cycle which consists of a number of steps, such as requirements analysis, system design, system implementation, deployment, and maintenance. Each step is a key stage in the continuous deployment pipeline. The security support in the continuous deployment pipeline development cycle is very important because the development cycle of the IoT should be as long as 5 years. As the development cycle of the IoT decreases, it becomes more important to maintain the IoT continuously. Therefore, it is critical to secure the system continuously throughout the development pipeline life cycle. The proposed framework is a secure continuous deployment pipeline framework which is designed to provide a secure continuous deployment pipeline environment to the continuous deployment pipeline developers. The framework will assist the continuous deployment pipeline developers to set the secure pipeline deployment environment and to maintain a secure pipeline deployment environment continually throughout the entire pipeline life cycle.

Planets around active G and F stars are thought to be likely. If this is correct, a stellar flare could be considered a detection of a planet. Several authors have investigated how a flare might be due to orbiting satellites, planetary perturbers, or an orbiting accretion disk. We report here that all three are possible in the case of the active early-type star AU Mic. In the first case we have found evidence that the inner magnetosphere of the star is connected with a ring surrounding a planet more massive than Earth. In the second case we found evidence that the flare could be interpreted as the consequence of an orbital precession of a planet on a circular, 3:2 mean-motion resonance with a star. Finally, in the last case we found a low-mass, sub-Jovian planet which is orbiting about AU Mic. On the basis of observations obtained in the optical and at radio wavelengths, we estimate the mass of the planet to be less than Jupiter's, indicating that an extrasolar gas planet is plausible. We conclude that it appears unlikely that an extrasolar planetary system orbiting AU Mic could be destroyed by the enhanced magnetic activity of the star, and that the planet is likely to be a low-mass object and thus is within the mass range where most of the known extrasolar planets may occur. - Planets around active G and F stars are thought likely.   - AU Mic is an active G0 star.   - The star is thought to be in a 3:2 mean-motion resonance with an inner, gas giant planet.   - The star is in a 3:2 mean-motion mean-field resonance with a planet orbiting in the same plane as the inner gas giant planet. - It is possible that the planet could be an extrasolar gas planet.   - It is likely that the planet is a low-mass object and thus is within most of the known extrasolar planets.
