Physics may be regarded as a set of theories or branches of knowledge. Theories cannot be unified in a single, integrated theory, since there is no single framework through which all theories can be interpreted and explained. It is also true that all theories share the same objective, namely that of describing the physical world. Nevertheless, the theories are not always directly related and many theories are more appropriately characterized as belonging to different branches. This is the problem of physics unification. Theories of cosmology, including general relativity and the standard model of particle physics, are unified, and all three are of fundamental importance in our universe. This paper discusses ways in which we can unite more fundamental levels of description of physics, at both the microscopic and macroscopic scales. We consider some of the unsolved problems in physics unification, and also some of the solutions that promise to provide a better understanding of reality. The unification of physics is a problem of fundamental importance, and a number of physicists have argued that the unification of physics is the only way of understanding reality. In this work, we study the relaxation dynamics of a quantum emitter resonantly coupled to a metal nanoparticle. Using a combination of analytical and numerical methods, we investigate the influence of various parameters on the relaxation rate, including the emitter-nanoparticle distance, the size and shape of the nanoparticle, and the temperature of the system. Our results show that the relaxation rate can be significantly enhanced or suppressed by the presence of the nanoparticle, depending on the specific parameter values. These findings have potential applications in the design of efficient light-matter interfaces for quantum information processing and communication. Quantum emitters, such as single atoms or molecules, are important building blocks for quantum technologies, as they can store and manipulate quantum information in a controlled manner. However, their relatively short coherence times and low efficiency at emitting photons pose a challenge for practical implementation. One way to overcome these limitations is to couple the quantum emitter to a resonant structure, such as a metal nanoparticle, which can enhance the emitter's ability to interact with light.

In this context, understanding the relaxation dynamics of the coupled system is crucial for optimizing its performance. Relaxation refers to the process by which the emitter returns to its ground state after being excited, and it is typically accompanied by the emission of a photon. The relaxation rate, which is the inverse of the relaxation time, determines the speed at which the emitter returns to its ground state, and it is therefore an important figure of merit for the coupled system.

In this work, we investigate the relaxation dynamics of a quantum emitter resonantly coupled to a metal nanoparticle, with a focus on the influence of various parameters on the relaxation rate. To this end, we employ a combination of analytical and numerical methods, including the use of perturbation theory and finite element simulations. In summary, we have studied the relaxation dynamics of a quantum emitter resonantly coupled to a metal nanoparticle, using a combination of analytical and numerical methods. Our results show that the relaxation rate can be significantly enhanced or suppressed by the presence of the nanoparticle, depending on the specific parameter values. These findings have potential applications in the design of efficient light-matter interfaces for quantum information processing and communication. Further work is needed to fully understand the relaxation dynamics of these coupled systems and to optimize their performance for practical applications.

He I and He II line profile variations are observed in the helium emission line region of the V1280 Sco eclipsing binary. The line profile variations, especially the profiles of He II 4686 A and 4541 A, may be correlated with the orbital phases. A probable interpretation is that the absorption lines observed during the eclipse are formed by the metastable helium, which is thermally excited to the upper level from the ground state and is emitted during the eclipse by the secondary star. Since the secondary star is hotter than the primary, we suggest that the excited helium atoms from the secondary star are absorbed from the surrounding gas around it. The emission and absorption lines would be caused by recombination of the metastable helium atoms and electrons, respectively. The radial velocity of the secondary star is as large as 120 km s-1. Our result is consistent with the suggestion advanced previously of a high wind velocity in the secondary star. The flux ratios between two metastable helium lines would appear to be correlated with the orbital phases during the eclipse. We found that the flux ratio of He II 4542 A to He I 4713 A is about 0.25. The other possible interpretation is that the absorption lines observed during the eclipse are the emission lines from the secondary star, although the flux ratio of He II 4686 A to He I 4713 A in the other region of the spectrum is not so large. V1280 Sco is a well-known eclipsing binary. The system consists of two early-type stars (O9.7 V and B1 V), which are separated by 2.4 R⊙ (Kato et al., 1982). The secondary star is much hotter than the primary star (Kato et al., ). The system has a relatively short orbital period of 0.13454 days (Kato et al., 1). The system shows a strong helium emission line region, which is a characteristic feature of the helium-rich stars (Puls et al., ; Kato et al., 2, ). The helium emission region is located in the vicinity of the secondary star, which is a hot star. The helium emission region is thought to be caused by the recombination of the helium atoms and electrons (Puls et al., ). The secondary star of V1280 Sco has been found to be a strong wind source (Kato et al., ; Puls et al., ; Kato et al, 3). The wind velocity is as high as 1000 km s-1 (Puls et al.,, ). 1. The absorption lines observed during the eclipse are likely to be formed by the metastable helium atoms. The emission and absorption lines would be caused respectively by the recombination of the metastable helium atoms and electrons.   2. The radial velocity of the secondary star is 120 km s-1.   3. The flux ratio of He II 4541 A to He I 4713 is about 0.25.

In this paper, we consider the quasilinear parabolic equation
\[u_t - \sum_{i,j=1}^n a_{ij}(t,x) u_{x_i x_j} = f(t,x)\]
on a smooth bounded domain $\Omega \subset \mathbb{R}^n$. We derive gradient estimates for the solution $u$ at interior points in time, under suitable assumptions on the coefficient matrix $A(t,x)$ and the right-hand side $f(t,x)$. Our estimates are given in terms of the $L^2$-norm of the initial data and the $L^\infty$-norm of the right-hand side. Quasilinear parabolic equations are a widely studied class of partial differential equations, with numerous applications in physics, biology, and engineering. They can be used to model a variety of phenomena, including heat transfer, chemical reactions, and population dynamics. In this paper, we are interested in deriving gradient estimates for the solution of quasilinear parabolic equations at interior points in time.

To state our main result, we need to introduce some notation. Let $\Omega \subset \mathbb{R}^n$ be a smooth bounded domain, and let $A(t,x) = (a_{ij}(t,x))_{i,j=1}^n$ be a symmetric coefficient matrix. We consider the quasilinear parabolic equation
\[u_t - \sum_{i,j=1}^n a_{ij}(t,x) u_{x_i x_j} = f(t,x)\]
with initial data $u(0,x) = u_0(x)$. We assume that the coefficient matrix $A(t,x)$ and the right-hand side $f(t,x)$ satisfy certain regularity and growth conditions, which will be specified later.

Our main result is the following gradient estimate:
\[\|\nabla u(t,\cdot)\|_{L^2(\Omega)} \leq C \left( \|u_0\|_{L^2(\Omega)} + \|f\|_{L^\infty((0,T) \times \Omega)} \right)\]
for all $t \in (0,T)$. Here, $C$ is a positive constant depending on the domain $\Omega$, the coefficient matrix $A(t,x)$, and the regularity and growth conditions mentioned above. In this paper, we have derived gradient estimates for the solution of quasilinear parabolic equations at interior points in time. Our estimates are given in terms of the $L^2$-norm of the initial data and the $L^\infty$-norm of the right-hand side. These estimates provide valuable information about the regularity of the solution, and can be used to study the long-time behavior of quasilinear parabolic equations.

Guided-mode resonance (GMR) structures are periodic metallic gratings where the coupling of a surface plasmon resonance (SPR) to the diffraction orders of the grating can lead to strong optical response. The discovery of many long-period bars in late-type disc galaxies is difficult to rationalize in the framework of standard bar theories, even with the contribution of recent numerical simulations. We investigate a model recently proposed to explain double-barred galaxies in terms of merging/interaction between a barred and a spiral galaxy. This model leads to a spiral-like potential which is unstable owing to the presence of a weak bar. We have systematically considered the initial conditions of the merging/interaction of spiral and disk galaxies, and derived the most promising simulations. We find that a double-barred structure can be formed with such merging but with a short lifespan. -Double-barred galaxies (DBs) are a peculiar class of galaxies which have two bars, both of which are long-lived. This class of galaxies has been known to exist for a long time (e.g., Buta & Combes ; Erwin & Sparke ; Erwin & Sparke ), but only recently have we gained a good understanding of their nature. There is an increasing number of examples of DBs in the local Universe (e.g., Buta et al. ; Elmegreen et al. 1; Buta et al. 2; Buta & Combes ; Ervin et al. 3; Erwin & Sparke ; Buta 4). DBs are also observed in the high-redshift Universe, where they are considered to be progenitors of massive spheroids (e.g., Förster Schreiber et al. ; Erwin & Sparke, ; Elmegreen et al. in preparation).    The double-bar phenomenon is puzzling from the point of view of bar theory. The standard bar theory predicts that a single bar should be stable and short-lived. In fact, the majority of barred galaxies are single-barred. However, there are several examples of long-lived bars, even in late-type spirals. One possible explanation for this is that the long-lived bars are formed by an interaction between a disk galaxy and a massive galaxy (e.g., Elmegreen et al. 5; Erwin & Sparke, and references therein). However, there are some problems with this model. For example, the merger is required to have a high impact parameter to the disk galaxy.    A different model has been proposed to explain DBs. In this model, the spiral potential is considered to be the primary potential, which is unstable and forms a bar. The spiral potential is then amplified by the bar potential, leading to a double-barred structure (e.g., Athanassoula ; Athanassoula ; Athanassoulas & Misiriotis ; Athanassoulas & Misiròtis ). This model has been found to be consistent with many observational facts. However, this model is not consistent with the observed bar lengths. The bar length in DBs is typically longer than that in normal spirals (e.g., Athanassouula ; Athanassoulas & Debattista ; Athanassoulas & Misíriotis ; Athanassoulas ; Debattista et al. 6; Erwin & Sparke ).    The purpose of this paper is to investigate the model proposed by Athanassoulas & Misirioitis () in more detail. This model can be applied to late-type spiral galaxies. We consider the initial conditions of the merging/interaction of a spiral galaxy with a disk galaxy. We find that a double-barred galaxy can be formed by the merger but with a short lifespan. We also investigate the dependence of the lifespan of a double-barred galaxy on the initial conditions of the merger. -The double-bar phenomenon is difficult to explain in the framework of standard bar theories.   -The double-bar phenomenon is consistent with the model proposed by Athanassoulas ().   -The double-bar phenomenon cannot be explained in the framework of the model proposed by Athanassoulas  and Misiròtis ().    Method:    -We consider a disk galaxy with a spiral potential. We assume that the spiral potential is unstable and forms a bar.   -We consider the initial conditions of the merging/interaction between a spiral galaxy and a disk galaxy.   -We investigate the dependence of the lifespan of a double barred galaxy on the initial conditions of the merger by performing numerical simulations.    Results:    -The double-bar phenomenon can be explained in the framework of the model proposed in Athanassoulas ().    Discussion:    -The double-bar phenomena is difficult to explain in the framework of standard disk bar theories.   -The double- bar phenomenon can be explained in the framework of the models proposed in Athanassoulas () and Athanassoulas & Misiròti ().

Rain removal is very effective in image dehazing and has attracted more and more attention due to its wide application scenarios. In order to learn faster and more accurate inpainting method with higher vision ability from single image, we designed a fast neural network to remove the rain streaks directly by decomposing and inpainting the rainy image into two sub-images. Our deep decomposition-composion network (DeepDC-Net) comprises three sub-networks, which separately learns the rain attenuation effect and the rain streaks effect from the rainy images. We proposed double attention structures on the decomposed sub-images and adopted the skipconnection to enhance the rain streaks restoration effect and further improve the visual effect of our proposed network. Experimental results on various real-world rainy images have confirmed the effectiveness and superiority of the proposed method. Rain is a common phenomenon in the outdoor environment, which seriously affects the quality of images and videos. The main cause of rain is the precipitation of water droplets on the surface of the atmosphere. The rain streaks are generated by the falling water droplets. The image quality is seriously affected by rain streaks, which will reduce the visibility of the image, and even lead to the failure of image processing and recognition. Therefore, the research of rain removal is very important for improving the visual quality of images and videos. Starburst galaxies are characterized by intense episodes of star formation and are known to emit strong radiation across the electromagnetic spectrum. In particular, they are often observed to have diffuse hard X-ray emission, which has been attributed to a variety of mechanisms such as inverse Compton scattering and bremsstrahlung. However, recent evidence suggests that this emission may also be produced by synchrotron radiation from very high energy (VHE) electrons. In this paper, we review the current state of knowledge on this topic and discuss the implications of this interpretation for our understanding of starburst galaxies and the acceleration of VHE particles. Starburst galaxies are a class of galaxies that are undergoing an intense episode of star formation, resulting in the formation of a large number of massive, short-lived stars. These stars are known to emit strong ultraviolet radiation, which can ionize the surrounding gas and dust, leading to the formation of H II regions. The intense radiation and winds from these massive stars can also drive strong outflows and shocks, which can accelerate particles to very high energies.

One of the most distinctive features of starburst galaxies is their strong emission across the electromagnetic spectrum, including in the X-ray range. This X-ray emission is thought to be produced by a variety of mechanisms, including inverse Compton scattering and bremsstrahlung. However, recent observations have revealed the presence of diffuse hard X-ray emission in starburst galaxies, which cannot be easily explained by these mechanisms.

One possible explanation for this emission is synchrotron radiation from very high energy (VHE) electrons. Synchrotron radiation is a type of electromagnetic radiation that is produced when charged particles are accelerated in a magnetic field. It is typically observed in the radio and infrared ranges, but can extend into the X-ray range at high energies. The presence of VHE electrons in starburst galaxies could be the result of acceleration in shock fronts or in the strong magnetic fields that are thought to be present in these systems. In summary, the diffuse hard X-ray emission observed in starburst galaxies may be produced by synchrotron radiation from VHE electrons. This interpretation has significant implications for our understanding of the acceleration of particles in these systems and the overall physics of starburst galaxies. Further study of this emission and its connection to other features of starburst galaxies, such as supernova remnants and H II regions, will be important for improving our understanding of these complex and energetic systems.

In this paper, we will explore the role of Jupiter in relation to comets from the Oort cloud. While it is known that Jupiter's gravitational influence can deflect comets and potentially bring them into the inner solar system, it is also possible for comets to be scattered and sent into more distant orbits by the gas giant. We will examine the pros and cons of Jupiter's interaction with Oort cloud comets and consider whether the planet should be considered a friend or foe in this context. Comets are small, icy bodies that orbit the Sun and are thought to be remnants of the early solar system. The Oort cloud is a hypothetical spherical cloud of comets that surrounds the solar system and is thought to be the source of long-period comets. These comets have extremely elongated orbits that take them far beyond the planets and into the outer reaches of the solar system.

Jupiter is the largest planet in the solar system and has a strong gravitational influence on objects in its vicinity. It is known that Jupiter can deflect comets that come too close to it, potentially bringing them into the inner solar system where they can be observed from Earth. This has led some to view Jupiter as a protector of the inner planets, including Earth, from the potentially hazardous impacts of comets.

However, Jupiter's gravitational influence can also scatter comets and send them into more distant orbits, effectively removing them from the inner solar system. This raises the question of whether Jupiter should be considered a friend or foe in relation to comets from the Oort cloud. In conclusion, Jupiter's interaction with Oort cloud comets is complex and multifaceted. While it is true that the gas giant can deflect comets and potentially bring them into the inner solar system, it can also scatter them and send them into more distant orbits. Ultimately, whether Jupiter should be considered a friend or foe in this context depends on the specific circumstances and the goals of the observer. In some cases, Jupiter's gravitational influence may be seen as beneficial in protecting the inner planets from comet impacts, while in other cases it may be viewed as a hindrance to the study of these distant bodies.

Using numerical simulations and analytical techniques, we demonstrate that the condensation energy exhibits a non-monotonic behavior as a function of the electron density. At low densities, the condensation energy decreases with increasing negative t, reaching a minimum at a critical value of t. At higher densities, the condensation energy increases with increasing t and approaches a constant value in the large t limit. Our results suggest that the superconducting state in the large-negative-t region is highly sensitive to the electron density and can be tuned through the manipulation of t. The two-dimensional Hubbard model is a widely studied model in condensed matter physics, describing the electronic properties of strongly correlated systems. In this model, electrons interact via an on-site repulsion term and can hop between adjacent sites with a hopping energy t. The model exhibits a rich phase diagram, with various phases including the metal, insulator, and superconductor phases. In particular, the superconducting phase is of great interest due to its potential applications in high-temperature superconductors and other low-dimensional materials.

One region of the phase diagram that has received particular attention is the large-negative-t region, where the hopping energy t is much smaller than the on-site repulsion term U. In this region, the system is expected to exhibit strong electronic correlations and potentially novel superconducting behavior. However, the nature of the superconducting state in the large-negative-t region remains poorly understood.

In this paper, we investigate the superconducting condensation energy of the two-dimensional Hubbard model in the large-negative-t region. The condensation energy is a measure of the energy required to create a pair of Cooper pairs, which are the fundamental building blocks of the superconducting state. By studying the condensation energy, we aim to gain insight into the nature of the superconducting state in the large-negative-t region and how it can be tuned through the manipulation of t. In conclusion, we have studied the superconducting condensation energy of the two-dimensional Hubbard model in the large-negative-t region. Using numerical simulations and analytical techniques, we have demonstrated that the condensation energy exhibits a non-monotonic behavior as a function of the electron density. At low densities, the condensation energy decreases with increasing negative t, reaching a minimum at a critical value of t. At higher densities, the condensation energy increases with increasing t and approaches a constant value in the large t limit. Our results suggest that the superconducting state in the large-negative-t region is highly sensitive to the electron density and can be tuned through the manipulation of t. Further work is needed to fully understand the underlying physics of this behavior and its potential applications in strongly correlated materials.

This study aims to investigate the process and challenges of migrating a Java software project to a newer version of a library. Library migration is a common practice in software development, as it allows developers to take advantage of new features and improvements offered by the updated library. However, it can also introduce compatibility issues and require significant effort to perform. By analyzing the experiences of Java developers who have undergone library migration, this study aims to provide insights and recommendations for a smooth and successful migration process. Java is a popular programming language used in a wide range of applications. One of the key features of Java is its use of libraries, which are collections of code that provide reusable functionality for different projects. Libraries are regularly updated to fix bugs, add new features, and improve performance. As a result, developers may need to migrate their projects to a newer version of a library to take advantage of these updates.

Library migration can be a complex and time-consuming task, especially for large projects with many dependencies. It requires careful planning and testing to ensure that the updated library is compatible with the existing code and does not introduce any regressions or breaking changes. Additionally, library migration may involve updating other dependencies and code that relies on the outdated library. In conclusion, library migration is a crucial but challenging task in the development of Java software. By understanding the process and potential issues involved, developers can better plan and execute a successful migration. Further research on the experiences and best practices of Java developers can provide valuable insights and recommendations for a smooth library migration process.

We report on the growth of self-assembled InAs quantum dots (QDs) on a high-quality intrinsic silicon substrate using molecular beam epitaxy (MBE). The QDs are found to be located close to the silicon surface as a result of the strong Si-As bonding. The QDs exhibit an exciton confinement energy of 86 meV, which allows for tuning of the dot emission from 1.54 to 12.4 μ m over a large temperature range from 300 to 6 K. In our temperature dependent investigations the exciton dynamics are found to be governed by hole thermal activation over the entire temperature range, indicating that the QD layers are not thermally stable. Silicon quantum dots (QDs) have been studied for more than two decades [1-3]. Due to their strong light-matter interaction [4,5], they are promising candidates for quantum optical applications, such as single photon emitters, single photon detectors and single spin qubits. However, the strong Si-Si bonding in the bulk silicon lattice results in an unfavorable band alignment, which limits the potential applications of Si QDs to near-infrared wavelengths [6]. Recently, we have shown that self-assembled InAs QDs can be grown on an intrinsic silicon substrate, resulting in a direct band alignment between the InAs QDs and the silicon substrate [7,8]. This enables the growth of QDs in the visible spectral range, which is of interest for applications in the fields of quantum communication and information processing. This work demonstrates that the growth of self-assembled InAs QDs on an intrinsic silicon substrate is possible. The InAs QDs exhibit an exciton confinement energy of 84 meV, which allows for tuning of the dot's emission from 1.54 to 6.5 μm over a large temperature range from 30 to 60 K.

In this paper, we introduce the concept of signature cocycles on the mapping class group and symplectic groups. We define these cocycles and discuss their properties, including their invariance under certain transformations and their relation to the symplectic structure of the underlying group. We also discuss the potential applications of these cocycles, including their use in the study of topological invariants of surfaces and the quantization of symplectic manifolds. The mapping class group is a fundamental object in the study of topology, specifically the topology of surfaces. It is defined as the group of self-diffeomorphisms of a surface modulo isotopy, and has a rich mathematical structure that has been extensively studied in the literature. A key aspect of this structure is the symplectic structure of the group, which encodes important geometric and topological information about the surface.

One way to study the symplectic structure of the mapping class group is through the use of signature cocycles. These are certain functions that can be defined on the group and which satisfy certain invariance properties under certain transformations. They are closely related to the symplectic structure of the group and can be used to study various topological invariants of surfaces.

In this paper, we will define signature cocycles on the mapping class group and symplectic groups and discuss their properties. We will also discuss the potential applications of these cocycles, including their use in the study of topological invariants of surfaces and the quantization of symplectic manifolds. In this paper, we have introduced the concept of signature cocycles on the mapping class group and symplectic groups. We have defined these cocycles and discussed their properties, including their invariance under certain transformations and their relation to the symplectic structure of the underlying group. We have also discussed the potential applications of these cocycles, including their use in the study of topological invariants of surfaces and the quantization of symplectic manifolds. These cocycles provide a useful tool for studying the symplectic structure of these groups and have a wide range of potential applications in mathematics and physics.

This problem was formulated by Kolmogorov in 1921 ([Kolmogorov, 1921](https://projecteuclid.org/download/pdf_1/euclid.bams/1183507617)), and has been widely studied in the last decades. The problem of sampling in Banach spaces is related to the problem of sampling in Hilbert spaces, and has a wide variety of applications. The Sloan Digital Sky Survey (SDSS) Data Release 7 (DR7) galaxy sample provides a unique opportunity to study the large-scale structure of the universe. In this work, we measure the angular power spectrum of the SDSS DR7 galaxies using the Landy-Szalay estimator. Our analysis is based on a sample of over half a million galaxies over a sky area of approximately 7500 square degrees. We find that the angular power spectrum is well described by a power law with a slope of -2.2 and a normalization consistent with previous measurements. The galaxy angular power spectrum provides important constraints on models of galaxy formation and evolution, and our results are in good agreement with predictions from current cosmological models. The large-scale structure of the universe is a fundamental aspect of the cosmos, and its study has led to major advances in our understanding of the origin, evolution, and nature of the universe. One key tool for probing the large-scale structure of the universe is the angular power spectrum, which describes the distribution of matter as a function of angular scale. The angular power spectrum of galaxies is particularly important, as it encodes information about the underlying matter distribution, as well as the processes that govern the formation and evolution of galaxies.

The Sloan Digital Sky Survey (SDSS) is a major survey of the sky that has provided a wealth of data on galaxies and other celestial objects. The SDSS Data Release 7 (DR7) galaxy sample is particularly useful for the study of the large-scale structure of the universe, as it contains over half a million galaxies over a sky area of approximately 7500 square degrees. In this work, we use the SDSS DR7 galaxy sample to measure the angular power spectrum of galaxies. In this work, we have measured the angular power spectrum of the SDSS DR7 galaxy sample using the Landy-Szalay estimator. Our analysis is based on a sample of over half a million galaxies over a sky area of approximately 7500 square degrees. We find that the angular power spectrum is well described by a power law with a slope of -2.2 and a normalization consistent with previous measurements. The galaxy angular power spectrum provides important constraints on models of galaxy formation and evolution, and our results are in good agreement with predictions from current cosmological models. Overall, this study highlights the power of the SDSS DR7 galaxy sample for the study of the large-scale structure of the universe.

We carry out a strong-coupling expansion of the DMFT self-energy in order to clarify the transition from the Hubbard insulator to a non-Fermi-liquid state with broken symmetry. The Mott-Hubbard transition in strongly correlated electron systems has been intensively studied in the last decades. In the strongly correlated limit, the Hubbard model can be mapped onto an effective spin-1/2 model. A non-Fermi liquid state emerges due to the breaking of the spin rotational symmetry. The Mott-Hubbard transition in the Hubbard model has been extensively studied within the dynamical mean-field theory (DMFT) [1-4].    The DMFT is a nonperturbative method which is able to treat the infinite-dimensional case. However, the DMFT is not able to treat the Mott-Hubbard transition in the infinite-dimensional case. In order to treat the Mott-Hubbard transition in an infinite-dimensional case, the strong-coupling expansion of the DMFT self energy has been developed [5-7].    In this paper, we develop the strong-coupling expansion of the DMFT in the infinite-dimensional case in order to clarify the transition from the Hubbard insulating state to the non-Fermi-liquid state with broken symmetry and to determine the critical exponents. The strong-coupling expansion of the DMFT selfenergy in the infinite-dimensional case reproduces the results of the strong-coupling expansion of the Gutzwiller variational theory (GVT) [6]. In the strong-coupling expansion of the DMFT, the transition from the Hubbard insulator to the non-Fermi-liquid state with the broken symmetry is described by a phase transition between the Mott insulator and the charge-density wave insulator. The critical exponents are determined to be 1.43 (in the 1/N expansion) and 1.23 (in the 1/N^2 expansion).

We show how a density-periodic modulation of the interaction strengths in an attractive Hubbard chain can result in the realization of synthetic magnetic flux and electric field terms in the tight-binding limit. We investigate the singleband tightbinding limit and show how the two different types of synthetic gauge fields are generated using Floquet theory. The Floquet Hamiltonian obtained in this way can be diagonalized and its band structure can be tuned by changing densities. A Floquet Floquet-Bloch theory, developed here, provides an insightful insight into the behavior and transport properties of the system, which can be useful for quantum information science and quantum simulation. In recent years, synthetic gauge fields have been a topic of great interest in the field of quantum simulation, with the aim of controlling the dynamics of particles in an optical lattice ; ; ; ; ;. The synthetic gauge fields can be realized using different approaches, such as by coupling the optical lattice to a microwave resonator ; ; ; ;, a Raman laser ;, or a flux-tunable microwave cavity. However, the most common approach is to use two optical lattices, with a synthetic magnetic flux term added to the hopping term of the lower band. This is the approach that we consider in this paper.  The experimental realization of synthetic magnetic fields in optical lattices is a very challenging task ; 1; ; ; 2; ; ;. This is because the gauge fields in such systems are generated by the lattice potential. In addition, the magnetic field is generated by the motion of the atoms in the lattice potential. Therefore, it is difficult to control the magnetic field strength and the direction of the magnetic field ; 3.  In this paper, we propose an alternative method to generate synthetic magnetic fields in optical lattices. In the proposed scheme, the magnetic field is generated by the motion and the interaction of the atoms. In other words, the magnetic field is generated by the interaction of the atoms. The interaction is modulated by the density, which in turn results in a periodic modulation of the magnetic field. In this way, the magnetic field can be controlled and tuned in different ways. The advantage of this method is that the magnetic field can be generated in a controllable and tunable way. We note that the density-periodic modulation of the interaction is also used to generate density-dependent gauge fields in a Bose-Hubbard model ; 4.  Figure 1: Schematic of the system under study. A one-dimensional optical lattice is formed by two counter-propagating laser beams with the same frequency. The intensity of the laser beams are modulated with a density-periodic function, \(f(x)\). The modulation is applied to the on-site interaction strength, \(U_{0}\), and the hopping strength, \(J\). In this paper, we investigate the single-band tight-binding limit of a Bose-Hubbard model with density-periodic modulation of the on-site interaction and hopping strengths. We find that the synthetic magnetic flux term can be generated using a density-periodic modulation of the interaction strengths. The density-periodic modulation of the interaction strength can be achieved by using a density-periodic modulation of the laser intensity in an optical lattice. We investigate the single-band tight-binding limit and show that the synthetic magnetic flux term can be generated by modulating the laser intensity with a density-periodic function. We also find that the synthetic electric field term can be generated by modulating the hopping strength with a density-periodic function. The Floquet theory is used to investigate the band structure and the transport properties of the system. We show that the band structure can be tuned by changing the density of the atoms. We find that the system can be used to simulate the Aharonov-Bohm effect. In addition, the system can be used to simulate the Berry phase.

Convective and radiative flows are both unstable to the development of thermal plumes in the absence of a magnetic field. Magnetic fields can stabilize such flows, but the critical conditions under which a magnetic field can stabilize convection and radiative flows is still unclear. Here we study the development of buoyant plumes produced by vigorous convection or radiation in a uniform horizontal background field using simulations that incorporate both radiative transfer and magnetic fields. To study the role of resolution we have conducted simulations at increasing resolutions, 256, 512 and 1024, for both convection and radiation. The resulting properties of plumes are compared at different spatial resolutions and compared with their radiative counterparts. It is shown that the maximum temperature at the top of the plume is higher, and the maximum velocity, smaller at lower resolutions. The turbulent heat flux at the top of the plume has similar values for all the resolutions used in these simulations. However, the turbulent temperature is higher at lower resolutions where there are fewer plume-like structures. - Radiative transfer and magnetic fields are both important factors that influence the thermal stability of the atmosphere. The interaction between radiative and convective motions can lead to the development of plumes in the atmosphere. These plumes are characterized by the high temperature at the top of the plume and the high velocity at the bottom of the plume (e.g., 1; 2; ; ; ; ). The presence of a magnetic field can stabilize the convective and radiative flows and hence suppress the development of plumes in the atmosphere (e.g., ; 3; 4; 5; 6; 7; ; 8).   - The presence of a magnetic field can also change the dynamics of the plumes (e.g., 9; ; 10; 11; ).   - The turbulent heat flux at the top of the plumes is also affected by the presence of a magnetic field. It is shown that the turbulent heat flux at the top of the plume is lower in the presence of a magnetic field (; 12; Aurnou et al. 2017). - The role of magnetic field strength and numerical resolution on the development of buoyant plumes in the presence of radiative transfer and magnetic fields is still not fully understood.  - We find that the maximum temperature at the top of the buoyant plume is higher, and the maximum velocity, lower at lower resolutions.

This paper addresses the optimal control problem of a continuous time switched dynamical system whose modes are represented by polynomials in $\mathbb{R}^n$. This system can be switched at a given set of polynomial time instants. The goal is to find a switching strategy by minimizing the corresponding quadratic cost functional subject to an appropriately chosen set of state and control system constraints, where polynomial constraints can be easily implemented by an LMI technique. A computational study is carried out on a benchmark problem in order to illustrate the validity of the proposed approach. \begin{equation}   \dot{x}(t) = A_\sigma(t)x(t) + B_\sigma(t)u(t),   \end{equation}   where $x(t) \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is the state, $u(t) \in \mathbb {R}^m$ is the control input, $\sigma(t) \in \mathbb S$ is the mode of the system, $\mathbb S$ is the finite set of switching modes. The set of admissible control inputs is $\mathbb U$. The system is assumed to be switched at a set of polynomial time instants. \begin{equation}   \min_{\sigma(t),u(t)} \int_0^{\infty} x^T(t)Qx(t) + u^T(t)Ru(t)dt  \end{equation}   subject to \begin{equation}  \dot{x}(t) \in \mathcal{A}_\sigma(t)x(t ) + \mathcal{B}_\sigma(t)u(t ) + \mathcal {C}_\sigma(t)w(t),  \end{equation}   where $w(t) \in \mathbb R^n$ is the disturbance input, $Q \succ 0$, $R \succ 0$, $\mathcal{A}_\sigma(\cdot) \in \mathbb R^{n \times n}$, $\mathcal{B}_\sigma(\cdot) : \mathbb R^m \rightarrow \mathbb R^{n \otimes n}$ and $\mathcal{C}_\sigma(\cdot) = \mathcal{B}_\sigma^T(\cdot) \in \mathcal{L}(\mathbb R^m, \mathbb R^{n \oplus n})$ are assumed to be known. It is shown that the above problem can be transformed into an LMI optimization problem.

Phase diagrams are important tools for understanding the behavior of materials under different conditions. In this paper, we will discuss the methods used to determine the pressure-temperature phase diagrams of materials, including experimental techniques such as high-pressure synthesis and computational modeling. The importance of accurately determining these phase diagrams will also be discussed. Phase diagrams are graphical representations of the phase behavior of a material as a function of temperature and pressure. They are used to predict the stability of different phases of a material and the conditions under which phase transitions occur. The pressure-temperature phase diagram is particularly useful for understanding the behavior of materials under high-pressure conditions, which are commonly encountered in industries such as oil and gas, metallurgy, and geology.

There are several methods that can be used to determine the pressure-temperature phase diagram of a material. One approach is through experimental techniques, such as high-pressure synthesis. In this method, the material is subjected to high pressures and temperatures using specialized equipment, and the resulting phase is observed. Another approach is through computational modeling, where the phase behavior of the material is predicted using computer simulations. Accurately determining the pressure-temperature phase diagram of a material is important for a variety of applications, including the design of new materials and the optimization of existing ones. The methods discussed in this paper, including experimental techniques and computational modeling, are crucial tools for understanding the behavior of materials under different conditions and predicting their phase behavior.

In this paper, we explore the concept of approximate KMS (Kubo-Martin-Schwinger) states for scalar and spinor fields in Friedmann-Robertson-Walker (FRW) spacetimes. KMS states are a special class of quantum states that satisfy a certain condition related to thermal equilibrium, and they have been extensively studied in the context of quantum field theory. We show that approximate KMS states can be constructed for scalar and spinor fields in FRW spacetimes, and we discuss the physical implications of these states. KMS states are a fundamental concept in quantum field theory, and they have been widely studied in various contexts. They are defined as states that satisfy a certain condition related to thermal equilibrium, and they are often used to describe systems in thermal equilibrium at a finite temperature. In recent years, there has been increasing interest in studying KMS states in the context of cosmology, particularly in the context of Friedmann-Robertson-Walker (FRW) spacetimes.

FRW spacetimes are a class of solutions to Einstein's equations of general relativity that describe the evolution of the universe on large scales. They are characterized by a scale factor that describes the expansion or contraction of the universe, and they are often used to model the early universe, as well as the current universe. In this paper, we explore the concept of approximate KMS states for scalar and spinor fields in FRW spacetimes. In this paper, we address the problem of maximizing asymptotic growth in an ergodic setting. We propose a new optimization framework that combines robust optimization with ergodic theory to achieve robust asymptotic growth. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach through several numerical experiments. Asymptotic growth is a crucial performance metric in many fields, including economics, engineering, and computer science. However, achieving high asymptotic growth is often challenging due to the presence of uncertainty and randomness in the system. In particular, traditional optimization methods that aim to maximize asymptotic growth are prone to be sensitive to small perturbations in the system, leading to poor performance in the presence of uncertainty.

To address this issue, we propose a new optimization framework that combines robust optimization with ergodic theory. Robust optimization is a method that seeks to find solutions that are not overly sensitive to small perturbations in the system, ensuring a certain level of robustness to uncertainty. Ergodic theory, on the other hand, is a mathematical framework that deals with the long-term behavior of systems with randomness. By combining these two approaches, we aim to achieve robust asymptotic growth in an ergodic setting. In this paper, we presented a new optimization framework for maximizing asymptotic growth in an ergodic setting. Our approach combines robust optimization with ergodic theory to achieve robust asymptotic growth. Through several numerical experiments, we demonstrated the effectiveness of our approach in achieving high asymptotic growth while maintaining a certain level of robustness to uncertainty. Our results suggest that our approach could be a promising tool for optimizing asymptotic growth in a wide range of applications.

The authors propose a new strategy to reduce the computational complexity of multiple-input, multiple-output (MIMO) decoding. Unlike the successive-interference-cancellation-decoding strategy, which is currently the best choice for the decoding of small MIMO systems with low complexity, their strategy allows blind identification of several users, thus allowing more general multiuser scenarios. In this strategy, each symbol is assigned a random integer identifier and the sequence of identifiers is generated so that two consecutive sequences differ by one bit. This bit allows the decoder to estimate the position of the symbol in the initial sequence of the identifiers. The random position of the symbol in the initial sequence assures that, no matter the received symbols, their identifiers do not allow the decoding of the symbol position when being used in the detection process. Simulations were conducted to assess the error-rate performance and the computational complexity of this novel decoding strategy when compared to the minimum distance decoding strategy. - Multiple-input, multiple-output (MIMO-OFDM) technology is a key component of modern wireless communication systems [1]. With this technology, the number of degrees of freedom available for data transmission is increased, thus providing the opportunity to achieve higher data rates and better spectral efficiency.   - For the MIMO-OFDM system to achieve higher data rates, the system needs to be able to identify and decode the data symbols. The MIMO-OFDM decoder consists of a demodulator and a channel decoder. The demodulator is responsible for extracting the information contained in the received symbols and the channel decoder is responsible for decoding the information contained in the received symbols.   - The MIMO-OFDM demodulator can be implemented using the minimum distance decoding strategy [2], the zero forcing strategy [3] or the successive interference cancellation strategy [4].   - The MIMO-OFDM channel decoder can be implemented using the maximum likelihood (ML) strategy [5] and the sphere decoding strategy [6].   - The ML strategy has a high error-rate performance, but it is very complex to implement.   - The sphere decoding strategy has a high error-rate performance, a low complexity and a good trade-off between error-rate performance and complexity.   - The sphere decoding strategy has the same complexity as the minimum distance decoding strategy and a similar error-rate performance.  The authors propose a new strategy to reduce the computational burden of the MIMO-OFDM channel decoder. This strategy allows the blind identification of several users, thus allowing more generalized multiuser scenarios. This strategy is based on the random selection of the identifiers of the symbols. - The proposed strategy allows the blind identification of several users, which can be useful in MIMO-OFDM systems in which several users are transmitting simultaneously.   - The proposed strategy allows the MIMO-OFDM channel decoder to achieve a lower complexity than the sphere decoding strategy and similar error-rate performance.   - The proposed strategy has the same complexity as the minimum distance strategy and a similar error-rate performance.

The detection of weeds on UAV is a task known as object detection and segmentation, however the process can benefit of methods using artificial intelligence, as deep learning, to perform this task. Deep learning has the ability to discover relevant features automatically in an unsupervised manner while it is capable of learning from a small number of labeled data. In this work, the authors propose the use of a new classification method based on two deep networks with unsupervised data labeling and a convolutional autoencoder (CAE). The method is applied on a UAV multispectral image dataset of 15 images, containing weeds in the field. The authors used 34,200 pixels to train the deep networks and 1,233 pixels to label the data. The performance shows an accuracy of 92%, sensitivity of 93.6% and specificity of 90.6%, while the CAE obtained an accuracy of 96.5 %. - In the last years, the use of Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAV) has increased significantly and they are becoming a very promising tool in agriculture, for example, to perform weed control. The use of UAV is not only limited to the detection of weeds, but also for other applications, such as crop monitoring, crop protection and even to detect other types of objects, such as vehicles, trees, and animals.   - Weeds are one of the main problems in agriculture, because they can reduce the productivity of the crop and affect the quality of the harvest.   - Weeds can be detected using different techniques, such as visual, radar, infrared, multispectral and hyperspectral.   - The main limitation of these techniques is the cost, and also the complexity of the equipment.   - Deep learning can be a useful alternative for detecting weeds on UAV images.   - In this work, the authors propose the use a new classification method based on two deep networks and a convolutional autoencoder (CAE) for weeds detection on UAV images.   - The proposed method is applied on a UAV multispectral image dataset, containing weeds in the field. The dataset consists of 15 images with 34,200 pixels to be used for training the deep networks and 1,234 pixels to label the data.   - The method was evaluated using 100 images with weeds and 100 images without weeds. The evaluation results show an accuracy of 92%, sensitivity 93.6% and specificity 90.6%, while the convolutional autoencoder obtained an accuracy of 96.5%. - The proposed method achieves a high accuracy and it can be used for weeds detection in UAV images.   - The proposed method has the advantage of being able to learn from an unsupervised data labeling.   - The method can be extended to other types of objects, such as vehicles, animals and trees.   - The method can be applied in other applications, such as crop monitoring, crop control and even to detect other types of objects, as vehicles, trees, and animals.   - The method can be used in other applications, such as crop monitoring, control and even to detect other types of objects such as vehicles, trees and animals.   - The method can also be used to detect other types of objects, such as trees, animals and vehicles.   - The method can be applied to other applications, such as crop monitoring, control, and even to detect other types of objects such trees, animals and vehicles.   -.

There is a debate within the higher education research community about the implications of different research methods in the scientific literature. This paper shows that both traditional publication methods such as journal articles and review articles, as well as novel publication methods such as conference proceedings are in use in higher education research, and that their relative proportions change with research field. In a cross-disciplinary, sample of researchers working in the education, business, law and medical fields in the UK, the frequency of both traditional and novel publications has been measured and shown to be sensitive to research area and type of field of study. It is proposed that it is important to account for field effects in any study on publication types in higher education research. As a first step in this direction, we used the UK Higher Education Database, which provided an extensive list of research papers published in the past two decades in all UK higher education venues. It can be seen, for example, that the proportion of papers published as review articles has more than doubled across the sample of researchers from 2000 to 2014. On the other hand, the proportion of papers published as research proposals has declined by a much lesser amount. This pattern is observed across a wide range of research fields and is also seen for research proposals in a sample from 1980. A second empirical finding is the sensitivity of the proportion of papers in a field published as review articles to the method of selecting the researchers. For example, the proportion of papers in the science field published as review articles tends to be larger when the researchers are identified using a list of all researchers of all years for whom the publication records were available than for a more selective sample of researchers. A third finding is that there is little indication in the data that the proportion of research proposals that are reviewed increases with the length of the research project. The debate in higher education research about the relative importance of different research methods in the scientific literature has been going on for some time (see for example 1; 2; 3; 4; 5). The debate is motivated by the fact that researchers in the higher education research community have traditionally published their research in a variety of ways. For example, a number of researchers publish their research in the form of journal articles (6; 7), whereas others publish their research in the form of review articles (8) and others publish their research in the form of research proposals (9). A debate has also arisen about the relative importance of these different publication methods in terms of their effect on the quality of the research.    This paper shows that both traditional publication methods such journal articles and review articles, as well as the novel publication methods such as conference proceedings are in the higher education research community in use, and that their relative proportions change with research area and type of field of study. In a sample of researchers working in the education, business and medical fields in the UK, the frequency with which both traditional and novel publications are used has been measured. The paper also explores the implications of these findings for the interpretation of research in higher education. The paper makes some important contributions to the higher education research literature. It provides a quantitative measure of the relative importance of traditional and novel publication methods in higher education research. It also provides evidence that the relative importance of traditional and novel publication methods can be sensitive to the method of selecting the researchers.    The rest of the paper is organised as follows. Section 2 provides a brief review of the literature on publication methods in higher education research. Section 3 describes the data and the sample of researchers included in the analysis. Section 4 presents the findings of the analysis, including the results of the analysis of the publication frequency of different publication methods, and the results of the analysis of the publication frequency as a function of the method of selecting the researchers. Section 5 provides a discussion of the results and concludes.    Acknowledgements:    The authors thank Professor Martin Fiorina for his comments on the earlier draft of this paper.    Appendix:    The Appendix provides the list of journals and conferences included in the sample, the publication dates of the researchers included in the analysis, and the sample sizes for each field of study.

In this paper, we present a method for synthesizing controllers for continuous-time systems using compositional abstraction. Our approach allows for the decomposition of the control synthesis problem into smaller, more manageable subproblems, making it well-suited for the design of large-scale systems. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our method through several case studies, showing that it can be used to synthesize controllers with good performance and robustness properties. The design of controllers for continuous-time systems is a central problem in control engineering. These systems are often complex, with a large number of variables and interactions, making the design of controllers that achieve the desired behavior challenging. One approach to addressing this complexity is to use compositional abstraction, which decomposes the control synthesis problem into smaller, more manageable subproblems. This allows for the use of techniques that are tailored to the specific characteristics of each subproblem, leading to more efficient and effective controller design.

In this paper, we present a method for synthesizing controllers for continuous-time systems using compositional abstraction. Our approach is based on the use of model reduction techniques to construct abstractions of the system dynamics, which are then used to synthesize controllers for the individual subproblems. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our method through several case studies, showing that it can be used to synthesize controllers with good performance and robustness properties. In this paper, we have presented a method for synthesizing controllers for continuous-time systems using compositional abstraction. Our approach allows for the decomposition of the control synthesis problem into smaller, more manageable subproblems, making it well-suited for the design of large-scale systems. We have demonstrated the effectiveness of our method through several case studies, showing that it can be used to synthesize controllers with good performance and robustness properties. Overall, our results demonstrate the potential of compositional abstraction as a tool for the design of controllers for continuous-time systems.

Recently there has been an effort to provide a framework for modelling the crowd dynamics. In these models, pedestrian movement is governed by their social interactions, which in turn are based on social psychology hypotheses. The models have been very popular because they seem to offer a more parsimonious explanation of crowds’ collective behaviour, which is difficult to explain by the simple rules of physics or engineering. In this talk we explain the difficulties associated with modelling crowds using crowd dynamics models. We have identified three main reasons why models based on social psychology are difficult to fit and analyse: 1. Crowd dynamics models are typically formulated in continuous, but not in discrete, space and time. 2. The models are typically highly idealized and neglect most aspects of the actual social interactions. 3. Most importantly, the models are typically complex and hard to understand and use. A.1. Crowd dynamics models    The main motivation behind the development of crowd dynamics models is to explain the collective behaviour of crowds. Such behaviour is usually referred to as a crowd phenomenon and is often observed in the real world, e.g., during the riots. Crowds are usually large groups of people that move through space, and the movements of these groups are often observed on the macroscopic level, i.e., on the level of a single crowd.    A.2. Modelling assumptions. 1. The most popular crowd dynamics models are based on the assumption that the crowd is an ensemble of individuals that move in a simple, continuous manner. 2. The models are typically highly simplified and neglect many of the details of the actual social interactions. 3. The models are typically complex and hard to understand and interpret.

In this document, we present the updates and improvements made in version 3 of {\tt RunDec} and {\tt CRunDec}, two computer programs for the evaluation of running and matching parameters in the Standard Model and its extensions. {\tt RunDec} is written in Fortran 77 and {\tt CRunDec} is written in C. The main focus of the updates is to increase the precision and speed of the calculations, as well as to add support for new parameterizations and models. We also discuss the testing and validation of the new versions. {\tt RunDec} and {\tt CRunDec} are computer programs that calculate the running and matching parameters of the Standard Model (SM) and its extensions at the energy scales relevant for high energy physics experiments. These parameters include the strong coupling constant, the electromagnetic coupling constant, and the quark and lepton masses and mixings.

In the SM and its extensions, the running of these parameters is governed by the renormalization group equations (RGEs), which describe how the parameters change as the energy scale of the theory changes. The matching parameters are defined at the energy scales where the theory undergoes a phase transition, such as the electroweak symmetry breaking or the quark confinement.

{\tt RunDec} and {\tt CRunDec} use the RGEs and matching conditions to evaluate the running and matching parameters at any given energy scale, allowing for the prediction of observables in high energy physics experiments.

In version 3 of {\tt RunDec} and {\tt CRunDec}, we have made several updates and improvements to increase the precision and speed of the calculations, as well as to add support for new parameterizations and models. These updates include the implementation of higher order perturbative corrections, the use of improved interpolation methods, and the incorporation of new data from experiments and lattice calculations. In this document, we have presented the updates and improvements made in version 3 of {\tt RunDec} and {\tt CRunDec}, two important tools for the evaluation of running and matching parameters in the Standard Model and its extensions. The main focus of the updates is to increase the precision and speed of the calculations, as well as to add support for new parameterizations and models. We have also discussed the testing and validation of the new versions to ensure their reliability and accuracy. These updates will greatly enhance the capabilities of {\tt RunDec} and {\tt CRunDec} in the study of high energy physics and the search for new physics beyond the Standard Model.

We have conducted a frequency analysis of the asteroseismic data of seven intermediate-mass (IM) main-sequence stars obtained with CoRoT. We investigated the sensitivity of the oscillation spectrum of the IM star KIC 9260596 to the differential rotation of the stellar inner core. After comparing the theoretical predictions of the oscillation spectrum with the observations, we found that the sensitivity of intermediate modes to the inner core differential rotation is weak, which allows us to constrain stellar parameters without assuming the internal rotation profile and without including in the mode stability calculations the effects of the rotational coupling with the magnetic field. We have studied different assumptions for the stellar differential rotation profile, and calculated the theoretical oscillation spectrum for both the rigid and differential rotation cases. We have found that the rigid rotation approximation can be used to fit the oscillation spectrum of the star KIC 9260596, which suggests that the angular rotation speed of the inner core is constant. However, assuming a uniform differential rotation profile for a star can lead to an incorrect evaluation of the asteroseismic stellar radius, because of the effects of the differential rotation on the radial mode frequencies. - The internal rotation profile of intermediate-mass (IM) main- sequence stars is a crucial parameter that is often ignored in asteroseismic studies. However, it is important to study the effects of the differential rotation on the oscillation spectrum, because the observed oscillation spectrum is sensitive to the angular rotation speed of the stellar inner core, which is the region where the differential rotation is expected to be the strongest.   - The differential rotation of IM main-sequence stars is difficult to measure, because the stellar rotation rate is usually very low and the stellar surface is not observable. In addition, the effects of the differential rotation on the stellar structure and oscillation spectrum are expected to be very small. However, these effects cannot be ignored, and they have to be taken into account in the asteroseismic analysis.   - The effects of the differential rotation on the oscillation spectrum of IM main-sequence stars are also important for the understanding of the internal rotation profile of these stars, because the internal rotation profile is determined by the interplay of the internal and external differential rotation.   - The main purpose of this work is to investigate the sensitivity of the oscillation spectrum of IM main-sequence stars to the differential rotation of the stellar inner core, and to determine the stellar differential rotation profile that best fits the observed oscillation spectrum. - We have studied different assumptions for the stellar differential rotation profile and calculated the theoretical oscillation spectrum for both the rigid and differential rotation cases. We have found that a uniform differential rotation profile can be used to fit the oscillation spectrum of a star, but it leads to an incorrect evaluation of the asteroseismic stellar radius.   - We have found that the effects of the differential rotation on the oscillation spectrum of IM main-sequence stars are very weak, and the observed oscillation spectrum can be fitted with the rigid rotation approximation.   - The observed oscillation spectrum of IM main-sequence stars can be fitted with the rigid rotation approximation, which suggests that the angular rotation speed of the inner core is constant.   - We have found that the observed oscillation spectrum of IM main-sequence stars cannot be fitted with the uniform differential rotation profile, and the differential rotation profile that best fits the observed oscillation spectrum has a peak at \(\sim\) 1.5 \(R_{\odot}\), which suggests that the differential rotation has a peak at the stellar surface. In this study, we demonstrate the guiding and trapping of electron spin waves in a gas of atomic hydrogen using a microstructured waveguide. The spin waves, which are also known as spin-density waves or magnons, are excited using a laser pulse and propagated through the hydrogen gas using the waveguide. By carefully designing the waveguide geometry, we are able to control the propagation of the spin waves and trap them in specific regions of the gas. Our results provide a promising approach for manipulating and controlling spin waves in atomic hydrogen, which has potential applications in spintronic devices and quantum computing. Spin waves, also known as magnons or spin-density waves, are collective excitations of the electron spin in a material. They have been widely studied in solid state systems, such as ferromagnetic materials, due to their potential applications in spintronic devices and quantum computing. However, the study of spin waves in gases, particularly atomic gases, has received less attention. Atomic hydrogen, in particular, is an attractive system for studying spin waves due to its simplicity and the fact that it is the most abundant element in the universe.

In this work, we demonstrate the ability to guide and trap spin waves in a gas of atomic hydrogen using a microstructured waveguide. The waveguide is made of a dielectric material and has a specific geometry that allows the spin waves to propagate through the hydrogen gas. By carefully designing the waveguide, we are able to control the propagation of the spin waves and trap them in specific regions of the gas. In summary, we have demonstrated the ability to guide and trap spin waves in a gas of atomic hydrogen using a microstructured waveguide. Our results provide a promising approach for manipulating and controlling spin waves in atomic hydrogen, which has potential applications in spintronic devices and quantum computing. Further studies are needed to optimize the waveguide design and to explore the potential applications of this technique.

The dynamic programming principle states that the optimal solution to a problem can be found by solving its subproblems and combining their solutions. This principle has been widely accepted and used in practice, but a formal proof of its existence has been elusive.

This document presents an analysis of the optimization parameters for the design of a circular e+e- Higgs factory. The Higgs factory is a type of particle accelerator that aims to produce Higgs bosons, a subatomic particle that was predicted by the Standard Model of particle physics. The design of the Higgs factory requires careful consideration of various optimization parameters, such as the energy and luminosity of the accelerator, the beam size and divergence, and the beam-beam interaction. In this study, we discuss the importance of these optimization parameters and present a detailed analysis of how they can be optimized for the best performance of the Higgs factory. The Higgs boson, discovered at the Large Hadron Collider (LHC) in 2012, is a fundamental particle that plays a crucial role in the Standard Model of particle physics. It is responsible for giving mass to other particles, and its discovery was a major milestone in the field of particle physics.

One way to study the Higgs boson and its properties in more detail is through the use of a Higgs factory, a type of particle accelerator that is specifically designed to produce Higgs bosons. A Higgs factory operates by colliding high-energy beams of particles, such as electrons and positrons, and using the energy of the collision to create new particles, including the Higgs boson.

The design of a Higgs factory requires careful consideration of various optimization parameters, such as the energy and luminosity of the accelerator, the beam size and divergence, and the beam-beam interaction. These parameters are important for ensuring that the Higgs factory is able to produce a sufficient number of Higgs bosons with high enough energy to allow for detailed study. In this study, we have presented an analysis of the optimization parameters for the design of a circular e+e- Higgs factory. We have discussed the importance of these parameters and provided a detailed analysis of how they can be optimized for the best performance of the Higgs factory. By carefully considering these optimization parameters, it is possible to design a Higgs factory that is capable of producing a sufficient number of Higgs bosons with high enough energy to allow for detailed study. This will be crucial for advancing our understanding of the Higgs boson and its role in the Standard Model of particle physics.

The High Scale Mixing Unification Hypothesis (HSMUH) is a theoretical framework that aims to unify the fundamental forces of nature by positing that they are all different manifestations of a single underlying force. In this paper, we outline the key predictions of the HSMUH and discuss their implications for our understanding of the universe. The HSMUH is based on the idea that the fundamental forces of nature, including electromagnetism, the weak nuclear force, and the strong nuclear force, are all different expressions of a single, unified force at extremely high energy scales. This hypothesis is motivated by the fact that these forces all exhibit similar behavior at high energies and have similar mathematical structures.

The HSMUH makes several key predictions about the nature of the universe. First, it predicts that there is a single, fundamental force that underlies all the known forces of nature. Second, it predicts that this underlying force will become more apparent at extremely high energy scales, leading to the unification of the different forces. Third, it predicts that there will be new particles and interactions that emerge at these high energy scales, which could provide insights into the fundamental nature of the universe. The HSMUH is an ambitious and intriguing theoretical framework that has the potential to revolutionize our understanding of the fundamental nature of the universe. While the predictions of the HSMUH are still being tested, the results of these tests will be crucial for determining the validity of this hypothesis. If the HSMUH is ultimately confirmed, it could lead to a deeper understanding of the fundamental forces of nature and the underlying structure of the universe.

The aim of this work is to study the possibility of the neutrino oscillations in the case when the mass matrix is not the Dirac mass, but is the 2 × 2 sub-matrix or the 2 × 3 block of the 4 × 4 mass matrix. - The mass matrix of the Dirac neutrinos can be presented in the following form   \[ \begin{split}M_{D}=&\displaystyle\frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{3}\left(e^{i\phi_{i}}\sigma_{i}+e^{i\phi_ {i+3}}\sigma_{i+3}\right)\\ &\displaystyle=\frac{1}{2}e^{i\phi_{3}}\sum_{i=1} ^{3}\sigma_{i}+\frac{1}{2}e ^{i\phi_{1}+i\phi_{2}}\left(\sigma_{1}+\sigma_{2}+\sigma_{3}\right)+\frac{1}{2}\sigma_{123},\end{split} \]   - The mass matrix of the Dirac neutrino can be presented in the following form   (5)   - The mass matrix of the Dirac particle can be presented in the following form   [2]   \[ M_{D}=m_{1}\left(\begin{array} {cc}1&0\\ 0&e^{i\phi}\end{array}\right)+m_{2}\left(\begin{matrix} 0&1\\ 1&0\end{matrix}\right)+m_{3}\left(\begin{matrix} e^{i\phi}&0\\ 0&1\end{matrix}\right). \].

The problem of human pose estimation from RGB-D, or RGB-D-based data is relevant to numerous fields in computer vision and robotics. In this work, we propose a real-time marker-free method for multi-person real-time 3D pose estimation from RGB-D camera networks. The method utilizes an off-the-shelf 3D human body model, which is used only for body localization. For pose detection, we first apply a model fitting that recovers the body and camera parameters. Based on the pose parameters, we propose a novel 2D-to-3D pose estimation algorithm which is composed of a 2D pose estimation algorithm and a robust 3D pose estimation algorithm. In our proposed algorithm, human joints are firstly inferred in the (depth, 2D) plane from the 2D images, and then used as priors in an object detection algorithm. This 2D-to-3D pose estimation algorithm enables the detection of 3D poses in the depth map of each RGB-D camera in realtime. We verify that the proposed method is able to estimate 3D poses robustly, despite the depth image noise and the inter-person occlusions. Finally, we show that the proposed method is able to run in real-time of 2 fps under the real-world conditions. A preliminary version of the proposed algorithm was presented at the 5th IEEE Int. Conf. on Autonomous Robots (ISAR 2017). This paper is an extension in which we further validate the proposed method on real-world data and conduct a quantitative evaluation. Human pose estimation from RGB-D or RGB-D-based data is a challenging task. The RGB-D data, which consists of a color image and a depth map, is more informative than the RGB data. For example, depth maps provide the information about the distance between the camera and the subject, which is not available in the RGB data.    RGB-D-based human pose estimation is also more challenging than RGB-based human pose estimation. The human body in RGB-D data is typically observed in a 3D space. The depth values are often noisy and the depth data is not available for the background. Moreover, the human body in RGB-D data is usually occluded by other persons in the scene.    Existing works for RGB-D-based human pose estimation can be divided into two groups: (1) 2D-to-3D pose detection, and (2) 3D-to-2D pose detection.    Methods in the first group estimate the 3D poses from the 2D images. A typical example of this group is the method by [1], which estimates the 3D poses from the 2.5D poses. This method utilizes a 3D human body model to estimate the 3D poses. Another example of this group is the method by 1, which estimates the 3D poses from 2D poses. The method by [1] and [2] are not suitable for real-time human pose estimation, because they require a time-consuming optimization process.    Methods in the second group estimate the 3D poses from the depth maps. A typical example of this group is the work by [3], which estimates the 3D poses using a monocular depth camera. The proposed method is a 2D-to-3D pose detector, which is a novel extension of the method by [3]. In the proposed method, the 2D poses are first estimated from the 2D images, and then these 2D poses are used as priors in an object detection algorithm. The 2D-to-3D pose estimator enables the detection of 3D poses in real-time.

In this paper, we explore the relationship between cluster algebras and the subalgebra constructibility of the seven-particle remainder function. Specifically, we show that the subalgebra generated by the cluster variables associated with the seven-particle remainder function is indeed constructible within the ambient cluster algebra.
