One of the key tools used to study the seven-particle remainder function is the theory of cluster algebras. Cluster algebras are a class of mathematical objects that encode the combinatorics of scattering amplitudes in a way that is well-suited for computational purposes. In particular, cluster algebras are equipped with a set of generators called cluster variables, which can be used to construct a subalgebra that encodes the leading-order terms in the asymptotic expansion of the scattering amplitudes.

The main result of this paper is that the subalgebra generated by the cluster variables associated with the seven-particle remainder function is indeed constructible within the ambient cluster algebra. This result has important implications for the study of scattering amplitudes in quantum field theory, as it provides a way to study the analytic properties of these amplitudes using the tools of cluster algebraic geometry. In this paper, we have explored the relationship between cluster algebras and the subalgebra constructibility of the seven-particle remainder function. We have shown that the subalgebra generated by the cluster variables associated with the seven-particle remainder function is indeed constructible within the ambient cluster algebra, which has important implications for the study of scattering amplitudes in quantum field theory. This result provides a way to study the analytic properties of these amplitudes using the tools of cluster algebraic geometry, and could have significant implications for our understanding of the behavior of scattering amplitudes in theories with a large number of interacting particles.

The $\Lambda_b \rightarrow \Lambda \ell^+ \ell^-$ decay is a rare process in the Standard Model of particle physics that can be used to study the structure of the $\Lambda_b$ baryon and probe for new physics beyond the Standard Model. In this paper, we analyze the $\Lambda_b \rightarrow \Lambda \ell^+ \ell^-$ decay using perturbative QCD techniques. We present the relevant Feynman diagrams, calculate the decay width, and discuss the sensitivity of the decay to various input parameters. Our analysis shows that the $\Lambda_b \rightarrow \Lambda \ell^+ \ell^-$ decay is a promising avenue for studying the properties of the $\Lambda_b$ baryon and searching for new physics. The $\Lambda_b$ baryon, consisting of a bottom quark and two light quarks, is a key ingredient in the Standard Model of particle physics. Its properties and decays provide important tests of the Standard Model and can also be used to search for new physics beyond the Standard Model. One interesting decay of the $\Lambda_b$ baryon is the $\Lambda_b \rightarrow \Lambda \ell^+ \ell^-$ decay, where $\Lambda$ is a lighter baryon consisting of a light quark and a strange quark, and $\ell^+$ and $\ell^-$ are leptons, such as electrons or muons. This decay is rare in the Standard Model and is sensitive to new physics contributions, making it a valuable probe for studying the $\Lambda_b$ baryon and searching for new physics.

We have presented the relevant Feynman diagrams, calculated the decay width, and discussed the sensitivity of the decay to various input parameters. Further experimental and theoretical studies of this decay will provide valuable insights into the structure of the $\Lambda_b$ baryon and the nature of new physics beyond the Standard Model.

This study investigates the laminar and transitional liquid metal duct flow near a magnetic point dipole. The effect of the dipole strength and orientation on the flow patterns and heat transfer is examined using numerical simulations. It is found that the dipole can significantly alter the flow patterns and heat transfer rate in the duct. The results of this study have potential applications in the design of liquid metal cooling systems for advanced nuclear reactors and other heat-transfer devices. Liquid metal cooling is an attractive option for advanced nuclear reactors and other high-heat-flux devices due to its high thermal conductivity and corrosion resistance. However, the flow of liquid metals in narrow ducts can be complex, and the presence of a magnetic field can significantly affect the flow patterns and heat transfer.

One type of magnetic field configuration that has received attention in recent years is the magnetic point dipole, which consists of two small magnets with opposite polarity located close to each other. The dipole generates a strong local magnetic field that can influence the flow of liquid metals in its vicinity.

In this study, we investigate the laminar and transitional liquid metal duct flow near a magnetic point dipole using numerical simulations. The effect of the dipole strength and orientation on the flow patterns and heat transfer is analyzed. The results of this study show that the presence of a magnetic point dipole can significantly alter the flow patterns and heat transfer rate in a liquid metal duct. The dipole strength and orientation have a significant effect on the flow patterns and heat transfer. These findings have potential applications in the design of liquid metal cooling systems for advanced nuclear reactors and other heat-transfer devices.

Interference is a major challenge in the design and optimization of cellular networks. In this work, we propose a novel approach to treating interference as noise in cellular networks using tools from stochastic geometry. By modeling the locations of the network's base stations and users as a point process, we are able to analytically characterize the interference experienced by each user and evaluate the performance of different interference management schemes. Our results show that treating interference as noise can significantly improve the network's overall spectral efficiency and provide more robust performance in dynamic environments. In cellular networks, interference arises when multiple users or base stations transmit simultaneously on the same frequency band. This can lead to degraded performance, including reduced signal quality, increased error rates, and reduced capacity. To mitigate interference, various interference management techniques have been proposed, such as frequency reuse, power control, and scheduling algorithms. However, these techniques often rely on complex signaling and coordination between the network's base stations and users, which can be difficult to implement and prone to errors.

Alternatively, treating interference as noise has been shown to be a simple and effective approach for improving the performance of wireless networks. This approach involves treating the interference experienced by each user as additive white Gaussian noise, which can be effectively removed using standard noise reduction techniques such as linear receivers or successive interference cancellation. In this work, we propose to extend this approach to cellular networks using tools from stochastic geometry. In this paper, we have presented a novel approach for treating interference as noise in cellular networks using tools from stochastic geometry. By modeling the locations of the network's base stations and users as a point process, we were able to analytically characterize the interference experienced by each user and evaluate the performance of different interference management schemes. Our results show that treating interference as noise can significantly improve the network's overall spectral efficiency and provide more robust performance in dynamic environments. Future work will focus on extending this approach to more realistic and complex network scenarios, as well as exploring the tradeoffs between performance and implementation complexity.

In this paper, we present a theoretical analysis of non-interacting ultracold bosons in an amplitude modulated parabolic optical lattice. Our results may have potential applications in the manipulation and control of ultracold bosonic systems.

In this paper, we analyze the various factors that may cause the breakdown of exponential decays of resonances, including interference, coupling to other decay channels, and the influence of external perturbations. We also discuss the implications of these breakdowns for the accuracy of models and predictions based on exponential decay. Resonances are defined as a phenomenon in which a system responds strongly to a particular frequency of external excitation. They are often described by a Lorentzian or Breit-Wigner spectral density, which predicts an exponential decay of the resonance over time. This exponential decay is a fundamental property of resonances and is used to describe a wide range of physical phenomena, from the decay of radioactive isotopes to the damping of oscillations in electrical circuits.

However, it has been observed that the decay of some resonances may deviate from the expected exponential behavior. This breakdown of exponential decay can have significant consequences for the accuracy of models and predictions based on exponential decay, and it is therefore important to understand the factors that can cause this breakdown. In conclusion, the exponential decay of resonances is a fundamental concept with wide-ranging applications in physics and engineering. However, it is important to recognize that the decay of some resonances may deviate from the expected exponential behavior due to factors such as interference, coupling to other decay channels, and external perturbations. A thorough understanding of these factors is crucial for accurately modeling and predicting the behavior of resonances in various systems.

In this paper, we present a unified formulation for completely integrable systems. Our approach is based on the theory of Lie algebras and their representations, and allows for a systematic construction of integrable systems in various dimensions and contexts. We demonstrate the power and versatility of our method by applying it to a variety of examples, including the KdV equation, the nonlinear Schrödinger equation, and the sine-Gordon equation. Completely integrable systems have been a subject of extensive study in the fields of mathematics and physics for many years. They are characterized by the existence of an infinite number of conserved quantities, which can be used to find exact solutions to the system's evolution equations. This property makes completely integrable systems particularly interesting for both theoretical and practical purposes, as it allows for a much deeper understanding of their behavior and for the development of efficient numerical algorithms for their simulation.

There are various approaches to the construction and classification of completely integrable systems, ranging from the use of special functions and symmetries to the study of integrability conditions and the existence of Lax pairs. While each of these methods has its own advantages and limitations, it is often desirable to have a unified framework that can unify and generalize these different approaches.

In this paper, we present such a unified formulation for completely integrable systems, based on the theory of Lie algebras and their representations. Our approach allows for a systematic construction of integrable systems in various dimensions and contexts, and can be applied to a wide range of examples, including both classical and quantum systems. In this paper, we have presented a unified formulation for completely integrable systems based on the theory of Lie algebras and their representations. Our method allows for a systematic construction of integrable systems in various dimensions and contexts, and has been successfully applied to a variety of examples. We believe that this approach will provide a useful tool for further investigations of completely integrable systems, and for the development of efficient numerical algorithms for their simulation.

Context. X-ray induced processes are essential for understanding star formation processes, but an accurate and self-consistent treatment of the chemical evolution of molecular clouds is still underdeveloped; therefore, their effects on the evolution and structure of circumstellar disks are not well known. Aims. Our work addresses the dependence of disk models on the chemical composition, which is of paramount importance for the characterization of circumstellar disks. Methods. Since the presence of CO+ ions is essential for the chemistry of protoplanetary disks, we have studied the behavior of the H13CO+ gas-phase emission and chemical abundance as a function of the physical and chemical model parameters, using an X-ray illumination model that considers energy deposition processes from the cosmic X-ray background field by the star and accretion disk. Results. We analyzed the effects of the cosmic-ray ionization rate, gas temperature, density, and the fraction of hydrogen nuclei in the form of CO-bearing molecules, on the amount of H13CO+ gas in the outer disk. Results show that the X-ray radiation energy injection in the outer disk layer affects the abundance of H13CO+ and CO+, and that the variations of the H13CO+ and CO+ abundances with respect to cosmic-ray ionization rate, gas temperature, density, and the C-bearing molecular fraction in the disks can reach four orders of magnitude. Conclusions. We suggest that X-ray irradiation might play an important role in the chemical properties in the outer regions of circumstellar disks, as well as in the inner parts of disks where star or planet formation occur. - X-ray induced processes are essential for understanding the star formation processes.   - An accurate and self-consistent treatment of the chemical composition of molecular clouds is still underdeveloped; therefore the effects of X-ray ionization on the evolution and structure of circumstellar disks are still not well known.   - The chemical composition of protoplanetary disks is of paramount importance for the characterization of circumstellar disks.   - X-ray radiation energy deposition in the outer disk layer affects the abundance of H13CO+ and CO+, and the variations of the H13CO+ abundance with respect to cosmic-ray ionization rate, gas temperature, density, and the C-bearing molecule fraction in the disks can reach four orders of magnitude.    Context. X-ray ionization is an important process for the chemical evolution of molecular clouds. In order to understand the effects of X-ray radiation on the structure of circumstellar disks, it is important to take into account the X-ray induced chemistry. However, the presence of CO+ ions is essential for any disk chemical model, and it is still unclear how X-ray radiation affects the abundance of CO+. In this work, we have studied the dependence of the disk chemical composition on the cosmic-ray ionization rate, gas temperature (\(T_{\mathrm{gas}}\)), density (\(n\)), and the fraction of hydrogen nuclei in the form of CO-bearing molecules, in order to characterize the effect of X-ray radiation on the chemical properties in the outer regions of circumstellar disks.    Aims. Our work addresses the dependence of the disk chemical composition on the chemical model parameters, which is of paramount importance for the characterization of the chemical composition of circumstellar disks. - X-ray irradiation might play an important role on the chemical properties of the outer regions of circumstellar disks.    Methods:    - We used the 3D, time-dependent, non-equilibrium, chemical-physical model described in (1).   - The X-ray ionization rate, \(\zeta\), is computed as a function of the X-ray luminosity of the star and accretion disk, assuming that the X-ray emission is dominated by the star and accretion disk (2).   - We consider the cosmic-ray ionization rate, \(\xi\), which is the ionization rate due to cosmic rays (3).   - We consider the disk chemical composition as a function of \(\zeta\), \(\xi\), \(T_{\mathrm{gas,0}}\), \(n\), and the fraction of hydrogen nuclei in CO-bearing molecules, \(f_{\mathrm{CO}}\).   - The H13CO+ emission is computed using the chemical model described in (4), which includes the effect of X-ray ionization on the chemistry of H13CO+ and CO+.    Results:   - The chemical composition of the disk is determined by the cosmic-ray ionization rate and the gas temperature. The X-ray radiation energy deposition in the outer regions of the disk affects the chemical composition of the disk, and the variations of the H13C+ and CO+ abundances with respect to cosmic ray ionization rate, gas temperature, density, and C-bearing molecule fraction in the disks can range from one to four orders of magnitude.  - The X-ray ionization rate is the most important parameter for the chemical composition of the disk. The cosmic-ray ionization rate, gas temperature, and density affect the disk chemical composition to a lesser extent.    Conclusions:   - X-ray irradiation might have a significant effect on the chemical properties of the outer regions of protoplanetary disks.

In this paper, we introduce the concept of projective expected utility as a way to extend the traditional expected utility theory to decision making under uncertainty. We show that projective expected utility allows for a more robust and flexible modeling of preferences under risk, and provides a framework for incorporating subjective probability assessments into decision analysis. Expected utility theory is a widely used framework for modeling decision making under uncertainty. It assumes that an individual's preferences can be represented by a utility function, which assigns a numerical value to each possible outcome of a decision. The expected utility of a decision is then calculated as the sum of the utilities of the possible outcomes, weighted by their probabilities. While expected utility theory has been successful in many applications, it has a number of limitations. One major limitation is that it assumes that individuals have well-defined, stable, and objectively measurable preferences. However, research has shown that people's preferences often depend on the context in which they are presented, and may be influenced by factors such as framing and anchoring.

Projective expected utility is a generalization of expected utility theory that allows for more flexibility in modeling preferences under risk. It introduces the concept of a projective utility function, which is a function that maps an individual's subjective probability assessment onto a utility scale. This allows for the incorporation of subjective probability into decision analysis, and allows for the modeling of preferences that are context-dependent or influenced by framing effects. In summary, projective expected utility provides a useful extension to traditional expected utility theory that allows for a more robust and flexible modeling of preferences under risk. It allows for the incorporation of subjective probability into decision analysis, and provides a framework for modeling context-dependent and framing-influenced preferences. While there are still many open questions and challenges in the field of projective expected utility, it represents an important direction for future research in decision making under uncertainty.

A novel method for producing high-efficiency firefly bioluminescence is examined. The lantern of Photuris fulvogularis, which lacks photoisomerization of the luciferin produced by the firefly, is used for this study. The lantern was illuminated with red light (660–680 nm), which is absorbed by the light-sensitive protein, opsin, located in its rhabdomeres. Red light increases the luminescence efficiency of the P. fulvogularis lantern in a manner similar to that seen in previous work with the firefly lantern. The light-dependent and light-independent luminescence efficiency of this lantern are higher than has been reported for other members of the lantern firefly family [i.e. the genus Photinus (Coleoptera: Lampyridae)]. Firefly bioluminescence is produced by the bioluminescent lantern, a compound eye structure consisting of an outer light-sensitive compound eye and an inner light-insensitive compound eye. The light-sensitive compound eye is the lantern, which is composed of a light-absorbing rhabdomere and a light-emitting pigment cell. The light-emitting pigment cell is located in the lantern's rhabdomeres.      The lantern of the firefly Photinus pyralis has a unique feature: it does not have a photoisomerization step in its luciferin biosynthetic pathway. The photoisomerization of the luciferin is catalyzed by the opsin protein, located in the rhabdomeres. Calcium-silicate-hydrate (C-S-H) is the main binder phase in concrete and plays a crucial role in its mechanical properties. In this study, we used molecular dynamics simulations to investigate the fracture toughness of C-S-H grains. We found that the fracture toughness of C-S-H grains increases with the size of the grains, but decreases with increasing temperature. Our results also showed that the presence of defects, such as voids and cracks, significantly decreases the fracture toughness of C-S-H grains. Concrete is a widely used construction material, and its mechanical properties are largely determined by the microstructure of the material. Calcium-silicate-hydrate (C-S-H) is the main binder phase in concrete, and its mechanical properties have a significant impact on the overall strength and toughness of the material. However, the mechanical behavior of C-S-H is not well understood, due in part to its complex microstructure and chemical composition.

In order to better understand the mechanical properties of C-S-H, it is important to study the fracture toughness of C-S-H grains. Fracture toughness is a measure of the resistance of a material to crack propagation, and it is an important factor in determining the overall strength and durability of a material. In this study, we used molecular dynamics simulations to investigate the fracture toughness of C-S-H grains. Our results show that the fracture toughness of C-S-H grains increases with the size of the grains, but decreases with increasing temperature. We also found that the presence of defects, such as voids and cracks, significantly decreases the fracture toughness of C-S-H grains. These findings provide valuable insights into the mechanical behavior of C-S-H and have implications for the design and performance of concrete structures.

Seismicity, or the occurrence of earthquakes, is a phenomenon that is typically associated with the Earth's lithosphere. However, recent studies have shown that seismicity can also occur in sheared granular matter, such as sandpiles and granular beds. This type of seismicity is known as granular seismicity. In this review, we will discuss the mechanisms behind granular seismicity and how it is related to the shear strength and deformation of granular matter. We will also discuss the potential applications of this phenomenon in fields such as geophysics and civil engineering. Granular materials are composed of a large number of particles that are typically held together by friction and interparticle forces. These materials can be found in various forms, such as sand, gravel, and grains. When granular materials are subjected to shear forces, they can deform and exhibit a range of mechanical behaviors, including compaction, dilatancy, and fluidization.

Recent studies have shown that granular materials can also exhibit seismicity, or the occurrence of earthquakes, under certain conditions. This type of seismicity is known as granular seismicity and has been observed in various granular systems, such as sandpiles and granular beds.

Granular seismicity is distinct from lithospheric seismicity in several ways. For example, granular seismicity typically occurs at much lower strain rates and at smaller magnitudes compared to lithospheric seismicity. Additionally, granular seismicity is more sensitive to the type and size of the granular particles, as well as the properties of the surrounding material. In summary, granular seismicity is a phenomenon that occurs in sheared granular matter and is characterized by the occurrence of earthquakes. It is distinct from lithospheric seismicity in terms of the strain rates, magnitudes, and sensitivity to material properties. Further research on granular seismicity has the potential to provide insights into the mechanical behavior of granular materials and has potential applications in fields such as geophysics and civil engineering.

Colloids are suspensions of small particles in a fluid medium, and they have been widely studied for their unique physical properties and potential applications in fields such as drug delivery and biotechnology. In this review, we focus on the dynamics of colloidal particles in light fields, specifically in the context of random and periodic energy landscapes. We discuss the various mechanisms by which colloidal particles can be manipulated in these landscapes, including optical trapping, holographic tweezing, and opto-thermal gradients. We also review recent advances in the use of machine learning algorithms to optimize and control the motion of colloidal particles in light fields. Colloidal particles have long been of interest due to their unique physical properties, which are intermediate between those of atoms and molecules on the one hand, and macroscopic objects on the other. Colloidal particles are typically in the nanometer to micrometer size range and exhibit Brownian motion, which makes them ideal for studying a wide range of phenomena at the mesoscale. In recent years, the ability to manipulate colloidal particles using light fields has attracted significant attention, due to the potential for precise control and the ability to study the underlying physical mechanisms.

One key aspect of colloidal particle dynamics in light fields is the presence of energy landscapes, which can either be random or periodic. Random energy landscapes can arise from the presence of disordered structures, such as random optical potentials, while periodic energy landscapes can be created using holographic or other structured light fields. These energy landscapes can significantly influence the motion of colloidal particles, leading to a range of interesting and complex behaviors.

Several techniques have been developed for manipulating colloidal particles in light fields, including optical trapping, holographic tweezing, and opto-thermal gradients. Optical trapping, also known as optical tweezers, uses focused laser beams to exert forces on colloidal particles, allowing for precise manipulation and control. Holographic tweezing uses a computer-generated hologram to create complex and customizable optical potentials for colloidal particles. Opto-thermal gradients, on the other hand, utilize temperature gradients induced by light absorption to drive the motion of colloidal particles.

In addition to these traditional techniques, machine learning algorithms have also been applied to optimize and control the motion of colloidal particles in light fields. Machine learning techniques have the potential to significantly improve the accuracy and efficiency of colloidal particle manipulation, and have already been demonstrated to be effective in a range of applications. Colloidal particles in light fields exhibit a rich range of behaviors that are influenced by the underlying energy landscape. The ability to manipulate and control these behaviors using techniques such as optical trapping, holographic tweezing, and opto-thermal gradients has significant potential for a wide range of applications. Machine learning algorithms are also beginning to play a role in optimizing and controlling the motion of colloidal particles in light fields. Further research in this area is likely to lead to significant advances in our understanding of colloidal particle dynamics and the development of new technologies based on these principles.

In this paper, we investigate the phases, transitions, and patterns in the one-dimensional extended Bose-Hubbard model. The extended Bose-Hubbard model is a generalization of the standard Bose-Hubbard model, which describes the behavior of bosons in a lattice potential. By adding additional terms to the Hamiltonian, the extended Bose-Hubbard model is able to capture more complex behavior, including the formation of supersolid phases. We use numerical simulations to study the phase diagram of the model and identify the various phases and transitions that occur. We also discuss the formation of patterns in the system, including the emergence of solitons and density waves. The Bose-Hubbard model is a fundamental model in the field of condensed matter physics, used to describe the behavior of bosons in a lattice potential. The model consists of a set of bosonic particles interacting with each other through an on-site repulsion, as well as a hopping term that allows the particles to move between different lattice sites. The competition between these two terms leads to a rich phase diagram, with a variety of phases including a Mott insulating phase, a superfluid phase, and a supersolid phase.

The extended Bose-Hubbard model is a generalization of the standard Bose-Hubbard model that includes additional terms in the Hamiltonian. These additional terms can capture more complex behavior, such as the formation of supersolid phases, which are characterized by the coexistence of both superfluid and Mott insulating behavior. In this paper, we use numerical simulations to study the phase diagram of the one-dimensional extended Bose-Hubbard model and identify the various phases and transitions that occur. In this paper, we have studied the phases, transitions, and patterns in the one-dimensional extended Bose-Hubbard model. We have used numerical simulations to investigate the phase diagram of the model and identify the various phases and transitions that occur. We have also discussed the formation of patterns in the system, including the emergence of solitons and density waves. Our results provide insight into the behavior of bosons in a lattice potential and may have applications in the design of quantum materials and devices.

In this paper, we explore the role of gauge invariance in nonequilibrium thermodynamics, specifically in the context of geometric phases. We show that the gauge invariance of a system can lead to the emergence of nontrivial geometric phases, even in the absence of external fields. These phases can have significant consequences for the thermodynamic properties of the system, such as its heat capacity and entropy. We conclude with a discussion of the potential applications of these results in the field of nonequilibrium thermodynamics. Gauge invariance is a fundamental symmetry principle that is prevalent in many areas of physics, including electromagnetism, quantum mechanics, and thermodynamics. It dictates that the physical properties of a system should be independent of the choice of gauge, or reference frame, used to describe it. This principle has led to numerous important discoveries, such as the existence of electromagnetic waves and the prediction of the Higgs boson.

In nonequilibrium thermodynamics, gauge invariance plays a particularly important role in the study of geometric phases. Geometric phases, also known as Berry phases, are topological phases that arise due to the adiabatic evolution of a system. They are characterized by a change in the geometric properties of the system, such as its curvature or phase space volume, and are often associated with the presence of external fields.

However, recent studies have shown that gauge invariance can also lead to the emergence of nontrivial geometric phases in the absence of external fields. These phases, known as gauge geometric phases, are due to the gauge invariance of the system and can have significant consequences for its thermodynamic properties. In this paper, we have discussed the role of gauge invariance in nonequilibrium thermodynamics, specifically in the context of geometric phases. We have shown that gauge invariance can lead to the emergence of nontrivial geometric phases, even in the absence of external fields. These phases can have significant consequences for the thermodynamic properties of the system, such as its heat capacity and entropy. Further research in this area has the potential to uncover new insights into the fundamental principles of nonequilibrium thermodynamics and to inform the design of new materials and technologies.

The author studies infinite ternary sequences related to the Fibonacci tree. As in the classical case, these sequences exhibit an isoperimetric feature: there is equality among the sums of consecutive terms. When it holds the isoperimetric sequence has a self-similar structure which will be studied in terms of the well-known Fibonacci sequence. It will be shown that every isoperimetric sequence for the Fibonacci tree is equal to the sum, over all terms, of a decreasing sequence of simple functions whose arguments are the Fibonacci sequence. In particular, this means that the sequences themselves are given by sums of simple terms and sequences of simple functions, the latter of which are given by a well-known sequence of sums of pairs of elements of the Fibonacci family. The result is applied to Fibonacci-like sequences related to the meta-Fibonacci tree and to binary partitions whose entries are signed natural numbers. The latter problem is also related to a recently introduced infinite family of infinite binary sequences. The aim of this paper is to study isoperimetric sequences for infinite complete binary trees. In the classical case, a sequence $a_0,a_1,a_2,\ldots$ is said to be isoperimetric if $$a_n+a_{n+1}+a_{n+2}+\cdots=\sum_{k=1}^n a_ka_{n+k}$$ for every $n\in\mathbb{N}$. For instance, the sequence $1,2,4,7,13,22,36,55,\ldots$ is isoperimetric. The sequences of the Fibonacci and Lucas families are well known to be isoperimetric. It is well known that the Fibonacci sequence is isoperimetric, and that the Lucas sequence is isoperimetric if and only if the ratio $\frac{\phi}{\sqrt{5}}$ is a Pisot number, where $\phi$ is the golden ratio.    The study of isoperimetric sequences for infinite trees has been initiated in [1] by the author. It is shown there that the sequence of the $n$-th level of the infinite Fibonacci tree is isoperimetric. The study of isoperimetric sequences for infinite complete binary trees has been initiated in [2] by the author and his collaborators. In this case, the sequence of the $n$-level of the infinite tree is isoperimetric if and only if the sequence of the $n$-levels of the Fibonacci tree is isoperimetric. This implies that the sequence of the $n$th level of the infinite binary tree is isoperimetric if and only if $\frac{\phi}{2}$ is a Pisot number, where the golden ratio $\phi$ is the unique positive real root of the polynomial $x^2-x-1$. It is worth mentioning that the study of isoperimetric sequences for infinite complete trees has been initiated by the author and his collaborators in [3].    In this paper, we study the structure of isoperimetric sequences for infinite complete binary tree. In particular, it is shown that the isoperimetric sequences are given by sums of simple functions whose arguments are the Fibonacci sequence, and that the sequence of the $n$level of the infinite binary tree is isoperimetric for every $n\in\mathbb N$ if and only if the sequence of the "$n$-th level of the meta-Fibonacci tree" is isoperimetric. This implies that the isoperimetric sequences for the meta-Fibonacci tree are given by sums of simple functions whose arguments belong to the set of the $n$-th levels of the meta-Fibonacci tree, for every $n\in\mathbb Z^+$. In the case of binary partitions, it is shown that the isoperimetric sequence for the $n$-th level of a binary partition is given by the sum, over all terms, of a simple function whose argument is the $n$-th term of the Fibonacci sequence. In the case of signed almost binary partitions, it is shown that the isperimetric sequence for the $n$-level of a signed almost binary partition is given by the sum, over the terms, of a decreasing sequence of simple functions. The result is applied to Fibonacci-like sequences and binary partitions whose entries are signed natural numbers. In the latter case, the isoperimetric sequence is given by the sum, over the terms of the sequence, of a decreasing sequence of simple functions whose argument is the Fibonacci sequence. This is also related to a recently introduced infinite family  of infinite binary sequences.

The main goal of a scenario forecasting study is to make a forecast on the outcome of a new project, typically one which is beyond the existing portfolio of investments under consideration. These predictions can be used by investment managers to determine the most advantageous portfolio for a new project in an environment characterized by different values for different parameters. However, traditional statistical models, such as regression and decision trees (DTs), often suffer from several practical downsides in the scenarios forecast setting, and often fail in the presence of noisy or missing data in the available inputs. This paper explores whether an unsupervised deep learning approach, which relies on a generative adversarial network (GAN), can be used as a practical alternative to overcome these practical barriers in modeling scenarios forecasts. The proposed approach incorporates the following contributions: i) a novel architecture for deep learning scenario forecasts, ii) a new method for model selection using a conditional version of a model, and iii) a novel method for feature selection using deep learning. For the task of modeling scenarios forecasts, the proposed model approach significantly outperforms traditional statistical methods, particularly when the number and the quality of the input features (characteristics of the new project) vary over time and across sites, such as what may be the case for the financial and environmental risks to newly build projects. Scenario forecasts are used to predict the outcomes of future investment projects, typically those which are beyond the existing portfolio of investments under consideration. The main goal of a scenario forecasting study is therefore to make a forecast on the outcome of a newly constructed investment project. These predictions can be used by investment managers in order to determine the most advantageous portfolio for a new investment project in an environment characterized by different values for the parameters of the investment. However, traditional statistical models, such as decision trees (DTs) and regression, often suffer from several practical downsides when applied to scenarios forecast. For example, DTs are often unable to handle noisy or missing data in the available inputs (characteristics of the new investment project), and can be prone to overfitting when there are a large number of input features (characteristics of the new investment project under consideration). Regression models often struggle to capture nonlinear interactions between the input features and the outcome, such as when the outcome of an investment project is a function of the parameters of the investment project and the characteristics of the environment in which the investment project is being constructed. We propose a novel approach for modeling scenarios forecasts based on a generative adversarial network (GAN). The proposed approach incorporates the following contributions:   1. A novel architecture for deep learning scenario forecasts that is based on the generative adversarial network (GAN).   2. A novel method for model selection using a conditional version of the model.   3. A novel method for feature selection using deep learning.    The proposed approach significantly outperforms traditional statistical methods, particularly when there are a large number of input features, and when the input features vary over time and across sites, such as when the financial and environmental risks to a newly constructed investment project are being assessed. Quantum phase transitions are usually induced by pressure and magnetic fields, but the direct connection between the two external stimuli has remained elusive so far, to the best of our knowledge. Here, we report the pressure-induced quantum phase transitions of topological insulator YbB 6 under pressure. A clear quantum critical behavior is discovered, characterized by the rapid suppression of the bulk quantum oscillations by 1.4 GPa and the evolution of the Fermi surface with increasing pressure. Specifically, a small hole pocket evolves into a large electron pocket at 1.6 GPa and the latter evolves into a small pocket around 2.1 GPa. The evolution of hole pockets at low pressure provides a direct evidence that the chemical pressure could tune the Fermi surface topology of a topological insulator and can be utilized to control the electrical transport properties of topological insulator based spintronic devices for realizing quantum computers. - Quantum phase transitions (QPTs) are the phenomena where the ground state of a quantum system changes as a function of the external control parameters, such as temperature, magnetic field, and pressure.  In condensed matter physics, QPTs can be generally classified into two types, namely, quantum-critical and quantum-discontinuous, depending on the behavior of the critical point. The QPTs in topological insulators (TIs) are the most intriguing examples of quantum-critical QPTs [1].  The discovery of TIs has opened a new avenue for the study of QPTs. TIs are the quantum materials with topologically protected surface states, which can be manipulated by an external magnetic field or pressure. The quantum Hall effect (QHE) and quantum anomalous Hall effect (QAHE) have been observed in TIs [2, 3]. The surface states of TIs have been demonstrated to be sensitive to external perturbations, such as an external magnetic field and pressure [4, 5, 6, 7]. The application of pressure can effectively tune the Fermi surface of TIs and control the electrical transport properties of TIs based spintronic devices for realizing quantum computers [8, 9, 10, 11].  In this letter, we report the pressure-induced quantum phase transition of a TIs YbB \({}_{6}\). The pressure-induced QPT is characterized by the rapid suppression of the bulk quantum oscillation (BO) by 1.4 GPa and the evolution with increasing pressure of the Fermi surface. The evolution of hole pockets at low pressure is the first direct evidence that the chemical pressure could tune the topology of the Fermi surface of a TI and can be utilized to control the transport properties of TIs based spintronic device for realizing quantum computers. - Pressure-induced QPTs of a TIs YbB \(\mathrm{{}_{6}}\) have been investigated. The pressure-induced QPT is characterized the rapid suppression of the bulk quantum oscillation by 1.4 GPa and the evolution by increasing pressure of the Fermi surface. The evolution at low pressure provides a direct evidence that the pressure could tune the Fermi surface topology of a TI and can be utilized to control transport properties of TIs based spintronic  devices for realizing quantum computers.     Methods:    Synthesis:     YbB \({}_{\mathrm{6}}\) single crystals were grown by the high-temperature solution growth method, as described in Ref. [12].    Pressure-induced quantum phase transitions:     Pressure-dependent resistivity was measured on a diamond anvil cell (DAC) at room temperature. The pressure was calibrated by the ruby fluorescence method. The electrical resistivity was measured with the standard four-probe method using a standard low-temperature measurement system (Quantum Design, PPMS). The pressure-induced quantum phase transitions were investigated by measuring the resistance in a magnetic field applied parallel to the surface of the sample. The quantum oscillation measurements were performed in a magnetic field applied perpendicular to the surface of the sample using a standard 3D magnet. Physical-layer network coding (PLNC) has been shown to be a promising technique for improving the performance of wireless networks. In PLNC, the network nodes perform linear operations on the transmitted signals, rather than on the data itself. This allows for the creation of new linear combinations of the transmitted signals, which can be used to increase the diversity and reliability of the communication. However, the decoding of PLNC signals can be challenging, especially in the presence of noise and interference. In this paper, we propose a neural network aided decoding approach for PLNC random access, which leverages the power of deep learning to improve the performance of PLNC decoding. Our simulation results show that the proposed approach is able to significantly improve the decoding performance, especially in the low signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) regime. Wireless communication systems are an essential part of modern society, providing the infrastructure for a wide range of applications, from mobile phones and Internet of Things (IoT) devices to wireless sensor networks and vehicular communication. One of the main challenges in wireless communication is to improve the reliability and efficiency of the communication, especially in the presence of noise, interference, and limited resources.

Our simulation results show that the proposed approach is able to significantly improve the decoding performance, especially in the low signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) regime. This suggests that the proposed approach has the potential to be a useful tool for improving the performance of PLNC systems in real-world scenarios. Further work is needed to validate the performance of the proposed approach in more realistic environments and to explore its potential for other types of PLNC systems.

Given certain classes of mirror pairs we will define and use two kinds of tropical objects: tropical and log corals. By mirror symmetry we mean a correspondence between the geometry underlying the two kinds of tropical objects and the behaviour of the holomorphic symplectic form and the holomorphic Euler class. We will use this correspondence to prove that the tropical curve is the dual corolla of the log curve associated to a Fano variety and prove that the tropical curve is a dual Newton polytope. The main result of this paper is to use mirror symmetry to prove that the tropical curve is the dual Newton polytope of the log curve associated to a Fano variety.    The first part of the paper is devoted to the definition of two kinds of tropical objects: tropical and log corollas. A tropical corolla is a finite graph, which we will denote $G$, with a set of vertices $V(G)$ and a set of edges $E(G)$ such that every vertex has degree at least 3.    The second part of the paper is devoted to the definition and study of two kinds of log corollas: log corollas and log tropical corollas. A log corolla is a finite graph, which we denote $G$, with a set $V(G)$ of vertices, a set $E(G)$ of edges and a set $F(G)$ of faces such that every vertex has degree at least 2. We will prove that the tropical curve is the dual Newton polygon of the log curve associated to a Fano variety (Theorem 4.4) and that the tropical curve is a dual corolla of the log curve associated to an elliptic curve (Theorem 4.13). We study regularity of the extremal solution for a fourth-order elliptic problem with a singular nonlinearity in Ω, Ω⊂R3. Our analysis is based on the topological degree of a nonlinear Fredholm equation associated to the minimal growth problem. In order to prove existence of a least energy solution and regularity results we develop an inverse estimate (Poincare’s inequality) for the extremal solution under a growth condition satisfied by the nonlinearity. Let Ω be a bounded domain of R3 with smooth boundary ∂Ω. Consider the fourth-order problem $$\left\{ \begin{array}{ll} -\Delta^2 u + u = \lambda |u|^{p-2}u & \mbox{in} \Omega \\ u = \Delta u = 0 & \mbox{in} ~ \Omega^c \end{array}\right. $$   where λ is a positive parameter and p>3 is a subcritical exponent. The problem is well known to have a least energy solution which is the extremal solution of the variational problem $$\lambda = \inf \left\{ \int_{\Omega} |\nabla u|^2 + |u|^{p} \; \; : \; \; u \in H^2(\Omega) \cap H_0^1(\Omega) \right\} $$   see e.g. [1]. In this paper we are interested in regularity results for this solution. We prove that the extremal solution is smooth in Ω if p>3+2/N, with N the dimension of the space. This result is new even for the case when p=3. In fact, the regularity result for the extremal solution of the p=3 case is known for a long time, see e.g. [2] and references therein. We have obtained a new regularity result for the extremal solution of the fourth-order problem $$\left( -\Delta^2 + 1 \right) u = \lambda |u^+|^{p-2}u^+ \quad \mbox{in} \; \; \Omega $$   with p>3. The regularity result holds under the weakest growth condition on the nonlinearity. We expect that our result can be extended to other fourth-order problems.

We propose to use the generalised superscalar scheme for the Kerr-Sterling system, and we show that the Kerr-Sterling system can be efficiently and accurately generalized. The generalised superscalar scheme can be generalized to any of the superposed $k$-body systems with mass control, and to any of the superscalar generalized $k$-body systems with a single-body mass. The generalised superscalar scheme can be applied to the Kerr-Sterling system by means of a Monte Carlo Monte Carlo algorithm. The system is also known as the Heisenberg system. In this paper, we will use the Heisenberg system as a generalised supersymmetric superintegrable supersymmetric system with logarithmic sensitivity and a constant radius of 1.0. In Sec. Discrete line segments are geometric patterns whose intersections with a hyperplane are discrete lines. We define a hypergraph H as discrete line segment hypergraph if every vertex of H is the intersection of an odd number of discrete lines with a fixed hyperplane. We give the intersection graph of such a hypergraph in an additive way. We determine the largest number of vertices of H that can be simultaneously contained in a simplex and show that such a number always divides the dimension. Some applications, in particular the proof of the Erdős–Faber–Lovász bound for hyperplane packing numbers of convex polytopes, to hyperplane contact numbers and the number of vertices of maximal convex polytope, are discussed. © 2004 Wiley Periodicals, Inc. J Graph Theory 41: 65–95, 2005. In this paper we study the intersection graph of discrete line segment hypergraphs. We define a hypergraph H as discrete line segment hypergraph if every vertex of H is the intersection of an odd number of discrete lines with a fixed hyperplane. We give the intersection graph of such a hypergraph in an additive way. We determine the largest number of vertices of H that can be simultaneously contained in a simplex and show that such a number always divides the dimension. We also determine the largest number of vertices of H with at least two edges that can be simultaneously contained in a simplex. (1) For the intersection graph of a discrete line segment hypergraph, we show that the largest number of vertices that can be simultaneously contained in a simplex is \({\binom {d+1}{2}}\) and the largest number of vertices with at least two edges that can be simultaneously in a simplex is \({\frac {d(d+1)}{2}}\).   (2) The intersection graph of a discrete line segment hypergraph has the maximum degree at most \(d-1\).   (3) The intersection graph of a discrete line segment hypergraph has the maximum clique number at most \(d+1\).   (4) We give some applications, in particular the proof of the Erdös–Faber–Lovász bound for the hyperplane packing numbers of convex polytopes, to the hyperplane contact numbers and the number of vertices of the maximal convex polytope.

In this paper we study moduli schemes of sheaves on curves, principally of Picard rank one. The general principle is that an interesting geometry can be captured in the simple case where the Picard rank of the curve is equal to the rank of the vector space of sections of the moduli sheaf. We show - using this strategy - how this works e.g. for torsion-free sheaves on a elliptic curve, and further we show how Mumford's method can be extended to a more general context, and to higher Picard ranks. The main goal of this paper is to study moduli schemes of sheaves on curves, principally with Picard rank one. The general principle is that the geometry of the moduli scheme can be captured in the simple case where the rank of the vector space of sections of a moduli sheaf is equal to the Picard rank of the curve. We illustrate this principle in the case of torsion-free sheaves on an elliptic curve, and further we show how Mumfords' method can be extended to a more general context. The paper is organized as follows:    Chapter 1:    The main results of this chapter are:    Section 1.1:    We show that the moduli space of torsion-free sheaves on an elliptic curve of rank $r$ and degree $d$ is isomorphic to the product $\mathcal{H}_{r,d} \times \mathcal{H}_{r,0}$, where $\mathcal{H}_{n,d}$ is the moduli space of rank $n$ degree $d$ torsion-free sheaves on $\mathbb{P}^1$.    Section 1.2:    We show that the moduli scheme of torsion-free sheaves on a curve of Picard rank one is an open subscheme of a product of moduli schemes of sheaves on the projective line.    Chapter 2:    We show how Mumford's method can be adapted to a more general context, and to higher rank.    Chapter 3:    We give a number of examples which illustrate the main results of the paper.

Theoretical Studies of the Fermi-LAT InterferometerIn this paper, we have studied the Fermi-LAT interferometer (FLL) and its theoretical properties in the context of the measurement of the Fermi-LAT field at the Tevatron. We have presented a theoretical analysis of the FLL, and its potential as a testbed for future experimental experiments. We have shown that the FLL is able to reproduce the measured fields of the Fermi-LAT field with a very high accuracy, which is significantly better than the FLL obtained by the same measurement method. In this paper, we examine the effect of form dominance on the CP asymmetry in leptogenesis. Leptogenesis is a proposed mechanism for the generation of the observed matter-antimatter asymmetry in the universe, which involves the decay of heavy Majorana neutrinos. The CP asymmetry, which is a measure of the violation of the combined symmetry of charge conjugation and parity, plays a crucial role in the generation of this asymmetry. We show that under certain conditions, the CP asymmetry can vanish in leptogenesis due to the dominance of form factors. Leptogenesis is a mechanism by which the observed matter-antimatter asymmetry in the universe may have been generated. According to the standard model of particle physics, the total amount of matter and antimatter present in the universe should be equal. However, observations indicate that there is a slight excess of matter over antimatter. One possible explanation for this is that a small asymmetry was generated in the early universe and has been preserved until the present day.

One proposed mechanism for generating this asymmetry is leptogenesis, which involves the decay of heavy Majorana neutrinos. These neutrinos can decay into lighter particles, such as leptons and Higgs bosons, and the resulting asymmetry in the number of leptons and antileptons can be converted into an asymmetry in the number of baryons and antibaryons through the process of baryogenesis.

One important factor in leptogenesis is the CP asymmetry, which is a measure of the violation of the combined symmetry of charge conjugation and parity. This asymmetry arises due to the interference between the tree-level decay of the heavy Majorana neutrinos and the one-loop diagrams that contribute to their decay. The CP asymmetry is therefore sensitive to the form factors of the heavy Majorana neutrinos, which describe the strength of their coupling to lighter particles.

In this paper, we examine the effect of form dominance on the CP asymmetry in leptogenesis. We show that under certain conditions, the CP asymmetry can vanish due to the dominance of form factors. In summary, we have shown that the CP asymmetry in leptogenesis can vanish due to the dominance of form factors. This result has important implications for the generation of the observed matter-antimatter asymmetry in the universe, and may provide new insights into the mechanisms behind leptogenesis. Further research is needed to fully understand the role of form dominance in leptogenesis and its impact on the generation of the matter-antimatter asymmetry.

The emergence of relativistic phenomena provides a significant step towards practical applications to solid-solid, solid-liquid, and solid-gas interfaces with strong electron correlations. So, a detailed study on the fundamental properties is of utmost interest in Dirac materials. Here, we report the giant nonsaturating magnetoresistance (MR) in ZrSiS with a non-magnetic single crystal in a wide temperature range including the weak magnetic field regime. At 8 T, the MR exhibits the pronounced maximum at ∼ 3 K and it changes with decreasing temperature. By increasing the external magnetic field above 8 T, the MR reduces sharply for temperatures above ∼ 5 K and vanishes in the ordered state with a value of ∼ 1000 Ω%, which increases to 4000 Ω% at 50 K. Such giant MR is a hallmark of a 3D metal in the presence of a strong Rashba-type spin-orbital coupling. However, the observation of Fermi level crossing is not possible. The magnetoresistance is sensitive to the angle between the magnetic field and the sample current. This MR response suggests the spin-orbit coupling in this material is mainly due to the non-degenerate  Dirac points. The results thus point towards a novel realization of topological phase in ZrSiS. -  Relativistic phenomena are ubiquitous in solid-state physics.   -  The relativistic electrons are described by the Dirac equation. Here, we report the giant nonsaturating MR in ZrSiS with a non-magnetic ZrSiS single crystal in a wide temperature range including the weakly magnetic field regime. At 8 T, it exhibits the pronounced maximum at ∼ 3 K, which changes with decreasing temperature. The MR reduces sharply for temperatures above ∼ 5-7 K and vanishes in the ordered state with a magnitude of ∼ 1000 Ω%. This MR response suggests the spin-orbit interaction in this material is mainly due to the noncentrosymmetric  Dirac points. -  The nonsaturating MR response in ZrSiS is a signature of the non-degenerate  Dirac points.   -  The nonsaturating MR in ZrSiS is a hallmark of a 3D metal with a strong Rashba-type spin-orbital coupling in the presence of a strong non-centrosymmetric spin-orbit coupling.   -  The nonsaturating magnetoresistance in ZrSiS is sensitive to the angle between the magnetic field direction and the sample current. In this paper, we derive new stabilization bounds for linear finite dynamical systems. These bounds provide a measure of the rate at which the system converges to its equilibrium state, and can be used to design controllers that stabilize the system. We demonstrate the usefulness of these bounds through several numerical examples. The study of stability and stabilization is a central topic in the field of control theory. In particular, the study of linear finite dynamical systems has received significant attention due to their widespread use in modeling and control of a variety of systems. One important aspect of stability is the rate at which the system converges to its equilibrium state, which is often quantified through the use of stabilization bounds. In this paper, we derive new stabilization bounds for linear finite dynamical systems, and demonstrate their usefulness through several numerical examples. In this paper, we have derived new stabilization bounds for linear finite dynamical systems. These bounds provide a measure of the rate at which the system converges to its equilibrium state, and can be used to design controllers that stabilize the system. We have demonstrated the usefulness of these bounds through several numerical examples, and believe that they will be a valuable tool for researchers and practitioners working in the field of control theory.

Few-layer graphene (FLG) has attracted significant attention due to its unique electrical and optical properties. In particular, the optical conductivity of FLG can be greatly enhanced by the presence of surface states, which are electronic states localized at the surface of the material. In this work, we study the effect of surface states on the optical conductivity of ABC-stacked FLG using a combination of experimental and theoretical techniques. Our results show that the presence of surface states leads to a significant increase in the optical conductivity of FLG, particularly in the visible and near-infrared range. These findings have important implications for the use of FLG in optoelectronic devices, such as solar cells and photodetectors. Graphene, a single layer of carbon atoms arranged in a hexagonal lattice, has garnered much attention due to its unique properties, including high electrical conductivity, high strength, and exceptional optical transparency. Few-layer graphene (FLG), which consists of a few layers of graphene stacked on top of each other, also exhibits interesting properties and has potential applications in various fields, including electronics, optoelectronics, and energy storage.

One of the key factors determining the optical and electrical properties of FLG is the stacking arrangement of the layers. Among the various stacking arrangements, ABC-stacked FLG has attracted particular interest due to its unique band structure and optical properties. In particular, ABC-stacked FLG exhibits surface states, which are electronic states localized at the surface of the material. These surface states have been shown to have a strong effect on the electronic and optical properties of ABC-stacked FLG.

In this work, we investigate the effect of surface states on the optical conductivity of ABC-stacked FLG using a combination of experimental and theoretical techniques. Our results show that the presence of surface states leads to a significant increase in the optical conductivity of FLG, particularly in the visible and near-infrared range. In conclusion, we have demonstrated that the presence of surface states in ABC-stacked FLG leads to a significant enhancement of the optical conductivity of the material, particularly in the visible and near-infrared range. These findings have important implications for the use of FLG in optoelectronic devices, such as solar cells and photodetectors. Further research is needed to fully understand the role of surface states in the optical and electronic properties of FLG and to develop new approaches for controlling and manipulating these states for practical applications.

The propagation of gap solitons with linear interconversion in two-component nonlinear media is studied. It is shown that a linear interconversion can effectively create a spatial phase gradient and thus form an effective potential for solitons. Consequently, the two-component gap solitons can propagate in both the repulsive or the attractive gap potential. The existence of gap solitons in two-component nonlinear media has been widely discussed in recent years [1-10]. The gap solitons can be regarded as a special type of solitons, which can exist in the gap of the linear spectrum of the media. They can also be viewed as a special case of the dark solitons in two-component nonlinear media [11, 12]. In the past few years, the existence of gap solitons in two-component nonlinear materials has been extensively studied. The gap solitons in two-component nonlinear materials can be realized in a variety of materials, such as, photonic crystals [13-16], optical fibers [17-19], and Bose-Einstein condensates [20-23].    The interconversion between the components in the two-component nonlinear media is a natural phenomenon. It is found that the interconversion can play an important role in the properties of gap solitons in the two-component nonlinear media [24-26]. For example, the interconversion can change the propagation direction of the gap solitons in the two-component nonlinear media, and can change the number of the gap solitons in the two-component media [24-26], etc. In this paper, we consider the propagation of gap solitons with linear interconversion in the two-component nonlinear media. It is shown that a linear interconversion between the components in the two-dimensional nonlinear media can create a spatial phase gradient and thus form an effecive potential for the solitons. Consequently, the two-component gap soliton can propagate in both the repulsive or the attractive potential. The two-component gap soliton can propagate in both attractive and repulsive potentials. This is different from the case of the gap soliton in the single-component nonlinear media, where the gap solitons can only propagate in the attractive potential [27-29].

The distributed alternating direction method of multipliers (DADMM) has been introduced to solve large-scale optimization problems with many constraints. This method takes into account both the equality and inequality constraints simultaneously. Hence, DADMM is more effective than the conventional centralized alternating direction method of multipliers. Although recent works have provided useful theoretical results on the analysis of DADMM via linear theory and matrix algebra, convergence rate of DADMM when solving separable objective functions with linear constraints has not yet been analyzed in detail. This paper studies the convergence rate of DADMM when solving an unconstrained convex-concave optimization problem with linear constraints. We show that the iteration complexity is an explicit function of a certain spectral gap of DADMM. In addition, the analysis implies that by increasing the ratio of the number of equality constraints compared to the number of inequality constraints, the convergence rate of DADMM can be lowered. \begin{abstract}     Distributed optimization problems with equality and inequality constraints are ubiquitous in many fields. However, due to the communication constraint, it is difficult to solve these problems in a distributed manner. In this paper, we consider a distributed alternating direction method of multipliers (D-ADMM), which is a distributed version of the alternating direction method of multipliers (ADMM). D-ADMM takes into account both equality and inequality constraints simultaneously. Hence, D-ADMM is more effective than the conventional centralized ADMM. The D-ADMM algorithm is a distributed algorithm in which each node only needs to exchange information with its neighbors. The convergence of D-ADMM has been studied in [1]-[3].     The main result in [2] is a convergence rate of D-ADMM for solving separable convex-concave optimization problems with linear constraints. However, it is unclear how to use this result to analyze the convergence of D-ADMM for solving general convex-concave optimization problems with linear constraints.     In this paper, we study the convergence rate of D-ADMM for solving an unconstrained convex-concave optimization problem with $m$ linear constraints. We show that the iteration complexity of D-ADMM is an explicit function of the spectral gap of the matrix in the linear constraints. The analysis implies that by increasing the ratio of the numbers of equality constraints compared to the number of inequality constraint, the convergence rate of D-ADMM can be lowered.   \end{abstract} \end{document}. \begin{abstract}     In this paper, we study the convergence rate and the explicit iteration complexity of D-ADMM for solving a general convex-concave optimization problem with linear constraints. The analysis implies that by increasing   the ratio of the number of equality constraints compared   to the number of inequality constraints, the convergence   rate of D-ADMM can be lowered and the iteration complexity   of D-ADMM can be reduced.   \end{abstract}.

In this work, we study the meson mass splittings in unquenched quark models using the EEF70 potential. We find that the inclusion of quark-antiquark pair creation and annihilation effects leads to significant modifications in the meson mass spectrum. The mass splittings between different meson states are found to be in good agreement with experimental measurements. Mesons are bound states of a quark and an antiquark, and play a crucial role in our understanding of the strong interaction. The masses of mesons are an important observable that can provide insight into the dynamics of the underlying quark-gluon theory. However, the theoretical calculation of meson masses is a challenging task due to the non-perturbative nature of the strong interaction.

One approach to calculate meson masses is through the use of quark models, which aim to describe the strong interaction in terms of the motion of constituent quarks. In these models, the quark-quark interaction is typically described by a potential, which can be either phenomenological or derived from first principles.

In recent years, there has been growing interest in studying the effects of quark-antiquark pair creation and annihilation on meson masses. These so-called "unquenched" quark models go beyond the simple quark-antiquark picture by allowing for the creation and annihilation of quark-antiquark pairs. The inclusion of these effects is important for obtaining more accurate predictions for meson masses, particularly for heavy quark systems.

In this work, we use the EEF70 potential, a commonly-used phenomenological potential, to study meson mass splittings in unquenched quark models. The EEF70 potential is a non-relativistic potential that includes both linear and Coulomb terms, and has been successful in reproducing many features of the meson spectrum. We have studied the meson mass splittings in unquenched quark models using the EEF70 potential. Our results show that the inclusion of quark-antiquark pair creation and annihilation effects leads to significant modifications in the meson mass spectrum. The mass splittings between different meson states are found to be in good agreement with experimental measurements. These findings demonstrate the importance of including quark-antiquark pair creation and annihilation effects in the calculation of meson masses.

The study of ordering dynamics in complex systems has been a topic of interest in recent years due to its potential applications in fields such as materials science and biology. In this work, we examine the role of anomalous lifetime distributions and topological traps in the ordering process. Using Monte Carlo simulations, we demonstrate the presence of long-lived states that can significantly affect the overall ordering dynamics. Additionally, we show that the presence of topological traps in the system can lead to slow relaxation and non-exponential decay of the order parameter. Our results highlight the importance of considering the influence of these factors in the understanding and prediction of ordering dynamics in complex systems. The ability of a system to spontaneously form ordered structures is a phenomenon that occurs in a wide range of systems, from materials to biological systems. The process of ordering, or self-organization, is driven by the competition between various energetic and entropic factors, and can result in the emergence of complex patterns and structures. In many cases, the ordering process is accompanied by a change in the system's thermodynamic state, such as a phase transition.

One area of interest in the study of ordering dynamics is the role of anomalous lifetime distributions. In some systems, the lifetime of a given state or configuration can exhibit non-exponential behavior, leading to the presence of long-lived states or "traps" that can significantly affect the overall ordering process. These traps can act as barriers to the system's evolution, hindering the formation of the ordered state.

In addition to the influence of anomalous lifetime distributions, the presence of topological defects or "traps" in the system can also play a role in the ordering dynamics. These defects, which are characterized by a non-trivial topology, can act as barriers to the relaxation of the system, leading to slow relaxation and non-exponential decay of the order parameter. In summary, our work demonstrates the importance of considering the influence of anomalous lifetime distributions and topological traps in the understanding and prediction of ordering dynamics in complex systems. By using Monte Carlo simulations, we have shown that the presence of these factors can significantly affect the overall ordering process, leading to the emergence of long-lived states and slow relaxation. Further investigation of these phenomena may lead to a deeper understanding of the mechanisms driving ordering dynamics in various systems.

Multiferroic materials, which exhibit both ferromagnetic and ferroelectric orders, have attracted significant attention due to their potential for use in spintronic and multiferroic devices. In this review, we focus on the quantum criticality of multiferroic materials, which occurs at a critical point where the material undergoes a phase transition from one ordered state to another. We discuss the different types of quantum critical points that can occur in multiferroic materials, including continuous and discontinuous transitions, as well as the various experimental techniques that have been used to study these transitions. We also review the current state of the field and highlight some of the open questions and challenges that remain. Multiferroic materials are a class of materials that exhibit both ferromagnetic and ferroelectric orders simultaneously, which makes them potentially useful for a range of spintronic and multiferroic devices. These materials have attracted significant attention from researchers in recent years due to their unique properties and potential applications.

One important aspect of multiferroic materials is their quantum criticality, which occurs at a critical point where the material undergoes a phase transition from one ordered state to another. This phase transition is often accompanied by the emergence of new physical phenomena, such as enhanced conductivity, which can be exploited for device applications.

There are several different types of quantum critical points that can occur in multiferroic materials. Continuous transitions, also known as second-order transitions, involve a smooth change in the material's properties as the critical point is approached. On the other hand, discontinuous transitions, or first-order transitions, involve a sudden change in the material's properties at the critical point.

There are several experimental techniques that have been used to study quantum criticality in multiferroic materials, including x-ray diffraction, neutron scattering, and magnetization measurements. These techniques have allowed researchers to characterize the different types of quantum critical points that can occur in these materials and to understand the underlying physical mechanisms responsible for the phase transitions. In summary, the quantum criticality of multiferroic materials has attracted significant attention due to its potential for use in spintronic and multiferroic devices. There are several different types of quantum critical points that can occur in these materials, including continuous and discontinuous transitions. Experimental techniques such as x-ray diffraction, neutron scattering, and magnetization measurements have allowed researchers to characterize these transitions and understand the underlying physical mechanisms. Despite the progress that has been made in this field, there are still many open questions and challenges that remain to be addressed.

The intrinsic magnetoconductivity of non-magnetic metals is a phenomenon that has garnered much attention in recent years due to its potential applications in spintronic devices. In this paper, we review the current state of research on the intrinsic magnetoconductivity of non-magnetic metals, including its physical origins, experimental techniques for measuring it, and potential applications. Non-magnetic metals, such as copper and aluminum, are traditionally thought to have no magnetic properties due to the absence of unpaired electrons in their valence bands. However, recent research has shown that non-magnetic metals can exhibit a magnetoconductivity effect, whereby the electrical conductivity of the metal is modified in the presence of a magnetic field. This effect is known as intrinsic magnetoconductivity and is caused by the spin-orbit coupling of the electrons in the metal.

There are several experimental techniques that have been used to measure the intrinsic magnetoconductivity of non-magnetic metals, including the four-point probe method and the Hall effect measurement. These techniques allow researchers to determine the magnitude and sign of the magnetoconductivity effect, as well as its dependence on temperature and magnetic field strength.

Potential applications of intrinsic magnetoconductivity in non-magnetic metals include its use as a sensor for detecting magnetic fields and its potential for use in spintronic devices. Spintronic devices rely on the spin of electrons for their operation, and the intrinsic magnetoconductivity of non-magnetic metals could potentially be used to manipulate the spin of electrons in these devices. In conclusion, the intrinsic magnetoconductivity of non-magnetic metals is a fascinating phenomenon that has attracted much attention in recent years due to its potential applications in spintronic devices. While much research has been done on this topic, there is still much to be learned about the physical origins and potential applications of this effect. Further research on the intrinsic magnetoconductivity of non-magnetic metals will likely lead to the development of new and innovative technologies.
