Smart wallet is the backbone of payment network where a user can transfer funds to another user by paying a small monetary amount. Smart-contract wallet is the version of traditional smart-wallets which is designed for the execution of smart contracts. The basic function of a smart-contract wallet is that the user can spend a small monetary amount through it’s smart contracts. Users may not know the information of their accounts which are embedded in the smart-contract wallet. A user may have access to the user credentials for the use only in his wallet and not in the wallets of all other entities. Therefore a user should be able to authenticate himself to all other wallets that are connected to his smart-contract wallet i.e. he must authenticate himself in air-gapped network. The paper introduces a novel method to do so and proposes SmartOTPs. It involves the incorporation of a new primitive called the Air-Gapped Ticket Passwords (AGTP) along with the existing ones i.e. E-Passwords, and also the adoption of hash-chain based authentication techniques. The proposed solution is highly scalable because of the availability of smart-contract wallets in the public cloud. The experiments showed that the average authentication success rate is as high as 99.9% with small error rates. [. Smart-wallets are the most widely used payment system in the world. In a smart-wallets, a user can spend a small monetary amount through it. The user may not know the information of his account which is embedded in the smart-contract wallet i.e the user may not know the identity of his wallet. A user may have access to his account credentials in his wallet but not in the wallets of all other users. Therefore, a user should be able to authenticate himself in air-gapped network i.e. the user should be able to authenticate himself in the wallets of all other entities that are connected to his smart-contract wallets i.e. in the public cloud. The proposed solution is highly scalable because of availability of smart-contract wallets.    In this paper we propose a novel method to do so and propose a new primitive called the Air-Gapped   Ticket Passwords (AGTP’s).    The proposed solution is based on the idea of the existing ones i.e. E-passwords, hash-chain based authentication techniques and also the integration of the proposed new primitive. The proposed solution is highly scalable because of availablity of smart-contract wallets in public cloud. The experimental results show that the average authentication success rate is as high   as 99.9% with small authentication error rates. The paper is a step towards the design of a novel and highly scalable 2-factor authentication for the use in air-gapped networks. The proposed method is based on the integration of the existing methods and also the adoption of new primitive. The proposed solution is highly scalable due to availability of smart-contract wallets and also because of the adoption of hash-chain based authentication techniques which are highly secure and scalable.

This work investigates quantum computation based on frustration-free Hamiltonians and proposes a robust measurement-based analog quantum computation strategy. We propose a general scheme that transforms generic frustration-free Hamiltonians into diagonal form and perform a two-qubit phase gate on pairs of qubits within the diagonal blocks of those Hamiltonians. The measurement results are used to determine the Pauli-Z or Pauli-X eigenvalues of the quantum states of interest without the need for any control system with significant decoherence. The proposed operation is shown to be robust against quantum noise. In recent years, there has been a tremendous interest in studying the power of quantum computers [1]. In particular, it has been proposed that quantum computers can be used to simulate quantum many-body physics [2] and solve hard optimization problems [3].  One of the main challenges in building a quantum computer is the difficulty in preparing the input quantum states. The standard approach for preparing an arbitrary quantum state is to encode the state into a superposition of different basis states and then apply a unitary gate to each basis state [4]. This approach, however, requires a large number of quantum gates for each basis state and is thus inefficient.  In contrast, analog quantum computation has been proposed as a potential alternative to the standard digital approach for preparing quantum states [5, 6]. In this approach, the quantum states are encoded in a single time-dependent wave function. The quantum dynamics of the system are governed by a Hamiltonian which is assumed to be a function of the control parameter. The control parameter is then slowly varied in time, and the system evolves adiabatically from one quantum state to another. The quantum state of interest is then prepared by measuring the quantum state at the end of the adiabatic evolution.  In this paper, we investigate the power of analog quantum computation for a class of Hamiltonians that are known as frustration-free Hamiltonians [7]. Frustration-free Hamiltonians are defined as Hamiltonians which do not contain any terms that commute with each other. As a result, the eigenstates of these Hamiltonians can be uniquely determined by the eigenvalues of the commuting terms. Frustration-free Hamiltonians have been proposed for a number of different applications, including quantum simulation [8, 9] and quantum computing [10, 11].  In this paper, we propose a measurement-based analog quantum computation strategy for a general class of frustration-free Hamiltonians. In particular, we transform generic frustration-free Hamiltonians into diagonal form and propose a two-qubit phase gate on pairs of diagonal blocks of the Hamiltonian. The measurement results are then used to determine the Pauli-Z or Pauli X eigenvalues of the quantum states of interest without the requirement for any control system with significant decoherence. We show that the proposed operation is robust against quantum noise. The measurement-based analog quantum computation strategy proposed in this paper can be used to prepare the ground states of generic frustration-free Hamiltonians and solve optimization problems with the same efficiency as digital quantum computation. In this paper, we present a solution for the Flowgraphs case in the 2013 edition of the Two-Track CEGIS (TTC) competition using Non-Negative Matrix Factorization (NMF). The Two-Track CEGIS (TTC) competition is an annual event that challenges participants to develop automated synthesis methods for various cases. The Flowgraphs case, which was part of the 2013 edition of the competition, involves synthesizing a flowgraph from a given Boolean specification. The goal of the Flowgraphs case is to find a flowgraph that satisfies the given specification while minimizing the number of nodes in the resulting flowgraph.

This paper proposes a Bayesian model-based survey design method for log-Gaussian Cox processes (LGCP). The method is based on a Bayesian hierarchical model that combines information on the spatial covariates and the intensity function obtained from a fixed survey by maximizing the joint posterior density function of the intensity function and the spatial covariates. We approximate the maximum likelihood estimation (MLE) and posterior distribution with integrated nested Laplace approximations (INLA). We compare the proposed method with a non-Bayesian approach for the spatial covariate and an MLE approach for both the spatial covariate and the intensity function. Positron Emission Tomography (PET) is a medical imaging technique that involves the use of radioactive tracers to visualize the functioning of organs and tissues in the body. The system matrix, which contains information about the response of the detector to single photon incidence, is a key component of PET image reconstruction. In this document, we describe the use of the point spread function (PSF) to derive the system matrix for PET image reconstruction and discuss the potential advantages and limitations of this approach. PET is a non-invasive imaging technique that allows for the visualization of physiological processes within the body. It involves the injection of a radioactive tracer, which is then taken up by cells and tissues and emits positrons as it decays. These positrons interact with nearby electrons, resulting in the emission of two gamma rays in opposite directions. These gamma rays are detected by the PET scanner, which consists of a series of detectors arranged in a ring around the patient.

One of the key challenges in PET image reconstruction is to accurately reconstruct the distribution of the tracer within the body based on the detected gamma rays. This process involves the use of a system matrix, which contains information about the response of the detector to single photon incidence. The system matrix can be derived using a variety of methods, including Monte Carlo simulations or measurements with a physical phantom.

In this document, we describe the use of the point spread function (PSF) to derive the system matrix for PET image reconstruction. The PSF is a measure of the spread of detected photons as a function of distance from the source. It can be calculated using a single photon emission source and the PET scanner geometry. The use of a PSF-derived system matrix for PET image reconstruction has the potential to improve the accuracy and resolution of PET images. However, it is important to consider the potential limitations of this approach, including the time and resources required to calculate the PSF and the potential for errors in the measurement or calculation of the PSF. Further research is needed to fully understand the potential benefits and drawbacks of using a PSF-derived system matrix for PET image reconstruction.

Spectrographs are essential tools in astrophotonics, the study of the interaction between light and matter in astrophysical environments. These instruments allow astronomers to analyze the spectral characteristics of celestial objects, such as stars, galaxies, and nebulae, and learn more about their physical properties, chemical composition, and motion. In this document, we will provide a brief overview of the principles of spectrography, the different types of spectrographs available, and their applications in astrophotonics research. Spectrographs are instruments that analyze the spectrum of electromagnetic radiation, such as visible light, infrared, or ultraviolet light. They work by separating the different wavelengths of light and measuring their intensity, allowing scientists to study the spectral characteristics of celestial objects and learn more about their physical properties, chemical composition, and motion. Spectrographs are used in a variety of fields, including astronomy, physics, chemistry, and biology, to study the properties of matter at various scales.

In astrophotonics, spectrographs are used to study the properties of celestial objects, such as stars, galaxies, and nebulae. They can provide information on the temperature, surface gravity, and chemical composition of stars, the distance and motion of galaxies, and the physical conditions in nebulae. Spectrographs can also be used to study the atmospheres of planets and exoplanets, as well as the interstellar medium, the gas and dust that fills the space between stars.

There are several types of spectrographs available, each designed to study different spectral ranges and provide specific information. For example, low-resolution spectrographs can provide broad information on the overall spectrum of an object, while high-resolution spectrographs can provide detailed information on specific spectral lines. There are also spectrographs designed for specific wavelengths, such as ultraviolet, infrared, or radio, which are useful for studying objects that emit radiation in these ranges. Spectrographs are essential tools in astrophotonics research, allowing astronomers to study the spectral characteristics of celestial objects and learn more about their physical properties, chemical composition, and motion. There are several types of spectrographs available, each designed to study different spectral ranges and provide specific information. These instruments have contributed significantly to our understanding of the universe and will continue to play a vital role in the field of astrophotonics for years to come.

The Laser Interferometer Space Antenna (LISA) is a proposed space-based gravitational wave detector that aims to detect and measure gravitational waves from astrophysical sources such as mergers of supermassive black holes. While LISA has the potential to revolutionize our understanding of the universe, there are a number of technical challenges that must be addressed in order to achieve its full scientific potential. In this paper, we propose a novel approach for expanding the LISA horizon from the ground by using a network of ground-based interferometers to augment the LISA data and improve its sensitivity to gravitational waves. Gravitational waves are ripples in the fabric of spacetime that are produced by the acceleration of massive objects. They were first predicted by Albert Einstein's theory of general relativity, and their existence was confirmed by the detection of gravitational waves from the merger of two black holes by the Laser Interferometer Gravitational-Wave Observatory (LIGO) in 2015. Since then, LIGO has detected a number of additional gravitational wave events, providing valuable insights into the behavior of black holes and other extreme objects in the universe.

The Laser Interferometer Space Antenna (LISA) is a proposed space-based gravitational wave detector that aims to detect and measure gravitational waves from a much wider range of astrophysical sources, including the mergers of supermassive black holes with masses in the range of millions to billions of solar masses. These events are expected to be much more common than the mergers of stellar-mass black holes detected by LIGO, and they could provide valuable information about the formation and evolution of galaxies and large-scale structures in the universe.

However, the technical challenges of building and operating a space-based gravitational wave detector are significant. LISA requires precise measurements of the distance between three spacecraft separated by millions of kilometers, which is a daunting task given the noise and disturbance that can be introduced by the environment and the spacecraft itself. In addition, the sensitivity of LISA is limited by the shot noise of the laser beams, which is a fundamental noise source that arises from the quantization of the light used to make the measurements. In this paper, we have proposed a novel approach for expanding the LISA horizon from the ground by using a network of ground-based interferometers to augment the LISA data and improve its sensitivity to gravitational waves. By combining the strengths of both space-based and ground-based detectors, we can overcome some of the technical challenges faced by LISA and unlock its full scientific potential. This approach has the potential to revolutionize our understanding of the universe and provide new insights into the behavior of black holes and other extreme objects.

The attached-eddy hypothesis is a theory that explains the behavior of turbulent flow near a solid wall. It posits that the flow can be broken down into a series of attached eddies, which are self-contained and move with the flow. In this paper, we investigate the two-point correlation of these attached eddies in wall turbulence. We find that the correlation decays exponentially with increasing separation distance, and that the decay rate is dependent on the Reynolds number. Turbulence is a common and complex phenomenon that occurs in many fluid flow systems. It is characterized by the presence of vortices, or eddies, which are regions of swirling flow. In wall-bounded turbulent flow, such as flow over a flat plate or inside a pipe, the flow near the wall is of particular interest. It is known that the behavior of this flow is different from that of the flow in the center of the channel.

The attached-eddy hypothesis is a theory that aims to explain these differences. It suggests that the flow near the wall can be decomposed into a series of attached eddies, which are self-contained and move with the flow. These eddies are thought to be responsible for the majority of the turbulent kinetic energy near the wall.

In this paper, we focus on the two-point correlation of these attached eddies in wall turbulence. Two-point correlation is a statistical measure of the degree to which two points in a flow field are correlated. We investigate how the correlation between two points in the flow field changes as the separation distance between them increases. Our analysis of the two-point correlation in wall turbulence according to the attached-eddy hypothesis shows that the correlation decays exponentially with increasing separation distance. We also find that the decay rate is dependent on the Reynolds number, which is a measure of the flow's intensity. These results provide further support for the attached-eddy hypothesis and offer new insights into the behavior of turbulent flow near solid walls.

Dans cet article, nous étudions les lois de répartition des diviseurs des entiers friables, c'est-à-dire des entiers qui peuvent être décomposés en produits de nombres premiers. Nous comparons ces lois à celles des entiers non friables et discutons des implications pour la théorie des nombres. Les entiers friables jouent un rôle important dans la théorie des nombres, en raison de leur décomposition en produits de nombres premiers. Cependant, peu d'études ont été consacrées à la répartition des diviseurs de ces entiers. Dans cet article, nous explorons cette question en comparant les lois de répartition des diviseurs des entiers friables et non friables. Nous avons montré que les lois de répartition des diviseurs des entiers friables et non friables sont différentes. Nos résultats ont des implications pour la théorie des nombres et pourraient être utilisés pour comprendre mieux la structure des entiers friables et non friables. Il serait intéressant de poursuivre cette étude en étudiant d'autres propriétés des entiers friables et en comparant ces résultats avec ceux obtenus pour les entiers non friables.

We study a high-dimensional stochastic linear bandit which has a large number of arms and the arm rewards are a subset ${\mathbb{R}}(1+\epsilon)$ of a large domain. In this case, any one-dimensional estimator $E_f(\mathbf{z})$, namely the expected value of feature $f: {\mathbb{R}}^{d}\to {\mathbb{R}}$ of arm’s context $\mathbf{z}\in {\mathbb{R}}^d$, cannot be used directly to recover an arm’s context. To be able to do so, we first establish a general theory of multi-dimensional estimators that can be derived from a single one-dimensional estimator. We then employ such a general multi-dimensional estimator for bandit learning and establish an upper confidence bound (UCB) policy for the mean of contexts that generalizes state-of-the-art contextual bandit (CB) algorithm, KLIS and has the same sample complexity. Our result also holds for high-dimensions with Bernoulli bandits where contextual bandit algorithms either fail or require a much higher sample complexity. Finally, we prove that this UCB policy can be extended to the case when we have a general distribution rather than a Bernoulli bandit. The contextual bandit problem (CB) is one of the most fundamental problems in online learning and has been studied extensively in recent years [3-7]. The problem is defined as follows:       At each time step $t\in [T]$, the learner chooses an arm $A_t\in [K]$ according to a policy $\pi: [T]\times [K]\to [0,1]$ and observes a reward $R_t\in [0,1]$ that is drawn i.i.d. according to some unknown distribution $\mathcal{D}$. The goal of the learner is to minimize the regret defined as $R_T=\sum_{t=1}^T (\mu_{A_t}-R_t)$ where $\mu_{A_t}=\mathbb{E}[R_t|A_t]$ is the mean reward of arm $A_t$. In the high-dimensional case, we assume that $K\ll d$ and the mean reward $\mu_{A_t,i}$ of each arm $A_t$ is a linear function of the context $\mathbf{z}_{A_t}$ of arm $A_t$. The goal is to design a policy that achieves low regret in this high-dimensional setting.       Bandit learning has been studied in a number of settings including linear bandits [8], Gaussian bandits [9], contextual bandits [3], multi-armed bandits with side information [10], and many others.       Bandit theory is a branch of machine learning that studies the optimal learning rate of a learning algorithm for a specific class of problems [11]. In a stochastic linear bandit, the learner is given a large number of arms $K$ and observes rewards $R_t$ which are a subset $[0,1](1+\epsilon)$ of the real line where $\epsilon\in[-1,1]$ is an unknown parameter. In this case, any one-dimensional linear estimator $E_f(\mathbf z)$, namely the expected value of a feature $f: {\mathbb R}^d\to {\mathbb R}$ of arm’s context $z\in {\mathbb R}^d$, cannot be used directly in the bandit learning algorithm to recover the arm’s context $\mathbf z_t$. To be able to do so, we need to establish a general theory of multi-dimensional linear estimators. In this paper, we study a high-dimensional stochastic linear bandit where the arm rewards are a subset $[0,1)(1+\epsilon)$ of a real line where $\epsilon\in [-1,1]$ is an unobserved parameter. We first establish a general theory of multi-dimensional linear bandit estimators that can be derived from a single one dimensional bandit estimator. We then employ such a general multi dimensional estimator for bandit learning and establish an upper-confidence bound (UCB) policy for the linear mean of contexts that generalizes state-of-art contextual bandit (CB) algorithms, KLIS and has the same sample complexity as the CB algorithm. Our result also holds for high-dimensions where the contextual bandit algorithms either fail or require a significantly higher sample complexity. Finally, we prove that our UCB policy can be extended to the case where we have a general distribution rather than a Bernoulli distribution.

This study presents an analysis of the ionized gas in two early-type galaxies, NGC 6762 and NGC 5966, using data from the CALIFA (Calar Alto Legacy Integral Field Area) survey. The ionized gas distribution and kinematics were mapped using emission line ratios and velocity fields. The results indicate that both galaxies have complex ionized gas structures and kinematics, with evidence of past or ongoing interactions in both cases. The ionized gas in NGC 6762 is more extended and exhibits more ordered rotation compared to NGC 5966, which shows more disturbed kinematics and a central gas concentration. These findings highlight the diversity of ionized gas properties in early-type galaxies and the importance of studying individual cases in detail. Early-type galaxies, characterized by their old stellar populations and lack of ongoing star formation, are traditionally thought to be gas-poor and dynamically hot systems. However, recent studies have revealed the presence of ionized gas in a significant fraction of early-type galaxies, suggesting that these systems may not be as simple as previously thought. The origin and evolution of this ionized gas is still poorly understood, and studying it can provide important insights into the processes that shape early-type galaxies.

The CALIFA survey is a large project that has obtained integral field spectroscopy data for a diverse sample of over 600 galaxies, covering a wide range of morphological types and masses. In this study, we focus on two early-type galaxies from the CALIFA sample: NGC 6762 and NGC 5966. These galaxies were selected for their different morphologies and apparent gas content, with NGC 6762 being an S0 galaxy with an extended gas disk and NGC 5966 being an E galaxy with a central gas concentration. By analyzing the ionized gas in these two cases, we aim to shed light on the diversity of ionized gas properties in early-type galaxies and the factors that may influence them. Our analysis of the ionized gas in the early-type galaxies NGC 6762 and NGC 5966 using CALIFA data has revealed complex and diverse gas structures and kinematics. In both cases, the presence of ionized gas suggests that these galaxies have undergone or are undergoing some kind of gas-rich event, such as a merger or accretion. The more extended and ordered gas distribution and kinematics in NGC 6762 suggest a more quiescent evolution, while the more disturbed kinematics and central gas concentration in NGC 5966 point to a more turbulent history. These results highlight the importance of studying individual cases in detail and the need for further research on the origin and evolution of ionized gas in early-type galaxies.

The obtained result can be applied to any isoperimetric body that has equal volume under any non-negative base measure. Biolocomotion refers to the movement of living organisms, and it is a complex process that involves a variety of physical and biological factors. In this paper, we focus on two specific factors that play a crucial role in biolocomotion: symmetry and dissipation. We discuss how symmetry can affect the efficiency and stability of movement, and how dissipation can influence the energy requirements of biolocomotion. We also discuss the ways in which these factors can be manipulated or optimized in various biological systems, and the potential implications for the design of artificial biolocomotion systems. Biolocomotion is a fundamental aspect of the biology of many living organisms, and it is essential for a wide range of activities, including foraging, mating, and escaping predators. The ability to move effectively and efficiently is therefore critical for the survival and reproduction of many species. In order to better understand the physical principles underlying biolocomotion, scientists have studied a variety of biological systems, including animals, plants, and microorganisms.

One important factor that has been the subject of much research is symmetry. Symmetry can affect the stability and efficiency of movement in several ways. For example, symmetrical body plans may be more resistant to external perturbations, such as gusts of wind or changes in terrain. Similarly, symmetrical appendages, such as wings or legs, can produce more balanced and efficient forces when they are moving in coordinated fashion.

Another important factor that has been studied in the context of biolocomotion is dissipation. Dissipation refers to the energy lost during movement, and it can have a significant impact on the overall efficiency of biolocomotion. For example, animals that rely on low-dissipation modes of movement, such as gliding or swimming, may be able to move more efficiently than animals that rely on high-dissipation modes of movement, such as running or jumping. In conclusion, symmetry and dissipation are two important factors that play a significant role in biolocomotion. By understanding how these factors influence movement in biological systems, we can gain insights into the design of more efficient and stable biolocomotion systems, both natural and artificial. Further research on these topics has the potential to lead to important advances in a variety of fields, including biology, engineering, and robotics.

In this paper, we propose a novel method for evaluating the performance of visual conversational agents (VCAs) based on cooperative human-AI games. VCAs are AI systems that can engage in natural language conversations with users while simultaneously displaying visual content. These systems have the potential to revolutionize human-machine interaction by enabling more intuitive and immersive communication. However, accurately evaluating the capabilities of VCAs is a challenging task. Traditional methods, such as human evaluations or task-based benchmarks, have limitations when it comes to evaluating the full range of capabilities of VCAs. In contrast, our proposed method leverages the collaborative nature of VCAs to engage users in a series of cooperative games where the VCA acts as a teammate. By analyzing the performance of the VCA in these games, we can gain a more comprehensive understanding of its abilities and limitations. Visual conversational agents (VCAs) are AI systems that are capable of engaging in natural language conversations with users while displaying visual content. These systems have the potential to revolutionize human-machine interaction by enabling more intuitive and immersive communication. VCAs have a wide range of potential applications, including customer service, education, and entertainment. However, accurately evaluating the capabilities of VCAs is a challenging task. Traditional methods, such as human evaluations or task-based benchmarks, have limitations when it comes to evaluating the full range of capabilities of VCAs.

To address this challenge, we propose a novel method for evaluating VCAs based on cooperative human-AI games. In these games, the VCA acts as a teammate to the human player, and the two work together to complete a shared goal. By analyzing the performance of the VCA in these games, we can gain a more comprehensive understanding of its abilities and limitations. Our method has several advantages over traditional evaluation methods. First, it allows us to evaluate the VCA's ability to understand and respond to complex, open-ended tasks. Second, it allows us to evaluate the VCA's ability to understand and respond to natural language input. Third, it allows us to evaluate the VCA's ability to learn and adapt over time. Finally, it allows us to evaluate the VCA's ability to work cooperatively with humans, which is a key factor in its potential usefulness in real-world applications. In this paper, we have proposed a novel method for evaluating the performance of visual conversational agents based on cooperative human-AI games. By analyzing the performance of the VCA in these games, we can gain a more comprehensive understanding of its abilities and limitations. Our method has several advantages over traditional evaluation methods, including the ability to evaluate the VCA's ability to understand and respond to complex, open-ended tasks, its ability to understand and respond to natural language input, its ability to learn and adapt over time, and its ability to work cooperatively with humans. We believe that this method has the potential to provide valuable insights into the capabilities and limitations of VCAs, and to guide the development of these systems in the future.

In this paper, we propose a new theory for the origin of dark matter, based on the idea of gravitational particle production during the reheating period following inflation. We show that this process can naturally lead to the production of dark matter particles with the correct abundance and properties needed to explain the observed distribution of dark matter in the universe. Our theory offers a compelling alternative to the traditional explanation for dark matter, which is based on the existence of new particles beyond the Standard Model of particle physics. Dark matter is a mysterious form of matter that makes up approximately 85% of the mass in the universe, but which does not interact with light or other forms of electromagnetic radiation. Despite decades of research, the nature of dark matter remains largely unknown, and it is one of the biggest mysteries in modern physics. There are many theories that attempt to explain the existence of dark matter, including the possibility that it is made up of new particles beyond the Standard Model of particle physics. However, none of these theories has yet been able to provide a satisfactory explanation for all of the observed properties of dark matter.

In this paper, we propose a new theory for the origin of dark matter, based on the idea of gravitational particle production during the reheating period following inflation. Inflation is a period of rapid expansion that is believed to have occurred in the very early universe, shortly after the Big Bang. During this period, the universe underwent a rapid expansion, causing the energy density of the universe to decrease rapidly. This rapid expansion was followed by a period of reheating, during which the universe cooled and the energy density of the universe increased again.

We show that during the reheating period, the rapid expansion and contraction of the universe can lead to the production of particles through a process known as gravitational particle production. This process occurs when the expansion and contraction of the universe causes the curvature of spacetime to change rapidly, leading to the production of particles. We argue that this process can naturally lead to the production of dark matter particles with the correct abundance and properties needed to explain the observed distribution of dark matter in the universe. In this paper, we have proposed a new theory for the origin of dark matter, based on the idea of gravitational particle production during the reheating period following inflation. We have shown that this process can naturally lead to the production of dark matter particles with the correct abundance and properties needed to explain the observed distribution of dark matter in the universe. Our theory offers a compelling alternative to the traditional explanation for dark matter, which is based on the existence of new particles beyond the Standard Model of particle physics. Further research is needed to test the validity of our theory and to better understand the nature of dark matter.

We study the effect of redshift space distortions on the distribution function (DF) in both the velocity space and real space. The key point is that the DF is defined on the hypersurface of constant density, not at constant velocity. The result implies that the effect of redshift space distortions on the DF is generally more complicated than what is usually supposed. An interesting example of such complication is studied in detail in two recent papers. (1) Kaiser (1981) found that the DF in Lagrangian is nonhomogeneous in the radial direction. (2) Kaiser and Peacock (1986) found that the DF varies linearly in the real space under the transformation from Lagrangian to Eulerian space. We examine these results theoretically based on the collisionless Boltzmann equation, and give physical interpretation to these surprising results. The effect of redshift space distortions on the DF is one of the most important problems in cosmology. In the past, the DF approach has been used to study the non-linear clustering in the real space (e.g., Bernardeau et al., 1985; Scoccimarro and Sheth, 1986; Peacock and Dodds, 1986; Bernardeau and Efstathiou, 1989; Sheth and Tormen, 1991; Peacock, 1991; Bernardeau et al., 1.993). In the velocity space, the DF approach has been used to study redshift space distortions (e.g., Kaiser, 1981; Kaiser and Peacock, 1986; Hamilton, 1986; Kaiser, 1991; Hamilton and Peacock, 1991). In these studies, the DF in the real space is assumed to be homogeneous, while it is generally non-homogeneous in the velocity space.    The goal of this paper is to study the effect of redshift space distortions on the DF in both the velocity space and real space. In particular, we show that the effect of redshift space distortions on the velocity DF is more complicated than what is usually supposed. We also show that the effect of redshift space distortions on real space DF is more complicated than what is usually supposed, and can be non-linear.    The paper is organized as follows. In the next section, we define the DF in the velocity space and real space. We also give the definition of the velocity DF in the Eulerian and Lagrangian space. In §3, we study the effect of redshift space distortions on the velocity distribution function. In §4, we study the effect of redshift space distortions in the real space. In §5, we discuss the results. We show that the effect of redshift space distortions on velocity DF is more complicated than what is usually assumed. We also show that the effect of redshift distortions on real space DF is more complicated than usually supposed, and can be non-linear in the linear regime.

In this paper, we study Koppelman formulas on affine cones over smooth projective complete intersections. These formulas provide a connection between the cohomology of the affine cone and the cohomology of the underlying projective complete intersection. We derive explicit formulas for the Koppelman residue map and the Koppelman fundamental class in this setting, and use these formulas to compute the cohomology of affine cones in a variety of examples. Koppelman formulas, first introduced by Herbert Koppelman in the 1970s, provide a powerful tool for computing the cohomology of singular varieties. They relate the cohomology of a singular variety to the cohomology of a smooth variety by means of a residue map. In this paper, we focus on Koppelman formulas on affine cones over smooth projective complete intersections. These cones are a class of singular varieties that are of particular interest in algebraic geometry, as they can be used to compactify moduli spaces and study degenerations of algebraic varieties.

The main result of this paper is the derivation of explicit formulas for the Koppelman residue map and the Koppelman fundamental class on affine cones over smooth projective complete intersections. These formulas allow us to compute the cohomology of these cones in a variety of examples, including affine cones over smooth projective curves and surfaces. In this paper, we have studied Koppelman formulas on affine cones over smooth projective complete intersections. We have derived explicit formulas for the Koppelman residue map and the Koppelman fundamental class in this setting, and used these formulas to compute the cohomology of affine cones in a variety of examples. These results provide a powerful tool for studying the topology of singular varieties and have numerous applications in algebraic geometry.

In this paper, we present a new approach for addressing the window problem in large volume renormalization schemes. The window problem arises when the volume of the system becomes too large, leading to a breakdown of the perturbative expansion. Our approach involves the use of non-perturbative techniques, such as Monte Carlo simulations, to calculate the renormalization factors with higher accuracy. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach through comparison with existing methods on a range of test systems. Renormalization is a fundamental concept in quantum field theory, allowing the calculation of physical quantities from a theoretical model. In many cases, the calculation of these quantities can be simplified by working in a large volume, where perturbative techniques can be applied. However, when the volume becomes too large, the perturbative expansion breaks down and the so-called "window problem" occurs. This problem has been a longstanding issue in the field, and various methods have been developed to address it.

In this work, we propose a new approach for dealing with the window problem in large volume renormalization schemes. Our method involves the use of non-perturbative techniques, such as Monte Carlo simulations, to calculate the renormalization factors with higher accuracy. By avoiding the use of perturbation theory, we are able to extend the range of volumes over which reliable calculations can be performed. In conclusion, we have presented a new approach for addressing the window problem in large volume renormalization schemes. Our approach involves the use of non-perturbative techniques to calculate the renormalization factors, resulting in a significant improvement in the accuracy of the calculations. We believe that this approach has the potential to greatly extend the range of volumes over which reliable calculations can be performed, and we hope that it will be widely adopted in the field.

This study presents a simulation-based approach for estimating queue lengths in signalized arterial networks under fixed time control. The proposed method utilizes microscopic traffic simulation to accurately capture the dynamic behavior of vehicles in the network and compute steady state queue lengths under various traffic conditions. The results of the simulation are compared to field measurements to validate the accuracy of the proposed method. The proposed method is found to provide reliable estimates of queue lengths, making it a useful tool for traffic engineering applications such as signal timing optimization and capacity analysis. Traffic congestion is a major issue in urban areas, leading to delays and reduced mobility for travelers. Signalized intersections are a common means of controlling traffic flow in arterial networks, with the objective of minimizing delays and maximizing capacity. Accurate estimation of queue lengths at signalized intersections is important for a variety of traffic engineering applications, including signal timing optimization and capacity analysis.

Traditionally, queue length estimation has been based on analytical models that make assumptions about the flow of vehicles in the network. These models often rely on simplifying assumptions, such as uniform arrival patterns and constant capacity, which may not accurately reflect the complex and dynamic nature of real-world traffic. As a result, there is a need for more accurate methods for estimating queue lengths in signalized arterial networks.

In this paper, we investigate the global stability of a class of virus models that incorporate both cytotoxic T-lymphocyte (CTL) immune response and antigenic variation. By constructing a suitable Lyapunov function, we prove that the disease-free equilibrium of the model is globally asymptotically stable under certain conditions on the model parameters. We also demonstrate the existence and global stability of a unique endemic equilibrium under certain conditions. Our results provide insight into the interplay between CTL immune response and antigenic variation in viral infections and have important implications for the control and prevention of infectious diseases. Viral infections are a major public health concern, as they can cause significant morbidity and mortality. A key component of the immune system's response to viral infections is the cytotoxic T-lymphocyte (CTL) response, which involves the activation and expansion of CTLs that recognize and kill infected cells. Antigenic variation is a strategy employed by some viruses to evade the CTL response by constantly changing the surface proteins that the CTLs recognize.

Mathematical modeling has proven to be a useful tool for understanding the dynamics of viral infections and the impact of immune responses on these dynamics. In this paper, we consider a class of virus models that incorporate both CTL immune response and antigenic variation. We investigate the global stability of the disease-free equilibrium (DFE) and the endemic equilibrium of the model, and provide conditions under which these equilibria are globally asymptotically stable. In this paper, we have analyzed the global stability of a class of virus models that incorporate both CTL immune response and antigenic variation. By constructing a suitable Lyapunov function, we have shown that the DFE of the model is globally asymptotically stable under certain conditions on the model parameters. We have also demonstrated the existence and global stability of a unique endemic equilibrium under certain conditions. Our results provide insight into the interplay between CTL immune response and antigenic variation in viral infections and have important implications for the control and prevention of infectious diseases.

This document discusses the letter frequencies and entropy of written Marathi, a language spoken in the western Indian state of Maharashtra. The analysis is based on a sample of written Marathi text and includes a comparison with the letter frequencies of other languages. The results show that Marathi has a relatively high entropy, indicating that the distribution of letters in the language is relatively random. Marathi is a language spoken by over 73 million people in the western Indian state of Maharashtra. It is written in the Devanagari script, which consists of 52 letters, including 36 consonants and 16 vowels. In this document, we will analyze the letter frequencies and entropy of written Marathi text in order to understand the structure of the language and how it compares to other languages.

Letter frequencies refer to the number of times a particular letter appears in a given text. They can be used to identify patterns in a language and to compare the structure of different languages. Entropy, on the other hand, is a measure of the randomness of a distribution. A high entropy indicates that the distribution is relatively random, while a low entropy indicates that the distribution is more predictable.

To perform the analysis, we collected a sample of written Marathi text and counted the frequencies of each letter. We then compared the results to the letter frequencies of other languages in order to see how Marathi compares. The results of the analysis show that Marathi has a relatively high entropy compared to other languages. This indicates that the distribution of letters in Marathi is relatively random and that there are no strong patterns in the language. While further research is needed to fully understand the structure of Marathi and how it compares to other languages, the results of this analysis provide a useful starting point for future studies.

The use of augmented reality (AR) in education has been growing in popularity due to its ability to engage and motivate learners. In this study, we propose an interactive instructional model using AR-based edutainment to enhance emotional intelligence. The model was developed and tested with a group of elementary school students. Results showed that the AR-based instructional model was effective in increasing emotional intelligence, as measured by pre- and post-tests. The students also reported high levels of enjoyment and engagement with the AR activities. Emotional intelligence is an important factor in success, both personally and professionally. It involves the ability to recognize and understand one's own emotions, as well as the emotions of others. Many traditional educational methods, however, do not adequately address the development of emotional intelligence. This is where AR-based edutainment can be an effective tool.

AR technology allows for the creation of interactive, immersive learning experiences that can engage and motivate learners. By incorporating elements of entertainment, such as games and challenges, AR-based edutainment can make learning fun and engaging. This can be especially effective for younger learners, who may be more motivated by interactive and entertaining activities.

In this study, we developed an interactive instructional model using AR-based edutainment to enhance emotional intelligence in elementary school students. The model was tested with a group of students, and the results were analyzed to determine its effectiveness. The results of this study show that the interactive instructional model using AR-based edutainment was effective in increasing emotional intelligence in elementary school students. The students also reported high levels of enjoyment and engagement with the AR activities. This suggests that AR-based edutainment can be a useful tool for enhancing emotional intelligence in a fun and engaging way. We present emission line observations that trace the gas surrounding the z~3 Lyman break galaxy (LBG) candidate GN20, using data from the Atacama Large Millimeter/submillimeter Array (ALMA). From a global comparison of the [CII] emission line from ALMA and near-infrared (NIR)/far-infrared (FIR) observations with Spitzer 3.6 and 24 μm imaging, we determine that the GN20 LBG has a far-IR luminosity of ${(L_\text {FIR}^{\nu \text {-} 6})}_{12} = (1.8 \pm 0.4)\times10^{13} L_\odot $ that places it at a redshift of $z = 3.0059 \pm 0.0005$. This distance places GN20 at a projected spatial extent of 40 kpc on the plane of the sky, which corresponds to a physical size of 170 kpc, roughly two times the radius of the host LBG. We also detect the [CII] emission line from ALMA continuum-subtracted images, which are an order of magnitude dimmer. At this distance, [CII] emission from individual LBGs would be undetected from the ALMA images; however, the combined emission of ~500 LBGs at this redshift would be sufficiently bright to be detected with the ALMA data presented here. Our detection allows the host LBG of GN20 to be resolved spatially, but does not determine its morphology, since the emission line observations could be sensitive to emission from both the host and close companions. These measurements represent the first spatially-resolved observations of the circumgalactic medium and star-forming gas surrounding a LBG at $z \approx 3$ and complement ongoing ALMA observing programs at lower redshift. - The first generation of large telescopes have opened a new window into the study of the high-redshift universe by enabling the detection of galaxies in the early universe. This has enabled the study of the formation of the first stars and galaxies and their relationship to the large scale structure of the universe.   - At the same time, these telescopes have revealed that the high-redshift universe is populated with galaxies that are not as massive as expected from theoretical models of galaxy formation (e.g., 1, 2). These galaxies may be missing from the standard picture of galaxy formation (e.g. 3) and may provide clues to the physical processes that are responsible for the missing baryons.   - The high-redshift universe is also a region of high physical density, making it important to understand how this environment affects the formation of galaxies.   - The high-redshift universe also has the potential to be the first place in the universe where the first stars formed. This is because the first stars may have formed in the densest regions of the universe, where the gas was initially hot and ionized, and the first galaxies were forming.   - At the same time, the high-redshift universe is also a region of active star-formation, making it important to understand how this star-formation is regulated.   - The high-redshift universe has also been studied as a site for the growth of supermassive black holes (SMBHs). - This study presents the first spatially-resolved observations of the circum-galactic medium and star-forming gas surrounding a Lyman Break galaxy at $z \approx 3.0$.   - We show that the [CII] emission line from the ALMA observations is ~5 times brighter than the continuum emission, and therefore the [CII] emission line is a promising tracer of the star-forming gas surrounding the LBG.   - Our measurements indicate that the LBG has a far-IR luminosity of L$_{\text{FIR}}$ = $1.8 \pm 0.6$ × $10^{13} L_{\odot}$, which places it at a redshift of $z=3.0059 \pm$ 0.0005.   - We detect the [CII] emission line of the LBG in the continuum-subtracted ALMA images, which is ~10 times fainter than the continuum emission.   - This suggests that the [CII] emission line may be sensitive to the star-forming gas in the host LBG and its close companions.   - These measurements represent the first spatially-resolved observations at $z \approx 3-4$ of the circumgalactic medium and star-forming regions surrounding LBGs.   - These measurements complement ongoing ALMA observing programs at lower redshift.

In this paper, we present a reanalysis of the Higgs-boson decay into two gluons (H $\to$ gg) at the $\alpha_s^6$-order level using the principle of maximum conformality (PMC). The PMC is a powerful method for resumming large perturbative corrections in gauge theories, and has been successfully applied to various processes in high energy physics. By implementing the PMC, we are able to improve the perturbative convergence and achieve better theoretical precision for the H $\to$ gg decay. Our results are in good agreement with existing calculations and provide valuable insights for future studies of the Higgs boson and its interactions with the strong force. The Higgs boson, discovered at the Large Hadron Collider in 2012, is a fundamental particle in the Standard Model of particle physics. It plays a crucial role in the mechanism of electroweak symmetry breaking, which gives mass to the W and Z bosons and all other elementary particles. The Higgs boson can decay into various final states, and the decay into two gluons (H $\to$ gg) is one of the dominant channels. However, due to the complexity of quantum chromodynamics (QCD), the theoretical prediction of this decay is challenging and requires higher-order perturbative calculations.

The principle of maximum conformality (PMC) is a novel approach for resumming large perturbative corrections in gauge theories. It is based on the observation that the perturbative expansion of physical observables should be independent of the renormalization scale. By imposing this scale-independence condition, the PMC determines the optimal scale for each perturbative order, which minimizes the dependence on the unphysical scale parameter and leads to improved perturbative convergence. The PMC has been applied to a wide range of processes in high energy physics, including hadron production, electroweak precision measurements, and top quark physics.

In this paper, we reanalyze the Higgs-boson decay H $\to$ gg up to the $\alpha_s^6$-order level using the PMC. Our goal is to improve the perturbative convergence and achieve better theoretical precision for this process. In conclusion, we have presented a reanalysis of the Higgs-boson decay H $\to$ gg up to the $\alpha_s^6$-order level using the principle of maximum conformality (PMC). The PMC is a powerful method for resumming large perturbative corrections in gauge theories and has been successfully applied to various processes in high energy physics. By implementing the PMC, we have been able to improve the perturbative convergence and achieve better theoretical precision for the H $\to$ gg decay. Our results are in good agreement with existing calculations and provide valuable insights for future studies of the Higgs boson and its interactions with the strong force.

This document provides an overview of graphical functions and single-valued multiple polylogarithms, including their definitions, properties, and applications in various fields of mathematics and physics. The concept of graphical functions is introduced and the relationship between graphical functions and single-valued multiple polylogarithms is explored. The paper also discusses the use of these functions in the study of quantum field theory and their connection to other mathematical objects such as knot invariants and Feynman integrals. Graphical functions, also known as Schnetz functions, are a class of functions that can be represented by graphs in a particular way. These functions were introduced by Marcelo Aguiar and Francis Brown in their paper "Graphical functions and single-valued multiple polylogarithms" as a tool to study multiple zeta values and other special functions in mathematics and physics.

A graphical function is a function of the form F(G), where G is a graph with vertices and edges labeled by positive integers. The value of the function F is determined by summing over all possible ways of assigning values to the edges of the graph such that the sum of the values at each vertex is equal to the label of the vertex.

Single-valued multiple polylogarithms, also known as iterated integrals, are a class of functions that can be represented by graphs in a similar way as graphical functions. These functions are defined as iterated integrals of the form

L_G(x_1,...,x_n) = \int_0^{x_1} \int_0^{x_{2}} ... \int_0^{x_n} f(t_1,...,t_n) dt_1...dt_n

where G is a graph with vertices and edges labeled by positive integers and f is a function of the variables t_1,...,t_n.

It is possible to express single-valued multiple polylogarithms in terms of graphical functions using a special type of graph known as a Feynman graph. Feynman graphs are used in quantum field theory to represent the perturbative expansion of a quantum mechanical amplitude or probability. Graphical functions and single-valued multiple polylogarithms are important mathematical objects with a wide range of applications in fields such as quantum field theory, knot theory, and the study of special functions. These functions are related through the use of Feynman graphs and have connections to other mathematical objects such as multiple zeta values and iterated integrals. Further research on graphical functions and single-valued multiple polylogarithms has the potential to deepen our understanding of these mathematical concepts and their applications in various fields.

We present a benchmarking approach to measure the performance of deep learning models on large healthcare datasets. We compare the performance of our benchmarking approach to a set of large medical data sets, which are currently being studied by various medical organizations. We find that our benchmarking approach is able to achieve a reasonable and accurate diagnosis of patients with a wide range of medical conditions. The Importance of Deep Learning for Data ScienceDeep learning is a powerful tool for many applications. It can be used to model, analyze, and improve the human body, health, and well-being. However, it can also be used to perform many other tasks, such as diagnosis, treatment, and prevention. In this paper, we propose a new class of deep learning models that are able to outperform traditional models for tasks such as visual recognition, face detection, and speech recognition. Our class of models is based on a set of deep learning models with a high-dimensional feature space. We show that our class outperforms the existing class by a factor of 10. The recent development of Rigid Objects (RODs) has generated significant interest in the field of robotic pose tracking. The goal of this paper is to study the potential of the new Rigid Objects (RODs) as a new tool for the analysis of robotic pose tracking. To this end, we present a wide-area, low-latency, and power-efficient 6-DoF Pose Tracking System (6-DOFPS) for Rigid Objects (RODs) with the goal of providing an accurate and robust estimation of the pose of a rigid object. The 6-DOFPS is designed to accurately measure the pose of a rigid object by taking into account the angular momentum of the object and the angular momentum of the rotation axis. The accuracy of the estimation is assessed using a series of six different pose-specific methods, including the 3D pose estimation method, the 3D pose estimation method, and the pose estimation method. We also present a method to directly measure the pose of a rigid object by applying the same pose estimation method to the 6-DOFPS. We evaluate the performance of the 6-DOFPS against other existing pose estimation methods on a variety of challenging object classification tasks. Our results show that the 6-DOFPS is capable of performing a large-scale, accurate and robust estimation of the pose of a rigid object. The hyperfine coupling constant of the cesium 7S1/2 state is an important parameter in the study of atomic physics. In this work, we describe a method for determining the hyperfine coupling constant using laser spectroscopy. Our method involves measuring the frequency splitting between the hyperfine levels of the cesium 7S1/2 state and fitting the data to a theoretical model. The resulting value for the hyperfine coupling constant is in good agreement with previously reported values. The hyperfine structure of an atomic energy level is due to the interaction between the magnetic moments of the nucleus and the electrons. In cesium, the 7S1/2 state is particularly interesting because it is the ground state of the atom and has a relatively large hyperfine coupling constant. This coupling constant can be determined by measuring the frequency splitting between the hyperfine levels of the 7S1/2 state and fitting the data to a theoretical model.

There are several methods that can be used to measure the hyperfine splitting in cesium, including microwave spectroscopy and laser spectroscopy. In this work, we describe a method based on laser spectroscopy. Our approach involves driving transitions between the hyperfine levels of the 7S1/2 state using a laser and measuring the resulting frequency splitting. We have described a method for determining the hyperfine coupling constant of the cesium 7S1/2 state using laser spectroscopy. Our method involves measuring the frequency splitting between the hyperfine levels of the 7S1/2 state and fitting the data to a theoretical model. The resulting value for the hyperfine coupling constant is in good agreement with previously reported values. This method provides a reliable and accurate way to determine the hyperfine coupling constant of the cesium 7S1/2 state, which is important for a variety of applications in atomic physics.

This article examines the influence of the mouth pressure dynamics on the regime change thresholds of flute-like instruments. The study makes use of data obtained from the first four years of recording, by the French Musical Conservatory, of the flute-like instrument called the “Mister F”. The pressure data are analysed directly by the use of the Hurst exponent (H) and detrended fluctuation analysis (DFA). The results obtained by this direct analysis are compared to those obtained from a previously used indirect method proposed by the author. This indirect method makes use of the linear relationship predicted by the Bernoulli’s law that relates the mouth pressure to the flute-like instrument opening opening. The study found (i) that, in contrast what one might expect, H does not show an increasing trend with respect to the number of regimes; (ii) that the direct and indirect methods lead to similar results; (iii) that the first value of H varies with respect to the operating regime: values of H are less than 1/2 in the high-order regime while values of H are higher than 1/2 in the low-order regime; and (iv) that the method proposed by the author is reliable in estimating the regime change thresholds when 2≤H≤0.7, as is the case for the data of the flute-like instrument called “Mister F,” since in this regime the opening is limited and the pressure data exhibit an approximately linear trend. The results obtained in the present study indicate that the H-method proposed by the author is reliable in estimating the regime change thresholds in flute-like instruments where H does not show an increasing trend to an infinity with an increasing number of regimes. The present article examines the influence of the mouth pressure dynamics of flute-like instruments on the regime change thresholds. The study makes use of data obtained from a flute-like instrument called “Mister” by the French Musical Conservatory. The pressure data are analysed directly by the application of the Hurst exponent (H) and the detrended fluctuation analysis (DFA; [1]). The results obtained by this direct analysis is compared to those obtained from a previously used indirect (but not completely independent) method proposed by the author [2]. This indirect method makes use of the linear relation predicted by the Bernoulli’s law that relates mouth pressure to flute-like instrument opening. The aim of the study is to investigate the reliability of the method proposed by the author and to compare the results obtained by the direct and indirect methods. The method proposed by the author is based on the fact that, in the flute-like instruments, the opening of the instrument is limited by the opening of the mouth. Therefore, the opening of the instrument can be considered as a function of the opening of the mouth. The opening of the mouth can be considered as a random process with an approximately linear trend. Therefore, the opening of the instrument can also be considered as a random process with an approximately random trend. The regime change thresholds in flute-like instruments are defined as the values of the opening of the mouth that lead to a change in the opening trend of the instrument. (i) The results obtained in the present study indicate that, in contrast what one might expect, the Hurst exponent (H) does not show an increasing trend with respect to a number of regimes. (ii) The direct and indirect methods lead to similar results. (iii) The first value of H varies with respect to the opening regime: values of H are less than 0.5 in the high-order regime and values of H are higher than 0.5 in the low-order regime. (iv) The method proposed by the author is reliable in estimating regime change thresholds when 2≤H≤1. (v) The results obtained in the present study indicate the method proposed by the author is reliable in determining the regime change thresholds in flute-like instrument where H does not show an increasing trend to infinity with an increasing number of regimes. In this paper, we first discuss the limitations of most weakly supervised object localization methods from the perspective of a general concept of contrastive learning. We find that these methods suffer from an inherent problem of complementary loss. In order to alleviate this problem, we make the following contributions: 1) We discover a new perspective that all weakly supervised learning problems can be described as a complementary learning framework. 2) We propose a novel adversarial complementary learning framework to learn complementary information, and propose novel complementary learning modules to tackle the challenge of the complementary learning problem. Our approach has been evaluated with several state-of-the-art weakly supervised object localization datasets and achieved the best results, showing the effectiveness. The code will be released online. The task of weakly supervised object localization (WSL) is to localize a target object in a given image. This problem is extremely challenging due to the lack of a large annotated dataset and the limited number of annotations. In most cases, the target object is not annotated in the training set. Therefore, it is necessary to learn a model that can predict the location of the object from the unlabeled data. In recent years, a large number of weakly supervised learning methods have been proposed [1, 2, 3, 4, ]. In these methods, the supervision is provided by the object annotations in the training set, and the model is trained by optimizing the localization accuracy. These methods can be divided into two main categories: the single-shot and the multiple-shot approaches. The single-shot methods train a model from scratch, which requires a large amount of training data. In contrast, the multiple-shot methods train the model with the assistance of a pre-trained model, which requires fewer annotations.  The core of these methods is to learn a discriminative representation that can distinguish the target object from the background. The commonly used methods in this category include the two-stage methods [5, 6, 7] and the one-stage methods [8,, 9, 10]. The two-stage methods [11, 12, 13, 14] first detect the object and then localize it. The one-stage methods [15,, 16] directly learn a discriminative model. Among the single-shot methods, the most widely used one is the multiple-instance learning (MIL) approach [17, 18, 19, 20, 21]. In this approach, the target object is treated as a bag of instances and the model is trained to distinguish the target object from the background by training the model on the bag of instances.  The core of these methods is the discriminative representation. However, these methods suffer from the following problems. First, the discriminative representation is usually learned from the image-level features. These methods do not fully exploit the semantic information of the image, which limits the performance of the model. Second, these methods do not fully utilize the complementary information between different instances. The complementary information between instances can provide useful information for the localization task. For example, in the case of two instances, if we can learn the complementary information between the two instances, then we can obtain a more accurate prediction. In this paper, we first discuss the limitations from the perspective of a general concept of contrastive loss. We find that these methods suffer from an intrinsic problem of complementary loss. To alleviate this problem, we make the following contributions. 1) We discover a new perspective that most weakly supervised learning problems can be described as a complement learning framework. 2) We propose a new adversarial complementary learning framework to learn complementary information, which consists of two complementary learning modules. 3) We conduct experiments on several popular weakly supervised object localization datasets and achieve the best results. The code will be released online.    Contributions:   We propose a novel adversarial complementary learning framework for weakly supervised object localization. The proposed framework consists of two complementary learning modules. The first complementary learning module is designed to learn the complementary information between different instances. The second complementary learning module is designed to learn the complementary representation. We conduct experiments on several popular weakly supervised object datasets and achieve the best results.    Related Work:    Weakly Supervised Object Localization:    In recent years, a large number of weakly-supervised object localization methods have been proposed. Most of these methods can be divided into two categories: the single-shot and the multiple shot methods. The single-shot methods train a model directly from the image-level features. The multiple-shot methods train a model with the assistance of a pre-trained detector. The core of these methods is the discriminative representation that can distinguish the target object from other objects. The commonly used methods in this category include two-stage methods [22, 23, 24], one-stage methods [25, 26, 27, 28,, 29, 30, 31]. The two-stage methods (e.g., DeepMask [32]) first detect the object and then localize it. One-stage methods (e.g. [33,  34]) directly learn the discriminative model. Among the single-shot methods [35], the most widely used one is the multiple instance learning (MIL) approach []. In this approach, the target object can be treated as a bag of instances. Rindler space is a concept in special relativity that describes the frame of reference of an observer accelerating through empty space. The Unruh effect, named after physicist Bill Unruh, refers to the phenomenon that an accelerating observer will detect a thermal bath of radiation, even in a vacuum. This effect demonstrates the intimate connection between acceleration and temperature in the context of special relativity. In special relativity, the concept of a frame of reference plays a crucial role in understanding how physical phenomena are perceived by different observers. One such frame of reference is known as Rindler space, which represents the perspective of an observer accelerating through empty space.

Rindler space is defined by a set of coordinate axes that are accelerated with respect to a stationary observer. This acceleration leads to the phenomenon known as the Unruh effect, which states that an accelerating observer will detect a thermal bath of radiation, even in a vacuum. This effect is a direct consequence of the intimate connection between acceleration and temperature in the context of special relativity. The Rindler space and Unruh effect provide a unique perspective on the nature of special relativity and the relationship between acceleration and temperature. These concepts have important implications for our understanding of the fundamental nature of the universe and continue to be the subject of much theoretical and experimental study.

In the near-future, climate change affects coastal environments throughout the world. Climate-change projections can, however, only be valid for some time. Since climate forecasts have large uncertainties, adaptation options depend on the availability of robust decision-making strategies that can respond to the worst possible scenario. The present study addresses the problem of identifying robust strategies in the adaptation to the simultaneous presence of sea level rise (SLR) and storm surge. To this end, a Direct Policy Search (DPS) method is combined with a three-dimensional hydrodynamic coastal model to determine the optimal adaptive action for the case of a coastal area in the Netherlands under two alternative climate futures: a business-as-usual future with the average climate and a future that assumes a maximum SLR and storm surge. The results enable decision makers to better understand how policy options could affect the coastal environment of a selected coastal area, taking into account uncertainties and the expected future environment. - The Netherlands is an example of a coastal region in which sea level rise and storm surges are likely to increase in the future [1].   - In the past decades, coastal areas have been exposed to increasing storm surges and sea level rise.   - In the future, coastal areas are expected to be exposed to a combination of sea level rise and storm surges.   - The present study addresses the problem of identifying robust adaptive strategies in the future that can be implemented in coastal areas exposed to sea level rise and storm surges. - The present study shows that the use of robust decision-making strategies can be beneficial for decision makers in the future.   - Robust adaptive strategies that can be implemented in coastal areas exposed to storm surges and sea level rise are identified.   - Robust adaptive strategies that can be applied in coastal areas exposed to sea level rise and a combination of storm surges and sea level rise are identified as well.

In this study, we investigate the $DN$, $\pi \Sigma_c$ interaction in finite volume and the resulting $\Lambda_c(2595)$ resonance using lattice QCD simulations. We find that the finite volume effects on the $\Lambda_c(2595)$ resonance are small, but non-negligible. We also observe a strong dependence of the resonance properties on the pion mass, suggesting the importance of chiral dynamics in the $DN$, $\pi \Sigma_c$ interaction. Our results provide valuable insights into the nature of the $\Lambda_c(2595)$ resonance and the $DN$, $\pi \Sigma_c$ interaction in the charm sector. The charm sector of the baryon spectrum has attracted much attention in recent years due to the discovery of several new states that do not fit well into the conventional quark model. Among these states, the $\Lambda_c(2595)$ resonance, which was observed in the $DN$ and $\pi \Sigma_c$ channel, has garnered particular interest due to its narrow width and proximity to the $DN$ threshold.

In order to understand the nature of the $\Lambda_c(2595)$ resonance and the underlying $DN$, $\pi \Sigma_c$ interaction, it is important to study these systems in finite volume using lattice QCD simulations. Finite volume effects can significantly alter the properties of resonances, and it is therefore important to carefully consider these effects when interpreting lattice QCD results.
