A series of novel sandwiched phosphide binary compounds, αP2Si ( αP-2Si ), αP4S3 ( αP-4S ), αP6N9 ( αP-6N ), αP6N12 ( αP-6N ), αP3C6 ( αP-3C ), αP3C82( αP-3C ), αP2C6 ( αP-2C ), βP3C6 ( βP-3C ), βP4N3( βP-4N ), βP4N62( βP-4N ), βP6O112( βP-6O ) and αP6O122( αP-6O ). Based on density functional theory combined with van der Waals correction, these 17 layered-structured phosphide compounds are systematically investigated for their structural, electronic and magnetic properties. All the calculated results demonstrate that the novel phosphide compounds have the direct band gaps in the range of 0.31–1.20 eV and the maximum optical absorption coefficient in the range of 6.8×10 5 ∼4.4×10 6 cm −1. Furthermore, the bulk moduli and Vickers hardness show that the αP-2Si, αP-4S and αP-6N 3D compounds are more robust and harder than other layered phosphide structures. Most importantly, anisotropic properties of these novel phosphide compounds sheets are determined by investigating their optical limiting performances. These sandwiched structures could be a promising candidate for high-efficient light-emitting diodes and solar cells. Layered materials have been widely studied in recent years due to their promising applications in optoelectronics, sensors, spintronics and other electronic devices [1–4]. The two-dimensional (2D) materials, which are also called the 2D materials, have attracted great attention because of their unique electronic properties [5–13]. Recently, the 2D phosphorene has been successfully synthesized [14]. The phosphorene, as a new 2D material, has been widely investigated because of its unique properties and potential applications in electronic devices. However, it is difficult to synthesize the 2D phosphorene with a thickness of less than 10 nm, which is much thinner than the traditional semiconductors such as silicon and germanium [15]. In order to obtain the 2D phosphorene with a desired thickness, it is necessary to prepare the 2D phosphide binary compounds.  The phosphide binary compounds, which have been widely studied due to their special physical properties and potential applications in solar cells, light-emitting diodes, thermoelectrics and so on [16–19], can be considered as the extension of the phosphorene. However, the 2D phosphide binary compounds have not been reported yet. Recently, the 2D phosphide ternary compounds have been synthesized [20]. For example, the 2D P2Si, P4S3, P4N3 and P6N9 ternary compounds were synthesized by the mechanical exfoliation method [20]. The theoretical research on the 2D phosphide ternary compounds has been carried out [21]. However, the 2D phosphide quaternary compounds have not been reported yet. Therefore, it is necessary to synthesize the 2D phosphide quaternary compounds.  In this work, a series of novel 2D phosphide quaternary compounds, αP2Si ( α-P2Si ), αP4S2 ( α-P4S ), αP6N6 ( α-P6N ), αP6N9 3D ( α-P6N ) and αP3C6 ( α-P3C ), αP3C62 ( α-P3C ) 2D, βP3C6 ( β-P3C ) 2D and αP2C6 ( α-P2C ) 2D were successfully synthesized by the mechanical exfoliation method. The structural, electronic, magnetic and optical properties of these novel 2D phosphide quaternary compounds were systematically investigated by the first-principles method combined with the van der Waals correction. The results demonstrate that the novel 2D phosphide quaternary compounds are stable and can be used in the electronic devices. The 17 novel 2D phosphide quaternary compounds, including αP2Si ( α-P-2Si ), αP-4S ( α-P-4S ), αP-6N ( α-P-6N ), αP-6N 2D ( α-P-6N 2D ), αP-3C ( α-P-3C ), αP-3C 2D, βP-3C ( β-P-3C ), βP-4N ( β-P-4N ), βP-4N 2D, βP-6O ( β-P-6O ), αP-6O ( α-P-6O ) and αP-6O 2D, were successfully synthesized by the mechanical exfoliation method, which is the first time to synthesize the 2D phosphide quaternary compound. The 2D phosphide quaternary compounds are stable. The αP-2Si, αP-6N and αP-3C 3D compounds are more robust and harder. Most importantly, the novel 2D phosphide quaternary compounds have the direct band gaps in the range from 0.31 eV to 1.20 eV. The maximum optical absorption coefficient in the range of 1.6×10 5 ∼4.4 ×10 6 cm −1. Furthermore the bulk moduli and Vickers hardness show that all the 2D phosphide quaternary compounds are more robust and harder than the other 2D phosphide compounds.    The 17 novel 20 2D phosphide quaternary compounds have the anisotropic properties. These sandwiched structures could be a promising candidate of high-efficient light-emitting diodes and solar cell.

The study of turbulent combustion is essential for the development of clean and efficient energy systems. In this paper, we present a jet-stirred apparatus designed for turbulent combustion experiments. The apparatus consists of a cylindrical reactor equipped with a fuel injector and a stirrer. A high-speed camera is used to visualize the combustion process in real-time. The apparatus is capable of operating under a wide range of conditions, including varying fuel compositions and flow rates. We present results from initial experiments showing the effect of stirrer speed on the flame structure and heat release rate. The jet-stirred apparatus provides a useful platform for studying turbulent combustion and has the potential to improve our understanding of this important process. Turbulent combustion is a complex process that occurs when a fuel is mixed with an oxidizer and ignited in a turbulent flow. It is an important process in a variety of applications, including internal combustion engines, gas turbines, and industrial boilers. However, despite its importance, the fundamental mechanisms of turbulent combustion are not fully understood, and it remains a challenging area of research.

One of the main difficulties in studying turbulent combustion is the need to generate a well-controlled and reproducible flow field. To address this challenge, researchers have developed a variety of experimental apparatuses, including jet-stirred reactors. Jet-stirred reactors consist of a cylindrical vessel equipped with a fuel injector and a stirrer, which generates a turbulent flow field by injecting a jet of fuel into the reactor. The jet-stirred reactor is a useful platform for studying turbulent combustion because it allows for the generation of a well-controlled flow field and the measurement of key parameters such as flame structure, heat release rate, and species concentrations.

In this paper, we present a jet-stirred apparatus designed for turbulent combustion experiments. The apparatus consists of a cylindrical reactor equipped with a fuel injector and a stirrer. A high-speed camera is used to visualize the combustion process in real-time. The apparatus is capable of operating under a wide range of conditions, including varying fuel compositions and flow rates. We present results from initial experiments showing the effect of stirrer speed on the flame structure and heat release rate. In conclusion, we have presented a jet-stirred apparatus for turbulent combustion experiments. The apparatus consists of a cylindrical reactor equipped with a fuel injector and a stirrer, and is capable of operating under a wide range of conditions. We have presented results from initial experiments showing the effect of stirrer speed on the flame structure and heat release rate. The jet-stirred apparatus provides a useful platform for studying turbulent combustion and has the potential to improve our understanding of this important process.

The g-factor anisotropy of hole quantum wires induced by the Rashba interaction is a phenomenon that has attracted significant attention in the field of condensed matter physics. In this paper, we present a theoretical investigation of the g-factor anisotropy of hole quantum wires in the presence of Rashba spin-orbit coupling. Using a combination of analytical and numerical methods, we show that the g-factor anisotropy is strongly dependent on the strength of the Rashba interaction and the wire geometry. Our results have important implications for the design and optimization of spintronic devices based on hole quantum wires. The g-factor, also known as the Landé g-factor, is a fundamental property of a quantum particle that determines its magnetic moment in the presence of an external magnetic field. In the case of electrons, the g-factor is approximately 2, but it can vary significantly for different materials and physical systems. In recent years, there has been growing interest in the g-factor anisotropy of quantum wires, which refers to the difference in the g-factor between different directions in the wire.

One mechanism that can give rise to g-factor anisotropy is the Rashba spin-orbit interaction, which arises due to the coupling between the spin and the momentum of the particles. This interaction is important in a variety of physical systems, including quantum wires, where it can lead to interesting phenomena such as spin-dependent transport and spin precession. In particular, the Rashba interaction has been shown to significantly affect the g-factor anisotropy of quantum wires, leading to the possibility of controlling the g-factor by tuning the Rashba interaction.

In this paper, we present a theoretical investigation of the g-factor anisotropy of hole quantum wires in the presence of Rashba spin-orbit coupling. We consider a two-dimensional hole gas confined to a quantum wire and use a combination of analytical and numerical methods to study the g-factor anisotropy as a function of the Rashba interaction strength and the wire geometry. Our results provide insight into the role of the Rashba interaction in determining the g-factor anisotropy of hole quantum wires and have important implications for the design and optimization of spintronic devices based on these systems. In conclusion, we have presented a theoretical study of the g-factor anisotropy of hole quantum wires in the presence of Rashba spin-orbit coupling. Using a combination of analytical and numerical methods, we have shown that the g-factor anisotropy is strongly dependent on the strength of the Rashba interaction and the wire geometry. Our results have important implications for the design and optimization of spintronic devices based on hole quantum wires, and provide insight into the role of the Rashba interaction in determining the g-factor anisotropy of these systems.

For the production of high current proton beams the production and acceleration of higher energy ion beams have significant advantages, especially for medical applications where high current pencil beams as demanded by clinical therapy are necessary. Two production systems with the cyclotron as a common driver are presently under construction in the Netherlands: a coupled cyclotron with its accelerator, DAEdALUS, and a tandem with its accelerator, IsoDAR. In this paper we report on the results obtained for the physics design of both systems. For the tandem the design of the production ring, the design of the magnetic rigidity selection with the beam collimation system and the design of the target chamber are discussed in detail. With reference to the IsoDAR project we present a conceptual design of the accelerator based on existing technology. For the DAEdALUS project a conceptual design of the production ring and for the cyclotron machine the initial design of the magnets and the machine layout were completed. The resulting production intensity of 2.7 A and the high efficiency and flexibility offer the DAEdALUS concept as an interesting alternative for the production of highly intense ion beams for hadrontherapy applications. For the production of high current proton beams with energies of a few hundred MeV/u the production and acceleration of higher energy ion beams has significant advantages, especially for medical applications where high currents are necessary.   Two production systems with the cyclotron as a common   driver are presently under construction in the. For the tandem the design of the production ring is discussed in detail. The design of the magnetic rigidity selection with the beam collimator and the design of the target chamber are discussed.   With reference to the IsoDAR project a conceptual design of the accelerator is presented. The production ring is designed to produce a beam of 2.7 A with a high efficiency.   For the cyclotron machine the initial design of the magnets was completed. The resulting production intensity is 2.3 A and the high efficiency and flexibility offer the IsoDAR concept as an interesting alternative for the production of high intensity ion beams for hadrontherapy applications.

We consider some classes of operators of type  $$A\bar u=\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}f(x,s)\bar u(x-s)\,ds$$  defined in L2(Rd) as operators  $$A_\varepsilon :L_2 \left( {\mathbb {R}}^d \right) \to L_2 \left( {\mathbb {R}}^d \right)$$ , where  $$f_\varepsilon (x,s)$$  converges to f uniformly in  $$\left\{ (x,s)\in{\mathbb {R}}^d\times {\mathbb {R}}: |s|<c\;\left| x\right|^{\theta }\right\} $$ , and we show that  $$A_\varepsilon \stackrel{\text {as }\varepsilon \rightarrow 0}{\longrightarrow }A$$  weakly, where A is a pseudo-differential operator  $$A=\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x,s)e^{i\Psi \left( x,y,\frac{s}{\rho (x)}\right) }\,ds$$  with  $$\Psi \left( x,y,\xi \right)=\int_0^1 a^t(t y)\xi dt$$. $$     \begin{aligned}     \text{ Let } f_\varepsilon (x,s) = \begin{cases}         \varepsilon^{-d} f(\varepsilon x,\varepsilon s), & |s|<c\;\varepsilon^\theta \\         0, & |s|\ge c\;\varepsilon^\theta     \end{cases}     \end{aligned} $$   Let $A_\varepsilon$ be defined by   $$     \begin{alignedat}{4}      A_\varepsilon u &= \int_{-\infty}^\infty f_\varepsilon (x,s-y)u(x-y)\,dy \\     &= \varepsilon^{-d} \int_{-\infty} ^\infty f(\varepsilon x,\varepsilon s-\varepsilon y)u(x-y)\,d(\varepsilon y) \\     &= \varepsilon ^{-d} \int_\mathbb R f(\varepsilon x,\varepsilon s - \varepsilon y)u(x-y/\varepsilon)\,d(\varepsilon y).     \end{alignedat} $$   If $\theta<1$ and $\varepsilon<1$, then $f_\varepsilon$ is a sequence of smooth functions with compact support in the variable $s$ and $f_\varepsilon$ converges uniformly to $f$ in the set $\{(x,s)\in{\bf R}^d\times {\bf R}: |s|<c\,|x|^\theta\}$. Therefore, by the dominated convergence theorem, $A_\varepsilon \stackrel{\rm{w}}{\longrightarrow}A$.    This is the case in the paper "[Homogenization of a Convolution Type Operator](http://dx.doi.org/10.1090/S0002-9947-1975-0220784-5)" by B.-Y. Chen, L.-Y. Chen, S.-C. Chou, C.-C. Liu, and W.-Y. Yeh.    The above example shows that it is not sufficient to assume that $f_\varepsilon$ converges uniformly in $\left\{ (x,s,y)\in{\mathbb {R^d}\times \mathbb {R}\times \mathbb {R^d}}: |s|<c|x|^\theta \right\} $. $A_\varepsilon \stackrel{{\rm{w}}}{\longrightarrow}A$ holds for pseudo-differential operators $A$ of the form $A=\int_{-\infty }^{\infty } f(x,s)e^i\Psi \left( x,s,\xi \right) \,ds$ with $\Psi $ as above and $f$ satisfying the following conditions:   1) $f$ is bounded and uniformly continuous in $\left\{ (s,y)\in \mathbb {R}\times\mathbb {R^d}: |y|<c|s|^{\theta }\right \} $;  2) $\lim_{|s|\rightarrow \infty} f(x,s)=0$ uniformly in $x$.

The paper introduces an integral representation of Runge–Kutta convolution coercive functions and uses this to establish boundary value properties for time-dependent BVPs of the form Δu + Au′ = φ(t), u(0) = u(T) = 0. An alternative viewpoint is used to study the coercivity properties of time convolution operators and a number of known results are recovered via our framework in the special case where A is a Fourier multiplier with non-negative symbol. Finally, by combining our framework with the existing theory for integral equation regularization, an improved stability result is obtained which does not require strict hyperbolicity of the boundary operator. In this way we show that time-dependent BVPs of the form Δu + Au′ = u can be solved with regularization which is optimal in that the number N of iteration steps are allowed to grow exponentially with the dimension d of the domain. The goal of this paper is to study the coercivity properties of time convolution operators of the form   \[ \begin{matrix} & T_{\alpha} u(t) = \int_{0}^{t} u(t-s) \alpha(s) ds, \end{matrix} \]  where α : [0,. This paper provides a new perspective on the coercivity properties of time convolution operators and uses this to establish boundary value properties for the abstract time-dependent BVP    Δu + Au′ = φ (t), u(0) - u(T) = 0.    The paper uses the following notation:     A convolution operator Tα is called coercive if there exists a constant C such that    \[ \int_{0}^{T} \alpha(s)^{2} ds \geq C \| T_{\alpha} \| ^{2}. \]    A convolution operator Tα is said to be convolution coercive if there exists a constant C such that for all u ∈ L2[END_SUP](0,T),    \[ \int_0^{T} \alpha(s+t)^{2} ds \geq \|T_{\alpha}u\|^{2} + C \|u\|^{2}. \]       The paper is organized as follows:    Section 2:      The paper introduces an integral representation of Runge-Kutta convolution coercive functions and uses this to establish a number of known results.    Section 3:      The paper uses the results in Section 2 to establish boundary value properties for the abstract time dependent BVP    Δu + Au′(t) = φ (t), u′(0) = u′(T) = 0.      Section 4:      By combining our framework with the existing theory for integral equations regularization, an improved stability result is obtained which is not required strict hyperbolicity of the boundary operator. In particular, the number of iteration steps N are allowed to grow exponentially with the dimension d.    The paper concludes with a brief discussion. The cross caps in projective n-space are defined by     $$     C_n(p, q) = \{x \in \mathbb{P}^n: \langle x, p \rangle = \langle x, q \rangle = 0\},     $$   where $\langle \cdot, \cdot \rangle$ denotes the standard scalar product in $\mathbb{R}^{2n+1}$.      We give expressions for the cross caps in projective 4-space which correspond to 4-planes of a binary space of order 4 when n≤4. The cross caps in projective n-spaces are defined by   $$ C_n(p, q) := \{x \in {\mathbb{P}}^n: \langle x, \omega \rangle = 0\} $$  where $\omega$ is a fixed non-zero vector in $\mathbb{R}^n$.  For n=4 we have   $$ C_4(p, q) = \left\{ x \in {\mathbb{P^4}}: \langle x, \omega_1 \rangle \langle x, \omega_2 \rangle \langle x, (\omega_1 + \omega_2)/2 \rangle = 0 \right\}, $$ where $\omega_1, \omega_2$ are two fixed non-zero vectors in $\mathbb{R}^4$.

Graphical models are defined and characterized in terms of the conditional independence properties resulting from conditional independencies among latent variables. This framework is applied to problems of estimating parameters of directed acyclic graphs. In a directed acyclic graph (DAG), the edges represent causal relations. A directed edge from node i to node j indicates that node j is a cause of node i. For example, in the DAG in Figure 1, node 2 is a cause of node 3.    Figure 1: Directed Acyclic Graph    In a Markov Random Field (MRF), the conditional independence properties are expressed in terms of cliques. A clique is a set of nodes that are conditionally independent given the values of the remaining nodes. For example, in Figure 1, the cliques {1,2,3} and {1,2,4} are conditionally independent given the values of the remaining variables.    In a graphical model with latent variables, the conditional independence properties are expressed in terms cliques and latent variables. A clique is a set of nodes that can be conditionally independent given the values of the remaining nodes and the latent variables. For example, in Figure 1, {1,2,3} is a clique given the values of the remaining variables and the latent variable 5.    In a graphical model with cycles, the conditional independence properties are expressed in terms a set of nodes and a set of cycles. A set of nodes is a cycle if any two nodes in the set are connected by a directed path. For example, the set {1,2,3,4,5} is a cycle in Figure 1.    For a graphical model with cycles and latent variables, the conditional independence properties are expressed as follows. A set of nodes is a cycle if some node in the set is a cycle. For example, {1,2,3,4} is a cycle in Figure 1. A set of nodes and a set of cycles are conditionally independent given the values of the remaining set if none of the nodes in the set is a cycle and none of the cycles in the set are contained in the remaining set.    The Markov properties of the graphical model are defined in terms of a set of nodes and a set of cycles, where the set of cycles is a subset of the set of nodes. The Markov properties of a graphical model are defined in terms of a set   of nodes and a set of cycles, where a set of cycles is a subset of the   set of nodes. The Markov properties of the graphical model are the conditional   independence properties implied by the conditional independencies among   latent variables and the conditional independencies among the nodes given the   latent variables.

We investigate the impact of the Young integral of a compact black hole on the evolution of the black hole's mass and evolution of the Brownian motion. We show that the Young integral of a compact black hole is strongly correlated with the mass of the black hole, and that the Brownian motion is strongly correlated with the mass of the black hole. In particular, we find that the Young integral of a compact black hole can be strongly correlated with the mass of the black hole, and that the Brownian motion can be strongly correlated with the mass of the black hole. This paper is organized as follows. In Section 2, we present the main results of the study of the interaction between the two young integral and the long-memory fractional Brownian motion. We will begin by introducing the concepts of hyperbolic tilings and formal language theory, and then delve into the ways in which these two fields intersect. We will also discuss some of the applications of this intersection, including its use in the study of complex systems and the construction of efficient data structures. Hyperbolic tilings are geometric structures that can be used to model the geometry of certain types of curved spaces. These tilings are made up of a repeating pattern of shapes, which can be thought of as tiles that fit together to form a larger structure. Formal language theory is a branch of mathematics that deals with the study of formal languages and their properties. A formal language is a set of strings of symbols that are used to represent information or to communicate with machines.

The connection between hyperbolic tilings and formal language theory lies in the fact that these two fields both deal with the manipulation and analysis of patterns. In hyperbolic tilings, the patterns are geometric in nature, while in formal language theory, the patterns are symbolic. By studying the ways in which these two types of patterns interact, we can gain insight into the underlying structure of both fields.

One area where the intersection of hyperbolic tilings and formal language theory has proven particularly useful is in the study of complex systems. Complex systems are systems that are made up of a large number of interacting components, and which exhibit emergent behavior. Examples of complex systems include biological ecosystems, social networks, and financial markets. By using techniques from both hyperbolic tilings and formal language theory, researchers have been able to better understand the structure and behavior of complex systems, and to develop algorithms and models that can be used to predict and control their behavior.

Another application of the intersection between hyperbolic tilings and formal language theory is in the construction of efficient data structures. Data structures are the ways in which data is organized and stored in a computer, and the choice of data structure can have a significant impact on the performance of an algorithm. By using techniques from both fields, researchers have been able to design data structures that are both efficient and robust, and which can be used to solve a wide range of problems. In this document, we have explored the connections between hyperbolic tilings and formal language theory, and discussed some of the applications of this intersection. We have seen that by combining the tools and techniques of these two fields, researchers have been able to gain insights into the structure and behavior of complex systems, and to design efficient data structures. There are many potential directions for future research in this area, and we can expect to see continued progress in our understanding of the relationships between hyperbolic tilings and formal language theory.

A0535+26 (the Be/X-ray transient Aql X-1) has been observed by ASCA, RXTE, and RXTE JET from February 1996 to February 1997. Spectral analysis of the ASCA data shows that the X-ray spectrum is similar to that of the normal X-ray binaries. The mass of the neutron star in A0535+26 is 1.9 ± 0.3 M⊙, and its distance is less than 3 kpc (1σ uncertainty) based on RXTE spectral shape. The spectral analysis also confirms that the neutron star accretes material from a Be star. The RXTE JET observations also reveal that A0535+26 exhibits a strong flare in early November 1997, during which the total X-ray flux increased by a factor of 30. In December 1998, the X-ray flux decreased by a factor of 4 and the burst in September 1996 appeared in July 1997. This variation of the X-ray flux suggests that the neutron star in A0535+26 switches between an active and a quiescent state. The mass accretion rate during the flare is about four times larger than that of the quiescent periods which appears to be caused by either the increase in the Be star mass-loss rate or the increase in the spin frequency of the neutron star. The quiescent luminosity is about twice the Eddington limit of a neutron star with the same mass and radius and it is lower than the flux calculated from the extrapolation of the spectral shape in the ASCA observation. This suggests that the accretion disk has an inner edge at a radius of about 3Rg, where Rg is the Schwarzschild radius of the neutron star. Based on RXTE spectral and timing analysis, A0535+26 was observed in a new state in February 1997. For that state, the mass of the neutron star is in the range 1.9–3.2 M⊙, the rotation period of the neutron star was about 7.5 s, and the accretion rate was as low as 1.0×10−14 –1.5×10−13 M⊙ yr−1. A0535+26 is a high mass X-ray binary system that was discovered by the All Sky Monitor (ASM) on the Rossi X-ray Timing Explorer (RXTE) on 20 February 1996 (Corbet et al., 1996). The source is located at a distance of 2.7 ± 0.4 kpc (1σ uncertainty) and is associated with a Be star (Walter et al., 1996; Clark et al., 1997). The optical counterpart of A0535+26 was found to be a B1-B2Ve star at a distance of 1.9 kpc (Kramer et al., 1997; Clark et al., 1989). The Be star is known to have an equatorial disk of matter with an inclination angle of about 60° (Mennickent et al., 1994; Clark et al., 1979). 1. An integrated theoretical model of ligand binding to the beta-2-adrenergic receptor (beta-2AR) is described. The model allows a detailed analysis of different interactions occurring upon the binding of a beta-2AR-dependent agonist (CGP12177). A Monte Carlo method is used to generate a conformational ensemble of the receptor, whereas a classical reaction and diffusion approach is used for the binding event. The approach is validated using experimental force spectra obtained upon addition of the agonist. It is found that the system is in multiple substates, depending on the location and orientation of the different ligand-protein complexes at the receptor. The model is also used to predict the relative stability of the complex that is occupied by the agonist. It is found to be the most stable upon binding to the second ligand-binding domain. In addition, the model is used to predict the structural changes in the receptor upon binding of the agonist. The theoretical model presented in this article is used to obtain important results concerning the relationship that exists between the ligand affinity and the structural features of the receptor. Understanding of this relationship is important as it relates to the design of compounds with improved binding and cellular activity, while providing insight as to the mechanism of ligand binding in general. In the present article we present an integrated theoretical model of ligand binding to the beta2-adrenergic receptor (beta2AR).    The model allows a detailed analysis of different interactions that occur upon the binding of a beta2AR-dependent agonist (CGP 12177).    The model is validated using experimental force spectra obtained upon addition the agonist.    The model is used to predict the structural changes in beta2AR upon binding of the agonist. - The model is used to predict the relative stability of the complex formed upon binding of the agonist.   - The model is used to predict structural changes in beta2AR upon binding of agonist.   - The model is used in order to predict the relative affinity of different ligands for the beta2AR.   - The model is used in the study of the relationship between the ligand affinity and the structural features of beta2AR.

An attempt is made to explain the anomalous X-ray scattering patterns produced by a dislocation, or more generally, a non-uniform lattice distortion. The theory takes account of the fact that the displacement of an atom from its equilibrium position can take place only in a set of discrete quantum-mechanical ways. It is assumed that such an atom is represented by a group of three or more non-overlapping spheres, with each sphere representing the volume of space available for the displacement of the atom. It is shown that if the distortion of the crystal lattice in each sphere is sufficiently small, the scattering pattern depends essentially on the density of atoms in each sphere. It can also be shown, for any distribution of atoms inside each sphere, for example one in which the displacement occurs in isolated atoms, that scattering may occur only in a set of discrete lines. Code-switching, the practice of alternating between two or more languages in the same conversation, is a common occurrence in multilingual communities. In this paper, we propose a method for generating code-switched sentences using generative adversarial networks (GANs). Our approach involves training a GAN on a dataset of code-switched sentences and using it to generate new, synthetic code-switched sentences. We also demonstrate the effectiveness of our method as a data augmentation technique for code-switched natural language processing tasks. Code-switching is a common practice in multilingual communities, and it has been shown to have a number of linguistic and social functions. Despite its prevalence, code-switching is often underrepresented in natural language processing (NLP) datasets, which can make it difficult to develop NLP models that are robust to code-switching. One potential solution to this problem is data augmentation, which involves generating synthetic data to supplement existing datasets.

In this paper, we propose a method for generating code-switched sentences using generative adversarial networks (GANs). GANs are a type of neural network that are capable of generating synthetic data that is similar to a given dataset. Our approach involves training a GAN on a dataset of code-switched sentences and using it to generate new, synthetic code-switched sentences. We evaluate the effectiveness of our method as a data augmentation technique by training NLP models on both the original dataset and a dataset augmented with synthetic code-switched sentences generated by our GAN. In this paper, we have presented a method for generating code-switched sentences using generative adversarial networks (GANs). Our approach involves training a GAN on a dataset of code-switched sentences and using it to generate new, synthetic code-switched sentences. We have demonstrated the effectiveness of our method as a data augmentation technique for code-switched natural language processing tasks. Future work could include exploring the use of GANs for generating code-switched sentences in other languages, or for generating code-switched sentences with specific linguistic properties.

The electron-phonon coupling strength at metal surfaces plays a crucial role in determining the physical properties of these materials. In this work, we present a method for directly determining the electron-phonon coupling strength at metal surfaces using helium atom scattering. By measuring the Debye-Waller factor of helium atoms scattered off the metal surface, we are able to extract the electron-phonon coupling strength with high accuracy. This method offers a simple and non-invasive way to study the electron-phonon coupling at metal surfaces, and has the potential to provide valuable insights into the mechanisms governing the transport and thermodynamic properties of these materials. The electron-phonon coupling strength at metal surfaces is a key parameter that determines the transport and thermodynamic properties of these materials. It is therefore of great interest to be able to accurately measure and understand this coupling strength. However, traditional methods for studying electron-phonon coupling at metal surfaces often require complex and invasive techniques, such as angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (ARPES) or scanning tunneling microscopy (STM).

In this work, we propose a new method for directly determining the electron-phonon coupling strength at metal surfaces using helium atom scattering. By measuring the Debye-Waller factor of helium atoms scattered off the metal surface, we are able to extract the electron-phonon coupling strength with high accuracy. In conclusion, we have presented a method for directly determining the electron-phonon coupling strength at metal surfaces using helium atom scattering. This method offers a simple and non-invasive way to study the electron-phonon coupling at metal surfaces, and has the potential to provide valuable insights into the mechanisms governing the transport and thermodynamic properties of these materials. Further studies are needed to fully explore the capabilities and limitations of this method, but it holds promise as a powerful tool for understanding the fundamental properties of metal surfaces.

The formation and evolution of stars and planets is a fundamental process in astrophysics, and a key area of study in computational astrophysics. Smoothed particle hydrodynamics (SPH) is a widely-used numerical technique for simulating the hydrodynamic evolution of these systems. In this work, we present SEREN, a new SPH code for star and planet formation simulations. SEREN combines advanced numerical techniques with a comprehensive set of physical models to accurately capture the complex physics of these systems. We discuss the capabilities of SEREN and demonstrate its performance in a series of test simulations. The formation and evolution of stars and planets is a fundamental process in astrophysics, and a key area of study in computational astrophysics. Stars and planets form through the collapse and fragmentation of molecular clouds, which are dense regions of gas and dust in the interstellar medium. The collapse and fragmentation process is driven by the action of gravity, and is accompanied by the release of large amounts of energy in the form of radiation and outflows. Understanding this process requires the ability to simulate the evolution of the gas and dust in molecular clouds on a wide range of scales, from the large-scale dynamics of the cloud down to the small-scale physics of individual protostars and protoplanets.

Smoothed particle hydrodynamics (SPH) is a widely-used numerical technique for simulating the hydrodynamic evolution of astrophysical systems. In SPH, the fluid is represented by a set of discrete particles, and the fluid properties are determined by smoothing the particle properties over a local region. This makes SPH particularly well-suited for simulations of systems with large dynamic range, such as star and planet formation. However, accurate simulations of these systems also require the inclusion of a wide range of physical models, such as radiation transport, magnetic fields, and chemical reactions.

In this work, we present SEREN, a new SPH code for star and planet formation simulations. SEREN combines advanced numerical techniques with a comprehensive set of physical models to accurately capture the complex physics of these systems. We discuss the capabilities of SEREN and demonstrate its performance in a series of test simulations. In summary, we have presented SEREN, a new SPH code for star and planet formation simulations. SEREN combines advanced numerical techniques with a comprehensive set of physical models to accurately capture the complex physics of these systems. We have demonstrated the capabilities of SEREN in a series of test simulations, and shown that it is well-suited for the simulation of these systems. We believe that SEREN will be a valuable tool for researchers studying the formation and evolution of stars and planets.

In this study, we investigate the interplay between charge transfer, hole doping, exchange interaction, and octahedron tilting angle on the conductivity of La1-xSrxFe0.75Ni0.25O3-{\delta} (LSFNO). Our results show that the conductivity of LSFNO is significantly influenced by these factors, with the octahedron tilting angle and exchange interaction being the most important. We also find that the conductivity increases with increasing hole doping, but only up to a certain point. These findings have important implications for the design and optimization of materials for use in electronic and energy-related applications. These findings have important implications for the design and optimization of materials for use in electronic and energy-related applications.

In this document, we will study the structure of $p$-subgroups of the group of units in the integral group ring $\mathbb{Z}G$ for a finite group $G$ and a prime number $p$. We will show that every $p$-subgroup of $\mathbb{Z}G^{\times}$ is isomorphic to a subgroup of $G$ and that the converse is also true under certain conditions. The integral group ring $\mathbb{Z}G$ is a ring constructed from a group $G$ and the ring of integers $\mathbb{Z}$. It is a generalization of the group algebra $\mathbb{C}G$ over the complex numbers and has many important applications in group theory and representation theory.

One interesting problem in the study of $\mathbb{Z}G$ is to understand the structure of its group of units $\mathbb{Z}G^{\times}$. This group is formed by the invertible elements of $\mathbb{Z}G$, which are elements of the form $\sum_{g\in G}a_gg$ such that $a_g\in\mathbb{Z}$ and $a_g=0$ for all but finitely many $g\in G$.

In this document, we will focus on the $p$-subgroups of $\mathbb{Z}G^{\times}$, where $p$ is a prime number. We will show that every $p$-subgroup of $\mathbb{Z}G^{\times}$ is isomorphic to a subgroup of $G$ and that the converse is also true under certain conditions. In conclusion, we have shown that the $p$-subgroups of the group of units in the integral group ring $\mathbb{Z}G$ have a close relationship with the subgroups of the underlying group $G$. This result has important implications for the study of the structure of $\mathbb{Z}G$ and its applications in group theory and representation theory.

Dynamic nuclear polarization (DNP) is a technique that can be used to increase the nuclear spin polarization in solids, which can lead to longer nuclear spin coherence times and improved sensitivity in nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) applications. In this work, we demonstrate that DNP can be effectively driven at defect centers in solids, leading to significant enhancement of nuclear spin coherence times. Our results suggest that this approach may have potential for improving the performance of NMR and MRI in a variety of systems. Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) are powerful techniques that rely on the presence of nuclear spin polarization to obtain spectroscopic and imaging information. However, the nuclear spin coherence times in solids are typically quite short, limiting the sensitivity and resolution of these techniques. Dynamic nuclear polarization (DNP) is a method that can be used to enhance the nuclear spin polarization in solids, leading to longer nuclear spin coherence times and improved sensitivity in NMR and MRI applications.

DNP involves the transfer of polarization from electron spins to nuclear spins through microwave irradiation and cross-relaxation processes. This can be achieved by introducing a spin-polarized species (such as radicals or triplet states) into the sample and driving their polarization transfer to the nuclear spins through microwave irradiation. While DNP has been successfully demonstrated in a variety of systems, there is still room for improvement in terms of the efficiency and robustness of the process.

In this work, we demonstrate that DNP can be effectively driven at defect centers in solids, leading to significant enhancement of nuclear spin coherence times. Defect centers are local structural imperfections that can introduce additional electron states into the system and can be attractive targets for DNP due to their strong spin-spin interactions with the surrounding nuclear spins. By driving DNP at these defect centers, we are able to achieve significantly longer nuclear spin coherence times compared to traditional DNP approaches. In summary, we have demonstrated that DNP can be effectively driven at defect centers in solids, leading to significant enhancement of nuclear spin coherence times. Our results suggest that this approach may have potential for improving the performance of NMR and MRI in a variety of systems. Further work is needed to fully understand the mechanisms behind this enhancement and to optimize the DNP process for different materials and defect types.  Overall, this work highlights the potential for using defect centers as a platform for driving DNP and improving the sensitivity of NMR and MRI techniques.

In this study, we explore the non-local behavior of two ultracold atoms that are trapped in separate potential wells. Using a combination of theoretical modeling and experimental techniques, we demonstrate that these atoms exhibit entangled quantum states, meaning that their properties become correlated even when separated by large distances. Our results provide new insights into the fundamental nature of quantum physics and have potential applications in the fields of quantum computing and communication. Quantum entanglement is a phenomenon in which two or more particles become correlated in such a way that their properties become linked, even when separated by large distances. This non-local behavior has been observed in various systems, including photons, electrons, and atoms. In this work, we focus on the non-local behavior of two ultracold atoms that are trapped in separate potential wells.

Ultracold atoms are atoms that are cooled to temperatures close to absolute zero, where their behavior is governed by the laws of quantum mechanics. Due to their low temperature and high purity, ultracold atoms are ideal systems for studying quantum phenomena. In particular, they can be used to study the fundamental nature of quantum physics, as well as to develop new technologies based on quantum principles, such as quantum computing and communication.

In our experiments, we trap two ultracold atoms in separate potential wells using laser cooling and trapping techniques. We then manipulate the interactions between the atoms using external fields, such as magnetic or electric fields. By measuring the properties of the atoms, such as their spin or energy, we can determine whether they exhibit entangled quantum states. In this study, we have demonstrated the non-local behavior of two ultracold atoms that are trapped in separate potential wells. Our results provide new insights into the fundamental nature of quantum physics and have potential applications in the fields of quantum computing and communication. Future work will focus on exploring the properties of these entangled quantum states in more detail, as well as on developing new technologies based on these principles.

The Pictor A galaxy is a radio-loud active galaxy located in the constellation Pictor. It is known for its jet-like structure, which is thought to be caused by the accretion of matter onto a supermassive black hole at the center of the galaxy. However, the origin of this jet has been a subject of debate, with some theories suggesting that it is powered by the inverse Compton scattering of cosmic microwave background (IC/CMB) radiation. In this study, we present optical observations of the Pictor A jet and its associated tidal tail, which provide evidence against the IC/CMB jet hypothesis. Active galaxies, such as quasars and radio galaxies, are characterized by their high levels of radiation across the electromagnetic spectrum. These emissions are thought to be powered by the accretion of matter onto a supermassive black hole at the center of the galaxy. One of the most striking features of active galaxies is their jet-like structure, which can extend for hundreds of thousands of light-years. The Pictor A galaxy is a well-known example of an active galaxy with a jet.

The origin of these jets is not well understood, but several theories have been proposed. One such theory is the IC/CMB jet hypothesis, which suggests that the jet is powered by the inverse Compton scattering of cosmic microwave background (CMB) radiation. According to this theory, high-energy particles in the jet collide with CMB photons, boosting their energy and producing a beam of radiation that is directed along the jet.

However, the IC/CMB jet hypothesis has faced criticism, as it has been difficult to observe the required high-energy particles in the jet. In this study, we present new optical observations of the Pictor A jet and its associated tidal tail, which provide evidence against the IC/CMB jet hypothesis. Our optical observations of the Pictor A jet and tidal tail show that the jet is not composed of high-energy particles, as would be expected if it were powered by the IC/CMB process. Instead, the jet appears to be made up of cooler, lower-energy gas. This is consistent with other studies that have also found evidence against the IC/CMB jet hypothesis for this and other active galaxies. While the true origin of the Pictor A jet remains uncertain, our findings suggest that it is not powered by the IC/CMB process.

Building-Integrated Photovoltaic (BIPV) systems are becoming increasingly popular as a means of generating renewable energy and reducing carbon emissions. However, BIPV systems can suffer from various types of faults, which can significantly reduce their efficiency and lifespan. In this paper, we propose a novel approach for automatically detecting faults in BIPV systems without requiring solar irradiation data. Our approach utilizes machine learning techniques to analyze the electrical characteristics of the BIPV system, such as current and voltage, and to identify patterns that indicate the presence of a fault. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach through extensive simulations and experiments on a BIPV system test bed. BIPV systems are photovoltaic panels that are integrated into the building structure, rather than being installed as a standalone system. This allows for the generation of electricity while also serving as a functional element of the building, such as a roof or façade. BIPV systems have several advantages over traditional photovoltaic systems, including improved aesthetic appeal, reduced installation costs, and improved energy efficiency.

However, BIPV systems are also prone to various types of faults, including partial shading, panel degradation, and inverter failure. These faults can significantly reduce the efficiency and lifespan of the BIPV system, leading to reduced energy generation and increased maintenance costs. Therefore, it is important to detect and diagnose faults in BIPV systems in a timely manner.

Traditionally, fault detection in BIPV systems has relied on solar irradiation data, which is used to calculate the expected power output of the system. Any deviation from the expected power output is then used as an indicator of a fault. However, obtaining accurate solar irradiation data can be challenging, especially in cases where the BIPV system is installed on a building with complex geometry or in an area with significant atmospheric variations.

In this paper, we propose a novel approach for detecting faults in BIPV systems without requiring solar irradiation data. Our approach utilizes machine learning techniques to analyze the electrical characteristics of the BIPV system, such as current and voltage, and to identify patterns that indicate the presence of a fault. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach through extensive simulations and experiments on a BIPV system test bed. In this paper, we have proposed a novel approach for automatically detecting faults in BIPV systems without requiring solar irradiation data. Our approach utilizes machine learning techniques to analyze the electrical characteristics of the BIPV system and to identify patterns that indicate the presence of a fault. We have demonstrated the effectiveness of our approach through extensive simulations and experiments on a BIPV system test bed. Our approach has the potential to significantly improve the reliability and maintenance of BIPV systems, leading to increased energy generation and reduced costs.

Preferential attachment is a widely observed phenomenon in complex networks, where the probability of a new node connecting to a particular node increases with the number of connections that the node already has. This process can lead to the formation of highly connected nodes, known as "hubs," which play a crucial role in the overall structure and function of the network. In this paper, we explore the concept of preferential attachment when it leads to stable network structures, and discuss the conditions under which this occurs. We also provide examples from real-world networks and discuss the implications of preferential attachment when stable. Networks are an important structure for representing relationships between entities in a wide range of fields, including biology, sociology, and computer science. One important aspect of network formation is the process of preferential attachment, whereby new nodes are more likely to connect to nodes that already have a high number of connections. This process can lead to the formation of highly connected nodes, known as "hubs," which play a crucial role in the overall structure and function of the network.

While preferential attachment has been widely observed in complex networks, it is not always clear under what conditions it leads to stable network structures. In this paper, we aim to explore this question by discussing the conditions under which preferential attachment can lead to stable network structures, and providing examples from real-world networks. In this paper, we have explored the concept of preferential attachment when it leads to stable network structures. We have discussed the conditions under which this occurs and provided examples from real-world networks. Our analysis suggests that preferential attachment can lead to stable network structures when certain conditions are met, such as the presence of a threshold for node connectivity and the absence of external perturbations. Understanding the conditions under which preferential attachment leads to stable network structures is important for predicting and controlling the structure and function of complex networks.

In this note, we will consider the electrostatic energy of two point charges with charges q1 and q2 and separated by a distance r. We will derive the formula for the electrostatic energy and discuss its properties and significance. . In summary, the electrostatic energy of two point charges is a measure of the potential energy stored in the electric field created by the charges. It is given by the formula U = k * (q1 * q2)/r, where k is the Coulomb constant, q1 and q2 are the charges of the two point charges, and r is the distance between them. This formula has important applications in many fields, including electrical engineering, physics, and chemistry.

The authors experimentally observe the collisions between Rydberg atoms and trapped ions. The Rydberg-atom ions are produced by photoionizing 61S of Xe2+ in a Paul trap, while the ions are captured by laser cooling in a quadrupole ion trap. The collisions between the Rydberg atoms and trapped ions is imaged. The collision time as a function of the kinetic energy of the ion is measured by adjusting the distance between the two species. Also, the collision energy dependence of the cross section of the single Rydberg-atom–ion collision is observed. The results are consistent with the theory of short-range interaction. Rydberg atoms have been proposed as a new type of atom for quantum information processing (QIP) 1. The basic idea of QIP is that the quantum state of the system can be transferred between different degrees of freedom by the interaction between the atom and the system. In QIP, the quantum state of the system is represented by a superposition of quantum states of atoms. The atoms are trapped by optical dipole traps (ODTs), and the quantum state is transferred by the interaction between the atom and the trapped ions.  The quantum state of the Rydberg atom is represented by a superposition of the electronic states with different principal quantum number n and orbital angular momentum l, i.e. \(\left|n,l\right\rangle\). The quantum state of the ion is represented by a superposition of the vibrational states of the vibrational modes of the ion. Fractals are a type of geometric pattern that exhibits self-similarity at different scales. They are often found in natural systems and have been the subject of much scientific study. In this paper, we examine the role of hierarchical growth in the self-assembly of discrete self-similar fractals. We show that hierarchical growth is necessary for the formation of these fractals, but it is not always sufficient. We also discuss the implications of our findings for the study of self-assembling systems and the potential applications of self-similar fractals in various fields. Fractals are a type of geometric pattern that exhibits self-similarity at different scales. They are often characterized by their complex, irregular shapes and have been found in a wide range of natural systems, including plants, rivers, and coastlines. The study of fractals has led to important discoveries in fields such as mathematics, physics, and computer science.

One type of fractal that has received much attention is the discrete self-similar fractal, which is a fractal that is composed of repeating units or building blocks. These fractals are particularly interesting because they can be formed through self-assembly, a process by which small, individual units spontaneously come together to form a larger, more complex structure.

One key factor in the self-assembly of discrete self-similar fractals is hierarchical growth, which refers to the process of building up a structure in a stepwise fashion, starting from smaller units and adding more units as the structure becomes larger. Hierarchical growth is often observed in natural systems and is thought to play a crucial role in the self-assembly of discrete self-similar fractals.

In this paper, we examine the relationship between hierarchical growth and the self-assembly of discrete self-similar fractals. We show that hierarchical growth is necessary for the formation of these fractals, but it is not always sufficient. We also discuss the implications of our findings for the study of self-assembling systems and the potential applications of self-similar fractals in various fields. In summary, our study has shown that hierarchical growth is necessary for the self-assembly of discrete self-similar fractals, but it is not always sufficient. These findings have important implications for the study of self-assembling systems and the potential applications of self-similar fractals in various fields. Further research is needed to fully understand the role of hierarchical growth in the self-assembly of discrete self-similar fractals and to identify other factors that may be important in this process.

The Andromeda galaxy (M31) and the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) are two of the closest galaxies to the Milky Way. There have been theories suggesting that M31 and the LMC may have had a close encounter in the past, possibly resulting in the LMC being ejected from the M31 system. In this paper, we will explore the possibility that M31 could have formed through a major merger, which could have played a role in the ejection of the LMC. M31, also known as the Andromeda galaxy, is the nearest major galaxy to the Milky Way and is located approximately 2.537 million light-years away. The Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) is a satellite galaxy of the Milky Way and is located approximately 160,000 light-years away. The LMC is the third closest galaxy to the Milky Way, after the Sagittarius Dwarf Spheroidal galaxy and the M31.

There have been theories suggesting that M31 and the LMC may have had a close encounter in the past, possibly resulting in the LMC being ejected from the M31 system. This theory is supported by the observed high velocities of both galaxies and their current positions in the sky.

One possible explanation for the close encounter between M31 and the LMC is that M31 formed through a major merger. Major mergers occur when two galaxies collide and merge to form a single, larger galaxy. These events are thought to play a significant role in the evolution of galaxies, as they can trigger intense bursts of star formation and alter the structure and morphology of the resulting galaxy.

If M31 did indeed form through a major merger, it is possible that the LMC was also involved in the collision. In this scenario, the gravitational forces of the merger could have caused the LMC to be ejected from the M31 system, resulting in its current position and velocity. In conclusion, the possibility that M31 formed through a major merger and ejected the LMC cannot be ruled out. Further study is needed to determine the exact events that led to the current positions and velocities of these two galaxies. However, the evidence suggesting a past close encounter between M31 and the LMC, combined with the role that major mergers can play in the evolution of galaxies, makes this theory a viable explanation for the observed characteristics of these two celestial objects.

Negative controls are widely used in experimental research as a means of identifying and correcting for unwanted variation in data. However, the application of negative controls can be highly context-dependent and is often limited to specific experimental designs or data types. In this paper, we present a general framework for using negative controls to remove unwanted variation in a wide range of experimental settings. We demonstrate the utility of this approach through simulations and case studies, and provide guidelines for implementing the method in practice. Unwanted variation in experimental data can arise from a variety of sources, including sampling error, measurement error, and uncontrolled confounding factors. Negative controls are a commonly used approach for identifying and correcting for such variation, as they allow researchers to compare the effects of a treatment or intervention against a control condition that is identical in all respects except for the absence of the treatment. By comparing the results of the treatment and control conditions, researchers can identify and correct for any unwanted variation that is present in the data.

However, the application of negative controls is often limited to specific experimental designs or data types, and there is a lack of a general framework for using negative controls to remove unwanted variation across a wide range of experimental settings. In this paper, we aim to address this gap by presenting a unifying and generalizing method for using negative controls to remove unwanted variation in experimental data. In summary, we have presented a general framework for using negative controls to remove unwanted variation in experimental data. Our approach is based on the identification of negative control variables, which are variables that are expected to be unaffected by the treatment or intervention being studied. By including negative control variables in the analysis, researchers can identify and correct for any unwanted variation that is present in the data. We have demonstrated the utility of this approach through simulations and case studies, and provide guidelines for implementing the method in practice. Overall, our approach represents a valuable tool for researchers seeking to improve the accuracy and robustness of their results by correcting for unwanted variation in their data.

In this edition of Matters of Gravity, we bring you updates on the latest research and developments in the field of gravitation. We begin with a review of the groundbreaking LIGO experiment, which recently detected gravitational waves for the first time. We also highlight the work of several prominent physicists, including Roger Penrose and Kip Thorne, who have made significant contributions to our understanding of black holes and the general theory of relativity. Gravitation is a fundamental force that shapes the universe and governs the motion of celestial bodies. Over the past century, physicists have made significant progress in our understanding of this enigmatic force, thanks in part to the work of giants like Einstein and Newton. In recent years, advances in technology have allowed us to probe the mysteries of gravitation with greater precision than ever before, yielding new insights into the nature of space, time, and matter.

In this edition of Matters of Gravity, we celebrate the achievements of the Topical Group in Gravitation of the American Physical Society, and bring you up to date on the latest research and developments in this exciting field. We begin with a review of the groundbreaking LIGO experiment, which recently detected gravitational waves for the first time, confirming a key prediction of Einstein's theory of general relativity. We also highlight the work of several prominent physicists, including Roger Penrose and Kip Thorne, who have made significant contributions to our understanding of black holes and the general theory of relativity. The field of gravitation continues to be a source of fascination and mystery for scientists and laypeople alike. With the recent detection of gravitational waves, we stand on the brink of a new era of gravitational research, one that promises to deepen our understanding of the universe and our place within it. We hope you have enjoyed this edition of Matters of Gravity, and we look forward to bringing you more updates and insights from the Topical Group in Gravitation in the future.

Nova outflows, formed during the explosive thermonuclear runaway on the surface of a white dwarf, are known to be composed of two components: a fast, collisionless component and a slower, radiatively cooled component. In this work, we investigate the role of shocks in the formation and evolution of the radiatively cooled component through the analysis of thermal emission from nova outflows. We find that the observed thermal emission is consistent with a model in which the radiatively cooled component is produced via the passage of shocks through the nova ejecta. Novae are a type of explosive event that occurs on the surface of a white dwarf, a dense, compact star. These events are caused by the thermonuclear runaway of hydrogen or helium on the surface of the white dwarf, resulting in the expulsion of a large amount of material at high speeds. The outflows from novae are composed of two components: a fast, collisionless component and a slower, radiatively cooled component. The fast component is thought to be produced by the initial thermonuclear explosion, while the slower component is thought to be produced via the passage of shocks through the nova ejecta.

In this work, we investigate the role of shocks in the formation and evolution of the radiatively cooled component through the analysis of thermal emission from nova outflows. We use data from a variety of telescopes, including the Chandra X-ray Observatory and the Hubble Space Telescope, to study the thermal emission from a sample of novae. Our analysis shows that the observed thermal emission from nova outflows is consistent with a model in which the radiatively cooled component is produced via the passage of shocks through the nova ejecta. This suggests that shocks play a crucial role in the evolution of nova outflows, and further study of these shocks may provide valuable insights into the physics of novae and the nature of white dwarfs.

In this paper, we calculate the two-loop electroweak next-to-leading logarithmic (EW NLL) corrections for processes involving heavy quarks. These corrections are important for improving the accuracy of predictions in the high energy regime, where logarithmic terms can become significant. We present the results of our calculation and discuss their implications for future analyses. Precision calculations in the high energy regime are crucial for testing the Standard Model (SM) of particle physics and searching for new physics beyond the SM. In particular, processes involving heavy quarks, such as top quarks, can provide sensitive probes of the SM and its extensions. To achieve high precision in these calculations, it is necessary to include higher order corrections, including electroweak (EW) corrections.

The leading logarithmic (LL) EW corrections to heavy quark processes are known to next-to-leading order (NLO). However, the next-to-leading logarithmic (NLL) corrections, which are suppressed by one power of the logarithm relative to the LL corrections, are currently unknown. In this paper, we calculate the two-loop EW NLL corrections for processes involving heavy quarks. We have calculated the two-loop EW NLL corrections for processes involving heavy quarks, which are important for improving the precision of predictions in the high energy regime. Our results can be used to improve the accuracy of future analyses of heavy quark processes, and can provide valuable constraints on the SM and its extensions.

In this paper, we study the L1-spectrum of Banach space valued Ornstein-Uhlenbeck operators. We provide a characterization of the L1-spectrum in terms of the underlying Banach space and the coefficients of the operator. As a result, we show that the L1-spectrum is always nonempty and consists of isolated eigenvalues with finite multiplicities. We also discuss the relation between the L1-spectrum and the spectrum of the operator in the Banach space. Ornstein-Uhlenbeck operators, also known as Kolmogorov operators, are linear operators that arise in the study of stochastic differential equations. These operators have applications in various fields, including mathematical physics, probability theory, and engineering. In this paper, we focus on the case where the operator acts on Banach space valued functions.

One important aspect of the study of linear operators is the spectrum, which consists of the set of complex numbers that are not in the resolvent set of the operator. The L1-spectrum, also known as the generalized point spectrum, is a variant of the spectrum that takes into account the action of the operator on a dense subset of the domain. In general, the L1-spectrum is larger than the spectrum and can contain additional eigenvalues with infinite multiplicities.

In the case of Banach space valued Ornstein-Uhlenbeck operators, the L1-spectrum is not well understood. In this paper, we provide a characterization of the L1-spectrum in terms of the underlying Banach space and the coefficients of the operator. Our main result shows that the L1-spectrum is always nonempty and consists of isolated eigenvalues with finite multiplicities. In this paper, we have studied the L1-spectrum of Banach space valued Ornstein-Uhlenbeck operators. We have provided a characterization of the L1-spectrum and shown that it is always nonempty and consists of isolated eigenvalues with finite multiplicities. Our results provide a deeper understanding of the properties of these operators and have potential applications in the analysis of stochastic differential equations.

Its production has an average transverse momentum of 2.4 GeV/, and it is dominantly produced in the proton fragmentation region. Its decay products contain a $\frac{1}{2}^{+}$ ground state proton and a weak pion. The production of this state is compared with the prediction based on the available theoretical models. A comparison of theoretical models with data is more difficult in this case, mainly because of the experimental limitations in determining the precise masses of the $\Omega^ - $ baryons. The observation of the double-strange baryon $\Omega ^-$ is of great interest due to the large production cross section of this baryon. In 1985, the first observation of $\Omega ^-$ was reported by the NA31 Collaboration in the $\mathrm{p}+\overline{\mathrm{p}}$ collision at 200 GeV/c [1]. The observation was based on the analysis of the decay products of $\Omega ^-$ into a $\frac{1}{3}^{-}$ $\overline{\mathrm{p}} + \pi^{-}$. Later, the STAR Collaboration reported the observation of $\Omega ^- $ in the $\mathrm{p}\mathrm{p}$ collision at 11.6 GeV with 1-250 million of events [2]. The analysis was based on the decay products of $\Omega $ into a $\frac{1 }{2}^{+}$ proton and a weak pion.    The production of $\Omega ^- \rightarrow \frac{1}{2}^+\overline{\mathrm{p}\mathrm{p }}\pi^{-}$ is illustrated in Fig. 1. The STAR Collaboration has reported the observation of $\Omega^- $ in $\mathrm{pp}$ collisions at 11.6 $\mathrm{GeV}$. In this paper, we investigate the relationship between Church-Rosser systems, codes with bounded synchronization delay, and local Rees extensions. We demonstrate that these three concepts are closely connected and can be used to analyze the behavior of concurrent systems. Our results provide insights into the design and analysis of concurrent systems, with potential applications in areas such as distributed computing and communication networks. Concurrent systems, which involve the simultaneous execution of multiple tasks, are prevalent in modern computing systems. These systems can be difficult to design and analyze due to the complex interactions between tasks. To address this challenge, researchers have developed various theoretical frameworks for understanding concurrent systems.

One such framework is the Church-Rosser system, which is a mathematical model that captures the behavior of concurrent systems. Church-Rosser systems are characterized by the property of confluence, which states that the order in which two concurrent actions are performed does not affect the final outcome. This property is useful for reasoning about concurrent systems because it allows us to consider different execution orders independently.

Another important concept in the study of concurrent systems is the synchronization delay, which is the time it takes for different tasks to communicate with each other. Bounded synchronization delay codes are codes that are designed to have a maximum synchronization delay, which can be useful for ensuring that concurrent systems perform efficiently.

Local Rees extensions, on the other hand, are algebraic structures that are used to represent concurrent systems. They are based on the concept of a Rees matrix, which is a matrix that encodes the interactions between tasks in a concurrent system. Local Rees extensions are a generalization of Rees matrices that allow for more complex interactions between tasks.

In this paper, we explore the relationship between these three concepts and demonstrate how they can be used to analyze concurrent systems. In summary, Church-Rosser systems, codes with bounded synchronization delay, and local Rees extensions are important tools for understanding concurrent systems. Our results show that these concepts are closely connected and can be used in combination to analyze the behavior of concurrent systems. These findings have the potential to contribute to the design and analysis of concurrent systems, with applications in areas such as distributed computing and communication networks.

In this paper, we propose a new approach to the design of communication protocols based on the principles of constructive simulation and topological design. Constructive simulation is a technique that allows for the systematic construction of protocols by iteratively building up the protocol from simple building blocks. Topological design, on the other hand, refers to the use of topological concepts and methods to analyze and optimize the structure and behavior of the protocol. By combining these two approaches, we are able to design highly efficient and resilient protocols that are well-suited for a wide range of applications. Communication protocols play a central role in the operation of modern computer networks and systems. They define the rules and standards that govern the exchange of information between devices, and are essential for ensuring the reliable and efficient operation of these systems. However, the design of communication protocols is a complex task that requires a deep understanding of both the technical and functional requirements of the system.

Traditionally, the design of communication protocols has been approached through the use of mathematical models and analysis techniques. While these approaches have been successful in many cases, they are often limited by their assumptions and approximations, and can be difficult to apply to more complex scenarios.

In this paper, we propose a new approach to the design of communication protocols that combines the principles of constructive simulation and topological design. Constructive simulation is a technique that allows for the systematic construction of protocols by iteratively building up the protocol from simple building blocks. This approach has the advantage of allowing the designer to focus on a small set of basic building blocks, while still being able to capture the complexity of the overall protocol.

Topological design, on the other hand, refers to the use of topological concepts and methods to analyze and optimize the structure and behavior of the protocol. By leveraging the power of topological analysis, we are able to identify and address potential bottlenecks and vulnerabilities in the protocol, and design protocols that are highly efficient and resilient. In summary, we have presented a new approach to the design of communication protocols based on the principles of constructive simulation and topological design. By combining these two approaches, we are able to design highly efficient and resilient protocols that are well-suited for a wide range of applications. We believe that this approach has the potential to significantly improve the design and performance of communication protocols, and we look forward to exploring its application in a variety of settings.

The spin dependent electron transmission from a ferromagnetic, Pt(5 nm)/CoFeB(8 nm)/MgO(3 nm)/Pt(2 nm) single layer junction has been directly imaged using spin polarized scanning tunneling microscopy. The results show coexistence of a spin-up and spin-down component of the current through the junction. We observed that the spin-up component of the current is enhanced in the presence of the CoFeB layer while the spin-down component vanishes. The measured I - d characteristics obtained by scanning the bias voltage across the junction demonstrate that the magnetoresistance ratio is enhanced when only the spin-up component of the current is allowed to flow through the junction. - The discovery of giant magnetoresistance (GMR) in CoFeB/MgO/CoFeB [1] and CoFeB/MgO/NiFe [2] trilayer structures has opened up the field of spintronics and has stimulated a tremendous amount of research into spin-dependent transport properties of magnetic nanostructures. [3] The spin-dependent transport properties of magnetic nanostructures are governed by the spin dependent electron scattering from the magnetic impurities and the spin dependent electron scattering from the interfaces. [4,5] The spin dependent electron scattering from the magnetic impurities is characterized by spin-flip scattering, which is a consequence of spin-orbit interaction (SOI). [6] In a recent study, the spin-dependent electron scattering from the interfaces has been observed in CoFeB/MgO/CoFe and CoFeB/MgO/Cu [7] trilayer structures, which is attributed to the Rashba-Edelstein effect. [8] In this study, we show that the SOI induced spin-dependent scattering from the interfaces of CoFeB/MgO/Pt single layer junction can be used to manipulate the spin-dependent transport properties of the junction. - We observed the coexistence of spin-up and spin-down component of current through the junction. The spin-up component of current is enhanced in the presence of the Co-FeB layer while the spin-down component is absent.   - The magnetoresistance ratio is enhanced when only the spin-down component of current is allowed to flow through the junction. The observed enhancement of the magnetoresistance ratio is attributed to the SOI induced spin-dependent scattering from the interface of the junction.
