In this paper, we consider divergence form elliptic systems with Dini-continuous coefficients and study the regularity of their solutions. We prove that under certain conditions, the solutions of such systems are $C^1$ regular, meaning that they have continuous first partial derivatives. This result is an important step in the analysis of the regularity of solutions to partial differential equations and has numerous applications in the fields of mathematics, physics, and engineering. Divergence form elliptic systems are a class of partial differential equations that arise frequently in various areas of mathematics and physics. They have the form
\[-\nabla\cdot(A(x)\nabla u(x)) = f(x)\]
where $A(x)$ is a matrix of coefficients and $u(x)$ and $f(x)$ are unknown functions. The regularity of solutions to such systems is an important question in the study of partial differential equations.

One key property of the coefficients $A(x)$ that affects the regularity of the solutions is their continuity. In this paper, we focus on the case where the coefficients are Dini-continuous, which is a stronger form of continuity than the usual Lipschitz continuity. We prove that under certain conditions, the solutions of divergence form elliptic systems with Dini-continuous coefficients are $C^1$ regular. In this paper, we have studied the regularity of solutions to divergence form elliptic systems with Dini-continuous coefficients. We have proved that under certain conditions, these solutions are $C^1$ regular. This result is an important step in the analysis of the regularity of solutions to partial differential equations and has numerous applications in the fields of mathematics, physics, and engineering.

The dynamics of solid-liquid interfaces in granular systems have garnered significant attention in recent years due to their importance in a variety of applications, including soil mechanics and industrial processing. In this study, we investigate the capillary-like fluctuations of a solid-liquid interface in a non-cohesive granular system using experimental and theoretical methods. Our results reveal that the frequency and amplitude of the fluctuations are strongly influenced by the liquid-solid adhesion, the grain size, and the liquid volume fraction. We also find that the fluctuation dynamics are well described by a modified capillary wave theory, which takes into account the non-cohesive nature of the granular material. These findings provide insight into the fundamental behavior of solid-liquid interfaces in granular systems and have implications for the design and optimization of granular-based technologies. Granular materials, such as sand and soil, are common in many natural and industrial systems and play a crucial role in a wide range of applications, including construction, pharmaceutical processing, and oil recovery. One important aspect of granular systems is the behavior of solid-liquid interfaces, which can have a significant impact on the overall performance of the system. For example, the stability and transport properties of a granular bed can be greatly affected by the presence of a liquid layer on the surface.

In recent years, there has been increasing interest in understanding the dynamics of solid-liquid interfaces in granular systems. A key aspect of this research is the investigation of capillary-like fluctuations, which are small-amplitude oscillations that occur at the interface due to the interplay between the surface tension and the granular material properties. These fluctuations have been observed in a variety of granular systems, including cohesive and non-cohesive materials, and can have significant implications for the stability and transport properties of the system.

In this study, we focus on the capillary-like fluctuations of a solid-liquid interface in a non-cohesive granular system, using both experimental and theoretical methods. Non-cohesive granular materials, such as sand and glass beads, are characterized by the absence of significant inter-particle forces, which makes them an ideal system for studying the influence of surface tension on the fluctuation dynamics. In this study, we have investigated the capillary-like fluctuations of a solid-liquid interface in a non-cohesive granular system using experimental and theoretical methods. Our results reveal that the frequency and amplitude of the fluctuations are strongly influenced by the liquid-solid adhesion, the grain size, and the liquid volume fraction. We also find that the fluctuation dynamics are well described by a modified capillary wave theory, which takes into account the non-cohesive nature of the granular material. These findings provide insight into the fundamental behavior of solid-liquid interfaces in granular systems and have implications for the design and optimization of granular-based technologies.

In this paper, we present a new class of numerical methods for solving initial value problems (IVPs) in ordinary differential equations (ODEs). These methods, which we refer to as blended general linear methods (BGLMs), are based on boundary value methods (BVMs) and belong to the family of generalized backward differentiation formulas (GBDFs). We show that BGLMs combine the stability and accuracy of BVMs with the simplicity and efficiency of GBDFs, making them an attractive alternative to traditional methods such as Runge-Kutta and Adams-Bashforth. We also present numerical results demonstrating the superior performance of BGLMs compared to other methods in a variety of test cases. The numerical solution of initial value problems (IVPs) in ordinary differential equations (ODEs) is an important problem in many scientific and engineering applications. Traditional methods for solving IVPs, such as the Runge-Kutta and Adams-Bashforth methods, are widely used and have proven to be effective in many cases. However, these methods can be computationally expensive and may not always provide the desired accuracy or stability.

Boundary value methods (BVMs) are an alternative class of numerical methods that are particularly well-suited for solving IVPs with boundary conditions. These methods rely on the approximation of boundary values rather than initial values, and have been shown to be more accurate and stable than traditional methods in certain cases. However, BVMs can be difficult to implement and may require the use of iterative techniques, which can increase the computational cost.

In this paper, we introduce a new class of numerical methods called blended general linear methods (BGLMs), which are based on BVMs and belong to the family of generalized backward differentiation formulas (GBDFs). GBDFs are a subclass of multistep methods that use a linear combination of previous time steps to estimate the solution at the current time step. BGLMs combine the stability and accuracy of BVMs with the simplicity and efficiency of GBDFs, making them an attractive alternative to traditional methods. Numerical results demonstrate the superior performance of BGLMs compared to other methods in a variety of test cases.

The results of previous treatments of neutron-antineutron transitions in quark matter are examined. It is shown that the annihilation contribution to this process is negligible. In order to avoid the interference of the annihilation contribution with the direct one, the process takes place in a finite box with free or hard-wall boundaries. Using a simple method, we find that the form of the spectrum close to the threshold is mainly determined by the boundary conditions, whereas the contribution of the hard-wall boundary is negligible. In the case of hard-wall boundary conditions, we make a qualitative analysis of the spectrum as a function of the mass difference between the particles. It is shown how the spectrum changes from purely Gamow-like to a quasi-bound one due to collisions with the wall, and in the extreme limiting case the spectrum near the lower bound turns to a Breit-Wigner shape. Possible applications, in particular, to the quark matter inside the neutron star, are discussed. Neutron-antineutron (n-n) transitions in quark matter are of interest in view of the possible existence of neutron stars with a core of quark matter [,, ]. It was shown in Refs. [, ] that the presence of a quark matter core could lead to the appearance of a bound state in the n-n system. In this case, the n-n system can be treated as a bound state of two neutrons, which are bound by the strong interaction. The binding energy of the n-n system is given by  \[ \displaystyle E_{B}=-2\mu_{n}\,, \] (1)  where \(\mu_{n}\) is the chemical potential of neutrons.  It is well known that the n-n system in vacuum has a pure Gamow-like spectrum. If we assume that the n-n system is immersed in a dense medium, then it is expected that the spectrum of this system will be modified due to the presence of the medium. It was shown in Ref. [] that the n-n system immersed in a dense medium with a constant density \(\rho\) has a quasi-bound spectrum. This means that the n-n system has a bound state near the threshold of the n-n system with a binding energy  \[ \displaystyle E_B=-2\mu_{n}-\Delta E\,, \] (2)  where \(\Delta E\) is the shift in the spectrum due to the medium.  In this paper, we consider the effect of collisions with the wall on the n-n transitions in quark matter. In order to avoid the interference of the collision contribution with the direct one, the process takes places in a finite box with free or hard-walls boundaries. The form of the spectrum close to the threshold is determined by the boundary conditions, whereas the contribution due to the hard-wall boundary is negligible. In this case, the spectrum is Gamow-like. As the mass difference between the particles increases, the spectrum changes from a pure Gamow-like to a quasi-bound form due to collisions with the wall. In the extreme limiting case, the spectrum near the lower bound turns to a pure Breit-Wigner form.

In this paper, we present a new method for evaluating zeta constants using a number theoretic approach. The method involves expressing the zeta constant as a sum over prime powers and using properties of the prime numbers to evaluate this sum. We demonstrate the efficacy of this method through various examples and compare it to existing methods. The zeta constant, also known as the Riemann zeta function, is a fundamental mathematical object with numerous applications in fields such as number theory, physics, and engineering. Despite its importance, finding closed-form expressions for the zeta constant has proven to be a difficult task. Many methods have been proposed for evaluating zeta constants, including the Euler product formula, the functional equation, and the Mellin transform.

In this paper, we propose a new method for evaluating zeta constants based on a number theoretic approach. Our method involves expressing the zeta constant as a sum over prime powers and using properties of the prime numbers to evaluate this sum. This approach has the advantage of being relatively simple and easy to implement, while still yielding accurate results. In this paper, we have presented a new method for evaluating zeta constants based on a number theoretic approach. We have demonstrated the efficacy of this method through various examples and have compared it to existing methods. This method offers a new perspective on the evaluation of zeta constants and may provide a useful tool for researchers working in this area.

The diffuse supernova neutrino background (DSNB) is a faint flux of neutrinos that is thought to be produced by the collective emissions of all past supernovae in the universe. These neutrinos are difficult to detect because they have very low energies and interact weakly with matter. However, advances in neutrino detection technology have allowed scientists to search for the DSNB and study its properties. In this review, we will discuss the current understanding of the DSNB, including its predicted features and detection prospects. Supernovae are the explosive deaths of massive stars that can outshine entire galaxies for a short period of time. During a supernova, a star collapses to form a compact object such as a neutron star or a black hole, and releases a tremendous amount of energy in the process. A significant fraction of this energy is carried away by neutrinos, which are produced in copious amounts in the supernova core and escape almost unscathed.

The flux of neutrinos from a single supernova is quite high, but the number of supernovae that have occurred in the history of the universe is vast, estimated to be on the order of 100 billion. The cumulative effect of these supernovae is to create a diffuse background of neutrinos that pervades the entire universe. This is known as the diffuse supernova neutrino background (DSNB).

The DSNB is expected to have certain characteristics based on our understanding of supernovae and neutrino physics. For example, the neutrino spectrum should follow a thermal distribution, with a peak energy of around 10-20 MeV. The total flux of the DSNB is expected to be on the order of 1 neutrino per cm^2 per sec, with a small uncertainty due to the uncertain rate of supernovae in the universe.

Despite these predictions, the DSNB has yet to be directly detected. This is due in part to the fact that neutrinos have such weak interactions with matter, making them difficult to detect even when they are present in large numbers. However, advances in neutrino detection technology have made it possible to search for the DSNB and study its properties. The diffuse supernova neutrino background (DSNB) is a faint flux of neutrinos produced by the collective emissions of all past supernovae in the universe. While it has yet to be directly detected, advances in neutrino detection technology have allowed scientists to search for the DSNB and study its properties. Further research on the DSNB has the potential to provide valuable insights into the history and evolution of the universe.

The perturbative unitarity of quantum field theories in the sense of Lee and Wick 1 is one of the most fundamental principles in physics. It states that the amplitude for any scattering process is positive definite, or real, for all energies. The idea of Lee and Wick is that the scattering matrix should be unitary at all energies. This is a consequence of the requirement that the total probability for a particle to be observed to be at a given position is given by the modulus square of the amplitude for the particle to be at that position. The Lee-Wick theorem has been proven to be valid for all known theories of particles, including gravity, and it is believed that it should be valid in any theory of quantum gravity. Biomarkers are measurable indicators of a biological state or condition. They play a crucial role in the diagnosis, prognosis, and treatment of various diseases. In this paper, we propose a method to rank biomarkers based on their mutual information with a given disease. Mutual information is a measure of the dependence between two variables and can be used to quantify the amount of information that a biomarker provides about a disease. We apply our method to a real-world dataset and demonstrate its effectiveness in identifying the most informative biomarkers. Biomarkers are essential tools in modern medicine, providing valuable information about the presence, severity, and progression of various diseases. They can be used to diagnose diseases, predict their outcomes, and monitor the effectiveness of treatment. There are many different types of biomarkers, including genetic, molecular, biochemical, and imaging biomarkers, each with its own strengths and limitations.

The selection of appropriate biomarkers is a crucial step in the development of diagnostic and prognostic tests. A biomarker that is highly informative about a particular disease can greatly improve the accuracy and reliability of a test. On the other hand, a biomarker that is not informative or is correlated with multiple diseases can lead to false positive or negative results.

One common approach to selecting biomarkers is to use statistical methods to identify the variables that are most strongly correlated with the disease of interest. However, this approach has several limitations. First, it does not consider the dependencies between the biomarkers, which can lead to the selection of redundant or conflicting variables. Second, it does not take into account the complexity of the underlying biological processes, which may involve multiple pathways and interactions between different biomarkers.

To overcome these limitations, we propose a method to rank biomarkers based on their mutual information with a given disease. Mutual information is a measure of the dependence between two variables and can be used to quantify the amount of information that a biomarker provides about a disease. It takes into account both the strength and direction of the dependence and is well-suited for analyzing complex systems with multiple variables. In this paper, we have presented a method for ranking biomarkers based on their mutual information with a given disease. We have demonstrated the effectiveness of this approach on a real-world dataset and shown that it can identify the most informative biomarkers. Our method is flexible and can be applied to a wide range of diseases and biomarkers, making it a valuable tool for the selection and evaluation of biomarkers in the development of diagnostic and prognostic tests.

In this paper, we study the behavior of Laplace-eigenfunctions on the torus with high vanishing order. These functions play a fundamental role in the study of various mathematical and physical systems, and understanding their behavior on the torus is important for a wide range of applications. We derive a series expansion for these functions in terms of Bessel functions, and use this expansion to study the asymptotic behavior of the eigenfunctions as the vanishing order becomes large. We also discuss the implications of our results for the convergence of certain Fourier series and the behavior of the corresponding eigenvalues. The Laplace operator is a fundamental mathematical operator that arises in many areas of mathematics and physics. One of the main tools for studying the properties of the Laplace operator is the study of its eigenfunctions, which are functions that satisfy a specific differential equation involving the Laplace operator. These eigenfunctions play a central role in the study of various systems, including vibrating membranes, heat diffusion, and quantum mechanics.

In this paper, we focus on the behavior of Laplace-eigenfunctions on the torus, which is a two-dimensional surface that can be obtained by identifying opposite sides of a rectangle. The torus is an important geometric object that arises in many areas of mathematics and physics, and the study of Laplace-eigenfunctions on the torus has applications in a wide range of areas, including fluid dynamics and quantum field theory.

One important aspect of the study of Laplace-eigenfunctions on the torus is the behavior of these functions as the vanishing order becomes large. The vanishing order of a function is a measure of the rate at which the function tends to zero at the boundary of the domain. In the case of the torus, the boundary consists of the two circles that form the top and bottom edges of the rectangle. Understanding the behavior of the eigenfunctions as the vanishing order becomes large is important for a wide range of applications, including the convergence of certain Fourier series and the behavior of the corresponding eigenvalues.

In this paper, we derive a series expansion for Laplace-eigenfunctions on the torus with high vanishing order in terms of Bessel functions. Using this expansion, we study the asymptotic behavior of the eigenfunctions as the vanishing order becomes large. We also discuss the implications of our results for the convergence of certain Fourier series and the behavior of the corresponding eigenvalues. In this paper, we have studied the behavior of Laplace-eigenfunctions on the torus with high vanishing order. We have derived a series expansion for these functions in terms of Bessel functions, and used this expansion to study the asymptotic behavior of the eigenfunctions as the vanishing order becomes large. Our results have implications for the convergence of certain Fourier series and the behavior of the corresponding eigenvalues, and have potential applications in a wide range of areas, including fluid dynamics and quantum field theory.

This paper presents a case study on the use of fine-grained language composition techniques to improve the performance of natural language processing (NLP) systems. We demonstrate how the use of these techniques can allow for the creation of more complex and nuanced language models, which can in turn lead to better performance on a variety of NLP tasks. We also discuss some of the challenges and limitations of using fine-grained language composition, and suggest directions for future research. Natural language processing (NLP) is a field of computer science that focuses on enabling computers to understand and generate human language. One of the key challenges in NLP is the complexity of human language, which can vary greatly depending on the context, audience, and purpose of the communication. To address this challenge, NLP researchers have developed various techniques for representing and processing language, ranging from simple rule-based approaches to more complex machine learning models.

One promising approach for representing and processing language is the use of fine-grained language composition techniques, which aim to capture the nuanced and context-dependent nature of human language. These techniques involve the use of smaller units of language, called "primitives," which can be combined in different ways to create more complex language structures and meanings. By using a large set of primitives and a flexible composition mechanism, it is possible to create language models that can capture a wide range of language phenomena, including syntactic and semantic dependencies, discourse structure, and pragmatic effects.

In this case study, we demonstrate the use of fine-grained language composition techniques in the context of a NLP task. Specifically, we present a model that uses a set of primitives to represent the meaning of a given text, and a composition mechanism to combine these primitives into more complex representations. We then evaluate the performance of this model on a variety of NLP tasks, including language translation, summarization, and question answering. Our results show that the use of fine-grained language composition can lead to significant improvements in performance, compared to more traditional approaches. In conclusion, this case study has demonstrated the potential of fine-grained language composition techniques for improving the performance of NLP systems. By using a large set of primitives and a flexible composition mechanism, it is possible to create language models that can capture a wide range of language phenomena and achieve better performance on a variety of NLP tasks. However, there are also challenges and limitations to using these techniques, which should be taken into account when designing and implementing fine-grained language models. Future research should explore ways to further improve the effectiveness and efficiency of these models, and to apply them to a wider range of NLP tasks and languages.

In this paper, we investigate the concept of fermionic quantum orthogonalization and its applications in the field of quantum chemistry. Specifically, we discuss the Gauss and Householder algorithms for constructing a fermionic orthogonalization matrix and demonstrate their utility in the context of Hartree-Fock and density functional theory calculations. We also provide a comparison of the computational cost and convergence properties of these algorithms. Orthogonalization is a fundamental concept in quantum chemistry, with applications ranging from the Hartree-Fock method to density functional theory. In particular, fermionic orthogonalization plays a crucial role in the treatment of electron correlation in many-electron systems.

There are several approaches to constructing a fermionic orthogonalization matrix, each with its own advantages and limitations. In this work, we focus on the Gauss and Householder algorithms, which are widely used in quantum chemistry software packages.

The Gauss algorithm, also known as the Gram-Schmidt process, involves iteratively constructing an orthogonal basis from a given set of vectors. It is simple to implement and has good convergence properties, but can be computationally expensive for large systems.

On the other hand, the Householder algorithm utilizes a sequence of reflections to orthogonalize a set of vectors. It is computationally efficient, but may converge more slowly than the Gauss algorithm in some cases. In this paper, we have presented an overview of fermionic quantum orthogonalization and described the Gauss and Householder algorithms for constructing a fermionic orthogonalization matrix. We have demonstrated the utility of these algorithms in the context of Hartree-Fock and density functional theory calculations and provided a comparison of their computational cost and convergence properties. Further investigation into the performance and applicability of these algorithms in various systems is an important direction for future research.

In this paper, we present a novel method for sampling from the rotation group using Gauss-Legendre quadrature. By expressing rotations as exponential maps of the Lie algebra, we are able to apply the Gauss-Legendre quadrature rule to approximate the integral of a function over the rotation group. We show that this method is efficient and accurate, and we demonstrate its performance on several examples. Sampling from the rotation group is an important problem in many fields, including computer graphics, molecular dynamics, and robotics. Generating random rotations is often used to initialize simulations or to explore the space of possible configurations. However, sampling from the rotation group is non-trivial due to the non-Euclidean geometry of the space and the constraints on the rotations.

One approach to sampling from the rotation group is to use numerical quadrature methods, which approximate the integral of a function over the rotation group by summing a finite number of samples. In this paper, we propose a method for sampling from the rotation group using the Gauss-Legendre quadrature rule. We have presented a novel method for sampling from the rotation group using the Gauss-Legendre quadrature rule. By expressing rotations as exponential maps of the Lie algebra, we are able to apply the Gauss-Legendre quadrature rule to approximate the integral of a function over the rotation group. We have demonstrated the efficiency and accuracy of this method on several examples, and we believe it has the potential to be a useful tool for sampling from the rotation group in a wide range of applications.

In this paper, we present a formalism for the dynamical evolution of a relativistic liquid body in the presence of internal constraints. We derive the equations of motion for the fluid variables using the relativistic Lagrangian formalism, taking into account the effects of the internal constraints on the fluid's motion. We then discuss the propagation of these constraints through the fluid, and show how the constraints can be used to eliminate degrees of freedom in the fluid's motion. Our results provide a framework for understanding the dynamics of relativistic liquid bodies, and may have applications in the study of compact objects such as neutron stars. The study of the dynamics of fluid bodies is an important and active area of research in both classical and relativistic physics. In the classical case, the dynamics of fluids can be described using the Navier-Stokes equations, which are derived from the principle of least action. However, when the fluid is moving at relativistic speeds, the Navier-Stokes equations are no longer valid, and a more general formalism is required.

One approach to describing the dynamics of relativistic fluids is the use of the relativistic Lagrangian formalism. In this approach, the fluid is described as a continuous medium with a well-defined energy-momentum tensor, and the equations of motion are derived from the principle of least action. However, in many cases, the fluid may be subject to internal constraints, such as the conservation of baryon number or the preservation of some internal symmetry. These constraints can have significant effects on the fluid's motion, and must be taken into account in order to accurately describe the dynamics of the fluid.

In this paper, we present a formalism for the dynamical evolution of a relativistic liquid body in the presence of internal constraints. We derive the equations of motion for the fluid variables using the relativistic Lagrangian formalism, taking into account the effects of the internal constraints on the fluid's motion. We then discuss the propagation of these constraints through the fluid, and show how the constraints can be used to eliminate degrees of freedom in the fluid's motion. In this paper, we have presented a formalism for the dynamical evolution of a relativistic liquid body in the presence of internal constraints. By deriving the equations of motion using the relativistic Lagrangian formalism, we have shown how the internal constraints can be taken into account in the description of the fluid's motion. We have also discussed the propagation of these constraints through the fluid, and have shown how they can be used to eliminate degrees of freedom in the fluid's motion. Our results provide a framework for understanding the dynamics of relativistic liquid bodies, and may have applications in the study of compact objects such as neutron stars.

In this article we revisit the complex oriented case of the notion of orientability appearing in the complex oriented quantum cohomology constructions of Faber [Fab07], McDuff and Sylvan [MS09] and McDuff [McD07]. We prove a version of orientability for a real Gromov-Witten invariant of a closed symplectic manifold when the contact or Legendrian embedding space of the fixed contact type admits a natural complex structure. This result is useful for a variety of applications to the real enumerative geometry of complex symplectic manifolds, including real mirror symmetry. We also remark that the result that the contact embedding space has a natural complex structure can be viewed as an instance of the ‘strong contact stability’ of the Gromov-Witten invariants of contact manifolds. In this article we consider the notion of orientability in the real Gromov-Witten theory of symplectic manifolds. The notion of orientability plays a central role in the complex oriented quantum cohomology constructions of Fabert [Fab07], McDuff [McD07] and McDuff and Sylvan [MS08, MS09].    In the complex oriented case, orientability of a Gromov-Witten invariant is defined as follows. Let \(X\) be a closed symplectic manifold. Suppose that \(\xi\) is a contact type of \(X\). Let \(\overline{\mathcal{M}}_{g,n}(\xi,A)\) be the moduli space of \(n\)-pointed, genus \(g\), stable maps \(u:\mathbb{P}^{1}\rightarrow X\) with image in the contact type \(\xi\) and asymptotic to the marked points at \(\infty\). Let \(ev_{i}:\overline{\mathcal{M}}(\xi,A)\rightarrow X\) be the evaluation map.  Definition 0.1.: The contact type \(\xi\) is orientable if the evaluation maps \(ev_{i}\) are orientation preserving, i.e. if  \[ \langle\sigma,ev_{i}^{*}\alpha\rangle>0\quad\text{for all $\sigma\in H_{2}(X,% \mathbb{Z})$, $i=1,\dots,n$}. \]  Here \(\alpha\) is a contact form of \(\xi\).  It is shown in [1] that orientability of a contact type is a geometric property. Moreover, for any contact type, there is an associated contact manifold \((X,\xi)\) and a complex structure on \(X\) so that the contact type is orientable if and only if the complex structure on \(X\) is compatible with the given contact structure \(\xi\). This result is an instance of a general phenomenon in symplectic geometry that contact structures on a given symplectic manifold are classified by the associated complex structures.  The real Gromov-Witten invariants are defined by integrating the virtual fundamental class of the moduli space of real curves over a certain real cycle. For the complex oriented case, the orientation of the virtual fundamental class is defined by choosing a complex structure on the target space \(X\) and the real cycle is the real locus of the moduli space of real curves. In the real oriented case, the orientation of the virtual fundamental classes is defined by choosing a complex structure on the source space \(\mathbb{P}^{1}_{\mathbb{R}}\) and the real cycle is the real part of the moduli space of real curves.  Definition 0.2.: A real oriented contact type \(\xi\) of \(X\) is orientable if there exists a complex structure on \(\mathbb{P}^ {1}_{\mathbb{R }}\) compatible with the real structure so that the evaluation maps \(ev_{j}\) are orientation preserving.  In the real oriented case, orientability of the contact type \(\xi\subset X\) is defined by the same condition as in Definition 0.1. In this article, we prove a version of orientability for a real oriented Gromov-Witten invariant of a closed symplectic manifold. In the complex oriented case, orientability is defined by the condition that the contact embedding space of the contact type admits a natural complex structure. In the real oriented case, orientability is defined by the same condition as in Definition0.1.

In this document, we discuss the concept of reducibility in the context of matrix weights in neural networks. We explain what it means for a matrix to be reducible and how it can affect the performance of the network. We also discuss how to test for reducibility and methods for reducing the number of weights in a matrix. In a neural network, the weights of the matrices play a crucial role in determining the output of the network. These weights are typically learned during training, and they are adjusted to minimize the error between the predicted output and the true output. However, in some cases, the number of weights in the matrices may be unnecessarily large, leading to what is known as overfitting. Overfitting occurs when the network has too much capacity and is able to fit the training data perfectly, but is unable to generalize to new, unseen data. One way to address this issue is by reducing the number of weights in the matrices, a process known as weight pruning.

One way to prune weights is by identifying and removing redundant weights, which are weights that do not contribute significantly to the output of the network. This process is known as reducibility. A matrix is considered reducible if it has redundant weights that can be removed without significantly affecting the output of the network. In conclusion, reducibility is an important concept in neural network optimization. By reducing the number of weights in the matrices, we can improve the generalization ability of the network and reduce overfitting. There are various methods for testing for reducibility and removing redundant weights, including weight pruning and low-rank decomposition. By understanding and utilizing these techniques, we can improve the efficiency and performance of our neural networks.

We use the Kepler Mission to examine the frequency and properties of in situ planetary assembly in giant planetary systems. Our results indicate that the Kepler sample of planets around Sun-like stars is approximately 1700 per cent of that surrounding our Sun, but has a slightly smaller incidence of gas giant planets (366 versus 462). Planets with initial separations smaller than Jupiter’s at the time of impact, or smaller than 4 AU, are more common than those wider. We find that the gas giant planets surrounding young (< 400 Myr) stars are more closely associated with forming clusters than are their solar system counterparts. Finally, planets larger than 1 MJ are most prevalent in the inner regions of planetary systems around young stars, while intermediate-mass companions are more likely in systems orbiting older stars. The Kepler Mission has recently revealed that more than 80 per cent of Sun-like stars host planets in their habitable zones, and that the frequency of such planets increases with stellar mass (Petigura et al., 1). The Kepler planets also appear to have a wide range of sizes and masses, with \(\sim\) 40 per cent of the sample having masses \(<\) 2 M\({}_{\oplus}\), and \(\sim\) 15 per cent having masses \(>\) 10 M\({}_{\oplus,}\) and radii \(>\) 3 R\({}_{\oplus}.\) The Kepler sample is also much smaller than the solar system, and thus provides a unique opportunity to study in situ formation of planets. 1. The Kepler sample of planets around Sun-like stars has a frequency of \(\sim\) 1700 per cent of that around our Sun.   2. The Kepler planets surrounding young stars are more closely associated with forming clusters than their solar system counterparts.   3. The Kepler planets are larger and more massive than those around older stars.  # 1 Introduction  The Kepler Mission (Borucki et al., 2) has recently revealed that more than 80% of Sun-like stars host planets in their “habitable zones”, defined as the region between the orbit of the planet and the orbit of the star where the equilibrium temperature of the planet is greater than \(T_{\mathrm{eq}}=380\) K (Kopparapu et al., 3). The frequency of such planets increases with stellar mass \(M_{\star}\), with the most massive planets around the most massive stars (Petigura et al., in prep). The Kepler planets also appear to have many different sizes and masses, with \(\approx\) 40% of the sample having masses \(<2\) M\({}_{\oplus\,}\), and \(\approx\) 15% having masses \(>10\) M\({}_{J}\), and radii \(>3\) R\({}_{\oplus\,,}\) (Petigura et al., priv. comm.).  The Kepler planets are also much smaller than those around our Sun, and thus provide a unique opportunity to study the formation of planets in situ. The Kepler sample is much smaller than the solar system, and thus the sample of stars with planets is small, with only 151 stars with planets and 151 without. This small sample limits the power of statistical studies to draw conclusions about the formation of planets in situ. However, the small number of stars with planets allows for statistical studies of the properties of the planets themselves.  # 2 Methods  # 2.1 Planet Sample  We use the Kepler Input Catalog (KIC) to identify stars with planets in their habitable zones (Borucki et al., in prep.). The KIC is an archive of stellar parameters, including stellar mass, radius, and age. We identify the stars with planets by searching for planets in the Kepler Input Catalog. The KIC also provides the stellar metallicity, which we use to estimate the initial metallicity of the star. We assume that the initial metallicity is the same as the metallicity of the star at the time of planet formation.  We identify the stars with planets in the Kepler Input Catalog by searching for planets with orbital periods between 1 and 100 days, with semi-major axes between 0.05 and 20 AU, and with masses between 1 and 50 M\({}_{J}.\) We then restrict our sample to stars with metallicities between 0.01 and 0.05, and ages between 0 and 400 Myr. We also require that the planet is in the habitable zone, defined as the region between the orbit of a planet and the orbit of the star where the planet’s equilibrium temperature is greater than 380 K. We use the equilibrium temperature for the planet’s surface temperature (Kopparapu et al 2008), rather than the temperature of the planet’s interior, because the interior temperature is not known for most of the Kepler planets. The equilibrium temperature is also more closely associated with the habitability of the planet than the interior temperature.  We restrict our sample to stars with planets in the habitable zone to eliminate the possibility that the properties of the planets are affected by the stellar irradiation. However, this restriction is necessary because the properties of the Kepler planets are poorly known.  # 2.2 Statistical Analysis  We perform a series of statistical tests to examine the properties of the Kepler planets. We first calculate the cumulative frequency of planets in each mass-separation bin. We then calculate the cumulative frequency of planets in each age-metallicity bin, and the cumulative frequency of planets in each age-separation bin. We use the Kolmogorov-Smirnov (KS) test to compare the cumulative frequency of planets in each bin. We use the KS test to compare the cumulative frequency of planets in bins of age, metallicity, and separation.  We also use the KS test to compare the cumulative frequency in each.

In our analysis, we have compared the calculated and experimentally observed force-displacement curves for swimming motion of the three-link swimmer, and have evaluated its accuracy and effectiveness. We present families of simply connected symplectic Calabi-Yau 6-manifolds, which are constructed by crepant blowup. In particular, 33 different pairs of symplectic form and complex structure ( (X,ω), J) are considered, and it is proved that the Kähler surfaces of 39 of them are minimal. Calabi-Yau manifolds are the most well-known and studied class of manifolds, and are defined to be Kähler manifolds with constant holomorphic sectional curvature equal to \(-1\). In 1963, Calabi conjectured that any Calabi-Yau manifold is simply connected, i.e., its fundamental group is trivial.    In 1970, Yau proved the Calabi conjecture, by showing that a Calabi-Yau manifold has vanishing first Chern class.    In 1990, Tian proved that a Calabi-Yau manifold has vanishing first Betti number.    In 1995, K. Kodaira, S.-T. Yau and S.-T. Yau proved that a Calabi-Yau manifold is minimal, i.e., the canonical bundle is trivial.    In 10 years later, in 2005, M. Gross, S.-T. Yau and Y. Tian proved that a Calabi-Yau manifold is a K3 surface.    In 2007, M. Gross, S.-T Yau and Y. Tian proved that a simply connected Calabi-Yau manifold is a K3 surface or an elliptic curve.    In 2011, M. Gross, S.-T Tian and Y. Zhang proved that a simply connected Calabi-Yau manifold has Picard number 1 and is a K3 surface.    The goal of this paper is to prove that a simply connected symplectic Calabi-Yau 6-manifold is a K3 surface or an elliptic curve, by constructing a family of simply connected symplectic Calabi-Yau 6manifolds. 1. For each pair of symplectic form and complex structure ( \((X,\omega),J\)), it is proved that the Kähler surface of 39 of them are minimal.  2. It was proved by Zippin that every infinite-dimensional Banach space has a basis of vectors x, y ∈ X such that ‖x+y‖=2 for some x, y ∈ X. In this short note it is proved that almost all Banach spaces satisfy another consequence of that result: for every ε>0 every infinite-dimensional Banach space X has a basis x, y ∈ X such that ‖x+y‖>2−ε. Let X be a Banach space and let ε>0. We say that x, y ∈ X are ε-separated (with respect to X) if ‖x+y‖≥2−ε. We say that x, y ∈ X have ε-disjoint range if ‖x+y‖≥2−2ε. We say that x, y ∈ B(X) are ε-separated (with respect to B(X)) if there is a countable subset S of X such that {x,y}∩S=∅ and ‖x+y‖≥2−ε for all x, y ∈ S. (1) Let X be a Banach space and let ε > 0. Then X has a basis x, y ∈ X that is ε-separated with respect to X.   (2) Let X be a Banach space. Then almost all Banach spaces have a basis x, y ∈ X that is 1-separated with respect to X.    Proof:   (1) Let x, y ∈ X be arbitrary. Since X is infinite-dimensional, there exists a sequence {x_n} of vectors in X such that ‖x_n+y‖≥2−ε for all n. Since the set of all infinite sequences in X is uncountable, we can find a subsequence {x_{n_k}} of {x_n} that is ε-separated with respect to X, that is, ‖x_{n_k}+y‖≥2−ε for all k. In this paper, we demonstrate that adaptivity can be exponentially powerful for testing monotonicity of halfspaces. Specifically, we show that for any fixed dimension and monotonicity error, the number of queries required by an adaptive algorithm is exponentially smaller than the number of queries required by a non-adaptive algorithm. Our results provide new insights into the power of adaptivity in the context of monotonicity testing and have implications for the design of efficient algorithms for this problem. A halfspace is a subset of Euclidean space defined by a linear inequality, and a function is monotone if it preserves the order of the inputs. Monotonicity is a desirable property in many applications, as it implies that the function is non-decreasing or non-increasing, depending on the direction of the inequality.

We focus on the setting where the function is defined by a halfspace, and the inputs are points in Euclidean space. This problem has been well-studied in the literature, and there are a number of algorithms with different trade-offs in terms of time and query complexity.

In this paper, we show that adaptivity can be exponentially powerful for testing monotonicity of halfspaces. Specifically, we demonstrate that for any fixed dimension and monotonicity error, the number of queries required by an adaptive algorithm is exponentially smaller than the number of queries required by a non-adaptive algorithm. In this paper, we have shown that adaptivity can be exponentially powerful for testing monotonicity of halfspaces. Our results provide new insights into the power of adaptivity in this context and have implications for the design of efficient algorithms for this problem. Future work could explore the application of these ideas to other monotonicity testing problems or to other types of functions.

In this paper, we consider the obstacle problem for a fractional Monge--Amp\`ere equation. We also discuss the connection between the obstacle problem for the fractional Monge--Amp\`ere equation and other related problems in optimal transportation and geometric analysis. The fractional Monge--Amp\`ere equation is a widely studied equation in mathematics and physics, with applications in diverse areas such as optimal transportation, geometric analysis, and fluid dynamics. One important aspect of the fractional Monge--Amp\`ere equation is the obstacle problem, which involves finding a solution to the equation that satisfies certain constraints or conditions at specific points or regions in the domain.

In this paper, we present a general framework for solving the obstacle problem for the fractional Monge--Amp\`ere equation. We begin by introducing the necessary background material on the fractional Monge--Amp\`ere equation and the obstacle problem. Polarized Raman spectroscopy is a powerful tool for studying the vibrational modes of a material and its structural and chemical properties. However, traditional Raman spectroscopy techniques are often limited by their sensitivity, especially at low temperatures. In this study, we demonstrate the use of off-axis parabolic mirror optics to enhance the sensitivity of polarized Raman spectroscopy at low temperatures. By utilizing these optics, we are able to detect vibrational modes with a greater signal-to-noise ratio, enabling more accurate and precise measurements. Raman spectroscopy is a widely used technique for studying the vibrational modes of a material and its structural and chemical properties. It involves the scattering of monochromatic light by a material, with the scattered light being shifted in frequency due to the vibrational modes of the material. Polarized Raman spectroscopy involves the use of linearly polarized light, which allows for the analysis of different vibrational modes in a material based on their polarization.

One limitation of traditional Raman spectroscopy techniques is their sensitivity, particularly at low temperatures. This can make it difficult to accurately measure the vibrational modes of a material, especially in samples with low signal-to-noise ratios. In order to overcome this limitation, we have implemented the use of off-axis parabolic mirror optics in our polarized Raman spectroscopy setup.

Off-axis parabolic mirror optics are mirrors with a parabolic shape that are designed to focus light onto a specific point. By using these optics, we are able to enhance the sensitivity of our polarized Raman spectroscopy measurements at low temperatures by increasing the signal-to-noise ratio. In this study, we have demonstrated the use of off-axis parabolic mirror optics to enhance the sensitivity of polarized Raman spectroscopy at low temperatures. These optics enable the detection of vibrational modes with a greater signal-to-noise ratio, resulting in more accurate and precise measurements. This approach has the potential to greatly improve the capabilities of polarized Raman spectroscopy, particularly in challenging samples or environments.

At least five species of aquatic birds inhabit inland waters, including the American coot (Fulica americana), a generalist migratory shorebird living in northern North America and occasionally being sighted off the East Coast of the United States as well as in southeastern Canada. American coot populations in inland waters have been poorly studied due to the long distance to where many coots reside and their cryptic presence on inland waters. Consequently, understanding the collective physical dynamic movements that facilitate movement on water and their relationship to prey resources still remains largely an unanswered question. Here, we utilized field and telemetry data to explore spatio-temporal movements of coots on inland waters on a daily and seasonal basis. We hypothesized a predictable distribution of coot movements within inland waters, dependent on environmental conditions and the number of nearby foraging areas. We used two independent methods-(1) movement kernel density estimation from home-range estimates with an adaptive smoothing parameter and (2) a novel and robust statistical approach based on distance from a home range boundary, which captured patterns of movement in a non-parametric fashion. We used this methodology to predict home ranges within a home range boundary, as opposed to a boundary based on a fixed size, and used this method repeatedly across three individuals, providing a means to compare patterns in movement between individuals. Our results reveal that coots use different time periods to utilize different locations within inland water. In addition, coots exhibit a bimodal distribution of movement distance, primarily due to periods when resting at foraging areas. This bimodal distribution is likely due to the limited space available for birds to use, as coots tend to use the water when the bottom is shallow and relatively inaccessible. We demonstrate that this approach allows an analysis of fine-scale spatial and temporal movements. A better understanding of the dynamic spatio-temporal use of space and resource use could reveal critical information regarding the use of marine and freshwater habitats by migratory coots. Consequently understanding the collective physical dynamic movements that facilitate movement in inland waters and their relationship to prey resources still remains largely unanswered. We demonstrate a novel approach to describe the dynamic spatio-temporal movements of coots on inland water. Our results reveal a bimodal distribution of movement distance, likely due to the limited space available for birds. We demonstrate that this approach allows an analysis to fine-scale spatial and temporal movements.

In this work, we present the results of numerical simulations of a spherically symmetric Yang-Mills system on a de Sitter manifold. The de Sitter manifold is a four-dimensional space-time characterized by a positive cosmological constant, and the Yang-Mills system describes the behavior of gauge fields in this space-time. We use a finite difference method to solve the Yang-Mills equations on a lattice and study the time evolution of the system. Our simulations show that the Yang-Mills fields exhibit oscillatory behavior and exhibit a well-defined energy spectrum. The study of gauge theories on curved space-times has attracted significant attention in recent years due to their relevance in various areas of physics, including cosmology and particle physics. In particular, the de Sitter space-time, which is characterized by a positive cosmological constant, has received considerable attention due to its role in describing the current stage of the expansion of the universe.

The Yang-Mills theory is a gauge theory that describes the behavior of non-abelian gauge fields, and it plays a central role in our understanding of the fundamental forces of nature. In this work, we consider a spherically symmetric Yang-Mills system on a de Sitter manifold and perform numerical simulations to study the time evolution of the system. In conclusion, we have presented the results of numerical simulations of a spherically symmetric Yang-Mills system on a de Sitter manifold. Our simulations show that the Yang-Mills fields exhibit oscillatory behavior and have a well-defined energy spectrum. These results provide important insights into the behavior of gauge fields on a de Sitter space-time and have implications for our understanding of fundamental physics.

In this paper, we study the decay widths of the lowest massive Regge excitations of open strings. These excitations, also known as massive string modes, are important in string theory as they contribute to the scattering amplitudes of string particles. We calculate the decay widths of these modes using perturbative string theory, and compare our results to those obtained from other approaches. Our findings suggest that the decay widths of these modes are sensitive to the choice of renormalization scheme and can vary significantly depending on the scheme used. String theory is a theoretical framework that attempts to unify all of the fundamental forces of nature, including gravity, into a single theoretical framework. The decay widths of these modes, which describe the probability of the mode decaying into other particles, are important for understanding the behavior of these modes and the role they play in string theory.

In this paper, we calculate the decay widths of the lowest massive Regge excitations of open strings using perturbative string theory. We compare our results to those obtained from other approaches, and discuss the implications of our findings. In conclusion, we have studied the decay widths of the lowest massive Regge excitations of open strings using perturbative string theory. Our calculations suggest that the decay widths of these modes are sensitive to the choice of renormalization scheme, and can vary significantly depending on the scheme used. We have obtained a high time resolution 8-10 minute optical monitoring of SS433 based on observations made with the T90 telescope in Crimea and with the 2.0 m telescope in Las Campanas. The 1993 data were also gathered with 50 cm telescope in Las Campanas. In our monitoring we discovered the quasi-periodic oscillations on a time scale of hundreds of seconds, and the power spectrum of the T90 data was analysed. It consisted of the strong narrow lines that could not be interpreted in terms of white noise. The broad bands are at the frequencies of 0.98 d−1 and 0.35 d−1. These bands are characteristic the X-ray emission of the source. We find the amplitude of the X-ray noise to be comparable with the intensity of the optical signal of the source during the orbital modulation. This suggests that the strong optical signals may be connected with the appearance and disappearance of the hot spots and with the ejection of matter with high velocities during the active state of these objects. -  SS 433 is the most luminous X-ray binary in the Galaxy. It is a binary system with a supergiant companion (O9.5III-IV). The mass of the compact object is about 2.8 M⊙, and the orbital period is 3.265 d (1). The X-ray emission is powered by the accretion of matter from the companion star. The radio emission is believed to be produced by the relativistic plasma ejected from the inner Lagrangian point (2).   -  The optical emission of SS433 is thought to be produced by the emission of the hot spots on the surface of the companion star (3). The hot spots are created by the collision of the gas streams coming from the inner Lagrangian point and the outer Lagrangian point.   -  The hot spots move in the orbital plane with a velocity of 2000 km s−1 (4).   -  The X-ray and optical light curves of SS433 are similar, which suggests that the hot spots are also moving in the orbital plane (5). -  The hot spots are moving in the orbital plane. The amplitude of the X-ray noise is comparable with the amplitude of the optical signal. This suggests that the strong optical signals may originate from the appearance and disappearance of the hot spots and from the ejection of matter with high velocities.   -  The strong narrow lines in the power spectrum of the T90 data could not be interpreted in terms of white noise, and this suggests that the strong optical signals are caused by the quasi-periodic oscillations.

The frequency dependence of the magnetization precession of a multilayered metallic film and the temperature dependence of a single resonance line are investigated within the framework of single-domain micromagnetic theory. As shown previously [J. Magn. Magn. Mater. 33, 277 (2003)], a metallic system consisting of a few thin ferromagnetic layers of thickness less than the spin-diffusion length is characterized by a unique magnetic resonance peak (a resonance “fingerprint”) which shifts to zero magnetic field with increasing temperature due to the damping caused by magnon–magnon scattering. Here we extend the phenomenological model to deal with multilayered films consisting of two different ferromagnetic materials (i.e., layers A and B). We also include in the model the effect of a local magnetic field induced by a ferromagnetic layer adjacent to the paramagnetic one, the latter of (finite thickness) metal. It is found that in agreement with the experimental data the magnetic frequency is dependent on the temperature in a peculiar way. When the frequency dependence is plotted in the form of a power function of temperature, it has a pronounced minimum (the shape of these dependences is similar to the one observed in spin valves structures). The theory is also used in an investigation of the ferromagnetic resonance line shift when varying the bias magnetic field. To investigate the nature of the main contributions to this resonance line shift we calculate the effective anisotropy field of the film (which is found to be nonzero but of very small value) and the magnetization precession angle of the layers (which is found to be large in the vicinity of the resonance frequency, i.e., the angle becomes more than 90°). The magnetic properties of multilayered metallic films and multilayers with ferromagnetic and/or antiferromagnetic interfaces have been studied extensively in recent years [1–6]. It has been demonstrated that the magnetic properties of such films can be well understood within the framework of the single-domain micromagnetic theory [7–9]. It is known that the magnetic properties of multilayers with ferromagnetic and/or antiferromagnetic interfaces can be well understood within the framework of the macrospin model [10]. In the case of multilayers with ferromagnetic interfaces, the magnetic properties of such films are characterized by the existence of a unique magnetic resonance peak (a resonance fingerprint) which shifts to zero magnetic field with increasing the temperature due to the damping caused by magnon– magnon scattering. The theory of such a phenomenon was developed in [11]. The theory of the temperature dependence of a single resonance line was developed in [12]. In the present work, we extend the theory to the case of multilayered films consisting of two different ferromagnetic materials [13]. The frequency dependence of the magnetization precession of a metallic multilayered film is calculated in the framework of the single-domain micromagnetic approach. It is shown that in agreement with the experimental data the magnetic resonance frequency is dependent on the temperature in a peculiar manner. When the frequency dependence is plotted in the power form of temperature, it has a pronounced minimum [14]. The theory is also used in an investigation the ferromagnetic resonance line shift when varying the bias external magnetic field. The theory is used to investigate the nature of the main contributions to this line shift.

In this paper, we investigate the universality of the I-Love-Q relations in magnetized neutron stars. The I-Love-Q relations are a set of scaling laws that describe the relationships between the moment of inertia, the Love number, and the quadrupole moment of a neutron star. These scaling laws have been widely studied in the literature, but their universality in the presence of strong magnetic fields has not been thoroughly explored. Using numerical simulations, we demonstrate that the I-Love-Q relations remain valid in the presence of strong magnetic fields, and we provide a physical interpretation for this universality. Neutron stars are the densest objects in the universe, with masses of up to two solar masses packed into a sphere with a radius of only a few tens of kilometers. These extremely compact objects are formed when the core of a massive star collapses at the end of its life. Neutron stars are composed almost entirely of neutrons, which are held together by the strong force. In addition to their dense matter, neutron stars also possess some of the strongest magnetic fields in the universe, with surface fields reaching up to 10^15 Gauss.

The structure and properties of neutron stars are determined by the interplay between their dense matter and strong magnetic fields. One of the most interesting and important properties of neutron stars is their moment of inertia, which determines how they respond to external perturbations such as a gravitational wave. The Love number is another important property of neutron stars, which describes their deformability in response to external tidal forces. The quadrupole moment is a measure of the asymmetry of the mass distribution within a neutron star.

In recent years, the I-Love-Q relations have been widely studied as a way to understand the relationships between these three important properties of neutron stars. These scaling laws have been shown to hold for non-magnetized neutron stars, but their universality in the presence of strong magnetic fields has not been thoroughly explored. In this paper, we have demonstrated that the I-Love-Q relations are universal in magnetized neutron stars. Using numerical simulations, we have shown that these scaling laws hold even in the presence of strong magnetic fields. We have also provided a physical interpretation for this universality, which suggests that the I-Love-Q relations may be useful for constraining the equation of state of dense matter in the presence of strong magnetic fields. This work has important implications for our understanding of the structure and properties of neutron stars, and will be valuable for future studies of these fascinating objects.

This document discusses the principles and applications of law invariant risk measures and information divergences in risk management. We will provide an overview of the fundamental properties of these measures and discuss how they can be used to compare and aggregate risk across different sources and time periods. We will also discuss the challenges and limitations of using these tools in practice. Risk is an inherent part of any decision-making process, and effective risk management is crucial for the success of any organization. Law invariant risk measures are mathematical tools that are used to quantify and manage risk in various fields such as finance, insurance, and operations research. These measures are designed to be consistent with the principles of coherence, which state that the risk measure should satisfy certain mathematical properties such as monotonicity, subadditivity, and homogeneity.

Information divergences are a class of risk measures that are based on the concept of relative entropy, which measures the distance between two probability distributions. These measures are useful for comparing and aggregating risk across different sources and time periods.

In this document, we will discuss the fundamental properties of law invariant risk measures and the role of information divergences in risk management. We will provide examples of how these measures can be applied in practice and discuss the challenges and limitations of using these tools. Law invariant risk measures and information divergences are valuable tools for quantifying and managing risk in various fields. These measures are designed to be consistent with the principles of coherence and provide a useful framework for comparing and aggregating risk across different sources and time periods. While there are challenges and limitations to using these tools in practice, they can be an important part of an effective risk management strategy.

First, for a fixed time-horizon, there is an important time scale separation between the small-scale behavior of the volatility and the large-scale behavior of the price. Second, and more importantly, since the volatility function is non-stationary, and consequently, the risk-adjusted returns, whose volatility enters into the price process, depend on the time-scale, the asymptotic behavior of the risk-adjusted return must take into account the relative importance of small and large scales. Third, the market has a unique stationary distribution, which is a mixture of two gamma distributions. In this paper, we propose a new similarity measure called generalized compression dictionary distance (GCDD) that can be used to compare any two objects, regardless of their type or structure. The GCDD measure is based on the idea of using a dictionary to compress the objects and then comparing the resulting compressed representations. We show that the GCDD measure is a universal similarity measure, in the sense that it can capture the similarity between any two objects, as long as they can be represented as strings of symbols. In this paper, we propose a max-plus based approach to solve a class of discrete time linear regulator problems. Our method is based on the idea of constructing a max-plus algebraic system and using the resulting max-plus fundamental matrix to compute the optimal control input. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach through numerical examples and compare it with existing methods. Linear regulator problems are a class of optimization problems that arise in a wide range of control systems applications. These problems involve finding an optimal control input that minimizes a cost function subject to a set of linear constraints. In the discrete time case, the dynamics of the system are described by a set of difference equations.

Traditionally, these problems have been solved using dynamic programming or linear quadratic control techniques. However, these methods can be computationally intensive, especially for large scale systems. In this paper, we propose a max-plus based approach to solve a class of discrete time linear regulator problems.

The max-plus algebra is a mathematical structure that has been used in the analysis and design of discrete event systems. In this algebra, the addition operation is replaced with the max operation and the multiplication operation is replaced with the addition operation. This algebra has several useful properties that make it well suited for control systems applications.

In our approach, we construct a max-plus algebraic system by replacing the addition and multiplication operations in the difference equations with the max and addition operations, respectively. We then use the resulting max-plus fundamental matrix to compute the optimal control input. In this paper, we have proposed a max-plus based approach to solve a class of discrete time linear regulator problems. We have demonstrated the effectiveness of our approach through numerical examples and have shown that it can provide a computationally efficient alternative to existing methods. Further research is needed to extend our approach to more general classes of linear regulator problems and to explore its potential applications in other areas of control systems.

In this paper, we investigate the displaced vertex signature of the type-I seesaw mechanism, which is a well-known extension of the Standard Model that can explain the smallness of neutrino masses. We demonstrate that the displaced vertex signature, which is characterized by a significant displacement of the interaction point from the primary vertex, is a distinctive feature of the type-I seesaw and can be used to differentiate it from other models. Using simulations based on the LHC experimental conditions, we show that the displaced vertex signature can be detected with high significance in the current and future LHC experiments, providing a promising new avenue for probing the type-I seesaw mechanism. The Standard Model of particle physics has been successful in describing a wide range of phenomena, but it has several limitations. One such limitation is the inability to explain the smallness of neutrino masses, which has led to the introduction of various extensions to the Standard Model. One such extension is the type-I seesaw mechanism, which postulates the existence of heavy right-handed neutrinos that mix with the left-handed neutrinos of the Standard Model, resulting in the observed small neutrino masses.

The type-I seesaw mechanism has several distinctive features that can be used to differentiate it from other models. One such feature is the displaced vertex signature, which is characterized by a significant displacement of the interaction point from the primary vertex. This signature arises due to the long lifetime of the heavy right-handed neutrinos, which can travel a significant distance before decaying into Standard Model particles.

In this paper, we investigate the displaced vertex signature of the type-I seesaw mechanism and demonstrate its potential for probing this extension of the Standard Model. Using simulations based on the LHC experimental conditions, we show that the displaced vertex signature can be detected with high significance in the current and future LHC experiments. In conclusion, we have demonstrated that the displaced vertex signature is a distinctive feature of the type-I seesaw mechanism and can be used to probe this extension of the Standard Model. Using simulations based on the LHC experimental conditions, we have shown that the displaced vertex signature can be detected with high significance in the current and future LHC experiments. This provides a promising new avenue for probing the type-I seesaw mechanism and exploring the origin of neutrino masses.

In this paper, we present our calculations of these quantities using a variety of theoretical approaches. The ANC is a related quantity that describes the probability of finding the proton and the beryllium-7 nucleus at a large distance from each other, in the asymptotic region where the nuclear force is no longer significant. The ANC is related to the astrophysical S-factor through a known mathematical relationship, making it a useful quantity for studying nuclear reactions in astrophysical environments.

Our results provide valuable insight into the reaction mechanism and may have implications for the production of boron-8 in the solar interior. In summary, we have presented our calculations of the ANC for the reaction $p+^7Be\to ^8B$ and the direct $^7Be(p,\gamma)^8B$ astrophysical S-factor at solar energies. Our results provide valuable insight into the reaction mechanism and may have implications for the production of boron-8 in the solar interior. In this paper, we study the phase transition behavior of low-temperature antiferromagnetic Potts models, a class of spin systems that exhibit a rich variety of phase transitions. By analyzing the entropy of the system, we demonstrate that the phase transition in these models is driven by entropy rather than temperature. This finding has significant implications for our understanding of phase transitions in spin systems and may have important consequences for the design of materials with desirable phase transition properties. Phase transitions, the transformation of a system from one phase to another, are a fundamental concept in physics and have been extensively studied in various systems ranging from liquids to magnets. The Potts model, a simple model of interacting spins, has been widely used to study phase transitions due to its simplicity and versatility. In particular, low-temperature antiferromagnetic Potts models, where the interactions between spins are antiferromagnetic (i.e., they favor an alternating pattern of spins) at low temperatures, have been shown to exhibit a rich variety of phase transitions.

Traditionally, phase transitions have been understood to be driven by temperature, with the transition occurring at a critical temperature where the system becomes unstable. However, recent studies have suggested that entropy, a measure of the disorder or randomness of a system, may also play a significant role in driving phase transitions. In this paper, we investigate the role of entropy in the phase transition of low-temperature antiferromagnetic Potts models. Through our analysis of the entropy of the system, we have demonstrated that the phase transition in low-temperature antiferromagnetic Potts models is driven by entropy rather than temperature. This finding has significant implications for our understanding of phase transitions in spin systems and may have important consequences for the design of materials with desirable phase transition properties. Further studies are needed to explore the full implications of this result and to determine the extent to which it holds for other types of phase transitions.

The Optimal Margin Distribution Machine is a machine learning algorithm designed to optimize the distribution of margins within a business. It takes into account various factors such as market competition, cost of goods sold, and target profit margins to determine the optimal price for a product or service. By using the Optimal Margin Distribution Machine, businesses can maximize their profits while still being competitive in the market. Pricing is a crucial aspect of any business, as it determines the profitability of a company. Setting the right price for a product or service can be challenging, as there are many factors to consider. The Optimal Margin Distribution Machine is designed to help businesses make informed pricing decisions by taking into account various factors that impact margins.

The Optimal Margin Distribution Machine uses machine learning algorithms to analyze data on market competition, cost of goods sold, and target profit margins. It then uses this information to calculate the optimal price for a product or service, ensuring that the business can maximize its profits while still being competitive in the market. The Optimal Margin Distribution Machine is a powerful tool for businesses looking to optimize their pricing strategies. By using machine learning algorithms to analyze data on market competition, cost of goods sold, and target profit margins, it helps businesses make informed pricing decisions that maximize profits while still being competitive in the market. Whether you are a small business owner or a large corporation, the Optimal Margin Distribution Machine can help you optimize your pricing strategies and increase your profitability.

It is a fundamental concept in the study of physics, and is essential for understanding the behavior of physical systems at a microscopic level. Point mechanics is based on a set of fundamental laws, including Newton's laws of motion, which describe the relationship between forces and the motion of objects.

One of the key concepts in point mechanics is the conservation of energy and momentum. Energy is the ability to do work, and it can take various forms, such as kinetic energy (the energy of motion) and potential energy (the energy stored due to an object's position or configuration). The conservation of energy states that energy cannot be created or destroyed, only converted from one form to another. Similarly, the conservation of momentum states that the total momentum of a system remains constant unless external forces act on the system.

Another important concept in point mechanics is work, which is defined as the product of the force applied to an object and the distance over which it is applied. Work is a measure of the amount of energy transferred to or from an object, and it is a key factor in determining the motion of a point. The kinetic energy of a point is another important factor, and is defined as the energy associated with the motion of the point. It is equal to one half of the point's mass multiplied by its velocity squared. In summary, point mechanics is the study of the motion of a single point in space and is based on a set of fundamental laws, including Newton's laws of motion and the conservation of energy and momentum.
