We present the results of a study on the spatially-resolved dust properties of the host galaxy of the gamma-ray burst (GRB) 980425, using multi-wavelength imaging and spectroscopy data. Our analysis shows that the dust in the host galaxy has a clumpy distribution, with variations in the dust-to-gas ratio and extinction across the galaxy. We also find evidence for the presence of multiple dust components, including a diffuse component and a more compact component associated with star-forming regions. Our results provide new insights into the role of dust in the evolution and physical conditions of GRB host galaxies. GRBs are some of the most energetic and luminous phenomena in the universe, and are thought to be the result of the collapse of a massive star or the merger of two compact objects. The host galaxies of GRBs provide an important laboratory for studying the physical conditions and evolution of star-forming galaxies, including the distribution and properties of dust.

In this study, we aim to investigate the spatially-resolved dust properties of the host galaxy of GRB 980425, one of the closest known GRBs. This particular event was notable for its relatively low energy and low redshift, making it an ideal target for studying the dust properties of its host galaxy in detail.

To conduct our analysis, we obtained multi-wavelength imaging and spectroscopy data from various telescopes, including the Hubble Space Telescope, the Spitzer Space Telescope, and the Gemini Observatory. We used these data to construct detailed maps of the dust distribution and extinction across the host galaxy. Our analysis of the spatially-resolved dust properties of the GRB 980425 host galaxy has revealed a clumpy distribution of dust, with variations in the dust-to-gas ratio and extinction across the galaxy. We also found evidence for the presence of multiple dust components, including a diffuse component and a more compact component associated with star-forming regions. These results provide new insights into the role of dust in the evolution and physical conditions of GRB host galaxies, and have implications for our understanding of the origin and nature of GRBs.

A typical goal for model-free RL systems is to approximate complex environments with a limited number of interactions and transitions. Therefore, the model should be able to generalize beyond learning a target policy online. In this work, we present Generalizing Multi-step Lambda-Returns (Gen-MLRaRe), which generalizes the $\lambda$-return family of algorithms designed for modeling environments with single-step returns. We achieve this by generalizing each $\lambda$-return, allowing it to mix multiple time-steps using the model to predict their mixture probabilities. Our method, Gen-MLRaRe, shows significant improvements on challenging domains, specifically the Arcade Learning Environment, reaching high rewards on problems with deep multi-step trajectories. Deep reinforcement learning (RL) has demonstrated impressive results in a variety of domains, ranging from video games to robotic control. The success of RL is based on the ability to learn an optimal policy that maximizes the expected return over an infinite horizon. However, in many domains, a model of the environment is not available, and only a finite number of interactions and transitions are available. This is referred to as the off-policy setting, and the model must be able to generalize beyond learning a target policy.    Existing approaches to modeling environments with multi-step returns include the Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (DDPG) algorithm (Lillicrap et al., ) and the Q-learning algorithm (Watkins and Dayan, ). Both algorithms use a model to predict the next state, which is then used to update the policy and value functions. However, both algorithms have several limitations. First, DDPG is restricted to single-step returns, and Q-learning can only be used to model single-step returns. Second, both algorithms have a high sample complexity, which scales with the length of the trajectory. We propose Gen-MLRaRe, a novel algorithm that generalizes the $\lambda$-return algorithms (Kumar et al., ), which are designed for modeling environments with single-step returns, to model environments with multi-step returns. Gen-MLRaRe is able to generalize across environments by mixing multiple time-steps using the model to predict the mixture probabilities. We demonstrate that Gen-MLRaRe is able to model environments with deep multi-step trajectories, and outperforms existing methods on challenging domains, specifically the Arcade Learning environment.

Horton Law states that the size of a stream is directly proportional to the area it drains. In this paper, we review the current state of research on pruning and its applications in various domains, including computer vision, natural language processing, and speech recognition. We also discuss the limitations and challenges of pruning, as well as potential future directions for research. Model compression is an important problem in machine learning, as it allows for the deployment of complex models on resource-constrained devices such as mobile phones and embedded systems. One popular technique for model compression is pruning, which involves removing redundant or unnecessary parameters from a trained model. Pruning has been shown to be effective in a variety of tasks, including image classification, language translation, and speech recognition.

There are several approaches to pruning, including magnitude-based pruning, which removes weights with the smallest magnitude, and structure-based pruning, which removes entire channels or layers from the model. Pruning can be performed either before or after training, and can be done either manually or automatically using algorithms such as genetic algorithms or reinforcement learning.

Despite its promising results, pruning is not without limitations. It is sensitive to the choice of pruning criteria and can lead to degraded performance if not carefully tuned. It is also computationally expensive, as it requires retraining the pruned model to recover lost performance. Finally, pruning can be brittle, as the removed parameters may be important for the model's generalization ability. In summary, pruning is a promising technique for model compression, with the potential to significantly reduce the size and computational requirements of a model while maintaining or even improving its performance. However, it is important to carefully consider the trade-offs and limitations of pruning, and to carefully tune the pruning criteria to ensure good performance. Future research should focus on developing more efficient and robust pruning algorithms, as well as exploring the potential for combining pruning with other model compression techniques such as quantization and knowledge distillation.

In this paper, we present a multi-dimensional model for the X-ray spectra of accretion-disk outflows in active galactic nuclei (AGN). We apply our model to a hydrodynamical simulation of an AGN accretion disk and compare the results to observational data. Our analysis shows that the multi-dimensional model is able to reproduce the observed X-ray spectra with high accuracy, providing strong support for the presence of accretion-disk outflows in AGN. Active galactic nuclei (AGN) are some of the most powerful objects in the universe, with luminosities that can exceed the combined output of all the stars in their host galaxies. These luminosities are thought to be powered by the accretion of matter onto a supermassive black hole at the center of the AGN. As the matter falls into the black hole, it forms an accretion disk, which can reach temperatures of up to a few million degrees Kelvin. At these temperatures, the accretion disk can emit X-rays, making AGN a major source of X-ray emission in the universe.

One of the key features of AGN is the presence of outflows, which are thought to be driven by the accretion disk. These outflows can take the form of either a wind or a jet, and can have a significant impact on the surrounding environment. The study of AGN outflows is therefore of great importance for understanding the physics of accretion disks and the role they play in the evolution of galaxies.

In previous papers (references 1 and 2), we developed a multi-dimensional model for the X-ray spectra of AGN accretion-disk outflows. In this paper, we apply our model to a hydrodynamical simulation of an AGN accretion disk and compare the results to observational data. Our analysis shows that the multi-dimensional model is able to reproduce the observed X-ray spectra of AGN accretion-disk outflows with high accuracy. This provides strong support for the presence of these outflows and the role they play in the physics of accretion disks. Further studies using our model will allow us to more accurately characterize the properties of AGN outflows and their impact on the surrounding environment.

Numerical simulations of chaotic dynamic systems have always been a challenging task due to their sensitivity to initial conditions and the difficulty in predicting their long-term behavior. In this work, we present a new strategy for obtaining reliable solutions for chaotic dynamic systems using clean numerical simulation. Our approach involves the use of advanced numerical methods and error control techniques to ensure the accuracy and stability of the simulation. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our strategy through several examples of chaotic dynamic systems and compare the results with those obtained using traditional methods. Chaotic dynamic systems are characterized by their sensitivity to initial conditions, which leads to unpredictable long-term behavior. This makes it difficult to accurately simulate their behavior using traditional numerical methods. In recent years, there has been a growing interest in developing new strategies for obtaining reliable solutions for chaotic dynamic systems.

One such strategy is clean numerical simulation, which involves the use of advanced numerical methods and error control techniques to ensure the accuracy and stability of the simulation. This approach has been shown to be effective in a variety of chaotic dynamic systems, including fluid dynamics, meteorology, and chemical reactions.

In this work, we present a detailed description of the clean numerical simulation strategy and demonstrate its effectiveness through several examples of chaotic dynamic systems. We also compare the results obtained using this approach with those obtained using traditional methods to demonstrate the superiority of clean numerical simulation. In conclusion, clean numerical simulation is a promising strategy for obtaining reliable solutions for chaotic dynamic systems. By using advanced numerical methods and error control techniques, it is possible to achieve high accuracy and stability in the simulation of chaotic dynamic systems. Our results demonstrate the effectiveness of this approach and highlight its potential for use in a variety of applications.

This paper discusses the process by which individuals form and change their opinions, specifically focusing on the phenomenon of swift collective disillusionment triggered by unmet expectations. We first provide an overview of the various factors that influence opinion formation and change, including social influence, personal experience, and cognitive biases. We then examine the role of expectations in shaping people's perceptions and attitudes, and how the failure to meet these expectations can lead to a rapid shift in opinion. We also discuss the potential consequences of such a shift, including the potential for widespread negative impacts on social cohesion and trust. Opinion formation is a complex process that is influenced by a variety of factors, including social influence, personal experience, and cognitive biases. People often rely on these sources of information to make sense of the world around them and to form opinions about various issues and events. However, opinions are not fixed and can change over time as people encounter new information or experiences.

One important factor that can influence opinion change is the role of expectations. Expectations are anticipations or assumptions about how something will turn out or how someone will behave. These expectations can be based on past experiences, social norms, or other sources of information. When expectations are not met, it can lead to a sense of disappointment or disillusionment.

In some cases, the failure to meet expectations can lead to a swift and dramatic shift in opinion. This can occur when a large number of people hold similar expectations and those expectations are not met. This can lead to a collective sense of disillusionment, which can have negative consequences on social cohesion and trust. In conclusion, opinion formation is a dynamic process that is influenced by a variety of factors, including expectations. When expectations are not met, it can lead to swift and dramatic shifts in opinion, which can have negative consequences on social cohesion and trust. Understanding the role of expectations in opinion formation and change is important for predicting and addressing potential shifts in public opinion and for promoting social cohesion and trust.

This paper presents the design and implementation of a novel integrated system for live 3D human reconstruction and motion capturing. It is an extensible system designed for diverse scenarios with various requirements. The system includes two stages: reconstruction and capturing. The former aims to reconstruct the shape and appearance of a human body, and the latter is used to understand and describe the motion of a human body. Our system reconstructs the whole human body from a calibrated stereo camera set or a monocular camera. By tracking feature points defined on a silhouette, the 3D shape of a body can be automatically reconstructed, and then texture mapping is performed on the reconstructed surface to produce the visible 3D human body. Additionally, a 45 degree view angle is adopted to reconstruct the silhouette of a human body in a low resolution. For a full body reconstruction, the extracted feature points are further used to reconstruct the 3D shape of a head in real time. In the capturing stage, a single wearable sensor system consisting of two kinds of inertial measurement units is developed to capture the 3D positions and angular velocities of body joints. Two kinds of data fusion algorithms, i.e., Kalman filter and weighted average, are employed to merge the information from multiple sensors. For further motion capturing, a motion acquisition device (MAD), which consists of sensors for acceleration and angular velocity measurements, is designed for simultaneous capture of both high- and low-frequency motions from different view angles. In the experiments, several users wearing sensors and MADs have taken part in four activities, including sitting to raise a hand, standing up, walking and climbing a staircase. By comparing with the results obtained by a commercial software system, the proposed system is shown to be able to provide a realistic and accurate 3D reconstruction. In recent years, the development of 3D reconstruction and motion capturing technology has attracted great attention in the fields of human computer interaction (HCI) and virtual reality (VR) [1, 2, 3, 4]. It is essential to reconstruct the shape and appearance of a human model in real time for various applications, such as virtual gaming, animation, and virtual human performance. The captured 3D motion can also be used to understand human actions and perform motion synthesis.  However, due to the complexity of a human body, the problem of reconstructing a 3D human body in real time is still challenging. In order to achieve a realistic reconstruction, the 3D shape of a human body should be reconstructed in a high resolution, and then the surface texture should be generated to produce a visible 3D human body. Although many 3D reconstruction systems have been proposed in the literature, most of them are designed for a specific application, and most of them are based on a single sensor [5, 6, 7, 8]. Some other systems use multiple sensors to capture the motion of a human body [9, 10, 11, 12]. However, these systems usually use a fixed camera setup and can only reconstruct a 3D human body from a single view angle. This paper presents a novel integrated system for live 3D reconstruction and motion capturing. It is an extensibility system designed for diverse scenarios with various requirements. Our system reconstructs the whole human body in real time, and it can also be used to track the motion of a human body. In the experiments, several users wearing sensors have taken part in four activities, including sitting up to raise a hand, standing up, walking, and climbing a staircase. The results have shown that our system can provide a realistic and accurate 3D reconstruction of a human body.    Author:      Xiaohui Zhang    Contact:      Xiaohui Zhang, zhangx@bnu.edu.cn.

In this paper, we investigate the existence and uniqueness of a density probability solution for the stationary Doi-Edwards equation. This equation arises in the study of non-equilibrium statistical mechanics, and is used to describe the time evolution of the density probability of a system in a state of nonequilibrium. We prove that under certain conditions, a unique density probability solution exists for the stationary Doi-Edwards equation. The Doi-Edwards equation is a widely used mathematical model in the field of non-equilibrium statistical mechanics. It describes the time evolution of the density probability of a system in a state of nonequilibrium, and has applications in a wide range of fields, including chemical kinetics, polymer physics, and biology. The stationary Doi-Edwards equation is a specific form of the Doi-Edwards equation that describes the density probability of a system in a steady state.

One of the fundamental questions in the study of the stationary Doi-Edwards equation is the existence and uniqueness of its solutions. In particular, it is important to know whether a unique density probability solution exists for a given set of initial conditions. This question has been studied extensively in the literature, but a complete understanding of the conditions under which a unique solution exists has not yet been reached.

In this paper, we aim to address this question by proving the existence and uniqueness of a density probability solution for the stationary Doi-Edwards equation under certain conditions. Our results provide a new insight into the behavior of the stationary Doi-Edwards equation and have important implications for the study of non-equilibrium systems. In this paper, we have proven the existence and uniqueness of a density probability solution for the stationary Doi-Edwards equation under certain conditions. Our results provide a new understanding of the behavior of this important equation and have important implications for the study of non-equilibrium systems. Further research is needed to fully understand the conditions under which a unique solution exists and to explore the practical applications of our results.

Cox [Cox, D. (1972). Regression Models and Life-Tables. John Wiley & Sons, New York] developed an extension of the standard univariate regression model for disease progression to multi-variate and non-linear scenarios. Using the maximum likelihood approach, Cox derived a partial likelihood (Coxian) based on the latent failure time of the subjects that allows for non-linear associations in outcome. Coxian models are widely adopted due to its robustness and intuitive interpretation; however, they require the calculation of latent failure times, which are not always available when computing complete lifetime data. This article proposes an extension of the Coxian approach to censored data, referred to as coxphMIC. The proposed regression model, coxphMIC incorporates a variable selection penalty and utilizes the sparse information from the partial survival estimate using the mixed interaction penalty. In addition, coxphMIC performs model estimation by maximizing the penalized partial likelihood, and uses the penalized profile likelihood approach to compute the maximum partial likelihood estimates. We conduct extensive simulation studies to demonstrate that our model offers improvements over the existing models, especially on the type I error control and computational efficiency. Our package coxphMIC is publicly available at CRAN https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/coxphMIC/index.html and the R vignettes. Coxian models (Cox, 1972; Cox, 1975; Fine and Gray, 1996) are widely adopted in the statistical literature for the analysis of censored data and survival data (Freedman, 1981; Therneau, 2002). Coxian models provide a framework for the analysis of a survival time (or failure time) in a non-linear setting by using a non-linear regression model. Coxian models have been used to model time-to-event data (e.g., survival, recurrence, time to a particular event), and have been applied to various research settings, such as survival analysis in medicine (Suzuki et al., 2008; Huang et al., 2015; Yang et al., 2017; Yu et al., 2018), epidemiology (Mazumder and Zhang, 2015; Zhang et al., 2016; Lin et al., 2017), and biostatistics (Kalbfleisch and Prentice, 1995; Li et al., 2012; Wang et al., 2013; Yu et al., 2020).    Coxian models are based on the partial likelihood approach, and thus, the estimates are based on the latent failure time (i.e., the time to the event of interest). However, the latent failure time is not always available when computing complete lifetime data. Thus, the Coxian approach is not appropriate in these situations.    In this article, we propose a novel extension of the Coxian approach to censored data (i.e., coxphMIC). The proposed model, coxphMIC, utilizes the sparse information from the partial survival estimate and utilizes the mixed interaction penalty to account for the interaction between the covariates and the treatment effect. The proposed model is motivated by the Cox model, where the treatment effect is modeled as a function of the covariates. The proposed model can be used for both complete and censored data. The model can also be extended to the case where the covariates have different levels of interactions with the treatment effect.    Our model is computationally efficient, and performs well in terms of the type I error control. We conduct extensive simulation studies to demonstrate that the proposed model offers improvements over the existing models. We apply the proposed model to a real dataset to demonstrate the usefulness of the proposed model.    The rest of the article is organized as follows: Section 2 discusses the background and related work; Section 3 provides the proposed model and its estimation procedure; Section 4 presents the simulation studies; Section 5 presents the analysis of a real dataset; and Section 6 concludes the article. * The proposed model is computationally efficient, and performs well in term of the type I error control.   * The proposed model is a flexible extension of the Coxian approach to censored data.   * The proposed model is a good alternative to the existing models when the latent failure time is not available.

The diamond product is a binary operation on matrices that has several important properties and applications in linear algebra and computer science. In this paper, we present a novel interpretation of the diamond product in terms of lattice paths. Specifically, we show that the diamond product of two matrices can be represented as the sum of all possible lattice paths between the corresponding elements of the matrices. This interpretation provides a new geometric understanding of the diamond product and allows for a more intuitive proof of its properties. The diamond product, denoted by a diamond symbol ($\diamond$), is a binary operation on matrices that is defined as follows: given two matrices $A$ and $B$ with the same size, their diamond product is a matrix $C$ with the same size, where each element $c_{ij}$ is defined as the sum of all possible products of elements from $A$ and $B$ with the same indices, i.e.

$$c_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^n a_{ik} \cdot b_{kj}$$

where $n$ is the size of the matrices. The diamond product has several important properties and applications, including being associative and having a distributive property over matrix addition. We develop a magnetohydrodynamic model for interactions of the planetary magnetic field with the disk gas within the framework of the magnetoproptotic equilibrium. Within this model, the magnetoproptotic equilibrium is the stationary solution of magnetohydrodynamics with the Maxwell stress balance replacing the classical Navier–Stokes equation. By using this equilibrium to develop an analytic magnetic model for gas giants in hydrostatic balance, we evaluate the effect of the planetary magnetic field on the disk gas. The torque due to the interaction between the field and the disk gas is shown to be large, especially when the field is aligned with a disk axis. Our model suggests that such a field is strong enough to open a magnetic gap at a disk midplane or to excite MRI turbulence locally. This result implies that planet–magnetosphere–disk interactions can strongly influence the structure of gaseous and dusty disks around gas giants. - Magnetohydrodynamics (MHD) is a fundamental theory of the magnetic field and the flow in a plasma. However, the applicability of MHD to astrophysical systems is limited because it is based on the ideal gas equation of state and the ideal Ohm’s law. These assumptions are violated in astrophysical plasmas, which are in general non-uniform and highly ionized. In particular, MHD cannot be applied to protoplanetary disks, which are highly ionized, non-uniform, and turbulent (e.g., (Pudritz et al., 1)).   - In the context of protoplanetary disks, MHD is often used to model the gas dynamics in the presence of a magnetic field. In this case, the gas flow is described by the Euler equations, which are similar to the Navier–Stokes equation except that the viscous stress tensor is replaced by the Maxwell stress tensor.   - In the context of planet formation, MHD is used to model the interaction of a planet with the disk gas. In this case, the gas flow is determined by the magnetohydrodynamical equations, which are similar to the Euler equations except that the viscous stress tensor is replaced by a generalized form of the Maxwell stress tensor, which is called the generalized Ohm’s law (e.g., (Ghosh and Lamb, 2; Desch, 3; Masset, 4; Zhu et al., 5; Bai and Stone, 6)).   - In the present paper, we use the magnetoproptotic equilibrium as a model of a disk gas. In this case, the gas is assumed to be in magnetohydrodynamical equilibrium with the magnetic field, and the magnetic field is assumed to be in magnetohydrodynamical equilibrium.   - We develop an analytic model for the magnetoproptotic equilibrium, which is a stationary solution of magnetohydrodynamics with the Maxwell stresses replacing the viscous stresses.   - We use the magnetoproptotic equilibrium to develop an analytic model for a gas giant in hydrostatic balance. The model includes the effects of the gas pressure, the magnetic pressure, and the magnetic tension.   - We evaluate the effect of the planetary magnetic field on a disk gas. Our model suggests that a planet with a strong magnetic field can open a magnetic gap at a disk midplane and excite MRI turbulence locally. - We have developed a model for the interaction of the planetary magnetic field with the disk gas. Our model suggests that planet–magnetosphere–disk interactions strongly influence the structure of gaseous and dusty disks.   - Our model is limited to the case when the gas pressure is dominant over the magnetic pressure and the magnetic tension. In the case when the magnetic pressure is dominant over the gas pressure and the magnetic tension, our model suggests that the magnetic field is not strong enough to open a magnetic gap at a midplane.   - In the case when the gas pressure and the magnetic pressure are comparable, our model suggests that a planet with a strong magnetic flux can open a magnetic gap at a disk mid-plane or excite MRI turbulence locally.   - In the case when the gas and the magnetic pressures are comparable, our model suggests that the disk gas is strongly magnetized. The magnetic field is strong enough to open a magnetic gap. The magnetic field is also strong enough to excite MRI turbulence locally.

With the vast amount of information available online, it is increasingly important to have effective methods for filtering and organizing this information in order to find what we are looking for. One key aspect of information retrieval systems is intelligent subject search, which involves using various techniques to accurately retrieve relevant information in response to a user's query.

One common approach to intelligent subject search is keyword-based search, which involves matching the user's query to the keywords or phrases present in the documents being searched. This approach can be effective for simple queries, but can become less accurate as the complexity of the query increases.

Another approach to intelligent subject search is natural language processing, which involves analyzing the structure and meaning of the user's query in order to better understand their intent. This approach can be more effective for complex queries, as it allows the search system to understand the meaning behind the words being used.

Another approach to intelligent subject search is the use of machine learning techniques, which involve training a model on a large dataset of documents and queries in order to accurately predict the relevance of a given document to a given query. Machine learning techniques can be highly effective, but also require a large amount of data and computational resources to train and maintain. In conclusion, there are various approaches to implementing intelligent subject search, each with their own advantages and disadvantages. Keyword-based search is simple but can be less accurate for complex queries, natural language processing can better understand the meaning behind queries, and machine learning techniques can be highly effective but require a large amount of data and computational resources.  Regardless of the approach taken, intelligent subject search is an essential aspect of information retrieval systems and will continue to be an active area of research and development.

This paper is devoted to the investigation of the influence of spin-exchange scattering of Cooper pairs on the transport properties of SFS weak links. The case of a weak coupling is considered, when the magnitude of the order parameters is so small that the phase difference φ between these order parameters plays an important role. As we have shown in recent works, the introduction of spin-exchange scattering of Cooper pairs to the dynamics of Cooper pairs leads to the suppression of a conventional superconductivity that can be characterized as a singlet superconductivity. As we have demonstrated, in the case of a weak singlet superconductivity the situation is substantially different, and the phase dependence of the pair current becomes strongly pronounced and differs from the one obtained when there is none. This leads to a modification of the phase dependence of the current-phase relation, which takes the form sin2 φ, and to a strong enhancement of the critical current, which can exceed some hundred percent of the critical current of the junction without spin–Exchange scattering. We report the first results of ALMA Frontier Fields 1.1 mm continuum and molecular line surveys of three rich galaxy clusters, Abell 2744, MACSJ0416.1-2403 and MACSJ1149.5+2223. The sources observed all have bright optical counterparts and are selected to be rich clusters at red shift 0.9 to 1.1. The ALMA observations are centered on the radio continuum emitting galaxy clusters and allow one to study the cold dust and molecular gas properties in the galaxy clusters. Our ALMA continuum observations trace the extended dust continuum emission with spatial resolutions of R<2''. At ~1.1 mm, we detect continuum emission in all three clusters with 2 sigma significance above the background and recover 79-84% of the total flux density in Abell 2744, 61-66% of the total flux density in MACSJ0416.1-2403 and 79-86% of the total flux density in MACSJ1149.5+2223. For Abell 2744, we detect continuum emission from 44 out of 52 spectroscopically confirmed cluster galaxies with rest-frame equivalent widths between 1 and 150 Å. For MACSJ0416.1-2403, we detect continuum emission from 27 out of 34 spectroscopically confirmed cluster galaxies with equivalent widths between 1 and 160 \AA, and for MACSJ1149.5+2223 we detect a continuum emission from all 30 spectroscopically confirmed cluster galaxies with rest-frame equivalent width between 1 and 150 \AA. We report observations of several CO and HCN line transitions in the ALMA data, which together with the radio observations allow us to estimate the molecular gas masses and dust masses of the observed galaxies. We also detect several [CII] line emission in the observations of all 3 clusters. - ALMA Frontier Fields 1.1 mm continuum observations of Abell 2744, MACS J0416.1-24 and MACS J1149.5+22   - ALMA Frontier Fields 1.3 mm continuum observations of Abell 2745, MACS J0416.0-2403 and MACS J1149.4+2223   - ALMA Frontier Fields [CII] observations of Abell 2744, Abell 2745, MACSJ0416.0-24 and MACSJ1149.4+22   - ALMA Frontier Fields CO observations of Abell 2744, A2745, MACSJ0043.7-11 and MACS J1149.6+2223   - ALMA [CII] observations of Abell 1835, MACS J0416-2403 and MACS J2135.3-0023   - ALMA [C II] observations of Abell 2744   - ALMA [C II] and CO observations of Abell 2744. - We detect continuum emission in Abell 2744, MACS0416.1-24, and MACSJ1149.8+2223 with 2 sigma significance above the background and find that 79-84% of the continuum flux density is recovered.   - We detect continuum emission in MACS0416.0-24 with 2 sigma significance above the background and 61-66% of the continuum flux density is recovered. This document presents a method for obtaining binary random sequences from decimal sequences. The resulting binary sequences are shown to have statistical properties similar to those of truly random binary sequences. This method can be useful in situations where it is necessary to generate random binary sequences, but only decimal sequences are readily available. Random binary sequences are sequences of 0s and 1s that are randomly generated and have no discernible pattern. These sequences are often used in various applications, such as generating keys for encryption and testing the statistical properties of algorithms. In some situations, it may be necessary to generate random binary sequences, but only decimal sequences are readily available. In such cases, it is useful to have a method for obtaining binary random sequences from decimal sequences.

One such method is as follows: take a decimal sequence and divide each number by 2. If the result is a whole number, assign a 0 to the corresponding position in the binary sequence. If the result is not a whole number, assign a 1. For example, given the decimal sequence [8, 7, 6, 5, 4, 3, 2, 1], the resulting binary sequence would be [0, 1, 0, 1, 0, 1, 0, 1].

It can be shown that this method results in binary sequences that have statistical properties similar to those of truly random binary sequences. Specifically, the probability of a 0 or a 1 occurring in a given position is equal, and the occurrence of 0s and 1s is independent from one position to the next. In conclusion, this document has presented a method for obtaining binary random sequences from decimal sequences. The resulting binary sequences have statistical properties similar to those of truly random binary sequences and can be useful in situations where it is necessary to generate random binary sequences, but only decimal sequences are readily available.

In the context of the dynamics of metastable systems, we prove qualitative properties of the invariant measure via a novel framework for hypocoercivity. We use our construction to derive a novel geometric mechanism for the slow evolution of the invariant measure leading up to kinetic simulated annealing. (© 2014 Wiley-VCH Verlag GmbH & Co. KGaA, Weinheim). We consider the Langevin dynamics $\mathrm{d}X_t=-\nabla U(X_t)\,\mathrm{d}t+\sqrt{2\beta^{-1}}\,\mathrm{d}B_t$, $X_0=x_0$, with $U\in C^2(\mathbb{R}^d)$, $\beta>0$.    The goal is to study the convergence of the invariant measure $\mu$ to the Boltzmann-Gibbs measure $\mu_\beta$,   \begin{equation}     \mu_\beta(\mathrm{d}x)=\frac{1}{Z_\beta}\exp\left(-\frac{U(x)}{\beta}\right)\,\mathrm{d}x,\qquad Z_\beta=\int_{\mathbb{R}^d}\exp\left(-\frac U{\beta}\right)\,dx.   \end{equation}    The main result of this work is a new framework for hypocoercivity, which applies to a wide range of situations in which the invariant measure has a slow component.    The main idea is to construct a diffusion process $\tilde{X}_t$ that is "almost" reversible with respect to the invariant measure $\mu$. In other words, the invariant measure of $\tilde{X}_t\,\mathrm{d}t$ is $\mu$.    The diffusion process $\tilde{X}$ is constructed in such a way that its invariant measure is a product measure, and the "fast" component of the dynamics is a reversible process with respect to the fast component of $\mu$.    Under suitable assumptions on the potential $U$, we prove that the invariant measure of the process $\tilde{X}_{\tau(t)}$ converges to the Boltzmann-Gibbs measure $Z_\beta^{-1}\exp(-U(x)/\beta)\,\mathrm{d}x$ as $t\to\infty$.    In particular, the convergence is exponential, and the rate of convergence is given by the second moment of the invariant measure of $\tilde{\mu}$.    As an application, we show that the invariant measure of the kinetic simulated annealing process (KSA) converges to the Boltzmann-Gibbs measure as $T\to\infty$.    A key ingredient in the proof of the hypocoercivity is a new geometric mechanism for the slow evolution of the invariant measures. This mechanism is based on the notion of "local entropy dissipation" introduced in [1].    The framework for hypocoercivity introduced in this work is new and complementary to the framework for hypocoercivity developed in [2, 3, 4]. $\bullet$   The framework for hypocoercivity introduced in [5] can be used to study a wide range of systems that can be described by a linear Fokker-Planck equation with a potential.    The framework for hypocoercivity in this work applies to a much wider class of systems.    In particular, it applies to the so-called "metastable" systems.    This is a class of systems that are typically described by a non-linear Fokker-Planck equation, which is of the form $\partial_t\mu=\partial_x(f(x)\mu)$, $\mu(0,x)=\mu_0(x)$, with $\mu_0$ a probability measure.    In the metastable setting, the invariant measure $\mu$ has a slow component that evolves on a slow time scale.    The main result of this work is a framework for hypocoercivity that applies to the metastable setting.    The framework for hypocoercivity is based on a new geometric mechanism for the slow evolution of $\mu$, and is complementary to the framework developed in [6, 7, 8].    In particular, it applies to the case of the kinetic simulated annealing process.    The hypocoercivity of the KSA process was recently established in [9] using a different framework for hypocoercivity.      $\bullet$   The framework introduced in this work is based on a novel construction of a diffusion process $\tilde{X_t}$ that is "almost" reversible with repect to the invariant measure $\mu$ of the Langevin dynamics.    The diffusion process $\tilde X$ is constructed in such a way that its "fast" component is reversible with respect to the fast component of $X$.    The "slow" component of the diffusion process $\tilde{X}(t)$ is defined as the solution of a deterministic "slow" ODE.    The diffusion process $\tilde\mu$ is constructed in such a way that its fast component is $\mu$.    The invariant measure of the diffusion process $\tilde X$ is a product measure, and the "slow" component of the diffusion process is a reversible process with respect to the fast components of $\mu$.    We prove that the invariant measure of the process $$\tilde X_{\tau(t)},\qquad\tau(t)=\int_0^t f(X_s)\,\mathrm{d}s,$$ converges to the Boltzmann-Gibbs measure (up to an error of order $1/t$) as $t\to\infty$, provided that the "slow" component of $\mu$ is "exponentially" fast.    This is a new geometric mechanism for the slow evolution that is based on the notion of "local" entropy dissipation introduced in [10, 11, 12]. Frustrated magnetic systems, in which the interactions between spins cannot be simultaneously satisfied, are known to exhibit a variety of exotic ground states and low-energy excitations. In this paper, we study the behavior of a frustrated magnetic system at criticality, where the system is poised between different ordered states. We focus on the case of a pyrochlore lattice, which is a three-dimensional lattice with corner-sharing tetrahedra, and consider the case of multiple-k partial order, in which the spins on the lattice form multiple wave-like patterns. We show that the fluctuation-driven selection at criticality leads to a preference for certain wave vectors over others, resulting in a rich phase diagram with multiple ordered phases. Frustrated magnetic systems have attracted a lot of attention in the field of condensed matter physics due to their rich phase diagrams and exotic ground states. These systems are characterized by the presence of competing interactions between the spins, which cannot be simultaneously satisfied. This leads to a frustration of the spins, resulting in a variety of low-energy excitations and ground states.

One particular type of frustrated magnetic system that has garnered significant interest is the pyrochlore lattice, which is a three-dimensional lattice with corner-sharing tetrahedra. The pyrochlore lattice has been shown to exhibit a variety of exotic ground states, including spin liquids and multiple-k partial order, in which the spins form multiple wave-like patterns.

In this paper, we study the behavior of a frustrated magnetic system on the pyrochlore lattice at criticality, where the system is poised between different ordered states. Specifically, we focus on the case of multiple-k partial order and show that the fluctuation-driven selection at criticality leads to a preference for certain wave vectors over others, resulting in a rich phase diagram with multiple ordered phases. In summary, we have studied the behavior of a frustrated magnetic system on the pyrochlore lattice at criticality, with a focus on the case of multiple-k partial order. We have shown that the fluctuation-driven selection at criticality leads to a preference for certain wave vectors over others, resulting in a rich phase diagram with multiple ordered phases. Our results provide insight into the behavior of frustrated magnetic systems at criticality and may have implications for the design of novel materials with novel magnetic properties.

The integer quantum Hall effect (IQHE) is a phenomenon observed in two-dimensional electron systems, in which the longitudinal resistance becomes quantized in units of h/e^2. In this work, we investigate the IQHE in a graphene channel with a p-n junction at a domain wall in a ferroelectric substrate. By applying a strong perpendicular magnetic field, we observe clear quantization of the Hall resistance at low temperatures. Our results suggest that the ferroelectric substrate and the presence of a p-n junction at a domain wall can significantly affect the IQHE in graphene. The IQHE is a fundamental phenomenon in condensed matter physics, first observed by Klaus von Klitzing in 1980 for which he was awarded the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1985. It is characterized by the quantization of the Hall resistance, which is defined as the ratio of the transverse voltage to the longitudinal current in a two-dimensional electron system. The IQHE has been observed in various two-dimensional systems, including semiconductor heterostructures, quantum wells, and graphene.

Graphene, a two-dimensional material composed of carbon atoms arranged in a honeycomb lattice, has unique electronic properties due to its massless Dirac fermions and high mobility. In particular, graphene has been shown to exhibit the IQHE under a strong perpendicular magnetic field, with the quantized Hall resistance being independent of the carrier density and the temperature. However, the IQHE in graphene can be significantly modified by external factors such as substrate effects, disorder, and interactions.

In this work, we investigate the IQHE in a graphene channel with a p-n junction at a domain wall in a ferroelectric substrate. A p-n junction is a region in a semiconductor where the concentration of dopants changes abruptly, creating a region of positive charge (p-type) and a region of negative charge (n-type). Domain walls in ferroelectric materials are regions where the direction of the spontaneous electric polarization changes abruptly. By applying a strong perpendicular magnetic field, we measure the Hall resistance of the graphene channel as a function of temperature and carrier density. In conclusion, we have studied the IQHE in a graphene channel with a p-n junction at a domain wall in a ferroelectric substrate. By applying a strong perpendicular magnetic field, we observe clear quantization of the Hall resistance at low temperatures. Our results suggest that the ferroelectric substrate and the presence of a p-n junction at a domain wall can significantly affect the IQHE in graphene. Further research is needed to fully understand the underlying mechanisms responsible for these effects.

In this paper, we prove that separable subgroups of a topological group have a bounded packing property. This result has important consequences for the study of group actions on metric spaces and the structure of topological groups. A topological group is a group equipped with a topology that is compatible with the group operations of multiplication and inversion. One important property of topological groups is separability, which means that the group can be approximated by countable dense subsets. Separable groups have a rich structure and have been studied extensively in the literature.

A packing of a subset A of a metric space (X, d) is a collection of non-overlapping balls with radius r > 0 such that the union of the balls covers A. The packing number of A, denoted by η(A), is the maximal cardinality of a packing of A. Bounded packing is a strong form of compactness that requires the packing number of any subset of the space to be bounded.

In this paper, we prove that separable subgroups of a topological group have a bounded packing property. To do this, we use the fact that separable groups can be approximated by countable dense subsets and the properties of group actions on metric spaces. Our result on separable subgroups and bounded packing has important consequences for the study of group actions on metric spaces and the structure of topological groups. It allows us to better understand the behavior of separable groups and their actions, and has potential applications in various areas of mathematics and physics.

In this study, we investigate the effects of hydrostatic pressure on the infrared spectra of unoriented single-walled carbon nanotube films. We used a diamond anvil cell to apply pressures up to 10 GPa and measured the transmission spectra in the mid-infrared region. Our results show that the vibrational modes of the nanotubes are red-shifted and broadened with increasing pressure, indicating a decrease in the average bond length and an increase in bond angle deviation from the ideal value. We also observe a pressure-induced shift in the bandgap energy of the nanotubes. These results provide insight into the structural changes of carbon nanotubes under hydrostatic pressure and have potential applications in pressure-sensitive sensors and other nanoelectromechanical systems. Carbon nanotubes (CNTs) are a promising material for a variety of applications due to their unique mechanical, electrical, and thermal properties. However, their behavior under external stimuli, such as hydrostatic pressure, is not well understood. Infrared (IR) spectroscopy is a powerful tool for studying the vibrational modes of CNTs, which can provide insight into their structural changes under external conditions.

In this work, we use IR spectroscopy to study the effects of hydrostatic pressure on unoriented single-walled CNT (SWCNT) films. Unoriented SWCNT films are a good model system for studying the pressure-induced changes in CNTs because they are not restricted by the constraints of CNT alignment, allowing for a more comprehensive understanding of the effects of pressure on CNTs. In summary, we have performed infrared spectroscopic studies on unoriented single-walled carbon nanotube films under hydrostatic pressure using a diamond anvil cell. Our results show that the vibrational modes of the nanotubes are red-shifted and broadened with increasing pressure, indicating a decrease in the average bond length and an increase in bond angle deviation from the ideal value. We also observe a pressure-induced shift in the bandgap energy of the nanotubes. These findings provide important insights into the structural changes of carbon nanotubes under hydrostatic pressure and have potential applications in pressure-sensitive sensors and other nanoelectromechanical systems.

In this paper, we propose a novel approach for improving the performance of cache-enabled wireless networks using opportunistic interference alignment (OIA). By carefully selecting the transmitters and receivers in each transmission, we are able to align the interference patterns in a way that significantly reduces the amount of interference experienced by the receivers. We demonstrate through simulations that our approach leads to significant improvements in the throughput and energy efficiency of the network. Cache-enabled wireless networks have gained significant attention in recent years due to their ability to reduce the amount of traffic transmitted over the network by storing frequently requested content at the edge of the network. However, the performance of cache-enabled networks is often limited by interference, which can significantly reduce the throughput and energy efficiency of the network.

Opportunistic interference alignment (OIA) is a promising approach for improving the performance of wireless networks by carefully selecting the transmitters and receivers in each transmission in order to align the interference patterns in a way that reduces the amount of interference experienced by the receivers. In this paper, we propose the use of OIA in cache-enabled wireless networks and demonstrate through simulations that our approach leads to significant improvements in the throughput and energy efficiency of the network. In this paper, we have proposed the use of opportunistic interference alignment (OIA) in cache-enabled wireless networks as a way to improve the performance of the network. Through simulations, we have demonstrated that our approach leads to significant improvements in the throughput and energy efficiency of the network. This suggests that OIA is a promising approach for improving the performance of cache-enabled wireless networks and warrants further investigation.

In this paper, we propose a real-time database Quality of Service (QoS)-aware service selection protocol for Mobile Ad-hoc NETworks (MANETs). The proposed protocol aims to ensure the delivery of real-time database queries with minimal delay and high reliability, while also considering the limited resources of MANETs. Our protocol utilizes a QoS prediction model to predict the QoS of potential service providers and selects the provider with the highest predicted QoS. We also present simulation results demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed protocol in terms of query delay and reliability. MANETs are decentralized networks that can operate without the need for a fixed infrastructure, making them an attractive solution for various applications such as military operations and disaster relief. However, the dynamic and unpredictable nature of MANETs poses challenges in terms of delivering real-time database queries with minimal delay and high reliability.

To address this challenge, various service selection protocols have been proposed. These protocols aim to select the most suitable service provider based on various factors such as resource availability, distance to the provider, and QoS. However, most existing protocols do not consider the real-time nature of database queries and the limited resources of MANETs, which can lead to poor performance in terms of query delay and reliability.

In this paper, we propose a real-time database QoS-aware service selection protocol for MANETs that addresses these challenges. The proposed protocol utilizes a QoS prediction model to predict the QoS of potential service providers and selects the provider with the highest predicted QoS. The prediction model takes into account various factors such as the provider's resource availability and the distance to the provider, as well as the real-time nature of the query. In summary, we have presented a real-time database QoS-aware service selection protocol for MANETs that aims to ensure the delivery of real-time database queries with minimal delay and high reliability. The proposed protocol utilizes a QoS prediction model to predict the QoS of potential service providers and selects the provider with the highest predicted QoS. Our simulation results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed protocol in terms of query delay and reliability.

We study propagation of the matrix product states (MPS) matrix entries in systems whose Hilbert space can be expressed as a direct sum of irreducible Hilbert spaces. We derive an analytic formula for the matrix product states matrix elements and an equation for the evolution of their support vectors which allows us to calculate the time-dependence of different types of operators. We use the method to study propagation of one- and two-site operators in the ergodic quantum Heisenberg XXZ spin chain. For the one-site operator we analyze the decay of the Loschmidt echo in arbitrary dimension. For the two-site operator we apply and prove the method for the case of XX model in the thermodynamic limit. Also, we show that the MPS approach agrees with exact diagonalization for arbitrary dimension of the Hilbert space for the case when the state is localized. In addition, we consider the entanglement evolution in Heisenberg XXZ spin-1 /2 chain which is analytically studied in the = 0 limit. Finally, we briefly discuss the implementation of the method in the case when the Hamiltonian is described in terms of the local matrix products whose parameters depend on the position. Matrix product states (MPS) are a powerful method to represent pure quantum states. MPS can be thought of as a tensor network with the matrices attached to the links, see Fig.1. The MPS representation of a state is an exact description of the state and can be obtained in a straightforward way from the density matrix by tracing out the degrees of freedom corresponding to the auxiliary space. The MPS representation can be used to simulate quantum systems with a large number of degrees of freedom.  Figure 1:  A graphical representation of the matrix product state (MPS).  The MPS approach has been successfully applied to study the dynamics of one-dimensional quantum systems. The MPS approach has been used to study the quantum phase transitions in the transverse field Ising model [1, 2], the dynamics of quantum systems after a global quench [3], and to study the thermalization of isolated quantum systems [4, 5, 6, 7].  In this work we study propagation of the matrix product states matrix entries in systems whose Hilbert space can be written as a direct sum of irreducible Hilbert spaces. We derive an analytic formula for the matrix product states matrices elements and an equation for the evolution of their supports.  We use the method to study propagation of one-site operators in the ergodic quantum Heisenberg XX model in arbitrary dimension. We analyze the decay of the Loschmidt echo for the one-site operator in arbitrary dimension. For the two-site operator we apply and prove our method for the case of XX model in the \(N\to\infty\) limit. We also study the entanglement evolution in Heisenberg XXZ spin-\(1/2\) chain. We obtain an analytic expression for the time-dependence of the von Neumann entropy in the \(N\to\mathrm{const}\) limit. The method can be used to study the dynamics of operators of arbitrary size in systems whose Hilbert space can be written as the direct sum of irreducible Hilbert spaces. We apply the method to the case of the XX model in the \(N\rightarrow\infty\) limit and show that it agrees with exact diagonalization for arbitrary dimension of the system. We also show that the MPS approach agrees with exact diagonalization in the case when the state is localized. We also discuss the implementation of the method in the case of the Hamiltonian being described in terms of the local matrix products whose matrices depend on the position. In this paper, we investigate the quantum speed limit of optimal controlled phasegates for trapped neutral atoms. We derive a general formula for the quantum speed limit and apply it to a specific system of trapped neutral atoms. Our results show that the quantum speed limit can be significantly reduced by the presence of external noise and decoherence, and that the optimal control strategy is strongly dependent on the specific parameters of the system. Quantum computation and quantum information processing hold great promise for solving problems that are intractable on classical computers. One key element of quantum computation is the ability to perform fast and accurate quantum gates, which are used to manipulate the quantum state of a system. Controlled phasegates are a type of quantum gate that can be used to perform arbitrary rotations on a qubit, and have been demonstrated using a variety of physical systems, including trapped neutral atoms.

However, the speed at which a quantum gate can be performed is limited by the laws of quantum mechanics, a phenomenon known as the quantum speed limit. Understanding the quantum speed limit is important for the design and optimization of quantum algorithms, as well as for the development of high-fidelity quantum gates.

In this paper, we derive a general formula for the quantum speed limit of optimal controlled phasegates for trapped neutral atoms, and apply it to a specific system. We consider the effects of external noise and decoherence on the quantum speed limit, and explore the optimal control strategies for minimizing the quantum speed limit in the presence of these factors. Our results demonstrate that the quantum speed limit of optimal controlled phasegates for trapped neutral atoms can be significantly reduced by the presence of external noise and decoherence. The optimal control strategy is strongly dependent on the specific parameters of the system, and can be used to minimize the quantum speed limit and improve the fidelity of the quantum gate. These findings have important implications for the design and optimization of quantum algorithms and the development of high-fidelity quantum gates using trapped neutral atoms.

In this paper, we propose a modified gravity theory based on the F(R) formalism that can unify inflation with late-time acceleration and satisfy the universality of the viscous ratio bound. By introducing a non-singular term into the action, we are able to avoid the singularities that plague many inflationary models while simultaneously providing a mechanism for driving the late-time acceleration of the universe. In addition, our model satisfies the viscous ratio bound, which has been shown to be a universal feature of the late-time universe. We demonstrate the viability of our model through detailed calculations and comparisons with observations. One of the major unsolved problems in modern cosmology is the mystery of the origin and evolution of the universe. Inflationary models have been proposed as a way to explain the observed uniformity and flatness of the universe, as well as the origin of structure in the form of galaxies and large-scale structure. However, many inflationary models suffer from singularities and other issues that limit their viability.

On the other hand, the late-time acceleration of the universe, as evidenced by the observation of supernovae and the cosmic microwave background, is another major mystery that has yet to be fully understood. The most widely accepted explanation for this acceleration is the presence of dark energy, a hypothetical form of energy that permeates all of space and drives the expansion of the universe.

In this paper, we propose a modified gravity theory based on the F(R) formalism that can unify inflation with late-time acceleration and satisfy the universality of the viscous ratio bound. By introducing a non-singular term into the action, we are able to avoid the singularities that plague many inflationary models while simultaneously providing a mechanism for driving the late-time acceleration of the universe. In addition, our model satisfies the viscous ratio bound, which has been shown to be a universal feature of the late-time universe. In summary, we have proposed a modified gravity theory based on the F(R) formalism that can unify inflation with late-time acceleration and satisfy the universality of the viscous ratio bound. By introducing a non-singular term into the action, we are able to avoid the singularities that plague many inflationary models while simultaneously providing a mechanism for driving the late-time acceleration of the universe. We have demonstrated the viability of our model through detailed calculations and comparisons with observations. Our results suggest that this model may provide a promising avenue for further study and a potential solution to some of the major mysteries of cosmology.

Conformal inference is a common method for estimating the performance of prediction methods. However, the method has several drawbacks. Firstly, it is limited in the number of predictions and hence the number of training sets to be trained. Secondly, it requires the use of multiple layers to learn the inference and the inference weights are often not available. To address these issues, we introduce a novel joint-packaging method for convex inference. This joint-packaging method is designed to be flexible and adaptable to any number of different inference methods. We evaluate our joint-packaging method on two datasets: a real-world and a real-world dataset. We show that our joint-packaging method outperforms the state-of-the-art methods in several performance benchmarks and show that it outperforms the state-of-the-art methods in a number of real-world datasets. Our joint-packaging method also outperforms the state-of-the-art methods in the performance of the proposed prediction framework in several real-world datasets. Conformal Inference.multi. High resolution observations of Ellerman bombs (Esbs) are presented. The observations were taken in Hα and He i λ10830 at Observatorio del Teide of Tenerife. We find that the structures of these He i bombs are strikingly similar to those of Hα, although their spatial distribution and dynamics are different from those of the Hα lines. The high temporal resolution observations of the Hα and He i lines show that the high-speed flows in the Hα bombs (up to ∼130 km s−1) are accompanied by small brightenings in He i λ10830 (up to 10–20%). The velocity vector field derived from the high-resolution observations of the He i λ10830 data confirms the results of the low-resolution observations: there is a strong correlation between the high-speed flows in the Hα and He i bombs. The strong correlation between the high-speed flows in the Hα and the He i bombs suggests a possible association between the reconnection in the photosphere and the strong He i bombs. Furthermore, the appearance of a Hα loop arcade around the top of one of the He i bombs supports such a reconnection configuration. Ellerman bombs (Esbs) were discovered in the late 1970s (Ellerman ). They are large-scale, brightenings in the solar photosphere, which are often observed at the edges of active regions (Ellerman ). The typical size of an Esb is 1–2 Mm in the vertical direction and ∼50 Mm in the horizontal direction (Ellerman ).    The physical nature of Esbs is not well understood. The most widely accepted theory is that Esbs are caused by the magnetic reconnection (Ellerman ). This is based on the observation that the bright cores of Esbs are located at the edge of a strong magnetic field region, and that the bright cores of Esbs are located near the footpoints of the magnetic field lines connecting the magnetic field regions. Therefore, it is reasonable to expect that the magnetic reconnection is taking place at the footpoints of the magnetic field lines connecting a magnetic field region and an Esb.    The reconnection in the photosphere is expected to occur at the footpoints of the magnetic field lines because of the magnetic field connectivity. However, there is no direct evidence for this magnetic reconnection. In the present paper, we present high-resolution observations of Esbs to directly investigate the magnetic reconnection in the photosphere. 1. Quantum information and statistical mechanics are two important fields that have had a significant impact on our understanding of the physical world. In this document, we provide an introduction to these fields, outlining the basic principles and discussing their interconnections. We also discuss some of the exciting research directions in these fields, including the development of quantum algorithms and the exploration of the relationship between quantum information and thermodynamics. Quantum mechanics is the foundation of the field of quantum information, which deals with the representation, manipulation, and transmission of quantum information. At the heart of quantum mechanics is the idea that the fundamental building blocks of the universe, such as atoms and photons, can exhibit both wave-like and particle-like behavior. This duality is captured by the wave function, a mathematical description of the state of a quantum system.

One of the key principles of quantum mechanics is the uncertainty principle, which states that certain pairs of physical properties, such as position and momentum, cannot be simultaneously measured with arbitrary precision. This uncertainty has important consequences for the way in which quantum systems can be manipulated and controlled.

Statistical mechanics, on the other hand, is the study of the statistical behavior of large ensembles of particles, such as gases, liquids, and solids. The laws of statistical mechanics provide a framework for understanding the behavior of these systems at the macroscopic level, in terms of the statistical distribution of their microscopic properties.

The relationship between quantum mechanics and statistical mechanics is a subject of ongoing research and debate. On the one hand, quantum mechanics provides a microscopic description of individual particles, while statistical mechanics deals with the statistical behavior of large ensembles of particles. On the other hand, there are strong connections between the two fields, as the behavior of quantum systems can often be understood in terms of statistical principles. Quantum information and statistical mechanics are two fields that have had a profound impact on our understanding of the physical world. Both fields continue to be active areas of research, with many exciting developments on the horizon. In particular, the development of quantum algorithms and the exploration of the relationship between quantum information and thermodynamics are key areas of research that hold great promise for the future.

Cardiogram signal processing is a crucial aspect of modern healthcare, as it allows for the monitoring and diagnosis of heart conditions. Traditional methods of cardiogram signal processing have limitations in terms of their ability to accurately measure boundary values and energy, which can lead to errors in diagnosis. In this paper, we propose a new method for cardiogram signal processing that utilizes adaptive optimal techniques to more accurately measure boundary values and energy. Cardiogram signals, also known as electrocardiograms (ECGs), are a vital tool in the diagnosis and monitoring of heart conditions. These signals are generated by the electrical activity of the heart and can be used to identify abnormalities such as arrhythmias and heart attacks. However, traditional methods of cardiogram signal processing have limitations in terms of their ability to accurately measure boundary values and energy, which can lead to errors in diagnosis.

To address this issue, we propose a new method for cardiogram signal processing that utilizes adaptive optimal techniques. These techniques involve the use of algorithms that are able to adapt and optimize their performance based on the specific characteristics of the signal being processed. This allows for a more accurate measurement of boundary values and energy, which can improve the accuracy of diagnosis. In conclusion, the use of adaptive optimal techniques for cardiogram signal processing represents a significant advance in the field of healthcare. By accurately measuring boundary values and energy, this method has the potential to improve the accuracy of diagnosis and ultimately lead to better patient outcomes. Further research is needed to fully evaluate the effectiveness of this approach, but the initial results are promising.

Selfish routing refers to the behavior of individuals in a network trying to optimize their own utility or cost, without necessarily considering the effect on the overall network. This behavior can lead to suboptimal outcomes for the network as a whole. In dynamic flows, the routing decisions are made in real-time and can change frequently, making it difficult to achieve an efficient global solution. This paper discusses the challenges and potential solutions for selfish routing in dynamic flow networks. Networks, such as transportation networks or communication networks, play a crucial role in our daily lives. The efficiency of these networks depends on the routing decisions made by the individuals or entities using them. However, when these routing decisions are based solely on the individual's self-interest, it can lead to suboptimal outcomes for the overall network. This behavior is known as selfish routing.

In dynamic flow networks, the routing decisions are made in real-time and can change frequently due to changes in the network conditions or the preferences of the individuals. This makes it difficult to achieve an efficient global solution. Selfish routing can lead to congestions, delays, and inefficiencies in the network. Selfish routing is a significant challenge in dynamic flow networks as it can lead to suboptimal outcomes for the overall network. To address this issue, various approaches have been proposed, such as price mechanisms, network design, and game-theoretic approaches. Further research is needed to understand the effectiveness of these approaches and to develop new solutions for selfish routing in dynamic flow networks.

In this paper, we present a novel approach for combining dithered images taken with the Advanced Camera for Surveys (ACS) on the Hubble Space Telescope. Our method involves directly fitting a model to the dithered images, rather than using traditional image alignment and stacking techniques. We demonstrate that this approach leads to improved image quality and reduced noise, particularly in the presence of bright sources or large extended structures. The ACS instrument on the Hubble Space Telescope is a powerful tool for studying the universe at a wide range of wavelengths, including visible and near-infrared light. One of the key features of ACS is the ability to take dithered images, which involves slightly offsetting the pointing of the telescope between exposures. This allows for better sampling of the point spread function and can help to mitigate the effects of detector artifacts and cosmic rays.

However, combining dithered images into a single, final image can be challenging. Traditional approaches involve aligning the images and then stacking them, which can be time-consuming and may not always yield optimal results. In particular, image alignment can be difficult in the presence of bright sources or large extended structures, which can cause the images to shift relative to one another.

In this work, we propose a new method for combining dithered ACS images that involves directly fitting a model to the data. This approach has several benefits. First, it allows for the incorporation of prior knowledge about the expected characteristics of the final image, such as the point spread function and the pixel scale. Second, it allows for the simultaneous fitting of multiple images, which can lead to improved signal-to-noise ratio and reduced noise compared to stacking. Finally, it allows for the inclusion of additional parameters, such as background level and sky brightness, which can improve the quality of the final image. In summary, we have presented a novel approach for combining dithered ACS images that involves directly fitting a model to the data. Our method offers several advantages over traditional image alignment and stacking techniques, including the incorporation of prior knowledge, the simultaneous fitting of multiple images, and the inclusion of additional parameters. We have demonstrated that this approach leads to improved image quality and reduced noise, particularly in the presence of bright sources or large extended structures. This method has the potential to significantly improve the scientific output of ACS and other imaging instruments.

In this paper, we study the large $\Omega$-deformations of $\mathcal N=2^{*}$ SYM theory in the Nekrasov-Shatashvili limit. We find that the partition function of the theory exhibits a non-trivial dependence on the deformation parameters, indicating the presence of interesting dynamical effects. The $\mathcal N=2^{*}$ SYM theory is a supersymmetric gauge theory that has received a lot of attention in recent years due to its rich mathematical structure and potential for applications in various areas of physics. One of the key features of this theory is the presence of a mass parameter for the adjoint matter fields, which breaks the conformal symmetry of the theory and introduces a mass scale.

The Nekrasov-Shatashvili limit is a particular limit of the theory in which the mass parameter is taken to infinity while the gauge coupling is held fixed. In this limit, the theory reduces to a topological field theory, which is believed to capture the low-energy dynamics of the original theory.

One way to study the properties of the $\mathcal N=2^{*}$ SYM theory is by deforming it with the so-called $\Omega$-background. This deformation involves introducing a non-trivial metric on the spacetime and adding a corresponding term to the action of the theory. The deformation parameters are the components of the metric, which are known as the "$\Omega$-deformation parameters".

In this paper, we investigate the large $\Omega$-deformations of $\mathcal N=2^{*}$ SYM theory in the Nekrasov-Shatashvili limit. Our main aim is to understand how the partition function of the theory depends on the deformation parameters in this limit. We have studied the large $\Omega$-deformations of $\mathcal N=2^{*}$ SYM theory in the Nekrasov-Shatashvili limit and found that the partition function exhibits a non-trivial dependence on the deformation parameters. This indicates the presence of interesting dynamical effects in the theory and may have important consequences for our understanding of the low-energy dynamics of $\mathcal N=2^{*}$ SYM. Further investigation of these effects may lead to a deeper understanding of this important gauge theory and its applications.

The fractional maximal operator is a well-known tool in the study of function spaces, particularly in the context of harmonic analysis. In this paper, we explore the properties of the fractional maximal operator in the setting of Orlicz spaces. We demonstrate that the fractional maximal operator is bounded on Orlicz spaces, and we provide estimates for its operator norm. We also discuss the relationship between the fractional maximal operator and other classical operators, such as the Hardy-Littlewood maximal operator and the fractional integral operator. Orlicz spaces are a class of function spaces that are defined using a convex, increasing function called an Orlicz function. These spaces generalize the classical Lebesgue spaces, and have been extensively studied in the literature due to their wide range of applications.

The fractional maximal operator is a non-linear operator that is defined as follows: given a function f and a parameter 0 < s < 1, the fractional maximal operator is defined as

M_{s}f(x) = \sup_{r > 0} \left( \frac{1}{r^{n-s}} \int_{B(x,r)} |f(y)|^{s} dy \right)^{1/s}

where B(x,r) denotes the ball centered at x with radius r. The fractional maximal operator is a powerful tool for studying the regularity and smoothness of functions, as well as for proving various estimates and inequalities.

In this paper, we investigate the properties of the fractional maximal operator in the context of Orlicz spaces. Specifically, we show that the fractional maximal operator is bounded on Orlicz spaces, and we provide estimates for its operator norm. We also discuss the relationship between the fractional maximal operator and other classical operators, such as the Hardy-Littlewood maximal operator and the fractional integral operator. In conclusion, we have shown that the fractional maximal operator is bounded on Orlicz spaces, and we have provided estimates for its operator norm. We have also discussed the relationship between the fractional maximal operator and other classical operators in this setting. Our results contribute to the understanding of the fractional maximal operator in the context of Orlicz spaces, and may have applications in various areas of mathematics and physics.
